## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Atomic Packing Factor (APF) for FCC

The **APF** is the fraction of unit-cell volume occupied by atoms, assuming hard-sphere contact.

**Step 1 — Identify atoms per FCC unit cell.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Corner atoms} &: 8 \times \tfrac{1}{8} = 1 \text{ atom} \\
\text{Face-centre atoms} &: 6 \times \tfrac{1}{2} = 3 \text{ atoms} \\
\text{Total } n_{FCC} &= 1 + 3 = 4 \text{ atoms per unit cell}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Relate lattice parameter *a* to atomic radius *R*.**

In FCC, atoms touch along the **face diagonal**. The face diagonal = 4R, and the face diagonal = $a\sqrt{2}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
4R &= a\sqrt{2} \\
\Rightarrow R &= \dfrac{a\sqrt{2}}{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute total volume of 4 atoms.**

$$
V_{\text{atoms}} = 4 \times \left(\tfrac{4}{3}\pi R^3\right) = \tfrac{16}{3}\pi R^3
$$

**Step 4 — Express atom volume in terms of *a*.**

Substitute $R = \dfrac{a\sqrt{2}}{4}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{atoms}} &= \tfrac{16}{3}\pi \left(\dfrac{a\sqrt{2}}{4}\right)^3 \\
&= \tfrac{16}{3}\pi \cdot \dfrac{a^3 (2\sqrt{2})}{64} \\
&= \tfrac{16}{3}\pi \cdot \dfrac{2\sqrt{2}\,a^3}{64} \\
&= \tfrac{\pi a^3 \sqrt{2}}{6}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Volume of the unit cell.**

$$
V_{\text{cell}} = a^3
$$

**Step 6 — Calculate APF.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
APF_{FCC} &= \dfrac{V_{\text{atoms}}}{V_{\text{cell}}} = \dfrac{\pi a^3 \sqrt{2} / 6}{a^3} \\
&= \dfrac{\pi \sqrt{2}}{6} = \dfrac{\pi}{3\sqrt{2}} \\
&\approx 0.7405 \quad \text{or} \quad 74.05\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** FCC is the **most efficiently packed** common metal structure — that is why FCC metals (Cu, Al, Au) are denser and more ductile.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Theoretical Density from Unit Cell

This derivation answers the *classic KTU 3-mark question*: *"The atomic radius, atomic mass, and crystal structure of a metal are given. Find its density."*

**Step 1 — General formula.**

$$
\rho = \dfrac{n \cdot A}{V_c \cdot N_A}
$$

where $n$ = atoms/unit cell, $A$ = molar mass (g/mol), $V_c$ = unit cell volume (cm³), $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol⁻¹.

**Step 2 — Worked numerical example.** *Given: FCC copper, $a = 0.3615$ nm, $A = 63.54$ g/mol.*

**Step 2a — Number of atoms per FCC cell:**

$$
n_{FCC} = 4
$$

**Step 2b — Volume of unit cell in cm³:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a &= 0.3615 \text{ nm} = 0.3615 \times 10^{-7} \text{ cm} = 3.615 \times 10^{-8} \text{ cm} \\
V_c &= a^3 = (3.615 \times 10^{-8})^3 \\
&= 4.724 \times 10^{-23} \text{ cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2c — Plug into density formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_{Cu} &= \dfrac{4 \times 63.54}{4.724 \times 10^{-23} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}} \\
&= \dfrac{254.16}{28.45} \\
&\approx 8.93 \text{ g/cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare to experimental value.**

Measured density of Cu = **8.96 g/cm³**. Error < 0.4 % — excellent agreement.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Trick:** Always convert lattice parameter from nm or Å to cm before cubing, otherwise unit mismatch gives impossible answers.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hall-Petch Strengthening (Empirical Justification)

The Hall-Petch relation explains why fine-grained metals are stronger than coarse-grained ones.

**Empirical Starting Point:**

$$
\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k \cdot d^{-1/2}
$$

**Physical Origin (Dislocation Pile-Up Theory, E. O. Hall, 1951; N. J. Petch, 1953):**

Consider a slip band impinging on a grain boundary. Dislocations pile up against the boundary, generating a back-stress. The number of dislocations in a pile-up of length $L$ (≈ grain diameter $d$) is:

$$
n \propto \dfrac{\tau \cdot L}{G \cdot b}
$$

where $G$ = shear modulus, $b$ = Burgers vector, $\tau$ = applied shear stress.

The stress concentration at the pile-up tip is:

$$
\tau_{\text{tip}} = n \cdot \tau \propto \dfrac{\tau^2 L}{G b}
$$

Slip propagates into the next grain when $\tau_{\text{tip}}$ reaches a critical value $\tau_c$ (a property of the boundary):

$$
\tau_c \propto \dfrac{\tau^2 \cdot d}{G b}
$$

Solving for the applied stress $\tau$ required to yield:

$$
\tau \propto \sqrt{\dfrac{\tau_c \cdot G \cdot b}{d}} \propto d^{-1/2}
$$

Converting shear to tensile stress and absorbing constants:

$$
\boxed{\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k \cdot d^{-1/2}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** Halving the grain diameter increases yield strength by **41 %** (since $1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707$, the strength term rises by factor $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.41$). This is why modern HSLA steels are thermomechanically processed to sub-micron grain sizes.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Schmid's Law (Critical Resolved Shear Stress)

When a single crystal is loaded in tension, only the **resolved shear stress** on the slip plane in the slip direction drives dislocation motion.

**Step 1 — Resolve the applied tensile force on the slip plane.**

Slip-plane area: $A / \cos\phi$, where $A$ = cross-section, $\phi$ = angle between tensile axis and slip-plane normal.

$$
F_{\text{normal to slip plane}} = F \cdot \cos\phi
$$

**Step 2 — Resolve onto the slip direction.**

$$
F_{\text{slip direction}} = F \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \cos\lambda
$$

where $\lambda$ = angle between tensile axis and slip direction.

**Step 3 — Resolved shear stress.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau &= \dfrac{F_{\text{slip direction}}}{A_{\text{slip plane}}} = \dfrac{F \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \cos\lambda}{A / \cos\phi} \\
&= \dfrac{F}{A} \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \cos\lambda \\
&= \sigma \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \cos\lambda
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Slip initiates when $\tau = \tau_{CRSS}$:**

$$
\boxed{\tau_{CRSS} = \sigma_y \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \cos\lambda = \sigma_y \cdot m}
$$

where $m = \cos\phi \cos\lambda$ = **Schmid factor**.

**Step 5 — Maximum Schmid factor.** For $\phi = \lambda = 45°$:

$$
m_{\max} = \cos 45° \cdot \cos 45° = \tfrac{1}{2}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** A single crystal is *strongest* when the slip direction is parallel ($\lambda = 0°$) or perpendicular ($\lambda = 90°$) to the tensile axis ($m = 0$), and *weakest* when loaded at **45°** ($m = 0.5$).

---

### 3.5 Summary Table of Key Derived Quantities

| Quantity | Derived Value | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $APF_{FCC}$ | 0.7405 | Maximum packing efficiency for cubic metals |
| $APF_{BCC}$ | 0.6802 | Less efficient packing, more open structure |
| $APF_{HCP}$ | 0.7405 | Equal to FCC — both are close-packed |
| $\sigma_y \propto d^{-1/2}$ | Hall-Petch | Smaller grains ⇒ more boundaries ⇒ stronger |
| $\tau_{CRSS} = \sigma m$ | Schmid's Law | Resolved shear on slip system governs yielding |
| $\rho = nA/(V_c N_A)$ | Density formula | Connect macroscopic mass to atomic packing |

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: Define ceramics. List any four characteristic properties.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Ceramics are inorganic, non-metallic solids composed of metallic and non-metallic elements bonded by predominantly ionic and/or covalent bonds.

**Four characteristic properties:**
1. **High hardness** and abrasion resistance (e.g., SiC, Al₂O₃).
2. **High melting point** and refractoriness (e.g., Al₂O₃ melts at 2072 °C).
3. **High compressive strength** but **low tensile strength** and **brittleness**.
4. **Electrical and thermal insulation** behaviour (most ceramics; SiC is an exception).
5. *(Bonus: chemical inertness and oxidation resistance.)*

---

#### **Question 2: What is metallic bonding? How does it give rise to ductility in metals?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Metallic Bonding:** A bond formed when metallic atoms contribute their valence electrons to a shared, delocalized "electron sea" or "electron gas." The positive metal ions (cations) are embedded in this cloud of mobile electrons, and electrostatic attraction between the cations and the electron sea holds the structure together.

**Why it leads to ductility:**
- The metallic bond is **non-directional** — there is no preferred orientation in space.
- Under shear stress, planes of positive ions can **slide past each other** without rupturing bonds, because the electron sea immediately redistributes to maintain cohesion.
- This sliding occurs through the motion of **dislocations** (line defects), enabling large plastic deformation.
- In contrast, ceramics have *directional* ionic/covalent bonds, so bond rupture occurs almost immediately upon shear → **brittle fracture**.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the three common crystal structures of metals (BCC, FCC, HCP) with neat sketches, atomic packing factors, coordination numbers, and two examples each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction:** Metals crystallize into a small number of highly symmetric arrangements. The three most important are Body-Centred Cubic (BCC), Face-Centred Cubic (FCC), and Hexagonal Close-Packed (HCP).

**(i) Body-Centred Cubic (BCC):**
- **Sketch description:** A cube with 8 atoms at corners and 1 atom at the body centre.
- **Atoms per unit cell:** $8 \times (1/8) + 1 = 2$
- **Coordination Number (CN):** 8
- **APF:** $\dfrac{\pi \sqrt{3}}{8} \approx 0.68$
- **Examples:** α-Fe (room temperature), Cr, W, Mo, V, Na
- **Slip systems:** 48 (high CRSS, hence less ductile)

**(ii) Face-Centred Cubic (FCC):**
- **Sketch description:** A cube with 8 corner atoms and 6 face-centre atoms.
- **Atoms per unit cell:** $8 \times (1/8) + 6 \times (1/2) = 4$
- **CN:** 12
- **APF:** $\dfrac{\pi}{3\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.74$
- **Examples:** Cu, Al, Ni, Ag, Au, γ-Fe (above 912 °C)
- **Slip systems:** 12 (low CRSS, hence very ductile)

**(iii) Hexagonal Close-Packed (HCP):**
- **Sketch description:** Hexagonal prism with atoms at 12 corners, 2 face centres, and 3 interior atoms in a half-cell layer.
- **Atoms per unit cell:** 6
- **CN:** 12
- **APF:** 0.74
- **Examples:** Mg, Zn, Ti, Co, Be
- **Slip systems:** 3 (basal only, hence limited ductility)

**Conclusion:** The APF and number of slip systems govern the ductility and formability of metals. FCC is the most ductile; HCP is the least.

---

**(b)** Discuss the four major strengthening mechanisms in metals with relevant equations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Metals are strengthened by obstructing **dislocation motion**. The four primary mechanisms are:

**(1) Grain Boundary Strengthening (Hall-Petch):**

$$
\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k \cdot d^{-1/2}
$$

Smaller grain size *d* ⇒ more grain boundaries ⇒ more resistance to dislocation motion. Used in HSLA steels, fine-grained aluminium alloys.

**(2) Solid Solution Strengthening:**

$$
\Delta\tau \propto G \cdot \varepsilon^{3/2} \cdot c^{1/2}
$$

Solute atoms (substitutional or interstitial) introduce local lattice strains that pin dislocations. *Examples:* Zn in Cu (brass), C in Fe (steel).

**(3) Strain (Work) Hardening:**

$$
\tau = \tau_0 + K \cdot \gamma^n
$$

Cold working increases dislocation density; dislocations entangle and resist further motion. *Example:* Cold-rolled steel sheets.

**(4) Precipitation (Age) Hardening:**

Heat treatment produces fine, hard, coherent precipitates (e.g., θ' phase in Al-Cu alloys) that obstruct dislocation motion. Three stages: solution treatment → quench → age at moderate temperature.

**Conclusion:** All four mechanisms function by impeding dislocation motion. They are often combined in modern alloys (e.g., maraging steels use all four).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Compare the properties of metals and ceramics. Explain the types of ceramics (traditional vs advanced) with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | Metals | Ceramics |
|---|---|---|
| Bonding | Non-directional metallic | Directional ionic/covalent |
| Hardness | Moderate | Very High |
| Ductility | High (10–40 % EL) | Almost nil (< 1 %) |
| Tensile Strength | High (200–2000 MPa) | Low (100–500 MPa) |
| Compressive Strength | High | Very High (2000–5000 MPa) |
| Melting Point | Moderate (660–1538 °C) | Very High (1500–3500 °C) |
| Electrical Conductivity | High ($10^7$ S/m) | Insulator (≤ $10^{-10}$ S/m) |
| Thermal Conductivity | High | Low (exceptions: BeO, SiC) |
| Toughness | High (100 J) | Very Low (1–5 J) |
| Optical | Opaque, lustrous | Transparent / Translucent |

**Types of Ceramics:**

**(i) Traditional Ceramics:** Made from naturally occurring raw materials (clay, silica, feldspar). Processing involves forming and firing.
- **Examples:** Bricks, tiles, porcelain, sanitary ware, cement, glass, refractories.
- **Applications:** Construction, cookware, insulators.

**(ii) Advanced (Technical / Engineering) Ceramics:** Made from high-purity synthetic powders with precisely controlled microstructures.

**Sub-classes:**
- **Structural:** Al₂O₃, SiC, Si₃N₄, ZrO₂ — used in cutting tools, engine parts, ballistic armour, hip implants.
- **Functional:** BaTiO₃ (capacitors), PZT (piezoelectric sensors), ferrite (magnetic cores), ZnO varistors.
- **Bio-ceramics:** Al₂O₃, hydroxyapatite — used in dental and orthopaedic implants.
- **Glass-ceramics:** Zerodur® — used in telescope mirrors and cooktop panels.

**Conclusion:** Ceramics are indispensable in high-temperature, high-wear, and insulating applications, complementing the use of metals.

---

**(b)** Explain the different types of point, line, and surface defects in crystalline solids with neat diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction:** Real crystals are never perfect. **Defects** dramatically affect mechanical, electrical, and optical properties.

**1. Point Defects (Zero-Dimensional):**

| Defect | Description | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **Vacancy** | Missing atom from lattice site | Increases with temperature; controls diffusion |
| **Interstitial** | Extra atom squeezed into gap | Significant lattice distortion |
| **Substitutional** | Foreign atom replaces host atom | Used in alloying / doping |
| **Frenkel Defect** | Cation displaced to interstitial site | Common in ionic crystals (e.g., AgCl) |
| **Schottky Defect** | Pair of cation + anion vacancies | Common in NaCl, preserves charge neutrality |

**2. Line Defects (One-Dimensional) — Dislocations:**

- **Edge Dislocation:** An extra half-plane of atoms inserted into the lattice. Defined by Burgers vector $\vec{b}$ perpendicular to the dislocation line.
- **Screw Dislocation:** Lattice planes shifted parallel to the dislocation line; $\vec{b}$ parallel to the line.
- **Mixed Dislocation:** Curved combination of edge and screw character.

**Importance:** Plastic deformation in metals is dominated by dislocation glide. Strength is governed by resistance to dislocation motion.

**3. Surface (Planar) Defects (Two-Dimensional):**

| Defect | Description |
|---|---|
| **Grain Boundary** | Interface between crystals of different orientation; 5–10 atomic layers wide |
| **Twin Boundary** | Mirror-symmetric plane across which lattice is reflected |
| **Stacking Fault** | Error in stacking sequence of close-packed planes (ABC vs ACB) |
| **Antiphase Boundary** | Occurs in ordered alloys; shift in sublattice ordering |

**4. Volume (Bulk) Defects:** Voids, inclusions, precipitates, cracks — three-dimensional defects that act as stress concentrators and failure initiation sites.

**Conclusion:** Defects are the *enablers* of most materials properties — they make diffusion possible, allow plastic deformation, and provide nucleation sites for phase transformations.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Ways Students Lose Marks on Ceramics & Metals Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing FCC and BCC examples:** α-Fe is **BCC** at room temperature; γ-Fe is **FCC** above 912 °C. Examiners specifically test this.
> 2. **Forgetting the constant in Hall-Petch:** The relation is $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k \cdot d^{-1/2}$, **not** $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k \cdot d$ (linear) or $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k \cdot d^{-1}$.
> 3. **Confusing APF with packing factor:** APF is *volume fraction* (always ≤ 1), not *number* of atoms.
> 4. **Mixing up HCP and FCC APF:** Both are 0.74. Many students write different values.
> 5. **Schmid factor sign:** Both $\phi$ and $\lambda$ are angles; both have positive cosines only when both are between 0° and 90°. State the maximum explicitly: $m_{max} = 0.5$ at $\phi = \lambda = 45°$.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Checklist — Ceramics and Metals**

- ✅ **Ceramics** = inorganic, non-metallic, ionic/covalent bonds, hard, brittle, insulating, high melting point.
- ✅ **Metals** = metallic bond (electron sea), ductile, conductive, lustrous.
- ✅ **FCC** = 4 atoms/cell, APF = 0.74, CN = 12, 12 slip systems, very ductile (Cu, Al, Ni, γ-Fe).
- ✅ **BCC** = 2 atoms/cell, APF = 0.68, CN = 8, 48 slip systems but high CRSS (α-Fe, Cr, W).
- ✅ **HCP** = 6 atoms/cell, APF = 0.74, CN = 12, 3 slip systems, brittle (Mg, Zn, Ti).
- ✅ **Density formula:** $\rho = nA/(V_c N_A)$.
- ✅ **Hall-Petch:** $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k d^{-1/2}$ — smaller grains ⇒ stronger.
- ✅ **Schmid's Law:** $\tau_{CRSS} = \sigma \cos\phi \cos\lambda$ — maximum at 45°.
- ✅ **Strain hardening:** $\tau = \tau_0 + K\gamma^n$, exponent $n \in [0.1, 0.5]$.
- ✅ **Strengthening mechanisms:** Grain refinement, Solid solution, Strain hardening, Precipitation — *all impede dislocations*.
- ✅ **Defect types:** Point (vacancy, interstitial, Frenkel, Schottky) → Line (edge, screw dislocations) → Surface (grain boundary, twin, stacking fault) → Volume (voids, cracks).
- ✅ **Ceramic types:** Traditional (clay, glass) vs Advanced (structural: Al₂O₃, SiC; functional: BaTiO₃; bio-ceramics).
- ✅ **Ceramic structures:** Rock salt, fluorite, perovskite, spinel, silica network.
- ✅ **Avogadro's number:** $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol⁻¹ — memorize.
- ✅ **Conversion tip:** 1 nm = $10^{-7}$ cm = 10 Å. Always unify units before cubing.
- ✅ **PSPP chain:** Processing → Structure → Properties → Performance — the *central* engineering logic of materials.
- ✅ **Environmental note (preview):** Mining & smelting of metals consume ~8 % of global energy; this will be revisited in Module 5.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polymer Production and Consumption
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT632 - POLYMERS & ENVIRONMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Polymer Production and Consumption

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Polymer Production

**Polymer Production** refers to the industrial-scale manufacturing of macromolecular materials (polymers) through the chemical conversion of low-molecular-weight monomers into high-molecular-weight polymeric substances via processes such as **addition (chain-growth) polymerization** and **condensation (step-growth) polymerization**. The production volume is typically quantified in **million metric tonnes (Mt)** per annum.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For the 2024 Scheme (PEPOT632), Module 1 emphasizes understanding the *scale*, *trends*, and *sustainability implications* of global polymer manufacturing rather than just synthetic routes. Every numerical cited must use **SI units (Mt or kg)** and base year data.

### 1.2 Formal Definition of Polymer Consumption

**Polymer Consumption** denotes the cumulative demand and end-use uptake of produced polymeric materials across sectors such as **packaging, construction, automotive, electronics, agriculture, healthcare, and textiles**, measured as the apparent consumption (Production + Imports − Exports) per capita in **kg/person/year**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a massive global "macaroni factory" 🍝 that produces billions of kilograms of plastic pellets each year — these tiny plastic granules are like LEGO bricks that get shipped worldwide and melted/molded into water bottles, car dashboards, pipes, and phone cases. The *production* is the rate at which the factory cooks these pellets, while the *consumption* is how many LEGO bricks are actually bought and assembled into finished products across the planet.

- **Global polymer production (2023):** ≈ **400 million metric tonnes/year**
- **Global plastic production (broader plastics family):** ≈ **390–400 Mt/year**
- **Per capita consumption (developed nations):** ≈ **100–150 kg/person/year**
- **Per capita consumption (global average):** ≈ **48 kg/person/year**
- **Per capita consumption (India):** ≈ **14–17 kg/person/year**

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** A 1 kg increase in per capita polymer consumption roughly correlates with a **0.8–1.2% rise in packaging-related municipal solid waste (MSW)**, making consumption data a direct environmental indicator.

### 1.4 Historical Context

The polymer industry began its exponential climb post-**1950**, when global plastic production was less than **2 Mt/year**. By 1970, it reached **35 Mt**; by 2000, it crossed **211 Mt**; and by 2023, it surpassed **400 Mt** — a **200-fold increase in ~70 years**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters in KTU PEPOT632:** The course links production/consumption data directly to **environmental burden** (carbon footprint, marine litter, microplastics). Module 1 sets the quantitative baseline needed to evaluate sustainability solutions covered in Modules 2–5.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Polymers by Production Volume

Polymers are commercially categorized into three major families based on production scale:

1. **Commodity Polymers (High-Volume / Bulk Polymers)** — produced in Mt scale, low cost, broad applications.
   - Polyethylene (PE) — LDPE, LLDPE, HDPE
   - Polypropylene (PP)
   - Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC)
   - Polystyrene (PS) and Expanded Polystyrene (EPS)
   - Polyethylene Terephthalate (PET)

2. **Engineering Polymers (Mid-Volume)** — superior mechanical/thermal properties.
   - Polyamide (Nylon 6, Nylon 6,6)
   - Polycarbonate (PC)
   - Acrylonitrile Butadiene Styrene (ABS)
   - Polyoxymethylene (POM)
   - Polybutylene Terephthalate (PBT)

3. **High-Performance / Specialty Polymers (Low-Volume)** — extreme conditions.
   - Polyetheretherketone (PEEK)
   - Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE / Teflon)
   - Polyimide (PI / Kapton)
   - Polysulfone (PSU)
   - Polyphenylene Sulfide (PPS)

### 2.2 Drivers of Polymer Consumption

Polymer demand is governed by four interlinked pillars:

- **Population Growth** → Linear rise in baseline demand
- **Urbanization** → Increased packaging & construction polymers
- **Disposable Income (GDP per capita)** → Direct correlation with plastics uptake
- **Substitution Effect** → Replacement of metals, glass, paper, wood

### 2.3 The Four Industrial Polymerization Routes

1. **Bulk (Mass) Polymerization** — Pure monomer + initiator; high purity, hard to control heat.
2. **Solution Polymerization** — Monomer dissolved in solvent; easier heat dissipation.
3. **Suspension Polymerization** — Monomer dispersed as droplets in water; used for PVC, PS.
4. **Emulsion Polymerization** — Monomer emulsified with surfactants; used for synthetic rubber, latex paints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Emulsion polymerization produces the smallest particle size (≈ 0.05–0.2 µm) and is the dominant route for **SBR rubber** and **PVC paste resins**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Metric | Unit | Application |
|-----------|------------------|------|-------------|
| **Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR)** | $\text{CAGR} = \left( \dfrac{V_{\text{final}}}{V_{\text{initial}}} \right)^{\tfrac{1}{n}} - 1$ | fraction / % | Production growth analysis |
| **Per Capita Consumption (PCC)** | $\text{PCC} = \dfrac{\text{Total Consumption}}{\text{Population}}$ | kg/person/year | Demand forecasting |
| **Apparent Consumption (AC)** | $\text{AC} = \text{Production} + \text{Imports} - \text{Exports}$ | Mt/year | National-level demand |
| **Yearly Increment (ΔV)** | $\Delta V = V_{n+1} - V_{n}$ | Mt | Annual production delta |
| **Recycling Rate (R)** | $R = \dfrac{M_{\text{recycled}}}{M_{\text{consumed}}} \times 100$ | % | Circular economy KPI |
| **Recycled Content Fraction (RCF)** | $\text{RCF} = \dfrac{m_{\text{recycled}}}{m_{\text{product}}}$ | fraction | Product-level sustainability |
| **Mass Balance (Reactor)** | $\dot{m}_{\text{in}} = \dot{m}_{\text{out}} + \dot{m}_{\text{accum}}$ | kg/s | Reactor design & scale-up |
| **Conversion (X)** | $X = \dfrac{[M]_0 - [M]}{[M]_0}$ | fraction | Polymerization kinetics |
| **Degree of Polymerization (DP)** | $\text{DP} = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ (step-growth) | dimensionless | Carothers' equation |
| **Number-Average MW (M̄n)** | $\bar{M}_n = \sum x_i M_i$ | g/mol | Polymer characterization |
| **Weight-Average MW (M̄w)** | $\bar{M}_w = \sum w_i M_i$ | g/mol | Polymer characterization |
| **Polydispersity Index (PDI)** | $\text{PDI} = \dfrac{\bar{M}_w}{\bar{M}_n}$ | dimensionless | Polymer uniformity |
| **Per Capita Waste** | $\text{PCW} = \dfrac{\text{MSW}_{\text{plastic}}}{\text{Population}}$ | kg/person/year | Environmental load |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why `|x|` is written as `\vert x \vert`:** KTU board examiners prefer unambiguous absolute value notation; the LaTeX `\vert` directive renders as `\|x\|` and never conflicts with markdown table pipes.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CAGR** is used by industry analysts (PlasticsEurope, AMI) to forecast 5- and 10-year demand curves.
- **PCC** is the benchmark used in UNEP reports to flag "plastic pollution hotspots."
- **PDI** is critical in process control — a tight PDI (< 1.1) indicates "living" or controlled polymerization (RAFT, ATRP).
- **Recycling Rate** drives the EU's **Single-Use Plastics Directive** and India's **Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR)** rules.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Global Polymer Production by Region (2010–2023)

| Year | Global Production (Mt) | Asia Share (%) | Europe Share (%) | NAFTA Share (%) | Rest of World (%) |
|------|------------------------|----------------|------------------|------------------|---------------------|
| 2010 | 313 | 41 | 23 | 21 | 15 |
| 2015 | 380 | 48 | 19 | 18 | 15 |
| 2020 | 390 | 51 | 17 | 17 | 15 |
| 2023 | 400 | 53 | 16 | 17 | 14 |

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: CAGR of Global Polymer Production (1950 → 2023)

We are given:
- Initial production, $V_{\text{initial}} = 2\ \text{Mt}$ (year 1950)
- Final production, $V_{\text{final}} = 400\ \text{Mt}$ (year 2023)
- Number of years, $n = 73$

**Step 1 — State the CAGR formula:**

$$
\text{CAGR} = \left( \frac{V_{\text{final}}}{V_{\text{initial}}} \right)^{\tfrac{1}{n}} - 1
$$

> *Conversion logic:* CAGR is the constant yearly growth rate that, when compounded over *n* years, transforms $V_{\text{initial}}$ into $V_{\text{final}}$.

**Step 2 — Substitute the known values:**

$$
\text{CAGR} = \left( \frac{400}{2} \right)^{\tfrac{1}{73}} - 1 = (200)^{0.01370} - 1
$$

> *Conversion logic:* We first compute the dimensionless ratio (200) and then raise it to the fractional power $1/73$.

**Step 3 — Evaluate the exponent:**

$$
0.01370 = \frac{1}{73} \approx 1.370 \times 10^{-2}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* This is a pure numerical transformation; the value 0.01370 represents the *per-year* fractional exponent.

**Step 4 — Compute the power using natural logarithms:**

$$
\ln(200) = 5.2983
$$

$$
(200)^{0.01370} = e^{0.01370 \times 5.2983} = e^{0.07259}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* Using $a^b = e^{b \ln a}$ converts an inconvenient power into a manageable exponential.

**Step 5 — Evaluate the exponential term:**

$$
e^{0.07259} \approx 1.07528
$$

**Step 6 — Subtract 1 to get the rate:**

$$
\text{CAGR} = 1.07528 - 1 = 0.07528
$$

**Step 7 — Express as a percentage:**

$$
\text{CAGR} \approx 7.53\ \% \text{ per year}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Global polymer production has grown at a compound annual rate of **≈ 7.5 %** over 73 years. This is roughly **3× the global population growth rate (≈ 1.2 %/year)** and **≈ 2× the global GDP growth rate (≈ 3 %/year)**, confirming polymers as one of the fastest-growing engineered material classes in human history.

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation: Per Capita Consumption (India, 2023)

Given:
- India's plastic consumption (2023), $C = 18\ \text{Mt} = 18 \times 10^{9}\ \text{kg}$
- India's population (2023), $P = 1.43 \times 10^{9}$ persons

**Step 1 — State the PCC formula:**

$$
\text{PCC} = \frac{C}{P}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* PCC is the intensive property that normalizes total demand by population.

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
\text{PCC} = \frac{18 \times 10^{9}\ \text{kg}}{1.43 \times 10^{9}\ \text{persons}}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the powers of ten:**

$$
\text{PCC} = \frac{18}{1.43}\ \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{person}}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the division:**

$$
\frac{18}{1.43} = 12.59
$$

**Step 5 — Final result:**

$$
\boxed{\text{PCC}_{\text{India, 2023}} \approx 12.6\ \text{kg/person/year}}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* This value is *much lower* than the global average (≈ 48 kg) and the OECD average (≈ 150 kg), which implies India's polymer sector has substantial *latent growth potential* — a key exam-relevant point in PEPOT632.

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Derivation: Apparent Consumption

A country produced 5.2 Mt of PVC, imported 0.8 Mt, and exported 1.1 Mt.

**Step 1 — Formula:**

$$
\text{AC} = P + I - E
$$

> *Conversion logic:* Apparent consumption captures what is *retained* in the domestic market.

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
\text{AC} = 5.2 + 0.8 - 1.1
$$

**Step 3 — Compute:**

$$
\text{AC} = 4.9\ \text{Mt}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

A 4.9 Mt apparent consumption means that **94.2 %** of produced PVC served the domestic market while **5.8 %** was re-exported (after adding imports).

### 3.5 Degree of Polymerization (DP) for Step-Growth Polymers

For a step-growth polymerization with conversion *p*:

$$
\text{DP}_n = \frac{1}{1-p}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* As conversion approaches unity, DP diverges — this is Carothers' equation and is foundational for understanding why step-growth polymers (PET, Nylon) need very high conversion (> 0.99) to achieve useful molecular weights.

**Example:** If $p = 0.98$:

$$
\text{DP}_n = \frac{1}{1-0.98} = \frac{1}{0.02} = 50
$$

If $p = 0.99$:

$$
\text{DP}_n = \frac{1}{0.01} = 100
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A mere 1 % increase in conversion (from 98 % to 99 %) doubles the molecular weight — a non-linear relationship often tested in KTU exams.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

### 4.4 Consumption by End-Use Sector (Block Diagram)
