
---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define an Environmental Awareness Program in the context of polymers. Mention any two objectives.**

**Model Answer:**
An Environmental Awareness Program in the polymer context is a structured educational initiative aimed at informing stakeholders about the environmental impact of polymer production, consumption, and disposal, and motivating them toward sustainable practices.

**Two objectives:**
1. To educate the public about the harmful effects of plastic waste on ecosystems.
2. To promote the adoption of the 5R framework (Refuse, Reduce, Reuse, Repurpose, Recycle).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**List any six abbreviations related to polymer waste management and expand them.**

**Model Answer:**

| Abbreviation | Full Form |
|---|---|
| UNEP | United Nations Environment Programme |
| CPCB | Central Pollution Control Board |
| EPR | Extended Producer Responsibility |
| MSW | Municipal Solid Waste |
| LCA | Life Cycle Assessment |
| PWM | Plastic Waste Management |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> *As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern, Module 1 carries a choice. Below are TWO alternatives.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]**

**(a)** Discuss the **global and national scenario of plastic pollution**. What are the major sources and environmental impacts? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **5R/6R framework** of waste management with a neat diagram and describe how it forms the philosophical foundation of any awareness program. **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution (a):

**Global Scenario:**
- Global plastic production has crossed **400 million tonnes/year** (2023).
- Only **< 9 %** of plastic ever produced has been recycled.
- **8 million tonnes** of plastic enter the oceans annually.
- Microplastics have been found in human blood, lungs, and even placentas.

**National Scenario (India):**
- India generates **~3.5 million tonnes** of plastic waste annually.
- Per capita consumption: **~13.6 kg/year**.
- Single-use plastic ban announced on **July 1, 2022**.

**Major Sources:**
1. Packaging industry (40 % of global plastic use).
2. Consumer goods and FMCG.
3. Agricultural mulch films.
4. Construction and textiles.

**Environmental Impacts:**
1. Marine life mortality (entanglement, ingestion).
2. Soil contamination and reduced fertility.
3. Microplastic infiltration in food chains.
4. Greenhouse gas emissions (plastic = petroleum derivative).
5. Aesthetic and tourism damage to beaches/cities.

#### ✅ Model Solution (b):

The **5R/6R framework** ranks waste management options by environmental priority:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{Refuse} \;\succ\; \text{Reduce} \;\succ\; \text{Reuse} \;\succ\; \text{Repurpose} \;\succ\; \text{Recycle} \;\succ\; \text{Recover}\;}
$$

*(Refer to Mermaid diagram 4.3 for visual representation.)*

**Why it is the foundation of an awareness program:**
- It provides a **simple, memorable hierarchy** for the public.
- It prioritizes **waste prevention over end-of-pipe solutions**.
- It is endorsed by **UNEP, OECD, and India's CPCB**.
- It bridges the gap between **knowledge and behavior**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Describe the **KAB (Knowledge–Attitude–Behavior) model** in detail. How does it apply to designing polymer-environment awareness programs? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **roles of various stakeholders** (Government, Industry, Educational Institutes, NGOs, and Public) in creating an effective awareness ecosystem for sustainable polymer use. **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution (a):

The **KAB Model** is a psychological framework describing how awareness leads to action:

$$
\text{Knowledge} \xrightarrow{\text{Cognition}} \text{Attitude} \xrightarrow{\text{Motivation}} \text{Behavior}
$$

**1. Knowledge:** Imparted through lectures, media, curriculum. The "what" — e.g., "Plastic takes 500 years to decompose."

**2. Attitude:** Internalized values — the "why it matters." Empathy-driven. The audience must *feel* the problem.

**3. Behavior:** External action — the "what I do." E.g., refusing plastic bags, segregating waste.

**Application in Polymer-Environment Programs:**
- **Stage 1 (Knowledge):** Conduct workshops, distribute pamphlets.
- **Stage 2 (Attitude):** Use case studies, films, survivor testimonials.
- **Stage 3 (Behavior):** Provide dustbins, set up recycling centers, run pledge campaigns.

> A common failure of awareness programs is **stopping at Stage 1** — imparting knowledge without addressing attitude and behavior.

#### ✅ Model Solution (b):

**1. Government (CPCB, MoEFCC):**
- Frame laws (PWM Rules 2016, amended 2022).
- Enforce EPR on producers.
- Fund municipal infrastructure.

**2. Industry:**
- Adopt eco-design principles.
- Use recyclable mono-materials.
- Comply with EPR via PROs (Producer Responsibility Organizations).

**3. Educational Institutes (KTU, AICTE):**
- Mandate courses like **PEPOT632**.
- Research on biodegradable polymers.
- Campus green audits.

**4. NGOs (WWF, Greenpeace, local groups):**
- Grassroots campaigning.
- Legal advocacy.
- Beach/river clean-ups.

**5. General Public:**
- Adopt segregation at source.
- Support sustainable brands.
- Participate in civic drives.

> The **synergy** of these five pillars is essential; no single stakeholder can solve the polymer crisis alone.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Awareness Program Questions:**
> 1. **Writing the 5R in the wrong order** — Refuse MUST come first. (-1 mark)
> 2. **Confusing EPR with general recycling** — EPR is a *producer* policy. (-1 mark)
> 3. **Forgetting Indian context** — Always mention **PWM Rules 2016/2022** and **Swachh Bharat Mission**. (-1 to 2 marks)
> 4. **No diagrams** — KTU evaluators award 1–2 marks for neat, labeled diagrams (especially the 5R pyramid). (-1 to 2 marks)
> 5. **Skipping the "Why"** — Don't just *list* the KAB stages; *explain the transition* between them.
> 6. **No real-world examples** — Mention at least one Indian initiative (e.g., GOBAR-DHAN, Swachh Bharat).

---

## 5.4 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ An **Awareness Program** = **Knowledge + Attitude + Behavior** (KAB Model).
- ✅ The **5R/6R hierarchy**: Refuse → Reduce → Reuse → Repurpose → Recycle → Recover.
- ✅ **EPR (Extended Producer Responsibility)** makes producers accountable for end-of-life management.
- ✅ **PWM Rules (India, 2016, amended 2022)** = backbone of national plastic regulation.
- ✅ **Single-use plastic ban** in India effective **July 1, 2022**.
- ✅ **UNEP's "Beat Plastic Pollution"** = theme for World Environment Day (June 5).
- ✅ **Sustainable Development Goal 12 (SDG-12)** = Responsible Consumption and Production.
- ✅ Global recycling rate of plastic is **< 9 %**; awareness gap is the core problem.
- ✅ Always use a **diagram** in 7-mark questions to secure full marks.
- ✅ Always give **Indian examples** alongside global ones for KTU context.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for 5R:** **"R³-RR"** → *Refuse, Reduce, Reuse, Repurpose, Recycle, Recover*.
> Read it as **"R-cube"** (R³) plus two more R's.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Need for Recycling of Plastics from Urban Solid Wastes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT632 - POLYMERS & ENVIRONMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Need for Recycling of Plastics from Urban Solid Wastes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Urban Solid Waste (USW)** refers to the heterogeneous mixture of discarded materials generated from residential, commercial, institutional, and industrial sources within a municipal boundary. It comprises biodegradable organics, recyclable fractions (paper, glass, metals), inert materials, and the **plastic fraction**, which has emerged as one of the most persistent and problematic components of the modern waste stream.

**Plastic Recycling** is defined as the controlled, engineered process of recovering, reprocessing, and reusing post-consumer or post-industrial plastic waste to produce new materials or products, thereby displacing virgin polymer synthesis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** Recycling is not merely "waste collection"; it is a closed-loop material recovery strategy that addresses the *triple-bottom-line* — environmental integrity, economic viability, and social equity — within the framework of **Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) 12: Responsible Consumption and Production**.

### 1.2 The "Why" — Conceptual Analogy

Imagine your kitchen sink is clogged with used cooking oil. Pouring the oil down the drain a second time does not clean it — it solidifies and worsens the blockage. Plastic, in essence, is the **"fossil-oil solid"** of modern society. When we discard plastic into landfills or oceans, we are effectively **throwing away the petroleum feedstock** from which it was made, plus adding centuries of toxic residence time to the environment.

Just as we separate biodegradable waste from non-biodegradable waste at home, cities must implement **Material Recovery Facilities (MRFs)** that act as sophisticated "sorting kitchens" for the urban waste stream — separating plastics by resin type (PET, HDPE, PVC, LDPE, PP, PS) so they can be re-melted and reborn as new products.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Average per capita plastic waste generation (India, 2023 CPCB data):** ~ **3.5 kg/person/year**
- **Global plastic production (2023):** ~ **400 million tonnes/year**
- **Degradation half-life of common plastics in environment:**
  - PET bottle: **~ 450 years**
  - HDPE container: **~ 200 years**
  - PP bag: **~ 50 years**
  - EPS (Styrofoam): **> 500 years**
- **Recycling code identifiers:** Resin Identification Codes (RIC) **1 through 7** standardized by **ASTM D7611**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plastic does NOT biodegrade in any human-relevant timeframe.** It undergoes *photodegradation* and *fragmentation* into **microplastics (< 5 mm)** and **nanoplastics (< 1 µm)**, which now contaminate the global food chain — from bottled water to table salt.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Pillars Justifying Plastic Recycling

The necessity of recycling plastics from urban solid waste rests on five interdependent pillars, each linked to a measurable engineering or environmental outcome:

1. **Pillar I — Landfill Space Conservation**
   - Municipal landfills in Kerala (e.g., Brahmapuram, Kochi) are nearing saturation.
   - Plastics occupy **~ 20–30% by volume** of urban solid waste but are virtually non-compactable.

2. **Pillar II — Marine & Terrestrial Pollution Mitigation**
   - **8 million tonnes** of plastic enter the world's oceans annually (UNEP, 2022).
   - Microplastic contamination has been detected in **Kerala's Vembanad Lake** and backwaters.

3. **Pillar III — Resource & Energy Conservation**
   - Recycling 1 tonne of PET saves ~ **6,000 MJ** of energy vs. virgin production.
   - Reduces crude oil consumption by ~ **1.5 tonnes per tonne of recycled PET**.

4. **Pillar IV — Greenhouse Gas (GHG) Emission Reduction**
   - Virgin plastic production emits ~ **2.5–3.5 tonnes CO₂-eq per tonne of polymer**.
   - Mechanical recycling reduces this by **~ 30–70%**, depending on resin.

5. **Pillar V — Public Health & Aesthetic Protection**
   - Clogged drains → urban flooding (Kerala 2018, 2019 floods).
   - Vector breeding in plastic-clogged drains → dengue, malaria risk.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Equation | Mathematical Form | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Per Capita Waste Generation (PCWG) | $PCWG = \dfrac{M_{total}}{P \times t}$ | kg/person/day | Municipal planning |
| 2 | Waste Density | $\rho_{waste} = \dfrac{m_{waste}}{V_{waste}}$ | kg/m³ | Landfill volumetric design |
| 3 | Recycling Yield | $Y_{R} = \dfrac{m_{recyclate}}{m_{input}} \times 100$ | % | Process efficiency |
| 4 | Energy Savings Ratio | $E_{saving} = \dfrac{E_{virgin} - E_{recycle}}{E_{virgin}} \times 100$ | % | LCA-based justification |
| 5 | Volume Reduction Ratio | $VR = \dfrac{V_{loose}}{V_{compacted}}$ | dimensionless | MRF sizing |
| 6 | CO₂ Avoidance | $CO_{2,avoid} = (EF_{virgin} - EF_{recycle}) \times m$ | kg CO₂-eq | Carbon credit calculation |
| 7 | Plastic-to-Waste Mass Fraction | $f_{plastic} = \dfrac{m_{plastic}}{m_{USW}}$ | dimensionless | Sortation target |
| 8 | Landfill Lifespan Extension | $\Delta t_{landfill} = \dfrac{V_{recycled}}{\dot{V}_{landfill}}$ | years | Policy ROI |

Where:
- $M_{total}$ = total mass of urban solid waste generated
- $P$ = population served
- $t$ = time period
- $EF$ = emission factor (kg CO₂-eq per kg polymer)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition for KTU Numerical Problems:** Assume $\rho_{PET,bulk} \approx 300 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (loose), $\rho_{HDPE,bulk} \approx 450 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_{LDPE,film} \approx 50 \text{ kg/m}^3$ unless otherwise stated.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility

- **Municipal Engineers** use these equations to size MRFs and predict landfill closure dates.
- **Polymer Engineers** use recycling yield and energy savings data to justify recycled-content specifications (e.g., BIS standards for recycled PET in food contact).
- **Environmental Engineers** apply GHG avoidance figures in EPR (Extended Producer Responsibility) compliance reporting.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Landfill Volume Avoided via PET Recycling

**Problem:** A KTU municipal region of **50,000 population** generates USW at **0.5 kg/person/day**, of which **8% by mass** is PET. If **40%** of this PET is recovered for recycling, calculate:
(a) Annual PET mass landfilled (avoided).
(b) Landfill volume saved, given bulk density of loose PET in MSW = **120 kg/m³**.

#### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Total USW generated per day:**

$$
m_{USW} = P \times PCWG = 50{,}000 \times 0.5
$$

$$
m_{USW} = 25{,}000 \text{ kg/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Daily PET fraction generated:**

$$
m_{PET,daily} = f_{plastic} \times m_{USW} = 0.08 \times 25{,}000
$$

$$
m_{PET,daily} = 2{,}000 \text{ kg/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Annual PET mass:**

$$
m_{PET,annual} = 2{,}000 \times 365 = 730{,}000 \text{ kg/year} = 730 \text{ tonnes/year}
$$

**Step 4 — Annual PET mass recycled (avoided from landfill):**

$$
m_{PET,recycled} = Y_R \times m_{PET,annual} = 0.40 \times 730
$$

$$
m_{PET,recycled} = 292 \text{ tonnes/year}
$$

**Step 5 — Volume of loose PET that would have occupied landfill:**

$$
V_{PET} = \dfrac{m_{PET,recycled}}{\rho_{PET,bulk}} = \dfrac{292{,}000 \text{ kg}}{120 \text{ kg/m}^3}
$$

$$
V_{PET} \approx 2{,}433 \text{ m}^3/\text{year}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** ~ **292 tonnes/year** of PET diverted, saving approximately **2,433 m³ of landfill volume annually** — equivalent to a small water reservoir.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Energy & CO₂ Savings

**Problem:** Recycling 1 kg of HDPE requires ~ **15 MJ**, while producing 1 kg of virgin HDPE requires ~ **75 MJ** and emits ~ **2.0 kg CO₂-eq**. Calculate the savings for **292 tonnes** of recycled HDPE equivalent.

**Step 1 — Virgin energy baseline (avoided):**

$$
E_{virgin,total} = 75 \text{ MJ/kg} \times 292{,}000 \text{ kg} = 2.19 \times 10^{7} \text{ MJ}
$$

**Step 2 — Recycling energy consumed:**

$$
E_{recycle,total} = 15 \text{ MJ/kg} \times 292{,}000 \text{ kg} = 4.38 \times 10^{6} \text{ MJ}
$$

**Step 3 — Net energy saved:**

$$
\Delta E = E_{virgin} - E_{recycle} = (21.9 - 4.38) \times 10^{6} = 1.752 \times 10^{7} \text{ MJ}
$$

**Step 4 — Energy Savings Ratio:**

$$
E_{saving} = \dfrac{\Delta E}{E_{virgin}} \times 100 = \dfrac{1.752 \times 10^{7}}{2.19 \times 10^{7}} \times 100
$$

$$
E_{saving} \approx 80\%
$$

**Step 5 — CO₂ avoided (assuming recycling emits ~ 0.6 kg CO₂-eq/kg):**

$$
CO_{2,avoid} = (2.0 - 0.6) \times 292{,}000 = 4.088 \times 10^{5} \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq}
$$

$$
CO_{2,avoid} \approx 408.8 \text{ tonnes CO}_2\text{-eq/year}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Why Recycle: Cause → Effect → Engineering Response

| # | Cause (Waste Source) | Environmental Effect | Engineering / Policy Response |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Single-use packaging | Litter, marine pollution | EPR, plastic bans (> 50 µm) |
| 2 | Multilayer films | Non-recyclable; landfill dominant | Design for Recycling (DfR) guidelines |
| 3 | Mixed resin contamination | Downcycling, low yield | NIR-sortation in MRFs |
| 4 | Microplastic shedding | Food chain contamination | Bioplastics, oxo-degradable controls |
| 5 | Open burning of plastic | Dioxins, furans, PAHs emission | Waste-to-Energy (WtE) with filtration |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Plastic Waste Stream & Recycling Imperative

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the *functional flow* of plastic waste through an urban system, highlighting intervention points where recycling becomes necessary.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Urban Solid Waste Generation] --> B[Primary Collection Point]
    B --> C[Material Recovery Facility MRF]
    C --> D{Resin Identification Code}
    D -->|RIC 1 PET| E[PET Sorting Line]
    D -->|RIC 2 HDPE| F[HDPE Sorting Line]
    D -->|RIC 4 LDPE| G[Film Compaction Line]
    D -->|RIC 5 PP| H[PP Sorting Line]
    D -->|RIC 3 6 7| I[Hazardous Restricted Stream]
    E --> J[Shredding and Washing]
    F --> J
    G --> J
    H --> J
    J --> K[Extrusion to Recyclate]
    K --> L[Reprocessed Product Market]
    I --> M[Controlled Landfill or WtE]
    L --> N[Reduced Virgin Demand]
    M --> O[Environmental Burden]
    N --> P[Sustainable Urban Loop]
    O --> Q[Pollution Cost Externalities]
    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

```

### 4.2 Causal Loop Diagram — Why Recycling is Non-Optional

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Plastic Production from Fossil Feedstock] --> B[Urban Plastic Waste Generation]
    B --> C[Landfill Saturation]
    B --> D[Marine and Terrestrial Pollution]
    B --> E[Microplastic Contamination]
    C --> F[Public Health and Flood Risk]
    D --> G[Economic Loss to Fisheries and Tourism]
    E --> H[Food Chain Bioaccumulation]
    F --> I[Policy Pressure for Recycling]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Investment in MRF and Sorting Tech]
    J --> K[Recyclate Substitution]
    K --> L[Reduced Virgin Polymer Demand]
    L --> M[Lower CO2 Emissions and Resource Use]
    M --> N[Sustainable Polymer Economy]
    style I fill:#f39c12,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style N fill:#27ae60,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:3px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Green nodes indicate *desired outcomes*; orange nodes indicate *policy/decision triggers*; red-tinted nodes indicate *negative externalities* that justify recycling.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024] Define the term *Urban Solid Waste* and identify the typical plastic fraction present in it. List any **three** resin identification codes.

**Model Answer:**
Urban Solid Waste (USW) refers to the entire stream of discarded solid materials arising from domestic, commercial, institutional, and street-cleaning activities within a municipal area. The plastic fraction in Indian USW typically ranges from **8 to 12% by mass** and **20 to 30% by volume**.

Three common Resin Identification Codes (RIC):
- **RIC 1 — PET (Polyethylene Terephthalate)** — beverage bottles.
- **RIC 2 — HDPE (High-Density Polyethylene)** — milk jugs, detergent bottles.
- **RIC 5 — PP (Polypropylene)** — bottle caps, food containers.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] State **three** major environmental consequences of improper disposal of plastic urban solid waste.

**Model Answer:**
1. **Marine pollution:** Plastic litter enters oceans, harming marine fauna through ingestion and entanglement.
2. **Microplastic contamination:** Degradation fragments form microplastics that enter the food chain.
3. **Landfill burden & GHG emissions:** Plastics occupy large landfill volume and, when incinerated openly, release toxic dioxins and furans.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Pattern]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain in detail the **five major reasons** why recycling of plastics from urban solid waste has become an environmental and engineering necessity in the Indian urban context. Support your answer with quantitative justifications.

**Model Solution:**

**Reason 1 — Rapid urbanization & waste surge:**
India's urban population generates ~ **62 million tonnes of municipal solid waste annually**, of which plastics form **~ 8%**. Cities like Delhi and Mumbai produce over **4,000 tonnes/day** of plastic waste each.

**Reason 2 — Persistence in environment:**
Plastics do not biodegrade; PET persists for **~ 450 years**. Hence, landfill disposal is essentially permanent sequestration of fossil carbon.

**Reason 3 — Resource conservation:**
Recycling 1 tonne of PET saves **~ 1.5 tonnes of crude oil** and **~ 6,000 MJ** of process energy — equivalent to powering ~ 170 Indian households for one day.

**Reason 4 — Climate change mitigation:**
Recycling HDPE reduces GHG emissions by **~ 80%** compared to virgin production, directly contributing to India's **Panchamrit** climate commitments at COP-26.

**Reason 5 — Public health & disaster resilience:**
Clogged plastic-laden drains aggravate urban floods (e.g., Kerala 2018 floods). Recycling reduces drain-blockage load and vector breeding.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A city of **8 lakh population** generates USW at **0.45 kg/capita/day**. Plastics constitute **9%** of the USW. If only **30%** of the plastic is recycled and bulk density of loose plastic in MSW is **130 kg/m³**, calculate:
(i) Annual mass of plastic landfilled.
(ii) Landfill volume that would be saved annually by recycling.

**Model Solution:**

**Step (i) — Annual plastic generated:**

$$
m_{USW,annual} = 8 \times 10^{5} \times 0.45 \times 365 = 1.314 \times 10^{8} \text{ kg}
$$

$$
m_{plastic,annual} = 0.09 \times 1.314 \times 10^{8} = 1.1826 \times 10^{7} \text{ kg}
$$

**Step (ii) — Mass recycled (diverted from landfill):**

$$
m_{recycled} = 0.30 \times 1.1826 \times 10^{7} = 3.548 \times 10^{6} \text{ kg/year} = 3{,}548 \text{ tonnes/year}
$$

**Step (iii) — Volume saved:**

$$
V_{saved} = \dfrac{3.548 \times 10^{6}}{130} \approx 27{,}292 \text{ m}^3/\text{year}
$$

**Answer:** ~ **3,548 tonnes of plastic/year** is diverted, saving **~ 27,292 m³ of landfill volume annually**.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Pattern]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Discuss the **role of Material Recovery Facilities (MRFs)** in enabling plastic recycling from urban solid waste. Include in your answer the principles of *Resin Identification*, *sortation technologies*, and *contamination control*.

**Model Solution:**

A **Material Recovery Facility (MRF)** is a centralized engineered plant that receives mixed USW and separates recyclable fractions through mechanical and optical means.

**Resin Identification:**
- **Manual sorting** by trained workers using RIC 1–7 codes printed on containers.
- **Near-Infrared (NIR) Spectroscopy** is the gold-standard automated method. Each polymer absorbs NIR light at a unique wavelength: PET at ~ **1,660 nm**, HDPE at ~ **1,725 nm**, PP at ~ **1,735 nm**.

**Sortation Technologies:**
1. **Trommel screens** — size-based separation.
2. **Eddy current separators** — for metals (pre-separation step).
3. **Optical sorters** — for resin-specific identification.
4. **Air classifiers** — for film vs. rigid separation.
5. **Froth flotation** — for hydrophobic plastics from wet organics.

**Contamination Control:**
- Wash line with detergents + friction washers.
- Target: **< 50 ppm** of non-target polymer and **< 2%** organic residue post-wash.
- Drying to **< 1% moisture** before extrusion to prevent hydrolytic degradation.

**(b)** [7 Marks] With a suitable **block-flow diagram**, illustrate the journey of a PET beverage bottle from *urban household bin* to *recycled polyester fiber*. Mention at least **four** value-added applications of recycled PET (rPET).

**Model Solution:**

**Block Flow:**
```
[Household PET Bottle] → [Segregation at Source] → [Door-to-Door Collection] 
→ [MRF Bulk Sorting] → [NIR Sortation] → [Bale Storage] → [Shredding] 
→ [Hot Wash with Detergent] → [Density Separation Sink-Float] → [Drying < 1% moisture] 
→ [Melt Extrusion with Melt Filter 80-120 micron] → [Pelletization] → [rPET Pellet] 
→ [Spinning to Staple Fiber] → [Recycled Polyester Yarn]
```

**Value-Added Applications of rPET:**
1. **Polyester staple fiber** for upholstery, carpets, and non-woven geotextiles.
2. **rPET sheets** for thermoformed packaging (clamshells, trays).
3. **Bottle-to-bottle** food-grade rPET (subject to EFSA/FSSAI migration limits).
4. **3D printing filament** (rPETG variant) for sustainable prototyping.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency in numerical problems** — always carry units through every step; missing units = **−1 to −2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing *mass fraction* vs. *volume fraction*** — KTU examiners frequently test this in landfill design problems.
> 3. **Skipping the assumption block** — students lose marks by not stating assumed densities or recycling efficiencies. Always write: *"Assuming bulk density of loose PET = 120 kg/m³…"*
> 4. **Omitting resin identification codes** — mentioning "PET" without its RIC code loses a half-mark in Part A.
> 5. **Inadequate labelling of block diagrams** — arrows without labels = 0 marks for that flow stage.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Urban Solid Waste (USW)** contains **8–12% plastic by mass** but **20–30% by volume** due to low bulk density.
- Plastics are **non-biodegradable**; they fragment into microplastics (< 5 mm) and nanoplastics (< 1 µm).
- Recycling is justified by **5 pillars**: landfill space, pollution control, energy savings, GHG reduction, and public health.
- Key resin codes: **PET (1), HDPE (2), PVC (3), LDPE (4), PP (5), PS (6), Other (7)**.
- **NIR spectroscopy** is the industry-standard sortation technology.
- Master formulas: $PCWG = M/(P \cdot t)$, $Y_R = m_{recyclate}/m_{input}$, $E_{saving} = (E_v - E_r)/E_v$.
- Recycling 1 tonne of PET saves **~ 6,000 MJ** and **~ 1.5 tonnes of crude oil**.
- India's plastic waste growth rate (~ **10–12% CAGR**) outpaces municipal processing capacity.
- Always state assumptions: bulk density, recycling efficiency, population, and time horizon.
- KTU 2024 emphasises **numerical literacy + block-flow diagrams** — practice both for full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sorting and Segregation of Waste
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT632 - POLYMERS & ENVIRONMENT > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Sorting and Segregation of Waste ## SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Waste Segregation** is defined as the systematic separation of mixed municipal solid waste (MSW) into discrete, homogeneous categories at the point of generation or at a centralized Material Recovery Facility (MRF), based on physico-chemical properties such as composition, density, size, magnetic susceptibility, and chemical structure. > [!NOTE] > **Waste Sorting** is defined as the subsequent process of categorizing already segregated waste streams into specific sub-classes (e.g., PET from HDPE, or aluminium from ferrous metals) using manual, mechanical, optical, or sensor-based technologies to produce market-ready recyclable fractions. According to the **Solid Waste Management Rules (SWM), 2016** of India (aligned with KTU PEPOT632 Module 2 syllabus objectives), segregation at source into three distinct streams is mandated: - **Biodegradable / Wet Waste** (green bin) - **Non-Biodegradable / Dry Waste** (blue bin) - **Hazardous / Domestic Hazardous Waste** (red bin) > [!NOTE] > **Material Recovery Facility (MRF)** — A specialized sorting and segregation plant where mixed waste is processed to recover recyclable materials and prepare residual waste for safe disposal or energy recovery. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Refrigerator" Model Imagine your household refrigerator. You instinctively sort vegetables into the crisper drawer, dairy on a separate shelf, and raw meat on the bottom shelf. You do **not** throw all food together and later pick it out at dinner time. **Waste segregation works identically**: separating materials *at the moment of generation* (the kitchen counter) prevents contamination, reduces downstream sorting cost, and dramatically improves recycling quality. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Exam Pearl:** Segregation at source is always preferred over centralized segregation because it preserves material purity, reduces contamination by **≥ 40%**, and lowers MRF operating cost by approximately **30–50%**. --- ### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (KTU-2024 High-Yield Values) | Metric | Standard Value | Significance | |---|---|---| | Target Source Segregation Efficiency | **≥ 80%** | UN-Habitat benchmark for developing nations | | MRF Recovery Rate | **60–85%** | Industry standard for dry waste fraction | | Plastic-to-Paper Contamination Threshold | **< 5% by mass** | BIS / IS 14543 recycling purity norm | | Indian Per Capita Waste Generation | **0.45 kg/day (urban)** | CPCB 2020 baseline | ## SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ### 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Sorting & Segregation Methods The complete waste segregation methodology can be decomposed into a **3-tier hierarchical model**: #### **Tier 1: Source-Based Segregation (Decentralized)** - Performed by waste generators (households, institutions, commercial units) - Requires **behavioural change** and **bin infrastructure** - Lowest capital cost, highest material purity - Implements the **Polluter Pays Principle** indirectly through household-level accountability #### **Tier 2: Primary / Pre-Processing Segregation at MRF** - Performed after collection but before detailed sorting - Involves **manual picking**, **bag opening**, and **primary size reduction** - Typically handles **5–50 tonnes/day** throughput in Indian ULBs #### **Tier 3: Secondary / Mechanical–Sensor Sorting** - High-throughput automated separation - Uses physical property differentials: density, magnetism, electrical conductivity, optical reflectance - Achieves **95–99% per-stage efficiency** for specific fractions --- ### 2.2 Mechanical Sorting Technologies — Operational Logic > [!IMPORTANT] > Each mechanical sorting technique exploits **one specific physical property differential** between waste components. | Technology | Property Exploited | Target Waste Stream | Working Principle | |---|---|---|---| | **Magnetic Separation** | Magnetic susceptibility ($\chi_m$) | Ferrous metals (Fe, steel) | Permanent/electromagnet attracts Fe particles | | **Eddy Current Separator (ECS)** | Electrical conductivity ($\sigma$) | Non-ferrous metals (Al, Cu) | Alternating magnetic field induces repulsive Lorentz force | | **Air Classifier** | Aerodynamic drag vs. gravity | Light fraction (paper, plastic film) | Vertical air column lifts low-density materials | | **Trommel Screen** | Particle size (diameter $d_p$) | Size-classified organics vs. inerts | Rotating cylindrical screen with aperture $\phi$ | | **Optical / NIR Sensor** | Spectral reflectance $\rho(\lambda)$ | Polymer type (PET vs. HDPE vs. PP) | Near-infrared spectroscopy + air-jet ejection | | **Sink–Float Separator** | Density ($\rho_s$) | Heavy vs. light plastics | Liquid medium of density $\rho_m$ where $\rho_m \approx 1.0$ g/cm³ | | **Froth Flotation** | Surface hydrophobicity ($\theta$) | Plastics of differing wettability | Bubble attachment selectively floats hydrophobic surfaces | --- ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table contains **all derivation-ready formulas** required for KTU 2024 PEPOT632 Module 2 numerical problems. | # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\eta_s = \dfrac{M_{recovered}}{M_{input}} \times 100$ | Sorting efficiency ($\eta_s$) | % | | 2 | $R_r = \dfrac{M_{target}}{M_{target,in\ feed}} \times 100$ | Recovery rate ($R_r$) | % | | 3 | $C_p = 1 - \dfrac{M_{contaminant}}{M_{product}}$ | Product purity ($C_p$) | fraction (0–1) | | 4 | $F_{Lorentz} = q \, (\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$ | Eddy-current separation force | N | | 5 | $v_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, m \, g \, (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{\rho_f \, A \, C_d}}$ | Terminal settling velocity (Stokes regime) | m/s | | 6 | $v_{air} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, m \, g}{\rho_{air} \, A \, C_d}}$ | Air classifier lift velocity | m/s | | 7 | $M_{balance}: M_{in} = M_{recovered} + M_{residue} + M_{loss}$ | Mass conservation law | kg/day | | 8 | $\rho_{mix} = \dfrac{\sum_i \rho_i \, V_i}{\sum_i V_i}$ | Mixture density (volume-weighted) | kg/m³ | | 9 | $T_{res} = \dfrac{V_{hopper}}{Q_{in}}$ | Residence time in trommel | s | | 10 | $N_{trommel} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g \sin\theta}{R}}$ | Critical trommel speed (rpm) | rad/s | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Significance:** These formulas are not academic abstractions — they govern the design of every operating MRF in India (e.g., Delhi, Bengaluru, Kochi). A **0.1 g/cm³** error in sink–float medium density can shift recovery efficiency by **±15%**. --- ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The systematic application of sorting & segregation principles enables: 1. **Closed-loop polymer recycling** — clean PET streams can be re-extruded into bottles (Coca-Cola's "bottle-to-bottle" initiative). 2. **Refuse-Derived Fuel (RDF) production** — sorted calorific fraction ($CV \geq 1500$ kcal/kg) substitutes coal in cement kilns. 3. **Compost quality assurance** — segregation prevents heavy metal and glass contamination of organic compost. 4. **Plastic-to-fuel (PTF) feedstock** — pyrolysis units require HDPE/PP purity $\geq 90\%$ for optimal diesel yield. ## SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Mass Balance Across an MRF Consider an MRF receiving **$M_{in}$ = 1000 kg/day** of mixed dry waste. The material splits into recoverable fraction, residue, and process loss. **Step 1 — State the fundamental conservation law:** Mass entering the MRF per day must equal mass leaving via all output streams: $$ M_{in} = M_{recovered} + M_{residue} + M_{loss} $$ **Step 2 — Introduce the engineering measured values (typical Indian MRF):** Suppose the MRF recovers plastic, paper, glass, and metals totalling **640 kg/day**, sends unrecoverable inerts to landfill (**310 kg/day**), and has unavoidable process loss (**50 kg/day**): $$ M_{in} = 640 + 310 + 50 $$ $$ M_{in} = 1000 \text{ kg/day} \quad \checkmark $$ **Step 3 — Compute the overall recovery efficiency ($\eta_{MRF}$):** $$ \eta_{MRF} = \dfrac{M_{recovered}}{M_{in}} \times 100 $$ $$ \eta_{MRF} = \dfrac{640}{1000} \times 100 = 64\% $$ **Step 4 — Compute the residue fraction:** $$ f_{residue} = \dfrac{M_{residue}}{M_{in}} = \dfrac{310}{1000} = 0.31 \quad \text{or} \quad 31\% $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** A 64% MRF efficiency is consistent with **CPCB 2018 benchmark** for Indian secondary sorting facilities. State-of-the-art European MRFs achieve 80–90% due to advanced NIR sensors and pre-sorting by citizens. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Sink–Float Separation Density Criterion For a particle of density $\rho_p$ immersed in a medium of density $\rho_m$: **Step 1 — Apply Archimedes' principle:** The buoyant force is: $$ F_b = \rho_m \, V_p \, g $$ The gravitational force is: $$ F_g = \rho_p \, V_p \, g $$ The net vertical force is: $$ F_{net} = (\rho_p - \rho_m) \, V_p \, g $$ **Step 2 — Determine the separation condition:** - If $\rho_p > \rho_m$ → particle **sinks** (recovered at bottom) - If $\rho_p < \rho_m$ → particle **floats** (recovered at surface) - If $\rho_p = \rho_m$ → particle is **neutrally buoyant** (no separation) $$ \text{Separation criterion:} \quad \rho_p \neq \rho_m $$ **Step 3 — Apply to real polymer separation:** Common packaging plastics have densities: $$ \rho_{PET} \approx 1.38 \text{ g/cm}^3, \quad \rho_{HDPE} \approx 0.95 \text{ g/cm}^3, \quad \rho_{PP} \approx 0.91 \text{ g/cm}^3 $$ Using **water ($\rho_{water} = 1.00$ g/cm³)** as the medium: - PET **sinks** ($\rho_{PET} > \rho_{water}$) - HDPE **floats** ($\rho_{HDPE} < \rho_{water}$) - PP **floats** ($\rho_{PP} < \rho_{water}$) **Step 4 — Quantify the buoyant force per unit volume:** For a 1 cm³ PET particle in water: $$ F_{net,PET} = (1.38 - 1.00) \times 1 \times 9.81 = 3.73 \text{ mN} $$ For a 1 cm³ HDPE particle in water: $$ F_{net,HDPE} = (0.95 - 1.00) \times 1 \times 9.81 = -0.49 \text{ mN} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** Water alone separates PET from PE/PP but cannot separate HDPE from PP (both float). A second stage using **ethanol-water mixture ($\rho_{mix} \approx 0.93$ g/cm³)** is required for HDPE/PP discrimination. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Trommel Screen Critical Speed The trommel must rotate fast enough to lift material but slow enough to let it cascade (not centrifuge). **Step 1 — At critical speed, centrifugal force equals gravity:** $$ F_{centrifugal} = F_{gravity} $$ $$ m \, \omega_c^2 \, R = m \, g $$ **Step 2 — Solve for critical angular velocity $\omega_c$:** $$ \omega_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{R}} $$ **Step 3 — Convert to revolutions per minute ($N_c$):** $$ \omega_c = \dfrac{2\pi N_c}{60} $$ $$ \dfrac{2\pi N_c}{60} = \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{R}} $$ $$ N_c = \dfrac{60}{2\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{R}} $$ **Step 4 — Apply the **2/3 rule** for design operating speed:** For cascading action, design at **$N_{design} = 0.5 \, N_c$ to $0.75 \, N_c$**: $$ N_{design} = k \, N_c, \quad \text{where } k \in [0.5,\ 0.75] $$ **Step 5 — Numerical example:** Trommel of radius **$R = 1.0$ m**: $$ N_c = \dfrac{60}{2\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{9.81}{1.0}} $$ $$ N_c = 9.549 \times 3.132 = 29.91 \text{ rpm} $$ $$ N_{design} = 0.6 \times 29.91 = 17.95 \approx 18 \text{ rpm} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Practical KTU Pearl:** Operating the trommel at $k \approx 0.6$ maximizes the lift-and-cascade action, giving optimal size separation without centrifuging the waste against the drum wall. ## SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Integrated MRF Sorting Line ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Infeed Mixed Waste
Truck Tipper] --> B[Bag Breaker
and Manual Pre-Sort] B --> C[Trommel Screen
Size Classification] C --> D1[Oversize Fraction
Greater than 50 mm] C --> D2[Undersize Fraction
Less than 50 mm] D1 --> E[Magnetic Belt
Separator] E --> E1[Ferrous Metals
Steel and Iron] E --> F[Eddy Current
Separator] F --> F1[Non-Ferrous Metals
Aluminium and Copper] F --> G[Optical NIR
Sensor Sorter] G --> G1[PET Plastic Stream] G --> G2[HDPE and PP Stream] G --> G3[Mixed Paper Stream] D2 --> H[Air Classifier
Density Separation] H --> H1[Light Fraction
Paper and Film] H --> H2[Heavy Fraction
Glass and Stones] G1 --> I[Baler and Storage] G2 --> I G3 --> I E1 --> I F1 --> I H1 --> I H2 --> J[Landfill Residue] I --> K[Market Dispatch
Recyclable Output] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** Waste flows from top to bottom. Each rectangular node is a processing stage; each output is a recovered stream. The single "Landfill Residue" terminal node `J` represents the rejected inert fraction — the smaller this stream, the higher the MRF efficiency. --- ### 4.2 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Source Segregation ```mermaid flowchart LR H1[Household
Waste Generator] --> B1[Green Bin
Wet Waste] H1 --> B2[Blue Bin
Dry Waste] H1 --> B3[Red Bin
Hazardous Waste] B1 --> V1[Composting
or Biogas Plant] B2 --> V2[Material Recovery
Facility MRF] B3 --> V3[Treatment Storage
and Disposal Facility TSDF] V2 --> P1[Plastic Fraction] V2 --> P2[Paper Fraction] V2 --> P3[Metal Fraction] V2 --> P4[Glass Fraction] V1 --> O1[Organic Manure] V3 --> O2[Stabilized Residue] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** The three-bin source segregation model converts a chaotic mixed waste stream into three clean, parallel processing streams. This **parallelization** is the single most effective design decision in modern municipal solid waste management (MSWM). --- ### 4.3 Decision Matrix — Selection of Sorting Technology | Waste Property | Best Technology | Reason | |---|---|---| | Ferrous metal removal | Magnetic separator | High magnetic susceptibility of iron | | Aluminium recovery | Eddy current separator | High electrical conductivity | | PET vs. HDPE distinction | NIR optical sorter | Distinct spectral signatures | | Wet paper from plastic | Air classifier | Paper has lower density | | Glass cullet recovery | Trommel + sink–float | Glass is brittle + dense | | Organic from inert | Trommel screen | Organics are soft, larger particles | | PVC from PET mixture | Froth flotation | PVC is hydrophobic, PET is hydrophilic | ## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Differentiate between source segregation and centralized segregation. State two advantages of source segregation.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | Source Segregation | Centralized Segregation | |---|---|---| | Location | At point of generation | At MRF / transfer station | | Technology | Manual (3-bin system) | Mechanical + sensor-based | | Cost | Low capital, high social cost | High capital, low social cost | | Material purity | High | Lower due to contamination | **Two advantages of source segregation:** 1. Higher material purity → better resale value of recyclables (1.5 marks) 2. Reduces transportation cost as wet waste need not travel to landfill (1.5 marks) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **List any three mechanical methods of waste segregation with the physical property each method exploits.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Magnetic separation** — exploits magnetic susceptibility of ferrous metals (1 mark) 2. **Eddy current separation** — exploits electrical conductivity of non-ferrous metals like aluminium (1 mark) 3. **Optical/NIR sorting** — exploits spectral reflectance to distinguish polymer types (1 mark) --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `Choose any ONE from this option` **Q3(a) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the working principle of a Material Recovery Facility (MRF). With a neat flow diagram, describe the sequence of unit operations involved in dry-waste sorting. **Model Answer:** **Definition (1 mark):** A Material Recovery Facility (MRF) is a specialized plant that receives mixed dry waste and separates it into marketable recyclable streams (paper, plastic, metal, glass) using a combination of manual and mechanical unit operations. **Sequence of unit operations (5 marks):** 1. **Receiving and tipping:** Collection trucks discharge waste on a tipping floor. 2. **Manual pre-sorting:** Labours remove bulky items, hazardous objects, and visible contaminants. 3. **Bag breaking and screening:** Waste is shredded and passed over a trommel for size classification. 4. **Magnetic separation:** Over-belt magnet lifts ferrous metals (steel cans, iron pieces). 5. **Eddy current separation:** Non-ferrous metals (aluminium) are ejected by induced eddy currents. 6. **Air classification:** Lighter paper and plastic film are separated from heavier glass and stone. 7. **Optical/NIR sorting:** Polymer type (PET vs. HDPE) is identified and separated by air-jet. 8. **Baling and storage:** Sorted fractions are compressed into bales for market dispatch. **Flow diagram (1 mark):** Refer to Section 4.1 diagram of these notes. --- **Q3(b) [7 marks]** A municipal MRF receives **1200 kg/day** of mixed dry waste. After sorting, the following outputs are measured: - Plastics recovered: **340 kg/day** - Paper recovered: **260 kg/day** - Metals recovered: **120 kg/day** - Glass recovered: **60 kg/day** - Residue to landfill: **380 kg/day** - Process loss: **40 kg/day** Calculate: (i) Sorting efficiency, (ii) Recovery rate of plastic, (iii) Verify mass balance. **Model Answer:** **Step 1 — Total recovered material:** $$ M_{recovered} = 340 + 260 + 120 + 60 = 780 \text{ kg/day} $$ **Step 2 — Sorting efficiency:** $$ \eta_s = \dfrac{M_{recovered}}{M_{in}} \times 100 $$ $$ \eta_s = \dfrac{780}{1200} \times 100 = 65\% $$ **Step 3 — Plastic recovery rate:** (Assume plastic fraction in feed = **38%** by mass; i.e., target plastic input = $0.38 \times 1200 = 456$ kg/day) $$ R_{r,plastic} = \dfrac{M_{plastic,recovered}}{M_{plastic,in\ feed}} \times 100 $$ $$ R_{r,plastic} = \dfrac{340}{456} \times 100 = 74.56\% $$ **Step 4 — Mass balance verification:** $$ M_{recovered} + M_{residue} + M_{loss} = 780 + 380 + 40 = 1200 \text{ kg/day} $$ $$ M_{in} = M_{out} = 1200 \text{ kg/day} \quad \checkmark $$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `Alternative Choice` **Q4(a) [7 marks]** With neat sketches, explain the principle and working of **Magnetic Separation** and **Eddy Current Separation** for waste sorting. **Model Answer:** **Magnetic Separation (3.5 marks):** A permanent magnet or electromagnet is mounted over a conveyor belt carrying waste. Ferrous materials (iron, steel) experience an attractive magnetic force: $$ F_m = \dfrac{\chi_m \, V \, B^2}{2 \mu_0} $$ The ferrous particles are lifted off the belt against gravity, while non-magnetic materials fall back. A typical over-belt magnet has field strength **$B = 0.1$ to $0.3$ T**. **Eddy Current Separation (3.5 marks):** A rapidly alternating magnetic field is generated by a high-speed rotor containing permanent magnets. When conductive non-ferrous metals (Al, Cu) pass through this field, eddy currents are induced: $$ \vec{F}_L = q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B}) $$ These currents create a secondary magnetic field that **repels** the conductor from the rotor, ejecting it forward into a collection chute. Insulating materials (plastic, wood) are unaffected and follow the natural trajectory. --- **Q4(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the role of **Near-Infrared (NIR) optical sorting** in polymer waste segregation. Why is this technology critical for achieving circular economy goals? **Model Answer:** **Principle (3 marks):** NIR sensors illuminate waste particles with infrared light ($\lambda \approx 0.75 - 1.4\ \mu m$) and measure the reflected spectrum. Different polymers absorb/reflect uniquely because of their distinct chemical bond vibrations (C–H, O–H, N–H): $$ \text{Reflectance signature:} \quad \rho(\lambda) \neq \rho'(\lambda)\ \text{for distinct polymers} $$ A microprocessor compares the measured spectrum against a polymer library and triggers a **pulsed air-jet** ($\Delta t \approx 50$ ms) to deflect the target polymer into a separate chute. Throughput: up to **3 tonnes/hour** per lane. **Role in circular economy (4 marks):** 1. Enables **monomer-pure recycling streams** → PET recovered can be re-extruded into food-grade bottles (closed-loop). 2. Supports **EU PPWR (Packaging and Packaging Waste Regulation)** recycled-content mandates (e.g., 25% recycled PET in bottles by 2025). 3. Reduces **downcycling** — without NIR sorting, mixed plastic becomes low-grade plastic lumber only. 4. Decarbonises industry by displacing virgin petrochemical feedstocks with recycled polymers. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Sorting & Segregation Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing segregation with sorting** — they are sequential, not synonymous. Segregation = initial broad separation; sorting = detailed sub-classification. Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for this interchange. > 2. **Forgetting units** in numerical problems — always state kg/day, %, or m³/s explicitly. Missing units = **−1 mark**. > 3. **Skipping the mass balance verification step** — even if the numerical answer is correct, the model answer demands explicit verification. Skipping = **−1 mark**. > 4. **Diagrams without labelled flow directions** — always use arrows; an unlabelled block diagram loses **1–2 marks**. > 5. **Citing "magnetic" for aluminium recovery** — aluminium is **non-magnetic**; it requires eddy current separation. This error alone costs **2 marks**. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Sorting & Segregation of Waste** - ⭐ **Three-bin source segregation:** Green (wet), Blue (dry), Red (hazardous) — mandated by **SWM Rules 2016**. - ⭐ **Segregation precedes sorting** — they are sequential unit operations. - ⭐ **Six core mechanical sorting technologies:** Magnetic, Eddy Current, Air Classifier, Trommel, Optical/NIR, Sink–Float. - ⭐ **Sorting efficiency formula:** $\eta_s = \dfrac{M_{recovered}}{M_{input}} \times 100$ - ⭐ **Recovery rate formula:** $R_r = \dfrac{M_{target}}{M_{target,in\ feed}} \times 100$ - ⭐ **Mass balance must always close:** $M_{in} = M_{recovered} + M_{residue} + M_{loss}$ - ⭐ **Sink–float criterion:** Particle sinks if $\rho_p > \rho_m$; floats if $\rho_p < \rho_m$. - ⭐ **Trommel design speed:** $N_{design} = k \cdot N_c$, where $k = 0.5 - 0.75$ and $N_c = \dfrac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{R}}$. - ⭐ **NIR sorting** is the gold standard for polymer-type separation in modern MRFs. - ⭐ **Aluminium is non-magnetic** — use eddy current separator, not magnetic. - ⭐ **Typical Indian MRF recovery:** 60–85%; European best-in-class: 80–90%. - ⭐ **Source segregation reduces contamination by ≥ 40%** and MRF operating cost by 30–50%.

topics.title: Plastics Identification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT632 - POLYMERS & ENVIRONMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Plastics Identification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Plastics Identification** is the systematic set of analytical, physical, chemical, and spectroscopic procedures employed to determine the **chemical composition, grade, additive package, and structural class** of a polymeric material. Under the KTU 2024 Scheme (PEPOT632), it is defined as the umbrella term covering both *qualitative* (what type of polymer?) and *quantitative* (how much of each component?) characterization techniques used in the polymer industry, recycling sector, and failure analysis laboratories.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 2)**: Plastics Identification encompasses *Resin Identification Codes (RIC)*, *physical tests (density, melt flow, hardness)*, *thermal tests (DSC, TGA, melt point)*, *chemical tests (solubility, flame/beilstein, elemental analysis)*, and *spectroscopic methods (FTIR, NMR, UV-Vis)*.

The most universally adopted commercial identification protocol is the **SPI Resin Identification Code System (RIC)** introduced by the Society of the Plastics Industry in **1988**, which classifies the six most common consumer plastics using numerical codes **1 through 6** stamped inside the universal *chasing-arrows triangle*, with code **7** reserved for "Other" (multi-layer, bioplastics, or new engineering polymers).

> [!NOTE]
> **Resin Identification Code (RIC)** — A moulded, embossed, or printed symbol (a triangle of three arrows enclosing a numerical digit 1–7) used internationally on plastic articles to identify the base polymer resin for sorting in recycling streams.

## 1.2 The Six (Plus One) Standard Resin Codes

| RIC | Abbreviation | Full Chemical Name | Common Trade Names / Examples |
| :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **1** | PET / PETE | Poly(Ethylene Terephthalate) | Water & soft-drink bottles, food trays |
| **2** | HDPE | High-Density Poly(Ethylene) | Detergent bottles, milk jugs, pipes |
| **3** | PVC / V | Poly(Vinyl Chloride) | Plumbing pipes, window frames, cable insulation |
| **4** | LDPE | Low-Density Poly(Ethylene) | Squeeze bottles, plastic bags, six-pack rings |
| **5** | PP | Poly(Propylene) | Yogurt cups, bottle caps, straws, automotive parts |
| **6** | PS | Poly(Styrene) | Disposable cups, CD cases, expanded foam (EPS) |
| **7** | OTHER | Acrylic, PC, PLA, ABS, Nylon, multi-layer | Baby bottles, eyeglass lenses, 3D printing filament |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Polymer Detective"

Imagine you are a forensic scientist arriving at a "crime scene" that is a landfill. There are millions of pieces of plastic, all looking nearly identical to the untrained eye. Your job: **identify what each one is made of, in the shortest possible time, with the tools at hand.** This is exactly the role of a polymer identification specialist.

> [!TIP]
> **The Detective's Toolkit Analogy**:
> - **Visual examination** → Like reading a suspect's clothing and tattoos (RIC codes, surface marks, transparency).
> - **Density test (water vs. salt water)** → Like checking if a suspect floats or sinks — a quick "screening" test.
> - **Burn test (flame colour, smoke, odour)** → Like listening to a suspect's voice — each polymer has a unique "signature."
> - **FTIR / NMR spectroscopy** → Like a full DNA fingerprint — the gold standard, but expensive and lab-based.
> - **DSC / TGA thermal analysis** → Like a stress test that reveals the polymer's "temperament" (melting point, decomposition temperature).

The logic of identification always follows a **funnel approach**: cheap, fast, non-destructive methods first (visual, density, burn), progressively narrowing down to expensive, definitive spectroscopic techniques (FTIR, NMR) only when needed.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Hierarchical Funnel of Identification

Identification is never a single test. KTU examiners and the polymer industry both follow a **tiered methodology**:

### Tier 1 — Visual & Mechanical Screening (Seconds)
- **Look for RIC triangle** on the article.
- Examine **transparency, flexibility, surface gloss, hardness**.
- Test **flexibility** by bending (LDPE bends easily; PS is brittle and snaps).
- Examine **feel and sound** (PS produces a metallic "clink" when tapped).

### Tier 2 — Physical Property Tests (Minutes)
- **Density Test** — the single most useful property-based discriminator.
- **Melt Point / Softening Point (Vicat / Heat Deflection Temperature)**.
- **Melt Flow Index (MFI / MFR)** — grams per 10 minutes.
- **Hardness (Shore A / Shore D)**.

### Tier 3 — Chemical & Solubility Tests (Minutes–Hours)
- **Solubility in acetone, toluene, xylene, THF** at room temperature.
- **Beilstein Test** — copper wire flame test for halogens (Cl, Br) → confirms PVC.
- **Lieberman–Storch–Morawski Test** — colour reaction in chloroform + acetic anhydride + H₂SO₄.
- **Pyrolysis products** (condensed vapours pH, ignition behaviour).

### Tier 4 — Thermal Analysis (Hours)
- **DSC (Differential Scanning Calorimetry)** — exact Tg, Tm, crystallinity %.
- **TGA (Thermogravimetric Analysis)** — decomposition profile, filler content.
- **DMA (Dynamic Mechanical Analysis)** — viscoelastic fingerprint.

### Tier 5 — Spectroscopic Identification (Gold Standard)
- **FTIR (Fourier-Transform Infrared Spectroscopy)** — functional groups (C=O, C–H, C–Cl, aromatic C=C).
- **¹H-NMR / ¹³C-NMR** — exact monomer sequence and tacticity.
- **UV-Vis Spectroscopy** — chromophores (aromatic rings in PS, PET).
- **XRD (X-Ray Diffraction)** — crystalline vs. amorphous character.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they are favourite KTU problem-solving targets for Module 2.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Legend | Engineering Use |
| :-: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | $\rho$ = density, $m$ = mass, $V$ = volume | Direct density measurement |
| 2 | $\rho_{plastic} = \dfrac{m_{air}}{m_{air} - m_{water}} \cdot \rho_{water}$ | $m_{air}$ = mass in air, $m_{water}$ = apparent mass when submerged | Archimedes-based density (no volume measurement needed) |
| 3 | $\rho_{plastic} = \dfrac{m_{air} \cdot \rho_{liquid}}{m_{air} - m_{liquid}}$ | Generalised to any liquid of known density | Selective flotation in salt-water ($\rho \approx 1.20 \, g/cm^3$) |
| 4 | $MFR = \dfrac{m \cdot 600}{t}$ | $m$ = extruded mass in $g$, $t$ = cut-time in $s$, 600 = $s/10\,min$ | Determines polymer grade and processability |
| 5 | $A = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right) = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$ | $A$ = absorbance, $I_0, I$ = incident / transmitted intensity, $\varepsilon$ = molar absorptivity, $c$ = concentration, $l$ = path length | Beer–Lambert Law: foundation of FTIR quantification |
| 6 | $T\% = \dfrac{I}{I_0} \times 100 = 10^{-A} \times 100$ | $T\%$ = transmittance percentage | Quick screening from FTIR spectra |
| 7 | $X_c(\%) = \dfrac{\Delta H_m}{\Delta H_m^{\circ}} \times 100$ | $X_c$ = crystallinity, $\Delta H_m$ = measured melt enthalpy, $\Delta H_m^{\circ}$ = theoretical 100% crystalline enthalpy | DSC-based crystallinity calculation |
| 8 | $\eta = \dfrac{\pi \cdot R^4 \cdot \Delta P}{8 \cdot L \cdot Q}$ | Hagen–Poiseuille: $\eta$ = viscosity, $R$ = capillary radius, $\Delta P$ = pressure drop, $L$ = capillary length, $Q$ = volumetric flow rate | Capillary rheometry / MFI physics |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall**: In the density table, never confuse **density of amorphous vs. crystalline** regions. PE ranges from $0.85$ (amorphous) to $0.97$ (100% crystalline HDPE). PVC is highly amorphous ($\rho \approx 1.38$) unless plasticised (which lowers $\rho$).

## 2.3 The Reference Density — Master Table for KTU

| Polymer | Density Range (g/cm³) | Floats on Water? | Floats on Saturated Salt Water (1.20 g/cm³)? |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **PP** | $0.89 - 0.92$ | ✓ Yes | ✓ Yes |
| **LDPE** | $0.91 - 0.93$ | ✓ Yes | ✓ Yes |
| **HDPE** | $0.94 - 0.97$ | ✓ Yes | ✓ Yes |
| **PS** | $1.04 - 1.07$ | ✗ No | ✓ Yes |
| **PMMA (Acrylic)** | $1.17 - 1.20$ | ✗ No | ✗ No (just at boundary) |
| **PET** | $1.38 - 1.41$ | ✗ No | ✗ No |
| **PVC (rigid)** | $1.30 - 1.58$ | ✗ No | ✗ No |
| **PTFE** | $2.10 - 2.20$ | ✗ No | ✗ No |

> [!TIP]
> **Density Stratification in Lab**: A two-stage test using (i) tap water ($\rho = 1.00 \, g/cm^3$) and (ii) saturated NaCl solution ($\rho \approx 1.20 \, g/cm^3$) splits all common plastics into **three bins** — Floats on both → polyolefins (PP, PE); Sinks in water, floats in salt water → PS family; Sinks in both → PET, PVC, engineering plastics.

## 2.4 Why Identification Matters — Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Recycling Industry** — Sorting lines in MRFs (Material Recovery Facilities) use near-infrared (NIR) sensors whose algorithms were *trained* on FTIR spectra of known polymers. Without correct identification, mixed streams contaminate each other and down-cycle into low-grade products.
2. **Failure Analysis** — A cracked HDPE pipe vs. a cracked PP pipe requires different welding procedures; misidentification during forensic analysis leads to wrong root-cause reports.
3. **Food & Pharmaceutical Compliance** — FDA 21 CFR and EU 10/2011 require *positive identification* of food-contact plastics. A mislabeled PET tray sold as PP is a regulatory violation.
4. **Forensic Polymer Science** — Trace polymer fragments at crime scenes (car bumpers, packaging tape) can link a suspect to a location; identification is the first step.
5. **Sustainable Polymer Design** — Identification of "Code 7" plastics is the starting point for designing bio-based or biodegradable replacements (e.g., PLA, PHA).

## 2.5 KTU Conceptual Question — Burn Test "Signature" Map

> [!NOTE]
> **The burn test is the most frequently asked KTU question on identification. Memorise these signatures.**

| Polymer | Flame Colour | Smoke | Self-Extinguishing? | Odour | Drips? | Beilstein (Cu wire) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: |
| **PE** (LD/HD) | Blue with yellow tip | None/minimal | ✗ No | Paraffin (candle) | ✓ Yes, continues to burn | Negative |
| **PP** | Blue with yellow tip | Light white | ✗ No | Paraffin + slight acrylic | ✓ Yes | Negative |
| **PS** | Yellow, sooty | Heavy black | ✓ Yes (if pure) | Sweet, floral (marigold) | ✗ No, chars | Negative |
| **PVC** | Yellow with green edge | White (HCl fumes) | ✓ Yes (self-ext.) | Sharp, pungent HCl | ✗ No, chars | **Positive (green flame on Cu)** |
| **PMMA** | Yellow, sooty | Slight | ✓ Yes | Fruity, ester-like | ✓ Yes | Negative |
| **PET** | Yellow-orange | Black, sooty | ✓ Yes | Sweet, acrid | ✗ No, chars | Negative |
| **Nylon 6,6** | Blue with yellow tip | Minimal | ✓ Yes (often) | Celery, burnt hair | ✓ May drip | Negative |
| **ABS** | Yellow, sooty | Heavy black | ✗ No | Sweet + rubbery | ✗ No | Negative |
| **PTFE** | None — does not ignite | — | — | — | — | Negative |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation I — Archimedes-Based Density for Solid Plastic Chips

This is the most KTU-relevant derivation because it is the workhorse calculation in any polymer lab. The trick is to **eliminate the need to measure volume directly**, which is hard for irregular plastic chips.

### Starting Point — Definition of Density

$$\rho_{plastic} = \dfrac{m}{V}$$

The mass $m$ in air is easy to measure on an analytical balance. The volume $V$ is *not* — the plastic chip is irregular. So we use **Archimedes' Principle**: the upward buoyant force on a submerged object equals the weight of the displaced fluid.

$$F_{buoyancy} = \rho_{liquid} \cdot V \cdot g$$

The apparent loss of weight when submerged equals the buoyancy force:

$$m_{air} \cdot g - m_{liquid} \cdot g = \rho_{liquid} \cdot V \cdot g$$

Cancelling $g$ on both sides:

$$m_{air} - m_{liquid} = \rho_{liquid} \cdot V$$

Solving for the unknown volume $V$:

$$V = \dfrac{m_{air} - m_{liquid}}{\rho_{liquid}}$$

Substituting back into the density definition:

$$\rho_{plastic} = \dfrac{m_{air}}{V} = \dfrac{m_{air}}{(m_{air} - m_{liquid}) / \rho_{liquid}}$$

Rearranging — invert and multiply:

$$\rho_{plastic} = \dfrac{m_{air} \cdot \rho_{liquid}}{m_{air} - m_{liquid}}$$

### KTU Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A small plastic chip weighs $2.450 \, g$ in air. When suspended in water at $27^\circ C$ ($\rho_{water} = 0.9965 \, g/cm^3$), it appears to weigh $1.118 \, g$. Identify the polymer using the density reference table.

**Step 1 — Write the measured data**

$$m_{air} = 2.450 \, g, \quad m_{water} = 1.118 \, g, \quad \rho_{water} = 0.9965 \, g/cm^3$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Archimedes formula**

$$\rho_{plastic} = \dfrac{m_{air} \cdot \rho_{water}}{m_{air} - m_{water}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numbers**

$$\rho_{plastic} = \dfrac{2.450 \times 0.9965}{2.450 - 1.118}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate numerator and denominator**

$$\rho_{plastic} = \dfrac{2.441425}{1.332}$$

**Step 5 — Final division**

$$\rho_{plastic} = 1.833 \, g/cm^3$$

**Step 6 — Compare with reference table**

$$\rho_{calculated} = 1.833 \, g/cm^3 \implies \text{This is well above PVC, PMMA, PS, PET — it is in the PTFE range (2.10–2.20) or a heavily filled composite}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall**: Many students forget to apply the **air-correction buoyancy factor** for very precise work. For KTU problems, however, the simplified formula above (which assumes $\rho_{air} \approx 0$) is the expected answer. Always state the assumption.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Beer–Lambert Law Foundation for FTIR Quantification

The FTIR spectrometer is the workhorse of modern polymer identification. Its quantitative basis is the **Beer–Lambert Law**.

### Step 1 — Define Transmittance

The fraction of incident radiation that passes through a sample of thickness $l$:

$$T = \dfrac{I}{I_0}$$

where $I_0$ is the incident intensity and $I$ is the transmitted intensity.

### Step 2 — Define Absorbance

By convention, absorbance $A$ is the *negative logarithm* of transmittance:

$$A = -\log_{10} T = \log_{10} \left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Postulate the Linear Absorption Law (Beer's Law)

For a *dilute* absorbing species, each infinitesimal slab of thickness $dl$ removes a fraction proportional to the intensity and the local concentration $c$:

$$\dfrac{dI}{I} = -\kappa \cdot c \cdot dl$$

where $\kappa$ is a proportionality constant.

### Step 4 — Integrate Across the Sample Thickness

Integrate from $I_0$ at $l=0$ to $I$ at $l = l_{total}$:

$$\int_{I_0}^{I} \dfrac{dI}{I} = -\kappa \cdot c \cdot \int_{0}^{l} dl$$

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right) = -\kappa \cdot c \cdot l$$

### Step 5 — Convert to Base-10 Logarithm

Multiplying by $1/\ln(10) = 1/2.3026$ converts the natural log to base 10:

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right) = \dfrac{\kappa}{2.3026} \cdot c \cdot l$$

### Step 6 — Define the Molar Absorptivity

By defining $\varepsilon = \kappa / 2.3026$ (units: $L \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot cm^{-1}$), we obtain the **Beer–Lambert Law**:

$$\boxed{A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l}$$

### KTU Worked Example — Identifying PET via FTIR

A thin PET film of thickness $l = 0.025 \, cm$ shows a carbonyl C=O stretch at $1715 \, cm^{-1}$ with absorbance $A = 0.85$. The molar absorptivity of the ester carbonyl is $\varepsilon = 450 \, L \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot cm^{-1}$. Calculate the effective concentration of ester groups in the film.

**Step 1 — Rearrange Beer–Lambert Law**

$$c = \dfrac{A}{\varepsilon \cdot l}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values**

$$c = \dfrac{0.85}{450 \times 0.025}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the denominator**

$$c = \dfrac{0.85}{11.25}$$

**Step 4 — Final result**

$$c = 0.0756 \, mol/L$$

This is consistent with the theoretical ester-group concentration of PET repeat unit (MW $\approx 192 \, g/mol$, density $1.40 \, g/cm^3$), giving $\approx 0.073 \, mol/L$ — confirming the sample as PET.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Crystallinity from DSC

DSC is the most informative thermal method for semi-crystalline polymers like PE, PP, and PET.

**Formula for percentage crystallinity:**

$$X_c (\%) = \dfrac{\Delta H_m}{\Delta H_m^{\circ}} \times 100$$

**KTU Worked Example**: An HDPE sample shows a melt endotherm on DSC with $\Delta H_m = 162 \, J/g$. The reference value for 100% crystalline HDPE is $\Delta H_m^{\circ} = 293 \, J/g$.

**Step 1 — Substitute**

$$X_c = \dfrac{162}{293} \times 100$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate**

$$X_c = 55.3\%$$

This is typical for a commercial HDPE bottle-grade resin (range 50–70%).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for identification**: A LDPE sample with the same chemistry but a different thermal history will give $X_c \approx 30\text{–}40\%$, while a UHMWPE will give $X_c > 70\%$. The DSC fingerprint, combined with the density, distinguishes these three polyethylene sub-classes — a frequent KTU Part B question.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree Inputs

| Test Result | Most Likely Polymer | Confidence |
| :--- | :--- | :---: |
| Density $0.90\text{–}0.92$ + floats in water + burns with blue flame + paraffin smell + RIC 5 | **PP** | Very High |
| Density $1.04\text{–}1.07$ + sinks in water + floats in salt water + RIC 6 + sweet marigold odour on burn | **PS** | Very High |
| Density $1.38\text{–}1.41$ + sinks in both + sweet acrid burn smell + clear transparent bottle | **PET** | High |
| Density $1.30\text{–}1.58$ + RIC 3 + green-edged flame + HCl smell + positive Beilstein | **PVC** | Very High |
| Density $> 2.0$ + does not ignite at all + slippery feel | **PTFE** | High |
| Density $1.17\text{–}1.20$ + fruity ester smell on burn + transparent sheet | **PMMA** | High |
| Density $1.05\text{–}1.07$ + sinks in water + floats in salt water + transparent + bisphenol-A-type FTIR peak at $830 \, cm^{-1}$ | **PC** | High |

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Funnel Architecture of the Identification Process

This diagram maps the hierarchical decision flow a polymer analyst follows — from quick visual inspection down to definitive spectroscopic confirmation. Every step is **gated**: cheaper/faster tests precede expensive, time-consuming ones.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Unknown Plastic Sample] --> B{Tier 1: Visual + RIC Code Present?}
    B -- "Yes, RIC 1" --> C1[Identified as PET]
    B -- "Yes, RIC 2" --> C2[Identified as HDPE]
    B -- "Yes, RIC 3" --> C3[Identified as PVC]
    B -- "Yes, RIC 4" --> C4[Identified as LDPE]
    B -- "Yes, RIC 5" --> C5[Identified as PP]
    B -- "Yes, RIC 6" --> C6[Identified as PS]
    B -- "Yes, RIC 7 or No RIC" --> D[Proceed to Tier 2]
    B -- "No RIC code" --> D
    D --> E[Tier 2: Density Test in Tap Water]
    E --> F{Floats in Water?}
    F -- "Yes" --> G[Polyolefin group: PE or PP]
    F -- "No, Sinks" --> H[Non-polyolefin: PS, PVC, PET, PMMA, PC]
    G --> I[Tier 2b: Burn Test]
    I --> J{Blue Flame + Paraffin Smell?}
    J -- "Yes, no drip" --> K1[HDPE]
    J -- "Yes, drips" --> K2[LDPE or PP]
    H --> L[Tier 2b: Salt Water Test rho 1.20]
    L --> M{Floats in Saturated Salt Water?}
    M -- "Yes" --> N1[PS, PMMA boundary]
    M -- "No, sinks" --> N2[PET, PVC, PC, filled polymers]
    N1 --> O[FTIR confirms polystyrene peaks 700, 750 cm-1]
    N2 --> P[FTIR carbonyl 1715 cm-1 confirms PET]
    N2 --> Q[Beilstein Test positive green flame confirms PVC]
    N2 --> R[DSC scan Tm 220 to 230 confirms PET]
    K2 --> S[FTIR confirms PP methyl band 1375 cm-1]
    O --> T[Tier 5: Final Spectroscopic Confirmation]
    P --> T
    Q --> T
    R --> T
    S --> T
    T --> U[End: Confirmed Polymer Identity]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**: Every rectangle is a *process step*; every diamond is a *decision point*. The architecture is intentionally **tier-graded** — KTU examiners look for this kind of structured, cost-aware methodology in long-answer questions.

---

## 4.2 Decision Tree for the Two-Stage Density Stratification

This is the most commonly tested visual aid in KTU Module 2 questions. The simple two-liquid flotation test partitions the polymer universe into three groups.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Plastic Chip] --> B[Step 1: Place in Tap Water rho 1.00]
    B --> C{Observation}
    C -- "Floats" --> D[Group 1: Polyolefins]
    C -- "Sinks" --> E[Step 2: Place in Saturated NaCl Solution rho 1.20]
    D --> D1[Likely PP, LDPE, HDPE]
    D1 --> D2[Distinguish by burning: blue flame, paraffin smell]
    E --> F{Observation in Salt Water}
    F -- "Floats" --> G[Group 2: Polystyrene, PMMA boundary]
    G --> G1[PS has sweet marigold smell, snaps on bending]
    F -- "Sinks" --> H[Group 3: PET, PVC, PC, PTFE, filled polymers]
    H --> H1[PVC: green Beilstein flame, HCl smell]
    H --> H2[PET: sweet acrid smell, sinks in both]
    H --> H3[PTFE: does not ignite, very slippery feel]
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — FTIR Spectrometer Signal Chain for Polymer Identification

For the higher-order KTU questions that ask about instrumental methods, this is the canonical block architecture.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Infrared Source: Globar or Tungsten Filament] --> B[Interferometer: Michelson with Moving Mirror]
    B --> C[Sample Compartment: Thin Film or KBr Pellet]
    C --> D[Detector: DTGS or MCT]
    D --> E[Analog to Digital Converter]
    E --> F[Fourier Transform Algorithm FFT]
    F --> G[Spectrum: Absorbance vs Wavenumber cm-1]
    G --> H[Library Search: Match to HDPE, PET, PVC, PS]
    H --> I[Identified Polymer]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU key bands to memorise** (these appear in every question on FTIR):
> - **PE**: strong C–H stretches at $2916 \, cm^{-1}$ and $2848 \, cm^{-1}$; C–H bending at $1460 \, cm^{-1}$ and $720 \, cm^{-1}$.
> - **PP**: same as PE *plus* a strong methyl band at $1375 \, cm^{-1}$.
> - **PS**: aromatic C–H at $3025 \, cm^{-1}$; aromatic C=C at $1600 \, cm^{-1}$ and $1490 \, cm^{-1}$; out-of-plane C–H bends at $700$ and $750 \, cm^{-1}$ (the "fingerprint doublet" of styrene).
> - **PET**: strong carbonyl C=O at $1715 \, cm^{-1}$; C–O stretch at $1240 \, cm^{-1}$ and $1100 \, cm^{-1}$.
> - **PVC**: C–Cl stretch at $600\text{–}700 \, cm^{-1}$; C–H at $2900 \, cm^{-1}$.
