# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**List any three physical/chemical tests used for the identification of plastics. Explain the principle behind the density test in detail.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — structured, 60–80 words):**

Three identification tests are: **(i) Density test, (ii) Burn test, and (iii) Solubility test.**
The **density test** is based on **Archimedes' Principle**. A plastic chip is weighed in air ($m_{air}$) and then re-weighed when suspended in a liquid of known density ($m_{liquid}$, usually water at controlled temperature). The buoyant force equals the weight of displaced liquid, giving the chip's volume indirectly:

$$V = \dfrac{m_{air} - m_{liquid}}{\rho_{liquid}}$$

The plastic's density is then:

$$\rho_{plastic} = \dfrac{m_{air} \cdot \rho_{liquid}}{m_{air} - m_{liquid}}$$

Comparing with a reference table identifies the polymer (e.g., $0.94$–$0.97$ → HDPE).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Explain the Resin Identification Code (RIC) system. Why is Code 7 reserved for "Other" plastics, and what is its environmental significance?**

**Model Answer (3 marks — structured, 60–80 words):**

The **Resin Identification Code (RIC)** is a triangular chasing-arrows symbol enclosing a number from **1 to 7**, developed by the **Society of the Plastics Industry (SPI) in 1988** to facilitate recycling sorting. The six most common consumer plastics (PET, HDPE, PVC, LDPE, PP, PS) occupy codes 1–6. **Code 7 ("Other")** covers *all remaining resins* — acrylic (PMMA), polycarbonate (PC), acrylonitrile butadiene styrene (ABS), bioplastics (PLA, PHA), multilayer films, and fibre-reinforced composites. Its **environmental significance** is dual: (a) it warns recyclers that the item requires *specialised sorting* (Code 7 plastics are generally not accepted in curbside streams); (b) it flags the item as potentially containing engineering polymers whose incineration or landfilling can release toxic additives like bisphenol-A.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a) Describe the Resin Identification Code (RIC) system in detail, including all six major codes with examples of typical applications.** **[7 marks]**

**(b) Describe the burn test for polymer identification. Prepare a comparative table showing flame colour, smoke, odour, and dripping behaviour for PE, PP, PVC, PS, and PET.** **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

The Resin Identification Code (RIC) is a globally adopted marking system that uses a **chasing-arrows triangle** (originally intended as a recycling symbol, now interpreted as a *resin identification* symbol) enclosing a **numerical digit 1 through 6**, with **7 reserved for "Other"**. It was developed by the **Society of the Plastics Industry (SPI, now the Plastics Industry Association) in 1988** to standardise the sorting of post-consumer plastic waste.

**The six major codes are:**

1. **Code 1 – PET (Polyethylene Terephthalate):** Used in beverage bottles (water, soft drinks), food trays, and polyester fibre. Clear, transparent, lightweight. Easily recycled into fibre, strapping, and new bottles.
2. **Code 2 – HDPE (High-Density Polyethylene):** Used in milk jugs, detergent bottles, shampoo containers, and HDPE pipes. Opaque, waxy feel, high chemical resistance. Recycled into pipes, benches, and plastic lumber.
3. **Code 3 – PVC (Polyvinyl Chloride):** Used in plumbing pipes, window frames, electrical cable insulation, and medical tubing. Rigid, can be plasticised. Recycled into flooring and traffic cones.
4. **Code 4 – LDPE (Low-Density Polyethylene):** Used in plastic bags, six-pack rings, food wraps, and squeeze bottles. Flexible, transparent. Recycled into bin liners and new packaging film.
5. **Code 5 – PP (Polypropylene):** Used in yogurt cups, bottle caps, straws, and automotive battery cases. Higher melting point than PE, allowing hot-fill applications. Recycled into signal posts and automotive parts.
6. **Code 6 – PS (Polystyrene):** Used in disposable cups, CD cases, and expanded foam (EPS) packaging. Brittle, transparent, and produces a characteristic metallic sound when tapped. Recycled into insulation and rulers.
7. **Code 7 – Other:** Includes acrylic (PMMA), polycarbonate (PC), ABS, polyamide (nylon), and emerging bioplastics like PLA. These are *not* generally accepted in curbside recycling streams.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

The **burn test** is a qualitative identification technique where a small sample of the plastic is ignited in a controlled environment (a fume hood, using tongs) and the **flame colour, smoke, dripping behaviour, self-extinguishing tendency, and characteristic odour** are observed. Each polymer class has a unique "signature" of combustion behaviour.

**Step 1 — PE (LDPE / HDPE):** Burns with a **blue flame with a yellow tip**, produces **little to no smoke**, has a **paraffin / candle wax odour**, and **continues to drip** molten material that keeps burning. The drip is flammable. **Self-extinguishing? No.**

**Step 2 — PP (Polypropylene):** Burns with a **blue flame with a yellow tip**, similar to PE, but the odour is **slightly more acrid** (almost like a mix of paraffin and burnt rubber). It **drips** molten material that continues to burn. The methyl group in PP can be distinguished from PE by the appearance of a small characteristic IR peak at $1375 \, cm^{-1}$.

**Step 3 — PVC (Polyvinyl Chloride):** Burns with a **yellow flame with a distinctive green edge at the base**, produces **white fumes of HCl**, has a **sharp, pungent, choking odour of hydrogen chloride**, and is **self-extinguishing** (the flame dies when the source is removed). The drip, if any, is dark and does not continue to burn.

**Step 4 — PS (Polystyrene):** Burns with a **yellow, sooty flame**, produces **heavy black smoke** (due to the aromatic ring), has a **sweet, marigold-like floral odour**, and **does not drip** — instead it chars and bubbles. It is generally **self-extinguishing** when pure.

**Step 5 — PET (Polyethylene Terephthalate):** Burns with a **yellow-orange flame**, produces **black sooty smoke**, has a **sweet but acrid chemical odour**, and is **self-extinguishing** — the flame dies as soon as the source is removed and the material chars to a hard black bead.

**Comparative Table (synthesis of the five signatures):**

| Polymer | Flame Colour | Smoke | Odour | Drips? | Self-Extinguishing? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: |
| PE | Blue + yellow tip | Minimal | Paraffin (candle) | Yes | No |
| PP | Blue + yellow tip | Light | Paraffin + slight acrid | Yes | No |
| PVC | Yellow + green base | White (HCl) | Sharp HCl, pungent | No | **Yes** |
| PS | Yellow, sooty | Heavy black | Sweet marigold | No | Yes |
| PET | Yellow-orange | Black sooty | Sweet + acrid | No | Yes |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**: Students frequently *conflate* the burn behaviour of PE and PP because both have a blue/yellow flame. To score full marks, **explicitly mention the slight odour difference** (PP has a faintly more acrid smell) and the supporting test (FTIR methyl band at $1375 \, cm^{-1}$ for PP). Marks are also lost for forgetting the **safety note**: PVC combustion releases HCl, hence the test must be done in a fume hood with a wet cloth or water beaker nearby.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a) Explain the working principle of FTIR spectroscopy. How is it used for the identification of polymers? List the characteristic IR absorption bands for PE, PP, PS, PVC, and PET.** **[7 marks]**

**(b) With a neat diagram, explain the two-stage density stratification method for polymer identification. Using a worked example, show how the Archimedes-based density formula is applied.** **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) Spectroscopy** is the most widely used spectroscopic technique for polymer identification. Its working principle is as follows.

**Step 1 — Infrared source:** A Globar (silicon carbide) or tungsten-halogen filament emits a broad polychromatic infrared beam covering the mid-IR range ($4000\text{–}400 \, cm^{-1}$).

**Step 2 — Interferometer (Michelson type):** The IR beam is split by a beamsplitter into two paths — one reflected by a fixed mirror, the other by a moving mirror. The two beams recombine to produce an **interferogram** as a function of mirror displacement.

**Step 3 — Sample interaction:** The recombined beam passes through (or reflects off) the polymer sample. Chemical bonds absorb IR radiation at frequencies matching their vibrational modes (stretching, bending). The transmitted beam carries the absorption fingerprint.

**Step 4 — Detector and Fourier Transform:** A DTGS (deuterated triglycine sulfate) or MCT (mercury cadmium telluride) detector records the time-domain interferogram. A **Fourier Transform algorithm** converts this into the frequency-domain spectrum: **absorbance $A$ (or transmittance $T\%$) vs. wavenumber $\bar{\nu}$ ($cm^{-1}$).**

**Step 5 — Identification:** The spectrum is matched against a reference library of > 200,000 polymer spectra (e.g., HR Spectra, KnowItAll, OMNIC). Each functional group has a characteristic absorption band; their pattern is the polymer's fingerprint.

**Beer–Lambert Law** governs the quantitative relationship:

$$A = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right) = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$$

**Characteristic IR Absorption Bands:**

| Polymer | Key IR Bands (cm⁻¹) | Functional Group / Assignment |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **PE** | $2916, 2848$ (C–H stretch); $1460, 720$ (C–H bend) | Aliphatic –CH₂– |
| **PP** | $2950, 2920, 2860$ (C–H stretch); $1455$ (–CH₂– bend); **$1375$ (–CH₃ bend)** | Methyl group is the discriminator |
| **PS** | $3025$ (aromatic C–H stretch); $1600, 1490$ (aromatic C=C); $700, 750$ (out-of-plane bend) | Aromatic ring — "fingerprint doublet" |
| **PVC** | $2900$ (C–H stretch); $1420$ (–CH₂– bend); $600\text{–}700$ (C–Cl stretch) | Carbon–chlorine bond |
| **PET** | $1715$ (C=O carbonyl stretch); $1240, 1100$ (C–O stretch); $725$ (aromatic C–H bend) | Ester linkage + aromatic ring |

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Two-Stage Density Stratification** is a low-cost, high-throughput screening method that uses two liquids of progressively higher density — **tap water ($\rho = 1.00 \, g/cm^3$)** and **saturated sodium chloride solution ($\rho \approx 1.20 \, g/cm^3$)** — to partition unknown plastic samples into three groups.

**Step 1 — Stage 1 (Tap Water):** The plastic chip is gently placed into a beaker of tap water. If it **floats**, the polymer's density is $< 1.00 \, g/cm^3$, immediately indicating a **polyolefin** (PP, LDPE, or HDPE). If it **sinks**, density is $> 1.00 \, g/cm^3$, indicating PS, PVC, PET, PMMA, or PC.

**Step 2 — Stage 2 (Saturated NaCl Solution):** Samples that sank in water are transferred to a saturated salt solution ($\rho \approx 1.20 \, g/cm^3$). Those that **float** here have density between $1.00$ and $1.20 \, g/cm^3$ — typically **PS** ($1.04$–$1.07$) or **PMMA** ($1.17$–$1.20$). Those that **sink** in both liquids have density $> 1.20 \, g/cm^3$ — typically **PET** ($1.38$), **PVC** ($1.30$–$1.58$), **PC** ($1.20$–$1.22$), or **PTFE** ($2.10$).

**Step 3 — Diagram (text-based, drawn neatly in the answer script):**

```
   ┌──────────────┐
   │ Plastic Chip │
   └──────┬───────┘
          │
          ▼
   ┌────────────────────┐
   │  Tap Water (ρ=1.00) │
   └────────┬───────────┘
            │ Floats?        Yes → Polyolefin group (PP/PE)
            │ No, sinks
            ▼
   ┌──────────────────────────────┐
   │ Saturated NaCl (ρ ≈ 1.20)    │
   └────────┬─────────────────────┘
            │ Floats?        Yes → PS / PMMA group
            │ No, sinks
            ▼
   ┌──────────────────────────────┐
   │ Density > 1.20: PET, PVC, PC │
   │ Confirm by FTIR / Beilstein  │
   └──────────────────────────────┘
```

**Worked Example:**

A polymer chip weighs $1.850 \, g$ in air and $0.420 \, g$ when suspended in water at $25^\circ C$ ($\rho_{water} = 0.997 \, g/cm^3$). It sinks in tap water and in saturated salt water. Identify the polymer and explain.

**Applying the Archimedes-based density formula:**

$$\rho_{plastic} = \dfrac{m_{air} \cdot \rho_{water}}{m_{air} - m_{water}}$$

$$\rho_{plastic} = \dfrac{1.850 \times 0.997}{1.850 - 0.420} = \dfrac{1.84445}{1.430}$$

$$\rho_{plastic} = 1.290 \, g/cm^3$$

**Comparison with reference table:** Density $1.29 \, g/cm^3$ falls within the range of **rigid PVC ($1.30$–$1.58$)**, or plasticised PVC (slightly lower density). The sinking behaviour in both liquids is consistent with this. To confirm, a Beilstein test (copper-wire flame) should turn the flame **green** (positive for chlorine), and FTIR should show a strong C–Cl band near $650 \, cm^{-1}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**: Two common mistakes in this question: (1) **Mixing up the formula** — students often write $\rho = m_{water} / (m_{air} - m_{water})$ instead of using $m_{air}$ in the numerator. Marks lost: 2. (2) **Not writing the formula symbolically first** before substituting numbers — examiners reward structured solutions. Marks lost: 1. (3) **Forgetting units** in the final answer. Marks lost: 0.5.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final-minute revision — read this list three times before entering the exam hall.**

- ☐ **Resin Identification Codes 1–7** must be memorised with examples (PET, HDPE, PVC, LDPE, PP, PS, Other).
- ☐ The **chasing-arrows triangle** is *not* a guarantee of recyclability; it is a *resin identification* symbol introduced in **1988 by SPI**.
- ☐ **Polyolefins** (PE and PP) are the only common plastics with density $< 1 \, g/cm^3$ — they *float* in water.
- ☐ **PS** ($1.04$–$1.07$) is the only common plastic in the "$1.00$–$1.20$" density window — *floats in salt water, sinks in tap water*.
- ☐ **PET, PVC, PC, PMMA** all have density $> 1.20$ — they *sink in both liquids*.
- ☐ **PTFE** has density $\approx 2.2$ — it is the *only* common plastic that *does not ignite* in a burn test.
- ☐ The **Archimedes-based density formula** is the most tested KTU equation: $\rho = m_{air} \rho_{liquid} / (m_{air} - m_{liquid})$.
- ☐ **Burn test signature map**: PE/PP = blue flame, paraffin; PVC = green-edged flame, HCl; PS = yellow sooty, marigold; PET = yellow-orange, sweet acrid.
- ☐ **Beilstein test** is the *specific* confirmatory test for PVC — a copper wire dipped in the polymer and held in a flame turns **green** if halogens (Cl) are present.
- ☐ **FTIR characteristic peaks** (must be memorised for Part B):
   - PE: $2916, 2848, 1460, 720 \, cm^{-1}$
   - PP: same as PE *plus* methyl band at $1375 \, cm^{-1}$
   - PS: aromatic $3025, 1600, 1490$ + fingerprint doublet at $700, 750 \, cm^{-1}$
   - PVC: $2900$ (C–H), $600\text{–}700$ (C–Cl)
   - PET: $1715$ (C=O carbonyl), $1240, 1100$ (C–O)
- ☐ **Beer–Lambert Law**: $A = \varepsilon c l$ — foundation of quantitative FTIR.
- ☐ **DSC crystallinity formula**: $X_c (\%) = (\Delta H_m / \Delta H_m^{\circ}) \times 100$; reference value for 100% crystalline HDPE is $293 \, J/g$.
- ☐ The **identification funnel** always moves from cheap visual/physical tests → chemical → thermal → spectroscopic.
- ☐ **Safety note** for burn test of PVC: must be performed in a fume hood with wet cloth nearby; HCl fumes are toxic and corrosive.
- ☐ **Code 7** is the "catch-all" — it includes bioplastics (PLA, PHA), engineering plastics (PC, ABS), and multi-layer films.
- ☐ Always state the **assumption** when using the simplified Archimedes formula (neglecting $\rho_{air}$).
- ☐ When a question says "list and explain," marks are split **50% for listing correctly** and **50% for the explanation** — a one-line answer for each will cost you at least 2 marks.
- ☐ For Part B derivations, **write the formula symbolically first**, then substitute numbers, then evaluate — this is the KTU examiner's structural pattern.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SPI Coding, Primary Recycling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT632 - POLYMERS & ENVIRONMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** SPI Coding, Primary Recycling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 SPI Resin Identification Coding System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SPI Coding** is a standardized numeric labelling convention introduced by the **Society of the Plastics Industry (SPI)** in **1988** to facilitate the segregation, sorting, and recycling of post-consumer plastic waste at a glance.

The **SPI Resin Identification Code (RIC)** system is a globally adopted scheme that assigns a numeric identifier (1 through 7) to the seven major categories of thermoplastic polymers. The code is depicted as a **chasing-arrows triangle (Möbius loop)** with the corresponding number placed centrally inside. It is *not* technically a "recyclability" symbol but rather a *material identification* code that helps recovery facilities sort mixed plastic streams.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Library Index System":**
> Imagine a massive library where books are written in seven different languages. A librarian, instead of opening every book to read the first page, simply looks at a colored sticker on the spine (Red = English, Blue = Hindi, etc.) to instantly route it to the right section. Similarly, the **SPI Code is the "colored sticker"** on every plastic product. When PET bottles (Code 1) arrive at a recycling plant, workers do not have to perform a chemical test; they simply read the "**1**" inside the triangle and channel it directly into the PET processing line. This single-digit code saves **millions of labor-hours** annually in material recovery facilities (MRFs).

### The Seven SPI Resin Codes — Master Reference

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Dec 2024 Expected):** Students are routinely asked to "List the SPI codes with their common applications." Memorizing the seven categories with examples is a **guaranteed 3-mark question**.

| SPI Code | Polymer (Full Name) | Polymer Abbreviation | Common Everyday Items |
|:--------:|:--------------------|:--------------------:|:----------------------|
| **1** | Polyethylene Terephthalate | **PET / PETE** | Water bottles, soft drink bottles, food jars |
| **2** | High-Density Polyethylene | **HDPE** | Milk jugs, detergent bottles, shampoo containers |
| **3** | Polyvinyl Chloride | **PVC / V** | Plumbing pipes, window frames, cable insulation |
| **4** | Low-Density Polyethylene | **LDPE** | Plastic bags, squeeze bottles, cling film |
| **5** | Polypropylene | **PP** | Yogurt cups, bottle caps, straws, automotive parts |
| **6** | Polystyrene | **PS** | Disposable coffee cups, CD cases, foam packaging |
| **7** | Other (Mixed / Multi-layer) | **OTHER** | Polycarbonate (PC), acrylic, nylon, bioplastics |

## 1.2 Primary Recycling — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Primary Recycling** is the simplest and most economically viable form of plastic recycling in which **pre-consumer** or **post-consumer** plastic scrap is mechanically reprocessed back into a product that possesses **equivalent functional properties** to the original parent material. It is also referred to as **"Closed-Loop Recycling"** because the material re-enters the same product life-cycle.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Worn-Out T-Shirt"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Repurposing Cotton Threads":**
> Take an old white cotton T-shirt. You cut it into small pieces, pull out the yarn, re-spin it, and weave a brand new white T-shirt. The new shirt is made of the **same cotton, same color, same quality**. No chemical transformation occurred — only physical reshaping. This is *exactly* what happens in primary recycling. An old PET water bottle is shredded, washed, melted, and re-extruded into pellets that can be blow-molded into a **new PET water bottle** — chemically and physically the same as before.

### Distinguishing Primary Recycling from Other Forms

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse **Primary** with **Secondary** recycling. The *difference lies in property retention*. Primary recycling preserves the original polymer's mechanical and chemical integrity, while secondary recycling leads to **downcycling** (inferior products like park benches from bottles). This distinction is a **favourite KTU question**.

| Recycling Tier | Property Retention | Product Example |
|:---------------|:-------------------|:----------------|
| Primary | **Identical** to virgin | Bottle → Bottle |
| Secondary | **Reduced** (downcycled) | Bottle → Park Bench |
| Tertiary | **Chemically altered** (monomers recovered) | Bottle → Fuel |
| Quaternary | **Energy recovered** (incineration) | Bottle → Electricity |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The SPI Coding System — Operational Logic

The rationale behind the SPI code structure is rooted in **polymer chemistry compatibility**. Each code represents a polymer family with distinct:

- **Chemical structure** (monomer composition)
- **Thermal behavior** (melting point range)
- **Density profile** (governs separation via float-sink tanks)
- **Reactivity with additives** (food-grade vs. industrial-grade)

### Why the Chasing-Arrows Triangle?

The symbol was intentionally designed with **three arrows** to communicate *recovery potential*. However, the triangle is sometimes misinterpreted by consumers as a "guaranteed recyclability" mark. In reality, the *number* inside the triangle is the *only* scientifically valid identifier.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The SPI code allows **automated Near-Infrared (NIR) spectroscopy** sorting systems to rapidly identify polymers on conveyor belts moving at speeds exceeding **2–3 meters/second**.

## 2.2 Primary Recycling — The Closed-Loop Process

Primary recycling is a strictly **mechanical** process (no chemical bond breaking occurs). It follows a sequential workflow:

### Process Flow — Step-by-Step

1. **Collection & Segregation**
   - Plastic scrap is gathered either from manufacturing waste (*pre-consumer / post-industrial*) or from household waste (*post-consumer*).
   - SPI codes are used at this stage to **manually or automatically sort** the scrap into seven streams.

2. **Washing & Contaminant Removal**
   - The sorted plastic is washed with water (sometimes with mild detergents or caustic soda) to remove **food residue, labels, adhesives, and dirt**.
   - Failure to properly wash leads to **hydrolysis degradation** during melting (especially critical for PET and PA).

3. **Shredding / Agglomeration**
   - Cleaned plastics are passed through **granulators** or **shredders** to reduce them into flakes of size **5–10 mm** or **agglomerates** of **2–8 mm** diameter.

4. **Melt Extrusion & Filtration**
   - Flakes are fed into an **extruder** where the rotating screw (L/D ratio typically **20:1 to 30:1**) melts the polymer (**150 °C – 280 °C** depending on polymer).
   - The molten polymer passes through a **screen pack / filter mesh** to remove fine contaminants.

5. **Pelletization**
   - The filtered melt is forced through a **die** and cut by a rotating blade into uniform pellets (typically **2–5 mm** length).

6. **Re-introduction into Manufacturing**
   - The recycled pellets are either blended with virgin resin or used **100% alone** to manufacture new products of the same application.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following equations are the most-tested quantitative tools for KTU Module 2 problems.**

### A. Melt Flow Index (MFI / MFR)

$$
\begin{aligned}
MFI &= \frac{m}{t}
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $m$ = mass of polymer extruded (grams)
- $t$ = time of extrusion (minutes)

The MFI of recycled polymer **increases** after primary recycling because the polymer chain undergoes slight **thermo-mechanical degradation**, reducing molecular weight.

### B. Degree of Polymerization (DP)

$$
\begin{aligned}
DP &= \frac{M_n}{M_0}
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $M_n$ = number-average molecular weight of polymer ($\text{g/mol}$)
- $M_0$ = molecular weight of the repeat unit ($\text{g/mol}$)

After multiple primary recycling cycles, $M_n$ decreases $\Rightarrow$ $DP$ decreases $\Rightarrow$ mechanical strength decreases.

### C. Density-Based Separation Equation (Stokes' Law)

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{2 r^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{9 \eta}
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $v$ = terminal settling velocity ($\text{m/s}$)
- $r$ = radius of plastic flake ($\text{m}$)
- $\rho_p$ = density of plastic ($\text{kg/m}^3$)
- $\rho_f$ = density of flotation fluid (water $\approx 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$)
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration ($9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$)
- $\eta$ = dynamic viscosity of fluid ($\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$)

**Key Insight:** Plastics with $\rho_p > 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (PET = 1380, PVC = 1400) **sink** in water, while those with $\rho_p < 1000$ (LDPE = 920, PP = 905) **float**.

### D. Mass Balance in a Closed-Loop System

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{virgin} + M_{recycled} &= M_{product}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a **100% closed loop** in primary recycling: $M_{virgin} = 0$, and $M_{recycled} = M_{product}$.

### E. Cumulative Property Retention Factor (Quality Decay Across Cycles)

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_n &= P_0 \cdot (1 - \alpha)^n
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $P_n$ = property (e.g., tensile strength) after $n$ cycles
- $P_0$ = original property of virgin material
- $\alpha$ = fractional loss per cycle (typically $\alpha = 0.05$ to $0.15$)
- $n$ = number of primary recycling cycles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** This decay equation tells engineers how many times a polymer can be **mechanically recycled** before it must be downcycled. For PET, the practical limit is **n = 4 to 6 cycles** before $\alpha$-crystallinity loss makes it unusable for food-grade bottles.

## 2.4 Applicability Matrix — Polymer vs. Primary Recycling Suitability

| Polymer | SPI Code | Suitable for Primary Recycling? | Reason |
|:--------|:--------:|:-------------------------------:|:-------|
| PET | 1 | **Highly Suitable** | Clean streams, low contamination |
| HDPE | 2 | **Highly Suitable** | Stable thermal profile |
| PVC | 3 | **Limited** | Releases HCl gas at processing temperatures |
| LDPE | 4 | **Suitable** | Soft, easy to reprocess |
| PP | 5 | **Highly Suitable** | High melting point stability |
| PS | 6 | **Limited** | Brittle; yellows on re-processing |
| Others (PC, PA) | 7 | **Case-specific** | Hydrolysis-prone, requires drying |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Density Sorting Works (from Stokes' Law)

We derive the **terminal velocity** of a spherical plastic flake falling through a fluid column (typical in an industrial float-sink separator).

### Given

A PET flake of radius $r = 4 \text{ mm} = 4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$ and density $\rho_p = 1380 \text{ kg/m}^3$ falls through water at **20 °C**, where $\rho_f = 998 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and $\eta = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$.

### Step 1 — Write Stokes' Law

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{2 r^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{9 \eta}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Numerical Values

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{2 \cdot (4 \times 10^{-3})^2 \cdot (1380 - 998) \cdot 9.81}{9 \cdot (1.002 \times 10^{-3})}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Numerator

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= 2 \cdot 16 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 382 \cdot 9.81 \\
&= 32 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 3747.42 \\
&= 0.119917 \text{ (units: kg·m/s²)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Denominator

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Denominator} &= 9 \cdot 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 9.018 \times 10^{-3} \text{ (units: Pa·s = kg/m·s)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Final Calculation

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{0.119917}{9.018 \times 10^{-3}} \\
v &\approx 13.29 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A PET flake sinks at ~13 m/s in water. Since $\rho_p > \rho_f$, PET sinks to the bottom of the float-sink tank and is collected separately — this is how an MRF sorts PET (1) from HDPE (2) and PP (5), which all have different densities.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Property Decay Across Multiple Primary Recycling Cycles

### Problem Setup

A virgin HDPE container has an initial tensile strength $P_0 = 31 \text{ MPa}$. The fractional property loss per cycle is $\alpha = 0.08$. Calculate the tensile strength after **3 cycles** and determine after how many cycles the strength falls below **20 MPa** (industrial threshold for re-blow-molding).

### Step 1 — State the Decay Equation

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_n &= P_0 \cdot (1 - \alpha)^n
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Values for $n = 3$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_3 &= 31 \cdot (1 - 0.08)^3 \\
&= 31 \cdot (0.92)^3
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute $(0.92)^3$

$$
\begin{aligned}
(0.92)^2 &= 0.8464 \\
(0.92)^3 &= 0.8464 \cdot 0.92 = 0.778688
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Final Strength After 3 Cycles

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_3 &= 31 \cdot 0.778688 \\
P_3 &\approx 24.14 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Find the Cycle at Which $P_n < 20 \text{ MPa}$

Set $P_n < 20$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
31 \cdot (0.92)^n &< 20 \\
(0.92)^n &< \frac{20}{31} \\
(0.92)^n &< 0.6452
\end{aligned}
$$

Take natural logarithm on both sides:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n \cdot \ln(0.92) &< \ln(0.6452) \\
n \cdot (-0.0834) &< (-0.4390) \\
n &> \frac{0.4390}{0.0834} \\
n &> 5.26
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Conclusion

Since $n$ must be an integer:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\max} = 5 \text{ cycles}
\end{aligned}
$$

After **5 cycles**, the HDPE loses its food-grade mechanical integrity and must either be **downcycled** (secondary) or **chemically recycled** (tertiary).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** This type of calculation has appeared in **KTU University Exams (June 2023 and Dec 2023)** as a 7-mark sub-part of a 14-mark question. Master this decay equation.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — SPI Code Identification Decision Tree

| Observation in Product | SPI Code | Verification Method |
|:-----------------------|:--------:|:--------------------|
| Transparent, rigid, food-contact bottle | 1 (PET) | Burn test: yellow flame, sweet odor |
| Opaque, waxy feel, milk jug | 2 (HDPE) | Density $\approx 950 \text{ kg/m}^3$ |
| Rigid pipe with chlorine smell when cut | 3 (PVC) | Beilstein test (green flame = chlorine) |
| Soft, flexible, transparent film | 4 (LDPE) | Density $\approx 920 \text{ kg/m}^3$ |
| Living hinge (bottle cap hinge) | 5 (PP) | Floats on water, floats on oil |
| Brittle, foamed, clear cup | 6 (PS) | Density $\approx 1050 \text{ kg/m}^3$ |
| Multi-layer or labelled "OTHER" | 7 | Cannot be sorted by simple methods |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SPI Resin Code Wheel — Hierarchical Classification

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["SPI RESIN IDENTIFICATION SYSTEM"] --> B["Code 1 - PET"]
    A --> C["Code 2 - HDPE"]
    A --> D["Code 3 - PVC"]
    A --> E["Code 4 - LDPE"]
    A --> F["Code 5 - PP"]
    A --> G["Code 6 - PS"]
    A --> H["Code 7 - OTHERS"]

    B --> B1["Bottles, Jars"]
    C --> C1["Milk Jugs, Detergent Bottles"]
    D --> D1["Pipes, Window Frames"]
    E --> E1["Plastic Bags, Cling Film"]
    F --> F1["Caps, Yogurt Cups, Straws"]
    G --> G1["Foam Cups, CD Cases"]
    H --> H1["Polycarbonate, Nylon, Bioplastics"]
```

## 4.2 Primary Recycling — Closed-Loop Process Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1["STEP 1: Collection and Sorting using SPI Codes"] --> S2["STEP 2: Washing and Contaminant Removal"]
    S2 --> S3["STEP 3: Shredding into Flakes"]
    S3 --> S4["STEP 4: Melt Extrusion at 150 to 280 C"]
    S4 --> S5["STEP 5: Filtration through Screen Pack"]
    S5 --> S6["STEP 6: Pelletization"]
    S6 --> S7["STEP 7: New Product of Same Grade"]
    S7 --> S1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Reading Guide:** The arrow looping from **STEP 7** back to **STEP 1** illustrates the **closed-loop (primary) nature** of this recycling tier. The same PET bottle can theoretically re-enter this loop continuously, although the property decay equation limits it to about **4–6 cycles**.

## 4.3 Float-Sink Density Sorting — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1["Mixed Plastic Feed"] --> P2["Shredder Unit"]
    P2 --> P3["Water Tank Float-Sink Separator"]
    P3 --> P4["FLOATING FRACTION - LDPE, PP, HDPE"]
    P3 --> P5["SINKING FRACTION - PET, PVC, PS"]
    P4 --> P6["Hydrocyclone Stage 2"]
    P6 --> P7["Sorted LDPE Stream"]
    P6 --> P8["Sorted PP Stream"]
    P5 --> P9["Sink Recovery Conveyor"]
    P9 --> P10["Sorted PET Stream"]
    P9 --> P11["Sorted PVC Stream"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** This is the *heart* of an industrial Material Recovery Facility (MRF). Stokes' law governs the separation — denser plastics (PET, PVC) sink, while lighter plastics (LDPE, PP) float. A second hydrocyclone stage separates the floating fraction by exploiting subtle density differences.

## 4.4 Primary Recycling vs. Other Tiers — Comparative Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    R["Plastic Recycling Tiers"] --> R1["Primary - Mechanical Closed Loop"]
    R --> R2["Secondary - Mechanical Downcycling"]
    R --> R3["Tertiary - Chemical Feedstock Recovery"]
    R --> R4["Quaternary - Energy Recovery"]

    R1 --> R1a["Same Quality Output"]
    R2 --> R2a["Reduced Quality Output"]
    R3 --> R3a["Monomer or Fuel Output"]
    R4 --> R4a["Heat and Electricity Output"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
*Define the SPI Resin Identification Code. List any four codes with their corresponding polymer names and one common application each.*

**Model Answer:**

The **SPI Resin Identification Code (RIC)** is a numerical coding system introduced by the **Society of the Plastics Industry** in **1988** for identifying plastic polymers to facilitate sorting and recycling. The code is a chasing-arrows triangle enclosing a number from **1 to 7**.

**Four codes with examples:**

- **Code 1 — PET (Polyethylene Terephthalate):** Used for soft drink and water bottles.
- **Code 2 — HDPE (High-Density Polyethylene):** Used for milk jugs and detergent containers.
- **Code 3 — PVC (Polyvinyl Chloride):** Used for plumbing pipes and cable insulation.
- **Code 5 — PP (Polypropylene):** Used for bottle caps, straws, and yogurt containers.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*What is primary recycling of plastics? Why is it also called "closed-loop recycling"?*

**Model Answer:**

**Primary recycling** is the simplest form of plastic recycling in which plastic scrap is mechanically reprocessed (shredded, washed, melted, and pelletized) to produce a new product that has **properties equivalent to the original parent material**.

It is called **"closed-loop recycling"** because the recovered polymer re-enters the **same application life-cycle** from which it originated — for example, a PET bottle is recycled back into a PET bottle, closing the material loop without any downgrading of quality.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*(a)* With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **mechanical process of primary recycling** of plastics. List any **three limitations** of this recycling method. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A virgin HDPE has a tensile strength of **30 MPa**. After each primary recycling cycle, the strength reduces by **9%**. Calculate the tensile strength after **4 cycles** and determine the maximum number of cycles before the strength drops below **18 MPa**. *(7 marks)*

#### Solution to Part (a)

The mechanical process of primary recycling consists of the following sequential stages:

1. **Collection and Sorting:** Plastic scrap is collected from industrial waste or consumer waste and segregated using the **SPI Resin Identification Codes** (manual sorting or automated NIR sorting).

2. **Washing:** The sorted plastic is washed with water and mild detergents to remove **food residues, labels, adhesives, and dirt**. Inadequate washing causes degradation during the melting stage.

3. **Shredding / Granulation:** The cleaned plastic is mechanically shredded into small flakes (**5–10 mm**) or agglomerated to increase the surface area for uniform melting.

4. **Melt Extrusion:** The flakes are fed into an **extruder** equipped with a heated barrel (**150 °C to 280 °C**) and a rotating screw. The polymer melts and is forced forward.

5. **Filtration:** A **screen pack** (mesh filter) removes fine contaminants and paper fibres.

6. **Pelletization:** The molten polymer is extruded through a die and cut into uniform pellets (**2–5 mm**).

7. **Re-introduction:** Pellets are fed back into manufacturing (blow-molding, injection-molding) to produce the **same-grade product**.

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.2 above for the schematic.)

**Three Limitations of Primary Recycling:**

1. **Quality Degradation:** Each cycle causes thermo-mechanical degradation, reducing molecular weight and mechanical strength.
2. **Contamination Sensitivity:** Even minor contamination (food residue, mixed polymers) downgrades the entire batch.
3. **Limited Cycle Count:** Polymers can typically be primary-recycled only **4 to 6 times** before becoming unusable.
4. (Bonus) Single-polymer requirement — multi-layer packaging (Code 7) cannot be primary recycled.

#### Solution to Part (b)

**Given:**
- $P_0 = 30 \text{ MPa}$
- $\alpha = 0.09$ (i.e., 9% loss per cycle)
- $n_1 = 4$ cycles
- Threshold $= 18 \text{ MPa}$

**Step 1 — Calculate tensile strength after 4 cycles:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_4 &= P_0 \cdot (1 - \alpha)^4 \\
&= 30 \cdot (0.91)^4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $(0.91)^4$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(0.91)^2 &= 0.8281 \\
(0.91)^4 &= (0.8281)^2 = 0.6857
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Final result:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_4 &= 30 \cdot 0.6857 \\
P_4 &\approx 20.57 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Determine maximum cycles before $P_n < 18 \text{ MPa}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
30 \cdot (0.91)^n &< 18 \\
(0.91)^n &< 0.6 \\
n \cdot \ln(0.91) &< \ln(0.6) \\
n \cdot (-0.0943) &< (-0.5108) \\
n &> 5.41
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\max} = 5 \text{ cycles}
\end{aligned}
$$

After **5 cycles**, HDPE loses sufficient strength (drops below 18 MPa) and can no longer be primary recycled for the original application.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative to Question A)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*(a)* Explain the **SPI coding system** in detail. Discuss how SPI codes assist in the **automated sorting** of plastics at Material Recovery Facilities (MRFs). *(7 marks)*

*(b)* With a suitable diagram, explain the principle of **float-sink separation** for plastic sorting. A PP flake of radius **3 mm** and density **905 kg/m³** falls through water (density **1000 kg/m³**, viscosity **1.0 × 10⁻³ Pa·s**). Calculate the terminal velocity. *(7 marks)*

#### Solution to Part (a)

The **SPI Resin Identification Code (RIC)** system is a standardized scheme established by the **Society of the Plastics Industry in 1988** to identify the seven major categories of thermoplastic polymers used in packaging and consumer goods. Each polymer family is assigned a unique number from **1 to 7**, displayed inside a chasing-arrows triangle.

**Detailed list of the seven codes:**

| Code | Polymer | Common Use |
|:----:|:--------|:-----------|
| 1 | PET | Beverage bottles |
| 2 | HDPE | Milk jugs |
| 3 | PVC | Pipes, frames |
| 4 | LDPE | Plastic bags |
| 5 | PP | Caps, containers |
| 6 | PS | Foam cups |
| 7 | Other | Multi-layer, bioplastics |

**Role in Automated Sorting at MRFs:**

Modern Material Recovery Facilities employ **Near-Infrared (NIR) spectroscopy** coupled with conveyor belts moving at **2–3 m/s**. The NIR sensor shines infrared light onto each plastic item; the reflected spectrum is a unique "fingerprint" of each polymer family. The SPI code essentially *corresponds* to these spectral fingerprints:

- **PET (Code 1):** Distinct C=O and C-H absorption peaks at specific NIR wavelengths.
- **HDPE (Code 2):** Sharp C-H stretching bands.
- **PP (Code 5):** Different crystallinity signature from HDPE.

A computer algorithm matches the reflected spectrum against a library and **actuates a jet of compressed air** that diverts the plastic item into the correct collection bin based on its SPI code. This system can sort **thousands of items per minute** with over **95% accuracy**, drastically reducing manual labour costs and contamination errors.

#### Solution to Part (b)

**Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.3 (Float-Sink Density Sorting Functional Architecture).

**Stokes' Law:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{2 r^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{9 \eta}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Given:**
- $r = 3 \text{ mm} = 3 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$
- $\rho_p = 905 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (PP)
- $\rho_f = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (water)
- $\eta = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Step 1 — Substitute values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{2 \cdot (3 \times 10^{-3})^2 \cdot (905 - 1000) \cdot 9.81}{9 \cdot (1.0 \times 10^{-3})}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the numerator:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= 2 \cdot 9 \times 10^{-6} \cdot (-95) \cdot 9.81 \\
&= 18 \times 10^{-6} \cdot (-931.95) \\
&= -0.016775 \text{ kg·m/s}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the denominator:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Denominator} &= 9 \cdot 10^{-3} \\
&= 0.009 \text{ kg/m·s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Terminal velocity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{-0.016775}{0.009} \\
v &\approx -1.864 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

The negative sign indicates that the **velocity vector is upward** (PP rises through water because $\rho_p < \rho_f$). The PP flake **floats upward at approximately 1.86 m/s**, allowing it to be skimmed from the surface and separated from denser plastics (PET, PVC, PS) that sink.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Trap:** Many students report $|v| = 1.86$ without acknowledging the **negative sign** indicating upward motion. Always state direction explicitly.
> 2. **Stokes' Validity Range:** Stokes' Law applies only for **laminar flow** (low Reynolds number, $Re < 1$). At the calculated velocity of 1.86 m/s with a 3 mm particle, $Re \approx 5000$, which is *turbulent*. In KTU exams, however, the simplified Stokes' equation is acceptable as long as you note this limitation.
> 3. **Unit Conversion:** Marks are routinely lost for forgetting to convert **mm to m**. Always do this **first**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2**

- **SPI Coding** was introduced in **1988** by the **Society of the Plastics Industry** (now Plastics Industry Association).
- The SPI system uses **7 codes** (1 to 7) inside a chasing-arrows triangle.
- **Code 1 = PET, Code 2 = HDPE, Code 3 = PVC, Code 4 = LDPE, Code 5 = PP, Code 6 = PS, Code 7 = Others.**
- SPI codes are **material identifiers**, **not recyclability guarantees**.
- **Primary recycling** = mechanical, closed-loop, identical quality output.
- Primary recycling is also called **"closed-loop recycling."**
- The closed-loop process: **Collection → Sorting → Washing → Shredding → Melting → Filtration → Pelletization → Re-use**.
- Property decay across cycles follows: $P_n = P_0 \cdot (1 - \alpha)^n$.
- Most polymers can only be primary-recycled for **4 to 6 cycles** before downcycling is necessary.
- Density sorting uses **Stokes' Law**: $v = \frac{2 r^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{9 \eta}$.
- Plastics with $\rho_p > 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (PET, PVC) **sink**; those with $\rho_p < 1000$ (LDPE, PP) **float**.
- Automated sorting in MRFs uses **NIR spectroscopy** matched to SPI codes.
- PVC (Code 3) is **limited** in primary recycling because it releases **HCl gas** at processing temperatures.
- PET (Code 1) is the **most successfully primary-recycled** polymer due to clean streams from beverage bottles.
- The **Melt Flow Index (MFI)** increases after primary recycling due to chain scission.
- Always convert **mm to m** before applying Stokes' Law in numerical problems.
- The four-tier recycling hierarchy: **Primary → Secondary → Tertiary → Quaternary**.
- Secondary recycling = **downcycling** (e.g., bottle → park bench) — *do not confuse with primary*.
- KTU frequently tests the **decay equation** $P_n = P_0 (1-\alpha)^n$ for 7 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equipments for Primary Recycling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT632 - POLYMERS & ENVIRONMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Equipments for Primary Recycling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Primary Recycling?

**Primary recycling** (also called *closed-loop recycling* or *re-extrusion*) is the simplest and most environmentally efficient method of plastic waste recovery. In this process, a *clean, single-polymer, uncontaminated* plastic scrap is reprocessed — typically by mechanical means — back into a product whose **physical and mechanical properties are nearly identical** to those of the virgin (original) material.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEPOT632, Module 2):**
> Primary recycling is restricted to *scrap generated in-house* during manufacturing (e.g., sprues, runners, trimmings, off-spec production rejects). It does **not** involve chemical breakdown of the polymer chain; the polymer retains its **molecular weight, melt flow index (MFI), and crystallinity**, though some minor degradation may occur due to thermo-mechanical history.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library Book Return" Analogy

Imagine a library where a borrowed book is returned. The book is still the **same story** (no new content written), but it has been *lightly used*. A librarian simply dusts it, re-shelves it, and lends it out again. Nobody rewrote the chapters.

Primary recycling works exactly the same way:
- The **book** = the polymer (its long-chain molecular structure)
- The **dust + glue rebinding** = mechanical cleaning, shredding, and re-extrusion
- The **re-shelving** = pelletization, ready for the next moulding cycle

The polymer chain is **not cut apart chemically** (no depolymerisation, no pyrolysis, no hydrolysis). It is just melted, filtered, and re-shaped.

## 1.3 The Equipment Chain — A Bird's-Eye View

The primary recycling line is an **integrated sequence of unit operations**. A typical industrial line contains the following equipment modules:

| Stage | Equipment Class | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Collection & Sorting** | Segregate by polymer type and colour |
| 2 | **Size Reduction** | Shredders, granulators, crushers |
| 3 | **Cleaning/Washing** | Removal of dirt, oil, labels |
| 4 | **Separation** | Float-sink, magnetic, electrostatic |
| 5 | **Drying** | Remove moisture before melting |
| 6 | **Melting & Filtration** | Extruder + screen changer (melt filter) |
| 7 | **Pelletization/Densification** | Strand, underwater, or die-face cutters |
| 8 | **Compounding** | Twin-screw extruder with additives |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metrics in Primary Recycling Equipment Design:**
> - **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC):** 0.2 – 0.6 kWh·kg⁻¹ (varies by equipment)
> - **Maximum permissible moisture content before extrusion:** < **0.02 %** (i.e., **200 ppm**)
> - **Melt filtration mesh size (typical):** 40 – 200 **µm**
> - **Regrind bulk density:** 200 – 500 kg·m⁻³ (vs. 600 – 1000 kg·m⁻³ for virgin pellets)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stage-wise Equipment Analysis

### 2.1.1 Size Reduction Equipment

Size reduction is the **first mechanical transformation** of bulky scrap into a free-flowing, melt-processable granular form.

**(a) Shredders (Slow-Speed, High-Torque)**
- Operate at **15 – 50 rpm**
- Suitable for large, bulky, thick-walled items (blow-moulded jerry cans, pipe off-cuts, automotive bumpers)
- Use **counter-rotating intermeshing blades** that cut, tear, and rip
- Throughput: **500 – 5000 kg·h⁻¹**
- Output flake size: **10 – 100 mm**

**(b) Granulators (High-Speed, Low-Torque)**
- Operate at **500 – 1500 rpm**
- Suited for thin-walled scrap (sprues, runners, injection moulded rejects, film)
- Rotating blades (typically 3 or 5) pass a stationary bed knife at a **clearance of 0.1 – 0.3 mm**
- Output particle size: **3 – 10 mm**
- Cutting speed: $v = \pi \cdot D \cdot N$ (where *D* is the rotor diameter, *N* is rpm)

**(c) Crushers / Pulverisers**
- Used for extremely tough or rigid plastics (e.g., fibre-reinforced thermoset scrap, thick sheets)
- Output size: < **1 mm** (pulverisers)

### 2.1.2 Separation & Washing Equipment

**(a) Float-Sink Separator (Hydrocyclone / Density Bath)**
- Exploits the **density difference** between polymers and contaminants.
- Operating principle: $F_b = \rho_{liquid} \cdot g \cdot V$ (buoyancy force); if $\rho_{polymer} > \rho_{liquid}$, the polymer sinks; if $\rho_{polymer} < \rho_{liquid}$, it floats.
- Common density bath liquid: **water (1000 kg·m⁻³)** separates PE (≤ 940) from PET (1380) and PVC (1400).
- For finer separation: **saturated CaCl₂ solution (~1300 kg·m⁻³)** or **saturated ZnCl₂ (~1800 kg·m⁻³)**.

**(b) Air Classifier / Zig-Zag Separator**
- Uses **air drag force** $F_d = \frac{1}{2} C_d \rho_{air} A v^2$ versus gravity $F_g = m g$.
- Light films and dust are carried upward; heavy contaminants fall.

**(c) Magnetic & Eddy-Current Separators**
- Remove ferrous and non-ferrous metals (e.g., aluminium caps, steel inserts).
- Eddy-current principle: a high-frequency alternating magnetic field induces **Foucault currents** in non-ferrous metals, producing a **repulsion force** $F \propto \sigma \cdot f \cdot B^2$ (where $\sigma$ is electrical conductivity, *f* is frequency, *B* is flux density).

**(d) Friction Washer / Turbo Washer**
- High-speed rotating paddles (~1000 rpm) create intense friction among flakes, **mechanically dislodging** adhered paper, glue, and dirt.
- Followed by a **rinsing stage** with clean water.

### 2.1.3 Drying Equipment

Moisture is the **silent killer** of polymer quality. Above **0.02 % (200 ppm)** moisture content, hydrolysable polymers (PET, PA, PC) undergo **chain scission** during extrusion, dramatically reducing **intrinsic viscosity [η]** and molecular weight.

Common drying systems:
- **Hot-air hopper dryer** (desiccant type for hygroscopic polymers) — dew point **≤ −40 °C**
- **Vacuum dryer** — for high-temperature polymers
- **Compressed-air dryer** — for inline continuous drying

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Drying Time Equation (Fickian Diffusion Model):**
> The minimum drying time $t_d$ to reach equilibrium moisture is:
> $t_d \propto \frac{L^2}{D_{eff}}$ where *L* is the characteristic flake/pellet thickness and $D_{eff}$ is the effective moisture diffusivity. Halving the flake thickness reduces drying time by **75 %** — a key engineering insight for shredder design.

### 2.1.4 Extrusion Equipment — The Heart of Primary Recycling

The **extruder** is the workhorse — it melts, mixes, conveys, filters, and pressurises the molten polymer.

**(a) Single-Screw Extruder**
- **Three functional zones** along the barrel:

| Zone | Function | Helix Depth |
|---|---|---|
| **Feed Zone** | Conveying solid pellets/flakes | **Deep** (e.g., 8 mm) |
| **Compression / Transition Zone** | Compaction + melting | Tapered |
| **Metering Zone** | Pumping + pressure build-up | **Shallow** (e.g., 2.5 mm) |

- **Compression Ratio (CR):** $\text{CR} = \dfrac{h_{feed}}{h_{meter}} = \dfrac{8}{2.5} = 3.2$ (typical for polyolefins)
- **L/D ratio:** 20 : 1 to 30 : 1 (longer for recycling due to venting needs)
- **Specific throughput:** $Q \approx \dfrac{1}{2} \pi^2 D^2 N d \sin\phi \cos\phi - \dfrac{\pi D h^3 \sin^2\phi}{12 \eta} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P}{L}$

where:
- *D* = barrel inner diameter
- *N* = screw rotational speed (rev/s)
- *d* = channel depth (varies along zones)
- *φ* = helix angle (typically 17.65° for square-pitch screws)
- *η* = melt viscosity
- *ΔP* = pressure rise along the metering zone
- *L* = length of metering zone

**(b) Twin-Screw Extruder (Co-rotating, Intermeshing)**
- Two screws rotate in the **same direction**, fully intermeshing.
- **Self-wiping** action — one screw cleans the other.
- Used for **compounding, devolatilisation, and reactive extrusion**.
- Throughput can be **3 – 5×** higher than single-screw for the same *D*.
- The **specific energy input (SEI)** is critical for recycling:
  $\text{SEI} = \dfrac{P}{Q}$ where *P* is motor power (kW) and *Q* is throughput (kg/h). Typical SEI in recycling = **0.1 – 0.4 kWh·kg⁻¹**.

**(c) Vented (Two-Stage) Extruder**
- Has a **vent port** typically at 60 – 70 % of barrel length.
- A **deep-flighted decompression zone** creates a pressure drop where residual moisture and volatiles flash off.
- Eliminates the need for pre-drying in non-hygroscopic polymers (PE, PP).
- **Vent efficiency:** $\eta_{vent} = 1 - \dfrac{C_{out}}{C_{in}}$ where *C* is the volatile concentration.

### 2.1.5 Melt Filtration (Screen Changer)

A **melt filter** is a mesh or sintered metal disc placed between the extruder and the die. It removes:
- Unmelted gels
- Carbonised particles
- Paper, wood, or aluminium fragments missed in upstream sorting
- Cross-linked polymer specks

**Filter types:**
- **Manual screen pack** (cheap, requires shutdown)
- **Hydraulic slide-plate** (continuous, no shutdown)
- **Self-cleaning rotary filter** (back-flushing, for high contamination)

The **pressure drop** across the filter (using the Hagen–Poiseuille model for the filter cake):

$$\Delta P_{cake} = \dfrac{150 \mu \cdot v \cdot L_{cake}}{D_p^2} \cdot \dfrac{(1-\varepsilon)^2}{\varepsilon^3}$$

where *μ* is melt viscosity, *v* is superficial velocity, *L_cake* is cake thickness, *D_p* is particle diameter, *ε* is cake porosity.

### 2.1.6 Pelletization Equipment

| Type | Mechanism | Throughput | Polymer Suitability |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Strand Pelletizer** | Polymer exits die as strands → water bath → cutter | 100 – 2000 kg/h | PE, PP, PS, PVC |
| **Underwater Pelletizer** | Die-face cutter submerged in water | Up to **20 t/h** | PE, PP (high MFR) |
| **Water-Ring Pelletizer** | Centrifugal cutter + water ring | 500 – 5000 kg/h | PE, PP, PET |
| **Die-Face Hot-Face Cutter** | Rotary blades at die face, air-cooled | 50 – 500 kg/h | Engineering plastics |

**Pellet Length Criterion:**
$L_{pellet} = \dfrac{Q}{N_{cutter} \cdot n_{holes} \cdot A_{hole}}$

where *Q* is volumetric throughput, *N_cutter* is cutter rpm, *n_holes* is number of die holes, and *A_hole* is hole cross-section. Typical pellet length: **2 – 4 mm**.

### 2.1.7 Densifier / Agglomerator

Used for **low-bulk-density scrap** (films, foams, fibres). A **friction-heated rotating blade** within a stationary casing:
- Generates frictional heat (no external heater)
- Compresses fluffy material into denser nodules
- Output bulk density: **400 – 600 kg·m⁻³**

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| $v = \pi D N$ | Cutting tip velocity | m·s⁻¹ | Granulator design |
| $F_b = \rho_L \, g \, V$ | Buoyancy force | N | Float-sink separator |
| $F_d = \tfrac{1}{2} C_d \rho A v^2$ | Air drag force | N | Air classification |
| $F \propto \sigma f B^2$ | Eddy-current repulsion | N | Non-ferrous metal separation |
| $t_d \propto L^2 / D_{eff}$ | Drying time (Fickian) | s | Hopper dryer sizing |
| $\text{CR} = h_{feed}/h_{meter}$ | Compression ratio | — | Screw geometry |
| $Q \approx \tfrac{1}{2}\pi^2 D^2 N d \sin\phi\cos\phi - \tfrac{\pi D h^3 \sin^2\phi}{12\eta}\cdot \tfrac{\Delta P}{L}$ | Extruder throughput | m³·s⁻¹ | Single-screw design |
| $\text{SEI} = P/Q$ | Specific energy input | kWh·kg⁻¹ | Recycling energetics |
| $\Delta P_{cake} = \tfrac{150 \mu v L_{cake}}{D_p^2} \cdot \tfrac{(1-\varepsilon)^2}{\varepsilon^3}$ | Filter cake pressure drop | Pa | Melt filter sizing |
| $L_{pellet} = Q/(N_c \cdot n_h \cdot A_h)$ | Pellet length | m | Pelletizer design |
| $\dot{\gamma} = \pi D N / h$ | Shear rate in extruder channel | s⁻¹ | Viscosity calculation |
| $\eta = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ | Power-law viscosity (Ostwald–de Waele) | Pa·s | Melt flow analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** The extruder throughput equation is the single most important design equation in any polymer processing lab. It allows a process engineer to **predict production rate** before commissioning a line, and to **scale-up** from a lab extruder (e.g., *D* = 20 mm) to a production extruder (e.g., *D* = 150 mm). The throughput scales approximately as **D²·⁵** for geometrically similar screws — a critical scaling rule in industrial practice.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Extruder Throughput Equation (Single-Screw, Newtonian Simplified Case)

We derive the *drag-induced forward flow* component of extruder throughput, treating the melt as a Newtonian fluid in the metering zone. (The pressure flow correction is added at the end as a superposition.)

### Step 1 — Geometry of the Screw Channel

Unwrap the screw channel around the barrel. The channel becomes a **rectangular trough** of:
- Width $W \approx \pi D \sin\phi - e$ (where *e* is the flight width)
- Depth $h$ (metering zone depth)
- Length along the down-channel direction $z$
- Cross-channel direction $x$

The barrel surface moves relative to the stationary screw at a **relative velocity** $V_b = \pi D N$ along the down-channel direction. The barrel surface forms the **upper wall**; the screw root forms the **lower wall**.

### Step 2 — Velocity Decomposition

$V_b$ is resolved into two components in the unrolled coordinate system:

- **Down-channel component:** $V_{bz} = \pi D N \cos\phi$
- **Cross-channel component:** $V_{bx} = \pi D N \sin\phi$

The down-channel component drives the **drag flow** (forward pumping). The cross-channel component induces **circulation** (mixing).

### Step 3 — Drag-Flow Velocity Profile (Newtonian Fluid)

Apply the no-slip condition: fluid at *y* = 0 (screw root) is stationary; fluid at *y* = h (barrel) moves at $V_{bz}$. For a Newtonian fluid, the steady-state velocity profile is **linear** in *y*:

$$v_z(y) = V_{bz} \cdot \dfrac{y}{h} = \pi D N \cos\phi \cdot \dfrac{y}{h}$$

### Step 4 — Volumetric Drag Flow

The volumetric flow rate per unit width is the integral of $v_z$ across the channel depth:

$$Q_{drag,1} = \int_{0}^{h} v_z(y)\, dy = \int_{0}^{h} \pi D N \cos\phi \cdot \dfrac{y}{h}\, dy$$

$$Q_{drag,1} = \pi D N \cos\phi \cdot \dfrac{1}{h} \cdot \dfrac{y^2}{2}\bigg|_{0}^{h} = \pi D N \cos\phi \cdot \dfrac{h}{2}$$

For a channel of width *W*:

$$Q_{drag} = \dfrac{1}{2} \pi D N \cos\phi \cdot W \cdot h$$

Since $W \approx \pi D \sin\phi - e \approx \pi D \sin\phi$ (when flight width is negligible):

$$Q_{drag} = \dfrac{1}{2} \pi^2 D^2 N \sin\phi \cos\phi \cdot h$$

### Step 5 — Conversion to Helix-Angle Form

Using the identity $2 \sin\phi \cos\phi = \sin 2\phi$:

$$Q_{drag} = \dfrac{1}{4} \pi^2 D^2 N h \sin 2\phi$$

### Step 6 — Pressure-Flow Correction (Poiseuille Component)

If a pressure gradient $\partial P / \partial z$ exists along the channel, a **reverse pressure flow** is superimposed. For a Newtonian fluid in a rectangular channel of aspect ratio $W/h \gg 1$, the pressure flow per unit width is:

$$Q_{press,1} = -\dfrac{h^3}{12 \eta} \cdot \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial z}$$

Multiplied by width *W*:

$$Q_{press} = -\dfrac{W h^3}{12 \eta} \cdot \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial z} \approx -\dfrac{\pi D h^3 \sin\phi}{12 \eta} \cdot \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial z}$$

### Step 7 — Net Throughput (Superposition)

For a Newtonian fluid, the **linear superposition** of drag and pressure flows is valid:

$$Q_{net} = Q_{drag} + Q_{press}$$

$$\boxed{\,Q_{net} = \dfrac{1}{2} \pi^2 D^2 N h \sin\phi \cos\phi - \dfrac{\pi D h^3 \sin^2\phi}{12 \eta} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P}{L}\,}$$

> This is the **canonical extruder throughput equation** that appears in every polymer processing textbook. It is the design foundation of every commercial single-screw extruder.

### Step 8 — Numerical Example (Recycling LDPE Film)

Given:
- $D = 0.090$ m (90 mm barrel)
- $N = 1.667$ rev·s⁻¹ (100 rpm)
- $h = 0.003$ m (3 mm metering depth)
- $\phi = 17.65°$ → $\sin\phi = 0.303$, $\cos\phi = 0.953$
- $\eta = 1000$ Pa·s at the operating shear rate (LDPE melt at 180 °C)
- $\Delta P / L = 5 \times 10^{6}$ Pa·m⁻¹ (typical for a screen pack + die)

Compute drag flow:

$$Q_{drag} = \dfrac{1}{2} \pi^2 (0.090)^2 (1.667) (0.003) (0.303)(0.953)$$

$$Q_{drag} = 0.5 \times 9.8696 \times 0.0081 \times 1.667 \times 0.003 \times 0.2887$$

$$Q_{drag} \approx 5.81 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}^3\text{·s}^{-1} \approx 58\ \text{cm}^3\text{·s}^{-1}$$

Compute pressure flow:

$$Q_{press} = \dfrac{\pi (0.090) (0.003)^3 (0.303)^2}{12 \times 1000} \times 5 \times 10^6$$

$$Q_{press} = \dfrac{3.1416 \times 0.090 \times 2.7 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.0918}{12000} \times 5 \times 10^6$$

$$Q_{press} \approx 3.13 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^3\text{·s}^{-1}$$

Net throughput:

$$Q_{net} \approx 5.81 \times 10^{-5} - 3.13 \times 10^{-6} \approx 5.50 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}^3\text{·s}^{-1}$$

Converting to mass throughput ($\rho_{LDPE} \approx 920$ kg·m⁻³):

$$Q_{mass} = 5.50 \times 10^{-5} \times 920 \approx 50.6\ \text{kg·h}^{-1}$$

This matches typical real-world throughput for a **90 mm recycling extruder** running post-consumer LDPE film.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Equipment Selection Decision Table

| Contaminant Type in Scrap | Recommended Sorting | Recommended Separation | Special Equipment Required |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mixed polymer (PE+PET) | Manual + NIR optical | Float-sink (water) | Hydrocyclone |
| Paper labels | NIR auto-sort | Friction washer | Turbo washer |
| Aluminium caps | Eddy-current | Magnetic drum | Eddy-current separator |
| Oil/grease | Pre-wash with detergent | Hot wash + rinse | Two-stage washer |
| Moisture (> 0.5 %) | Centrifugal dry | Desiccant dryer | Hopper dryer, −40 °C dew point |
| Carbonised gels | Visual inspection | Melt filtration | Self-cleaning rotary filter |
| Foamed / low-bulk | Compaction | Densification | Friction agglomerator |
| Cross-linked specks | None possible upstream | Melt filtration | Fine mesh (60 µm) screen |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Complete Primary Recycling Line

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Scrap Collection Bins] --> B[Pre-Sort by Polymer Type]
    B --> C[Belt Conveyor with Metal Detector]
    C --> D[Shredder Slow-Speed High-Torque]
    D --> E[Granulator High-Speed 1000 rpm]
    E --> F[Air Classifier Zig-Zag]
    F --> G[Friction Washer Turbo]
    G --> H[Floatsink Tank Density Separation]
    H --> I[Centrifugal Dewatering Dryer]
    I --> J[Desiccant Hopper Dryer minus 40 C Dew Point]
    J --> K[Single Screw Vented Extruder with Melt Filter]
    K --> L[Hydraulic Screen Changer]
    L --> M[Strand Die]
    M --> N[Water Bath Cooling]
    N --> O[Strand Pelletizer Rotary Cutter]
    O --> P[Classification Sieve]
    P --> Q[Finished Recycled Pellets Storage Silo]
    K --> R[Twin Screw Co-rotating Compounder Branch]
    R --> S[Additive Side Feeder]
    S --> T[Underwater Pelletizer]
    T --> Q
    H --> U[Reject Heavy Fraction PVC PET]
    U --> V[Downstream Secondary Recycling Pyrolysis]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Single-Screw Extruder (Detailed Internal View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Hopper[Feed Hopper]
        H1[Dried Regrind Flakes]
    end
    subgraph FeedZone[Feed Zone L over D 0 to 8]
        FZ1[Deep Flight Channel h1 8 mm]
        FZ2[Conveying Solids Forward]
    end
    subgraph CompZone[Compression Zone L over D 8 to 16]
        CZ1[Tapered Flight Channel]
        CZ2[Compaction and Melting Initiated]
        CZ3[Air Trapped Expelled via Venting]
    end
    subgraph MeterZone[Metering Zone L over D 16 to 24]
        MZ1[Shallow Flight Channel h2 2.5 mm]
        MZ2[Homogeneous Melt Pumping]
    end
    subgraph Filter[Melt Filter Section]
        FL1[Screen Pack 80 to 200 micrometer]
        FL2[Removal of Gels and Contaminants]
    end
    subgraph Die[Die Assembly]
        DA1[Strand Die or Profile Die]
        DA2[Pressure Buildup 5 to 20 MPa]
    end
    H1 --> FZ1
    FZ1 --> FZ2
    FZ2 --> CZ1
    CZ1 --> CZ2
    CZ2 --> CZ3
    CZ3 --> MZ1
    MZ1 --> MZ2
    MZ2 --> FL1
    FL1 --> FL2
    FL2 --> DA1
    DA1 --> DA2
```

## 4.3 Twin-Screw Extruder Architecture (Co-rotating, Intermeshing)

        T1[Feeding Port]
        T2[Conveying Element Right Hand Pitch]

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: Define primary recycling of plastics. Why is it restricted to in-house scrap and not post-consumer waste?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Primary recycling, also called **closed-loop recycling**, is the mechanical reprocessing of *clean, uncontaminated, single-polymer* plastic scrap into a product with **properties equivalent to the original (virgin) material**. No chemical modification of the polymer chain takes place.
>
> **Reason for restriction (1.5 marks):**
> Post-consumer waste is:
> (i) Contaminated with food, oil, paper labels, and other polymers
> (ii) Degraded by UV, heat, and weathering (chain scission reduces molecular weight)
> (iii) Heterogeneous in colour and grade
>
> Therefore post-consumer waste typically undergoes **secondary recycling** (downcycling) rather than primary recycling, because the property retention required for primary recycling is impossible without extensive and uneconomical sorting/cleaning.

### Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: List any three differences between a shredder and a granulator used in plastic recycling.**

**Model Answer (3 × 1 = 3 marks):**

| Parameter | Shredder | Granulator |
|---|---|---|
| **Operating speed** | 15 – 50 rpm (slow) | 500 – 1500 rpm (high) |
| **Torque** | High torque, low speed | Low torque, high speed |
| **Feed material** | Bulky, thick-walled items (pipes, bumpers) | Thin-walled items (sprues, runners, films) |
| **Output size** | 10 – 100 mm flakes | 3 – 10 mm granules |
| **Cutting mechanism** | Counter-rotating intermeshing blades | Rotating blades vs. stationary bed knife |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### 🔹 Choice A

#### Question 3(a) (7 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**Q: With a neat schematic, describe the construction and working of a single-screw extruder used in primary recycling. Highlight the role of the three functional zones.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Construction (3 marks):**
A single-screw extruder consists of a **barrel** heated externally by electric heater bands (or liquid heating), a **rotating screw** mounted on a shaft driven by a variable-speed motor through a gearbox, a **feed hopper** at the rear, and a **die** at the front. A **breaker plate and screen pack** (melt filter) sit between the screw tip and the die. The screw has a single helical flight with a constant pitch.

**(ii) Three Functional Zones (3 marks):**

| Zone | Barrel Position | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Feed Zone** (L/D 0–8) | Near hopper | Conveys cold, solid scrap. Deep flight depth (8 mm). No melting. |
| **Compression / Transition Zone** (L/D 8–16) | Middle | Tapered flight depth. Compacts the solid bed, melts polymer against the hot barrel. Melt film forms. |
| **Metering Zone** (L/D 16–24) | Near die | Shallow flight depth (2.5 mm). Homogenises melt, builds pressure, pumps polymer at constant rate. |

**(iii) Role in recycling (1 mark):**
In recycling, an **additional vent zone** is often introduced at L/D ~ 20, where the screw channel is deepened to allow volatiles and residual moisture to escape — critical when the input regrind has variable moisture content.

#### Question 3(b) (7 Marks)

**Q: Calculate the volumetric throughput of a single-screw extruder with the following data:**

- Barrel diameter *D* = 60 mm
- Screw speed *N* = 60 rpm
- Metering zone depth *h* = 2.5 mm
- Helix angle $\phi = 17.65°$
- Pressure gradient $\Delta P/L = 8 \times 10^6$ Pa·m⁻¹
- Melt viscosity $\eta = 500$ Pa·s

**Model Solution:**

Given data:
- $D = 0.060$ m
- $N = 60/60 = 1.0$ rev·s⁻¹
- $h = 0.0025$ m
- $\phi = 17.65°$ → $\sin\phi = 0.3033$, $\cos\phi = 0.9528$
- $\Delta P/L = 8 \times 10^6$ Pa·m⁻¹
- $\eta = 500$ Pa·s

**Step 1 — Drag flow** (from the derived equation):

$$Q_{drag} = \dfrac{1}{2} \pi^2 D^2 N h \sin\phi \cos\phi$$

$$Q_{drag} = 0.5 \times 9.8696 \times (0.060)^2 \times 1.0 \times 0.0025 \times 0.3033 \times 0.9528$$

$$Q_{drag} = 0.5 \times 9.8696 \times 0.0036 \times 0.0025 \times 0.2890$$

$$Q_{drag} \approx 1.284 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}^3\text{·s}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Pressure flow**:

$$Q_{press} = \dfrac{\pi D h^3 \sin^2\phi}{12 \eta} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P}{L}$$

$$Q_{press} = \dfrac{3.1416 \times 0.060 \times (0.0025)^3 \times (0.3033)^2}{12 \times 500} \times 8 \times 10^6$$

$$Q_{press} = \dfrac{3.1416 \times 0.060 \times 1.5625 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.0920}{6000} \times 8 \times 10^6$$

$$Q_{press} \approx 3.61 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^3\text{·s}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Net throughput**:

$$Q_{net} = Q_{drag} - Q_{press} = (1.284 - 0.361) \times 10^{-5} = 9.23 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^3\text{·s}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Mass throughput** (for PP, $\rho \approx 905$ kg·m⁻³):

$$Q_{mass} = 9.23 \times 10^{-6} \times 905 \times 3600 \approx 30.1\ \text{kg·h}^{-1}$$

**Final Answer:** $Q_{net} \approx 9.23 \times 10^{-6}$ m³·s⁻¹ (**≈ 30 kg·h⁻¹** for PP).

---

### 🔹 Choice B (Internal Choice)

#### Question 4(a) (7 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**Q: Explain the working of a float-sink separator and a hydrocyclone used in polymer separation during recycling. How does the density of the bath liquid affect the separation?**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Float-Sink Tank (3 marks):**
A float-sink separator is a **static density-bath tank** where plastic flakes are fed onto the surface of a liquid of carefully selected density. Polymers with density *less* than the liquid **float**; polymers with density *greater* than the liquid **sink**.

The buoyant force is given by **Archimedes' principle**: $F_b = \rho_L \, g \, V$. Equilibrium of forces yields:

$$\rho_L > \rho_{polymer} \Rightarrow \text{Float}$$
$$\rho_L < \rho_{polymer} \Rightarrow \text{Sink}$$

Common bath liquids:
- **Water (1000 kg·m⁻³):** separates PE (≤ 940) from PET, PVC, fillers
- **Ethanol (789 kg·m⁻³):** separates PP (905) from PE (≤ 940)
- **Saturated CaCl₂ (~1300 kg·m⁻³):** separates PVC (1400) from PET (1380)
- **Saturated ZnCl₂ (~1800 kg·m⁻³):** separates fillers and pigments

**(ii) Hydrocyclone (3 marks):**
A hydrocyclone is a **dynamic, centrifugal density separator**. The slurry of plastic flakes and water is injected **tangentially** at high pressure, creating a vortex. Heavy particles (sand, metals, glass) are flung to the wall and exit at the **underflow**; light plastics (PE, PP) spiral upward and exit at the **overflow**.

The separation efficiency depends on the **centrifugal acceleration**:

$$a_c = \dfrac{v_t^2}{r} \gg g$$

where $v_t$ is tangential velocity and *r* is cyclone radius. This effectively **amplifies gravity** by 100 – 1000×, enabling separation of particles with very small density differences (Δρ ≈ 30 kg·m⁻³).

**(iii) Effect of Bath Density (1 mark):**
- Too high density → both polymers sink → no separation
- Too low density → both polymers float → no separation
- **Optimal density** lies **between the densities of the two polymers** to be separated.

#### Question 4(b) (7 Marks)

**Q: What is a vented extruder? Why is it especially important in primary recycling of polyolefins? With a sketch, show the location of the vent port and explain the two-stage screw design.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Definition (1 mark):**
A **vented extruder** (also called a *two-stage extruder*) is a single-screw extruder equipped with an additional **decompression zone** and a **vent port** along the barrel, typically located at **60 – 70 % of the total L/D**.

**(ii) Two-Stage Screw Design (3 marks):**
- **Stage 1 (first screw):** identical to a conventional three-zone screw (feed → compression → metering). It melts, mixes, and pressurises the polymer.
- **Vent zone (deep-flighted decompression):** the screw flight depth is *increased abruptly*, causing a **pressure drop to atmospheric** at the vent.
- **Stage 2 (second metering zone, downstream of vent):** a *second metering section* re-pressurises the melt to push it through the screen pack and die.

**(iii) Importance in polyolefin recycling (3 marks):**
Post-industrial polyolefin (PE, PP) scrap — though non-hygroscopic — often carries:
- Surface moisture from washing
- Residual solvents, oils, or monomer
- Volatile degradation products from prior processing

In a **non-vented extruder**, these volatiles would flash at the die, causing:
- **Surging** and unstable throughput
- **Bubbles, voids, and surface defects** (die-lip drool, screw slip)
- **Safety hazard** from vented vapour at the operator station

In a **vented extruder**, these volatiles escape at the vent port to a vacuum or atmosphere, allowing **stable, void-free melt** delivery. For non-hygroscopic polymers (PE, PP), a vented extruder can **eliminate the need for a separate pre-drying step**, reducing capital and energy cost.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes Students Make:**
> 1. ❌ Confusing **primary recycling** with **secondary recycling** — primary requires *property equivalence* with virgin; secondary allows property degradation.
> 2. ❌ Writing the extruder throughput equation **without the pressure-flow correction term** — KTU expects the *full* expression; omitting the second term loses **2 marks** out of 7 in numerical problems.
> 3. ❌ Stating that a vented extruder "removes all moisture" — for *hygroscopic* polymers (PET, PA, PC), pre-drying is still mandatory because vented extruders cannot extract **bound (chemisorbed) moisture**, only free (physisorbed) moisture.
> 4. ❌ Confusing **compression ratio (CR)** with **L/D ratio** — CR refers to *flight depth ratio*; L/D refers to *screw length to diameter ratio*.
> 5. ❌ In float-sink problems, forgetting that the *bath density* must lie **between** the densities of the two polymers being separated.
> 6. ❌ Not converting **rpm to rev·s⁻¹** in extruder numerical problems — KTU consistently awards partial credit for correct unit handling.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points — Equipments for Primary Recycling (Module 2)**

- ☐ **Primary recycling** = mechanical reprocessing of *clean, single-polymer* in-house scrap into equivalent-grade product. No chemical change.
- ☐ Equipment chain order: **Sort → Shred → Granulate → Wash → Separate → Dry → Extrude → Filter → Pelletize**.
- ☐ **Shredder** = slow (15–50 rpm), high torque, for bulky scrap. **Granulator** = fast (500–1500 rpm), for thin-walled scrap.
- ☐ **Float-sink** separation uses Archimedes' principle; bath density must lie **between** the densities of the two polymers.
- ☐ **Eddy-current separator** exploits induced Foucault currents in non-ferrous metals; force $F \propto \sigma f B^2$.
- ☐ **Drying threshold** for hydrolysable polymers (PET, PA) = **0.02 % (200 ppm)**; desiccant dryer dew point = **−40 °C**.
- ☐ **Single-screw extruder** has **three zones**: feed (deep flight), compression (tapered), metering (shallow flight).
- ☐ **Compression ratio (CR)** = $h_{feed}/h_{meter}$, typically **2.5 – 3.5** for polyolefins.
- ☐ **L/D ratio** for recycling extruders = **24 : 1 to 30 : 1** (longer than moulding extruders due to venting).
- ☐ **Extruder throughput equation** has TWO terms: **drag flow** (forward) and **pressure flow** (reverse).
- ☐ **Twin-screw extruders** are *co-rotating, intermeshing*, self-wiping; used for compounding and devolatilisation.
- ☐ **Vented extruder** removes volatiles at L/D ≈ 20; eliminates pre-drying for **non-hygroscopic** polymers (PE, PP).
- ☐ **Melt filter (screen changer)** removes gels, carbonised particles, and metal fragments; mesh size **40 – 200 µm**.
- ☐ **Pelletizer types:** strand (low–medium throughput), underwater (high throughput, PE/PP), water-ring, die-face hot-face.
- ☐ **Densifier/agglomerator** uses frictional heat (no external heater) to compact low-bulk films/foams.
- ☐ **Specific Energy Input (SEI)** for recycling = **0.1 – 0.4 kWh·kg⁻¹**; lower is better.
- ☐ **Shear rate in extruder channel** = $\dot{\gamma} = \pi D N / h$; high shear may cause **viscous heating** and polymer degradation.
- ☐ **Power-law viscosity** model: $\eta = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ — *n* < 1 for shear-thinning polymer melts.
- ☐ **Maximum permissible contamination** in primary recycling = **< 1 %** by mass (else the product fails quality specs).
- ☐ **NIR (Near-Infrared) optical sorting** is the modern industrial standard for polymer identification, but is **not** a piece of recycling equipment per se — it is a sorting adjunct.
- ☐ For KTU numerical problems: **always carry units**, **convert rpm to rev·s⁻¹**, and **state the final answer in kg·h⁻¹** (preferred over m³·s⁻¹ for industrial interpretation).

---
