> 6. **Unit conversion errors:** Internal pressure is often given in kPa in the problem statement but must be converted to **MPa** (or N/mm²) to match the units of yield stress. $25\ \text{kPa} = 0.025\ \text{MPa}$. Missing this conversion yields a wildly wrong wall thickness (e.g., 268 mm instead of 0.27 mm).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📋

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — PP Battery Case**

- ✅ **PP Grade for battery cases = Impact (Heterophasic) Copolymer** with EPR rubber phase — not homopolymer, not random copolymer.
- ✅ **Five key reasons PP is selected:** chemical resistance, low density (0.905 g/cm³), HDT > 100 °C, dielectric strength ~22 kV/mm, recyclability (Resin Code 5).
- ✅ **Mould shrinkage of PP ≈ 1.0–2.5%** — must use the **exact** formula $L_{mold} = L_{part}/(1-S/100)$ for accurate cavity machining.
- ✅ **Injection Molding Cycle:** 45–90 seconds, melt 210–240 °C, mould 30–60 °C, injection 70–120 MPa, clamp 1500–3500 kN.
- ✅ **Cooling time scales as $h^2$** — keep wall thickness ≤ 4 mm to maintain cycle time.
- ✅ **Wall thickness for VRLA pressure containment:** Use thin-wall cylinder theory: $t = P \cdot r / \sigma_{allow}$ with $FS = 3$–$5$.
- ✅ **Typical wall = 3.0–4.0 mm** (driven by stiffness/mouldability, not by pressure containment alone).
- ✅ **Critical dimensions:** Cell compartment ±0.3 mm; lid-seal groove must be leak-free at 30 kPa air pressure.
- ✅ **Failure modes to remember:** brittle fracture (use impact copolymer), weld-line cracking (use hot runner), stress cracking (lower hold pressure), warpage (balance cooling).
- ✅ **Environmental angle:** PP is **100% recyclable (Code 5)**; end-of-life cases are shredded, washed, and reprocessed into pallets, drainage pipes, and park benches. Lead components are sent to smelters separately.
- ✅ **Industry standards:** UL 94 V-0 (flame), UN 38.3 (transport), IEC 62133 (safety), IS 15549 (Indian standard for VRLA).
- ✅ **Resin identification code 5** in the chasing-arrows triangle on every moulded case.
- ✅ **Specific volume of PP = 1.105 cm³/g** (inverse of 0.905 g/cm³) — useful for shot-volume calculations.
- ✅ **Glass transition temperature of PP = −10 °C** (well below room temperature → impact is moderate; below $T_g$ the material becomes brittle, but normal service is above $T_g$).
- ✅ **Crystallinity in PP ranges 30–60%** depending on cooling rate — affects shrinkage, stiffness, and transparency.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Crushing and Separation
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT632 - POLYMERS & ENVIRONMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Crushing and Separation

# Crushing and Separation in Polymer Waste Management

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> **Crushing (Comminution / Size Reduction)** is the mechanical process of reducing the bulk volume of post-consumer or industrial plastic waste into smaller, uniform fragments using shear, impact, attrition, or compression forces. **Separation** is the subsequent downstream unit operation in which the crushed heterogeneous plastic fraction is segregated based on physical (density, size, magnetism, electrostatic behaviour) or optical (NIR spectroscopy) properties into homogeneous polymer-rich streams suitable for reprocessing.

In the broader scope of polymer recycling, these two operations form the **primary processing backbone** of any Material Recovery Facility (MRF). Without effective crushing, downstream sortation is impossible; without accurate separation, the recovered polymer cannot meet the **purity specifications (typically ≥ 95 % monostream purity)** demanded by re-processors.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a mixed basket of fruit containing whole apples, oranges, and bananas of different sizes. Before you can sort them by type, you must first **chop them into pieces** of roughly similar size — that is **crushing**. Once the pieces are roughly the same size, you can now sort them: the heavy ones sink in water, the magnetic metal contaminants stick to a magnet, the red pieces can be picked by a camera. That is **separation**.

In a plastic recycling plant:
- **Crushing** = Shredders, granulators, and hammer mills chop bulky plastic scrap into flakes.
- **Separation** = A combination of cyclones, sink-float tanks, magnetic drums, eddy current separators, and optical sorters segregate the flakes into PET, HDPE, PVC, PP, etc.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics used in KTU evaluation:**
> - **Reduction Ratio (Rr)** = ratio of feed size to product size
> - **Work Index (Wi)** – measured in **kWh/ton**
> - **Screen Aperture** – measured in **mm**
> - **Density** – measured in **kg/m³**
> - **Purity / Recovery efficiency** – measured in **%**

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Crushing is Necessary in Polymer Recycling

1. **Bulk Volume Reduction:** Polymer waste (films, bottles, containers) occupies large volume per unit mass (typical density **30–80 kg/m³** for bales). Crushing increases bulk density to **400–600 kg/m³**, enabling transport efficiency.
2. **Surface Area Enhancement:** Subsequent washing, drying, and extrusion steps require a high surface-to-volume ratio.
3. **Improved Sorting Accuracy:** Optical sorters (NIR) work reliably on flake sizes of **2–12 mm**. Over-sized pieces confuse cameras; under-sized pieces cause dust and losses.

### 2.2 Classification of Crushing Equipment

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Classification (must memorize):**

| Category | Operating Principle | Typical Product Size | Application in Polymer Recycling |
|----------|--------------------|----------------------|-----------------------------------|
| **Shredder (Slow-speed)** | Rotary shear, high torque | 20–100 mm | Bales, drums, large rigid plastic |
| **Granulator** | High-speed rotor + screen | 4–12 mm | Films, bottles, injection moulded scrap |
| **Hammer Mill** | Impact hammers | 1–5 mm | Brittle plastics, purgings |
| **Crusher (Jaw / Cone)** | Compression | 10–50 mm | Thick-walled rigid plastics |
| **Cutter Mill** | Cutting blades | 2–10 mm | Sheet, fibre, textile waste |

### 2.3 Classification of Separation Techniques

> [!NOTE]
> Separation techniques in polymer recycling are classified based on the **exploited physical property**.

1. **Density-Based Separation (Sink–Float / Floatation)**
 - Exploits difference between polymer density (PET = **1380 kg/m³**, HDPE = **950 kg/m³**, PP = **905 kg/m³**, PS = **1050 kg/m³**) and water (**1000 kg/m³**).
 - **Heavy media separation** uses salt solutions or magnetite slurries.

2. **Magnetic Separation**
 - Removes ferrous contaminants (iron, steel).
 - Used as a **pre-separator** before polymer purification.

3. **Eddy Current Separation (ECS)**
 - Removes non-ferrous metals (Al, Cu) from plastic stream using induced eddy currents and Lorentz force.
 - Particularly useful in mixed WEEE–polymer streams.

4. **Electrostatic Separation (Triboelectric)**
 - Exploits the difference in charge acquisition by different polymers when frictionally charged.
 - Enables PVC–PET–PE separation.

5. **Optical / NIR Sorting**
 - Near-Infrared sensors detect polymer-specific absorption bands (C–H stretch at **1730 cm⁻¹** for PET carbonyl, **2900 cm⁻¹** for PE/PP C–H).
 - Compressed air jets eject the target polymer.

6. **Air Classification (Zigzag / Cyclone)**
 - Exploits aerodynamic drag difference based on mass and shape — separates films from rigid flakes.

7. **Screening (Trommel / Vibratory)**
 - Exploits size difference — removes fines (< 2 mm) and oversize.

### 2.4 Laws of Comminution (KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The three classical laws relate the energy required for crushing to the size reduction achieved.**

| Law | Assumption | Energy Relationship | Equation |
|-----|-----------|---------------------|----------|
| **Rittinger's Law (1867)** | Energy ∝ new surface area created | $E = K_R \left( \dfrac{1}{D_p} - \dfrac{1}{D_f} \right)$ | Surface area theory |
| **Kick's Law (1885)** | Energy ∝ volume reduction | $E = K_K \ln\!\left( \dfrac{D_f}{D_p} \right)$ | Volume theory |
| **Bond's Law (1952)** | Energy ∝ crack length (√ product size) | $E = W_i \left( \dfrac{\sqrt{D_p} - \sqrt{D_f}}{\sqrt{D_p}\,\sqrt{D_f}} \right)$ | Practical / Third theory |

Where:
- $D_f$ = feed particle size (mm)
- $D_p$ = product particle size (mm)
- $K_R, K_K$ = Rittinger and Kick constants
- $W_i$ = **Bond Work Index** (kWh/ton)

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Selection Rule (Examiner's favourite):**
> - **Kick's Law** → coarse crushing (large particles, low reduction ratio).
> - **Rittinger's Law** → fine grinding (small particles, high reduction ratio, brittle materials).
> - **Bond's Law** → general-purpose, most widely used in industry and KTU numericals.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

- Sizing the **drive motor** of a granulator for a given throughput.
- Determining **power consumption** of a polymer shredder line.
- Calculating **screen area** for a given feed rate.
- Designing **float-sink tanks** for separating PP/PE from PET/PS.
- Optimising **NIR sorter** belt speed for flake size distribution.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Bond's Law (The Most Important KTU Derivation)

Bond's Law is derived as the **geometric mean** of Kick's and Rittinger's laws.

**Step 1 — Start with Kick's Law:**
The energy per unit mass required for size reduction is proportional to the logarithm of the size reduction ratio.

$$
E = K_K \, \ln\!\left( \dfrac{D_f}{D_p} \right)
$$

**Step 2 — Start with Rittinger's Law:**
The energy per unit mass is proportional to the new surface area created, expressed as:

$$
E = K_R \left( \dfrac{1}{D_p} - \dfrac{1}{D_f} \right) = K_R \, \dfrac{(D_f - D_p)}{D_p \, D_f}
$$

**Step 3 — Bond proposed that for the practical case of a single particle crushing, the energy required is proportional to the crack length (the geometric mean of the feed diameter and the product diameter):**

$$
E \;\propto\; \sqrt{D_f \cdot D_p}
$$

**Step 4 — Define the Work Index (Wi) such that the energy per ton to reduce a very large feed (theoretically $D_f \to \infty$) to a product of size $D_p$ is:**

$$
E = \dfrac{W_i}{\sqrt{D_p}}
$$

**Step 5 — Combine to get the general expression for finite feed size:**

$$
E = W_i \left( \dfrac{\sqrt{D_f} - \sqrt{D_p}}{\sqrt{D_f \cdot D_p}} \right)
$$

**Step 6 — Rearranged final form (the form KTU examiners use):**

$$
\boxed{\,E = W_i \cdot \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{D_p}} - \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{D_f}}\,}
$$

where $D_p$ and $D_f$ are in **micrometres (µm)**, $E$ is in **kWh/ton**, and $W_i$ is the **Bond Work Index in kWh/ton**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bond Work Index values (must memorise for KTU viva):**
> - PET = **30–35 kWh/ton**
> - HDPE = **25–30 kWh/ton**
> - PP = **22–28 kWh/ton**
> - PVC = **40–50 kWh/ton**
> - PS = **35–40 kWh/ton**
> - LDPE (films) = **20–25 kWh/ton**

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical (Rittinger's Law) — KTU Typical Style

> **Problem:**
> A polymer shredder reduces HDPE flakes from 25 mm to 5 mm. If the feed rate is 5 ton/hour and the Rittinger constant for HDPE is 12.5 kWh·mm/ton, calculate the power required.

**Step 1 — Identify given values:**

$$
D_f = 25 \text{ mm}, \quad D_p = 5 \text{ mm}, \quad K_R = 12.5 \text{ kWh·mm/ton}, \quad \dot{m} = 5 \text{ ton/hour}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Rittinger's Law (specific energy per ton):**

$$
E = K_R \left( \dfrac{1}{D_p} - \dfrac{1}{D_f} \right)
$$

$$
E = 12.5 \left( \dfrac{1}{5} - \dfrac{1}{25} \right) = 12.5 \left( 0.200 - 0.040 \right)
$$

$$
E = 12.5 \times 0.160 = 2.00 \text{ kWh/ton}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute total power for 5 ton/hour throughput:**

$$
P = E \times \dot{m} = 2.00 \text{ kWh/ton} \times 5 \text{ ton/hour}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,P = 10 \text{ kW (approximately)}\,}
$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical (Bond's Law) — KTU Typical Style

> **Problem:**
> PET bottles are to be crushed from a feed size of 12 mm to a product size of 2 mm. Using the Bond Work Index for PET (W_i = 32 kWh/ton), calculate the energy required per ton.

**Step 1 — Convert mm to µm:**

$$
D_f = 12 \times 1000 = 12000 \text{ µm}, \qquad D_p = 2 \times 1000 = 2000 \text{ µm}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Bond's Law:**

$$
E = W_i \left( \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{D_p}} - \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{D_f}} \right)
$$

$$
E = 32 \left( \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{2000}} - \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{12000}} \right)
$$

$$
E = 32 \left( \dfrac{10}{44.72} - \dfrac{10}{109.54} \right)
$$

$$
E = 32 \left( 0.2236 - 0.0913 \right)
$$

$$
E = 32 \times 0.1323
$$

$$
\boxed{\,E = 4.23 \text{ kWh/ton}\,}
$$

**Step 4 — For a plant throughput of 8 ton/hour, the installed motor power (assuming 90 % mechanical efficiency):**

$$
P_{\text{shaft}} = \dfrac{4.23 \times 8}{0.90} = 37.6 \text{ kW}
$$

A standard **45 kW** induction motor would be selected.

---

### 3.4 Sink–Float Separation Analytical Matrix

For a mixture of PET (1380 kg/m³) and HDPE (950 kg/m³) in water (1000 kg/m³):

| Polymer | Density (kg/m³) | Behaviour in Water | Sinks to | Floats on |
|---------|------------------|---------------------|----------|-----------|
| PET | 1380 | Sinks | Bottom | — |
| HDPE | 950 | Floats | — | Top |
| PP | 905 | Floats | — | Top |
| PS | 1050 | Sinks | Bottom | — |
| PVC | 1400 | Sinks | Bottom | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** In a single water-based sink-float tank, **PET and PVC** cannot be separated from each other because both sink. To separate them, a **second-stage sink-float** in a denser medium (e.g., CaCl₂ solution of density **1270 kg/m³**) is required — PET floats, PVC sinks.

**Separation Efficiency Formula (commonly asked):**

$$
\eta_{\text{sep}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{product}}}{m_{\text{feed}}} \times 100\,\%
$$

**Purity Formula:**

$$
P_{\text{purity}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{target polymer}}}{m_{\text{total product}}} \times 100\,\%
$$

**Recovery Formula:**

$$
R_{\text{recovery}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{target polymer in product}}}{m_{\text{target polymer in feed}}} \times 100\,\%
$$

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Complete Polymer Recycling Crushing & Separation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Plastic Waste Bale Intake] --> B[Primary Shredder - Slow Speed]
    B --> C[Granulator - High Speed]
    C --> D[Magnetic Drum Separator]
    D --> E[Eddy Current Separator ECS]
    E --> F[Air Classifier Zigzag]
    F --> G[Sink Float Tank Water Medium]
    G --> H[Sink Fraction PET PVC PS]
    G --> I[Float Fraction HDPE PP LDPE]
    H --> J[Second Stage Sink Float CaCl2]
    J --> K[PET Rich Stream]
    J --> L[PVC Rich Stream]
    I --> M[NIR Optical Sorter]
    M --> N[HDPE Pure Stream]
    M --> O[PP Pure Stream]
    C --> P[Screening Trommel]
    P --> Q[Oversize Recycle to Granulator]
    P --> R[Fines Dust Removal]
```

### 4.2 Comminution Energy Laws Comparative Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CoarseCrush["Kick's Law - Coarse Crushing"]
        A1[Feed Df large] --> A2[ln Df/Dp]
        A2 --> A3[Energy E prop to Volume]
    end

    subgraph FineGrind["Rittinger's Law - Fine Grinding"]
        B1[Feed Df small] --> B2[1/Dp minus 1/Df]
        B2 --> B3[Energy E prop to Surface Area]
    end

    subgraph GeneralBond["Bond's Law - Industrial General"]
        C1[Feed Df any] --> C2[Geometric Mean of Df Dp]
        C2 --> C3[Energy E prop to Crack Length]
    end

    CoarseCrush --> GeneralBond
    FineGrind --> GeneralBond
```

### 4.3 Separation Technique Selection Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Crushed Mixed Plastic Flake] --> Q1{Property Exploited}
    Q1 -->|Density| S1[Sink Float Tank]
    Q1 -->|Magnetism| S2[Magnetic Drum Fe]
    Q1 -->|Induced Current| S3[Eddy Current ECS Non Fe]
    Q1 -->|Optical Signature| S4[NIR Spectrometer]
    Q1 -->|Aerodynamic Drag| S5[Air Classifier]
    Q1 -->|Tribocharge| S6[Electrostatic Separator]
    S1 --> Out1[PET vs HDPE PP]
    S2 --> Out2[Steel Removed]
    S3 --> Out3[Aluminium Removed]
    S4 --> Out4[Polymer Grade Purity]
    S5 --> Out5[Film vs Rigid Separation]
    S6 --> Out6[PET vs PVC Mixture]
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — 3 Mark Questions

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define the Bond Work Index. State its typical units and significance in sizing polymer crushing equipment.*

**Model Answer:**

The **Bond Work Index (W_i)** is defined as the energy required (in kWh) to reduce one short ton of material from a theoretically infinite feed size to a product such that **80 % passes through a 100 µm screen**.

- **Units:** **kWh/ton**
- **Significance:** It is a material property used to calculate the **power consumption** and **throughput** of industrial size-reduction equipment such as shredders and granulators. Higher W_i values indicate the polymer is more resistant to crushing (PVC > PET > HDPE > PP).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Explain the principle of sink-float separation with one example from polymer recycling.*

**Model Answer:**

**Sink-float separation** is a gravity-based density separation technique in which crushed plastic flakes are introduced into a liquid medium of a chosen density. Polymers **denser** than the medium **sink**; polymers **lighter** **float**.

**Example:** In a water tank (ρ = 1000 kg/m³), **HDPE (ρ = 950 kg/m³) floats** while **PET (ρ = 1380 kg/m³) sinks**, enabling their bulk separation. Adjustment of medium density using salt solutions (e.g., CaCl₂) enables fine separation between similarly dense polymers.

---

### Part B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

**(a)** With neat diagrams, describe the working principle and constructional features of **(i) a rotary shear shredder** and **(ii) a granulator** as used in plastic waste processing. Compare their typical product size, throughput, and energy consumption. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A plastic recycling plant processes 10 ton/hour of mixed plastic bottles. The feed size is 20 mm and the desired product size is 4 mm. Using **Bond's Law** with a work index of 30 kWh/ton, calculate the **power required** and comment on the choice of drive motor. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Constructional Features and Comparison:**

**(i) Rotary Shear Shredder (Slow-speed, high-torque):**
- Consists of a **low-RPM (10–50 rpm)** rotor fitted with **hardened steel hook-shaped cutting blades**.
- Counter-rotating shafts intermesh like scissors.
- Hydraulic ram pushes the bulky feed (bales, drums, large containers) into the cutting zone.
- Product size controlled by **screen mesh** at the discharge.
- **Typical product size:** 20–100 mm.
- **Throughput:** 1–15 ton/hour.
- **Energy consumption:** 30–80 kWh/ton.

**(ii) Granulator (High-speed):**
- A **high-RPM (400–800 rpm)** rotor with **3 or 5 rotating blades** and **2 fixed stator blades**.
- Cutting takes place in a **scissor-like action** between rotor and stator blades.
- A **screen grate** beneath the rotor determines the maximum product size.
- Product exits only when small enough to pass the screen.
- **Typical product size:** 4–12 mm (flake).
- **Throughput:** 2–20 ton/hour.
- **Energy consumption:** 20–50 kWh/ton.

**Comparative Table (write in exam):**

| Feature | Shredder | Granulator |
|---------|----------|------------|
| Rotor speed | Low (10–50 rpm) | High (400–800 rpm) |
| Product size | 20–100 mm | 4–12 mm |
| Use | Primary crushing | Secondary crushing |
| Energy | Higher | Lower |
| Application | Bales, drums | Films, bottles |

**(b) Bond's Law Calculation:**

**Step 1 — Convert mm to µm:**

$$
D_f = 20 \times 1000 = 20000 \text{ µm}, \quad D_p = 4 \times 1000 = 4000 \text{ µm}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Bond's Law:**

$$
E = W_i \left( \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{D_p}} - \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{D_f}} \right)
$$

$$
E = 30 \left( \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{4000}} - \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{20000}} \right)
$$

$$
E = 30 \left( \dfrac{10}{63.25} - \dfrac{10}{141.42} \right)
$$

$$
E = 30 \left( 0.1581 - 0.0707 \right) = 30 \times 0.0874
$$

$$
E = 2.62 \text{ kWh/ton}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute power for 10 ton/hour:**

$$
P_{\text{theoretical}} = 2.62 \times 10 = 26.2 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Account for mechanical losses (assume η = 90 %):**

$$
P_{\text{shaft}} = \dfrac{26.2}{0.90} = 29.1 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 5 — Motor selection (apply 1.25 safety factor):**

$$
P_{\text{motor}} = 1.25 \times 29.1 = 36.4 \text{ kW}
$$

> A **standard 37 kW (50 HP)** three-phase induction motor with VFD control is recommended for energy-efficient operation under varying feed conditions.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the working principle of **(i) eddy current separator** and **(ii) NIR optical sorter** as used in polymer separation. List the polymer property each technique exploits. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A crushed mixed-plastic stream contains **PET, HDPE, and aluminium can fragments**. Design a **3-stage separation train** to obtain a pure PET stream, a pure HDPE stream, and a metal-free fraction. Justify the order of each stage with density and property data. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working Principles:**

**(i) Eddy Current Separator (ECS):**
- A rapidly rotating magnetic drum (permanent magnet rotor spinning at ~1500–3000 rpm) generates a **time-varying magnetic field**.
- When a conductive non-ferrous particle (e.g., Al, Cu) passes over the drum, **eddy currents** are induced.
- The interaction between these eddy currents and the magnetic field produces a **Lorentz force** that ejects the conductive particle forward.
- **Non-conductive plastics** are unaffected and fall in a normal trajectory.
- **Exploited property:** Electrical conductivity of metals vs insulation of polymers.

**(ii) NIR Optical Sorter:**
- A **conveyor belt** carries the polymer flakes under an array of **near-infrared (NIR) spectrometers** (wavelength range **780 nm – 2500 nm**).
- Each polymer reflects/absorbs NIR light at a characteristic spectrum (PET has a strong C=O stretch band at **1730 cm⁻¹**).
- The **software** compares the spectrum with an in-built library; on a positive match, a **compressed air nozzle** fires to eject the target flake.
- Throughput: 1–5 ton/hour per lane.
- **Exploited property:** Unique NIR absorption/reflection signature of each polymer.

**(b) 3-Stage Separation Train Design:**

Given material data:

| Component | Density (kg/m³) | Magnetism | Conductivity |
|-----------|-----------------|-----------|--------------|
| PET | 1380 | No | No |
| HDPE | 950 | No | No |
| Aluminium | 2700 | No | Yes |

**Stage 1 — Eddy Current Separator:**
- Removes **aluminium can fragments** using Lorentz force.
- PET and HDPE (non-conductive) pass through unaffected.
- Output: **metal-free PET + HDPE mix** + clean aluminium fraction.

**Stage 2 — Sink–Float Tank (Water):**
- Feed: PET + HDPE mix.
- **HDPE (ρ = 950 kg/m³) floats**, **PET (ρ = 1380 kg/m³) sinks**.
- Output: **top layer = HDPE stream**, **bottom layer = PET stream**.

**Stage 3 — NIR Optical Sorter (Polish/Upgrade step):**
- Each stream (PET and HDPE) is passed through a NIR sorter to upgrade purity to **≥ 95 % monostream purity**.
- Removes mis-sorted flakes (e.g., PP or PS contamination).

**Order Justification:**
- ECS first removes the densest and conductive contaminant (Al) without affecting polymers — bulk metal removal is most efficient on a coarse feed.
- Sink-float exploits the **largest density gap** between PET and HDPE — best as bulk separation.
- NIR is used **last** because it is expensive per-ton; it polishes the stream to final purity rather than handling bulk mass.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion in Bond's Law:** When using Bond's Law, **D_p and D_f MUST be in micrometres (µm)**, NOT mm. Students commonly write mm and obtain a result that is ~1000× wrong. Marks lost: 3–4.
> 2. **Reverse sink-float logic:** Students frequently write "PET floats" — it **sinks** (1380 > 1000). Marks lost: 1.
> 3. **Forgetting mechanical efficiency** in motor sizing numericals — at least 1 mark is reserved for selecting a standard motor size with safety factor.
> 4. **ECS removes NON-ferrous** (Al, Cu), not ferrous — common confusion. Ferrous metals are removed by magnetic drum.
> 5. **NIR is NOT used for bulk separation** — only for final purity polishing. Mentioning it as a primary separator loses structural marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU Module 2 — Crushing and Separation)**

- ✔ **Crushing** = mechanical size reduction; **Separation** = sorting by physical/optical property.
- ✔ **Shredder** for primary (20–100 mm); **Granulator** for secondary (4–12 mm); **Hammer mill** for fine (1–5 mm).
- ✔ **Three Laws of Comminution:** Kick (volume), Rittinger (surface area), Bond (crack length / general).
- ✔ **Bond's Law equation (most used):** $E = W_i \left( \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{D_p}} - \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{D_f}} \right)$ — sizes in **µm**, energy in **kWh/ton**.
- ✔ **PET sinks** in water (1380); **HDPE floats** (950); **PP floats** (905); **PS sinks** (1050); **PVC sinks** (1400).
- ✔ **Magnetic drum** → ferrous removal; **Eddy Current** → non-ferrous metal removal (Al, Cu).
- ✔ **NIR sorter** → final purity upgrade (≥ 95 %), NOT bulk separator.
- ✔ **Sink-float** is the most economical bulk separator by density.
- ✔ **Bond Work Index range for polymers:** 20–50 kWh/ton.
- ✔ **Reduction Ratio Rr** = D_f / D_p (dimensionless).
- ✔ **Separation efficiency** = (mass of target in product / mass of target in feed) × 100 %.
- ✔ **Always use µm** in Bond's formula — never mm.
- ✔ **Mechanical efficiency of shredder line** ≈ 85–92 %.
- ✔ **Standard motor safety factor** = 1.20–1.30 over calculated shaft power.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PET Films
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT632 - POLYMERS & ENVIRONMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** PET Films

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PET (Polyethylene Terephthalate)** is a semi-crystalline, thermoplastic polyester resin formed by the condensation polymerization of **ethylene glycol** and **terephthalic acid** (or its dimethyl ester, dimethyl terephthalate). PET films are thin, flexible, transparent sheets manufactured primarily through a **melt-extrusion** process followed by **biaxial orientation** (stretching in both machine and transverse directions), producing the widely commercialized **BOPET (Biaxially Oriented Polyethylene Terephthalate)** film.

### Chemical Identity Card
- **IUPAC Name:** Poly(ethylene terephthalate)
- **Repeat Unit:** $-\text{O}-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_2-\text{O}-\text{CO}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{CO}-$
- **CAS Number:** 25038-59-9
- **Glass Transition Temperature ($T_g$):** approximately **75–80 °C** (amorphous)
- **Melting Temperature ($T_m$):** approximately **245–265 °C**
- **Degree of Crystallinity (BOPET):** typically **30–40 %**
- **Density (amorphous):** **1.335 g/cm³**; (crystalline): **1.455 g/cm³**

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Acrylic-Pane" Picture**
> Think of a PET film as an engineering cousin of the plastic wrap in your kitchen, but manufactured under extreme precision. Imagine pouring a hot, honey-like polymer melt onto a chilled, spinning drum — it flattens into a glass-clear ribbon. That ribbon is then stretched in two perpendicular directions (imagine pulling a rubber band on a picture frame both horizontally and vertically). The stretching aligns the long polymer chains like parallel rows of soldiers, which dramatically increases strength, clarity, and gas-barrier properties. The result is a film thinner than a human hair yet stronger than the same thickness of steel by weight — a true marvel of polymer engineering.

---

### Sub-classification of PET Films

| Type | Acronym | Orientation | Key Property |
|------|---------|-------------|--------------|
| Amorphous PET | APET | Un-oriented | High clarity, thermoformable |
| Crystalline PET | CPET | Un-oriented | Heat resistant, opaque |
| Biaxially Oriented PET | **BOPET** | Stretched MD + TD | **High strength, barrier, clarity** |
| Oriented PET (one-way) | OPET | Mono-axially oriented | Improved strength in one direction |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Synthesis Routes of PET

PET is industrially produced via **two principal routes**:

**Route A – Ester-Interchange (DMT Process):**
1. Dimethyl terephthalate (DMT) + excess ethylene glycol → bis(2-hydroxyethyl) terephthalate (BHET) + methanol.
2. BHET undergoes **polycondensation** at $T \approx 270–285\,^\circ\text{C}$ under vacuum with catalysts (e.g., $\text{Sb}_2\text{O}_3$, $\text{GeO}_2$).
3. By-product **ethylene glycol** is removed to drive equilibrium toward high molecular weight.

**Route B – Direct Esterification (PTA Process — preferred modern route):**
1. Purified terephthalic acid (PTA) + ethylene glycol → BHET + water.
2. Same polycondensation step as Route A.

### 2.2 The BOPET Film Manufacturing Logic (Step-by-Step)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The transformation from a PET resin pellet to a BOPET film follows a **four-stage logic chain**. Memorize this — it is a KTU favourite.

1. **Drying:** PET resin is hygroscopic. Moisture > **0.005 %** causes hydrolytic degradation during melting. Drying is carried out at **150–180 °C for 4–6 hours** in a desiccant hopper.
2. **Extrusion:** Dried pellets are melt-extruded through a flat die (T-die) at **275–290 °C**, forming a molten sheet cast onto a chilled chill-roll.
3. **Biaxial Orientation (the heart of BOPET):**
   - **Machine Direction (MD) stretch:** the cast sheet passes over heated rollers rotating at progressively higher speeds, stretching it 3.0–3.5× in length.
   - **Transverse Direction (TD) stretch:** the MD-stretched film enters a **tenter frame** (clip-oven), where clips on diverging rails stretch it 3.0–4.0× in width while annealing at **230–240 °C**.
4. **Heat-Setting / Crystallization:** Tension is maintained while the film is heated above $T_g$ to relieve stresses and lock in crystallinity (typically 30–40 %).

### 2.3 Why Biaxial Orientation Improves Properties

During stretching, the amorphous polymer chains are pulled into a near-**planar, biaxially aligned configuration**. This:
- Induces **strain-induced crystallization** (SIC), creating a micro-fine crystalline network.
- Reduces free volume, raising density from **1.335 → 1.40 g/cm³**.
- Creates tortuous pathways for gas molecules → **barrier properties improve 3–5×**.
- Refractive index becomes uniform → **high optical clarity, low haze (< 1 %)**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All key equations for PET film engineering condensed in one reference table.

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning / Use |
|---|-------------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ | Number-average degree of polymerization; $p$ = extent of reaction |
| 2 | $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{N_0}{N}$ | DP from initial monomer count $N_0$ and unreacted ends $N$ |
| 3 | $\rho_c = 1.455\,\text{g/cm}^3$, $\rho_a = 1.335\,\text{g/cm}^3$ | Reference crystalline / amorphous densities |
| 4 | $\%\, \chi_c = \dfrac{\rho_c(\rho_s - \rho_a)}{\rho_s(\rho_c - \rho_a)} \times 100$ | Per cent crystallinity from bulk density $\rho_s$ |
| 5 | $\Delta H_f = \chi_c \cdot \Delta H_f^{\circ}$ | Total enthalpy of fusion; $\Delta H_f^{\circ} \approx 140\,\text{J/g}$ for PET |
| 6 | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | Tensile stress; modulus $E$ (BOPET ≈ 4–5 GPa) |
| 7 | $P = \dfrac{D \cdot S}{t}$ | Permeability $P$ (cc·mil/100 in²·day·atm); $D$=diffusivity, $S$=solubility, $t$=thickness |
| 8 | $\eta_{\text{inh}} = \dfrac{\ln(t/t_0)}{c}$ | Inherent viscosity used to grade PET resin (typical 0.6–0.9 dL/g) |
| 9 | $T_g \approx \frac{2}{3} T_m$ (Boyer–Beaman rule, in K) | Quick estimate of glass transition |
| 10 | $\text{OTR} = \dfrac{q \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta p}$ | Oxygen Transmission Rate; $q$ = volume permeated, $L$ = thickness |

> [!WARNING]
> **Equation 4** (per cent crystallinity from density) is **the** most frequently tested numerical problem in KTU for this topic. Memorize it along with the two reference densities **1.335 and 1.455 g/cm³**.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Flexible food packaging** (BOPET laminates with PE/PP for shelf-stable pouches, replacing glass/metal).
- **Electrical insulation:** PET film (Mylar®) is the dielectric in motors, transformers, capacitors.
- **Solar photovoltaic backsheets:** weatherable BOPET layer protects PV cells for **25+ years**.
- **Imaging and graphics:** photographic film base, X-ray films, label stocks.
- **Hot-fill and retort applications:** CPET trays survive **220 °C oven** temperatures.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Per-Cent Crystallinity from Bulk Density

**Statement:** Given a BOPET film with measured bulk density $\rho_s$, derive the per-cent crystallinity using the rule of mixtures.

**Given Data (standard):**
- Density of fully crystalline PET: $\rho_c = 1.455\,\text{g/cm}^3$
- Density of fully amorphous PET: $\rho_a = 1.335\,\text{g/cm}^3$
- Measured bulk density: $\rho_s = 1.390\,\text{g/cm}^3$

**Assumption:** The specific volume of a semi-crystalline polymer is a linear combination of amorphous and crystalline specific volumes (two-phase model).

**Step 1 — Define volume fractions:**
Let $V_c$ and $V_a$ be the volumes occupied by the crystalline and amorphous phases respectively, in a sample of total volume $V_s = V_c + V_a$.

Define volume fraction crystallinity:

$$
\phi_c = \frac{V_c}{V_s}, \qquad \phi_a = \frac{V_a}{V_s} = 1 - \phi_c
$$

**Step 2 — Express total mass and volume in terms of densities:**
Mass is conserved, so total mass $M$ can be written in two ways:

$$
M = \rho_c V_c + \rho_a V_a = \rho_s V_s
$$

Dividing through by $V_s$:

$$
\rho_c \, \phi_c + \rho_a \, (1 - \phi_c) = \rho_s
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\phi_c$:**

$$
\rho_c \, \phi_c + \rho_a - \rho_a \, \phi_c = \rho_s
$$

$$
\phi_c (\rho_c - \rho_a) = \rho_s - \rho_a
$$

$$
\phi_c = \frac{\rho_s - \rho_a}{\rho_c - \rho_a}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert volume-fraction crystallinity to per-cent crystallinity (mass-fraction form):**
The percentage crystallinity typically reported is the **mass fraction**:

$$
\%\,\chi_c = \frac{\rho_c}{\rho_s} \cdot \phi_c \times 100
$$

Substituting the expression for $\phi_c$:

$$
\%\,\chi_c = \frac{\rho_c(\rho_s - \rho_a)}{\rho_s(\rho_c - \rho_a)} \times 100
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical evaluation:**

$$
\%\,\chi_c = \frac{1.455 \times (1.390 - 1.335)}{1.390 \times (1.455 - 1.335)} \times 100
$$

$$
= \frac{1.455 \times 0.055}{1.390 \times 0.120} \times 100
$$

$$
= \frac{0.08003}{0.16680} \times 100 \approx 47.98\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** A BOPET film with $\rho_s = 1.390\,\text{g/cm}^3$ has a crystallinity of **≈ 48 %**, consistent with high-quality commercial BOPET film (30–50 % range).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Carothers' Equation for Number-Average Degree of Polymerization

**Statement:** Show that for a step-growth polymerization of PET (AA + BB type) with stoichiometric balance, the number-average degree of polymerization $\bar{X}_n$ is given by $\bar{X}_n = 1/(1-p)$, where $p$ is the fraction of functional groups reacted.

**Step 1 — Define the system:**
For PET, $N_A$ diacid groups and $N_B$ diol groups are present. With exact stoichiometry, $N_A = N_B = N_0$.

**Step 2 — Count unreacted functional groups after extent $p$:**
At reaction extent $p$, the number of unreacted groups is:

$$
N = N_0(1-p)
$$

**Step 3 — Count the number of polymer molecules $N_{\text{mol}}$:**
By definition, each polymer chain has exactly **two unreacted end-groups** (one A, one B). Therefore:

$$
N_{\text{mol}} = \frac{N_A + N_B}{2} = \frac{2N_0(1-p)}{2} = N_0(1-p)
$$

**Step 4 — Define number-average DP:**

$$
\bar{X}_n = \frac{\text{Initial monomer units}}{\text{Polymer molecules}} = \frac{N_0}{N_{\text{mol}}}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute:**

$$
\bar{X}_n = \frac{N_0}{N_0(1-p)} = \frac{1}{1-p}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This explains why PET manufacturers must push $p$ beyond **0.99** (often **> 0.995**) to obtain commercial-grade $\bar{X}_n \approx 100–200$ suitable for film extrusion.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Orientation Ratios vs. Properties

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Orientation Draw Ratio** is the ratio of stretched length to original length (in each axis). BOPET uses $\lambda_{MD} \approx 3.0\text{–}3.5$ and $\lambda_{TD} \approx 3.0\text{–}4.0$.

| Property | $\lambda_{MD} \uparrow$ | $\lambda_{TD} \uparrow$ | Balanced BOPET |
|----------|------------------------|------------------------|----------------|
| Tensile strength (MD) | ↑↑ Strongly | ↑ Mildly | High |
| Tensile strength (TD) | ↑ Mildly | ↑↑ Strongly | High |
| Elongation at break (MD) | ↓ | ↑ | Moderate |
| Optical clarity | Mild improvement | Mild improvement | **Excellent** |
| Oxygen barrier (OTR) | ↓ | ↓↓ | **3–5× better** than APET |
| Dimensional stability | Improves | Improves | **< 1 % shrinkage @ 150 °C** |
| Tear strength (initiation) | Slight decrease | Slight decrease | Acceptable |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — PET Repeat Unit & Synthesis Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph reactants
        A["Terephthalic Acid PTA"] --> C["Bis 2 Hydroxyethyl Terephthalate BHET"]
        B["Ethylene Glycol EG"] --> C
    end
    C --> D["Polycondensation at 280 C under vacuum"]
    D --> E["PET Repeat Unit O CH2 CH2 O CO C6H4 CO"]
    E --> F["PET Resin Pellets"]
    F --> G["Drying 180 C 6 hours"]
    G --> H["Melt Extrusion 285 C T die"]
    H --> I["Cast on Chill Roll Amorphous Sheet"]
    I --> J["MD Stretch 3.0 to 3.5x"]
    J --> K["TD Stretch 3.0 to 4.0x in Tenter Frame"]
    K --> L["Heat Set and Crystallize 240 C"]
    L --> M["BOPET Film Roll"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The flowchart maps the **industrial BOPET process** from PTA + EG feedstocks to the finished biaxially-oriented PET film roll.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a BOPET Film

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph polymer_matrix["PET Polymer Matrix"]
        direction LR
        amorph["Amorphous Phase 50 to 70 percent"]
        crystal["Crystalline Lamellae 30 to 50 percent"]
        amorph --- crystal
    end

    subgraph orientation["Chain Orientation Map"]
        direction TB
        md_axis["Machine Direction MD alignment vector"]
        td_axis["Transverse Direction TD alignment vector"]
        md_axis --- td_axis
    end

    polymer_matrix --> prop_block
    orientation --> prop_block

    subgraph prop_block["Resulting Engineering Properties"]
        p1["Optical Clarity Haze less than 1 percent"]
        p2["Tensile Strength 200 to 250 MPa"]
        p3["Oxygen Barrier OTR 50 to 100 cc per m2 per day"]
        p4["Dimensional Stability Shrinkage less than 2 percent at 150 C"]
        p5["Thermal Resistance Service up to 200 C"]
    end

    subgraph env_apps["Engineering Applications"]
        a1["Food Packaging Laminates"]
        a2["Photovoltaic Backsheets"]
        a3["Electrical Insulation Mylar"]
        a4["Label and Graphic Films"]
    end

    prop_block --> env_apps
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — PET Environmental Recycling Loop (Resin Codes)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["PET Resin Code 1 Virgin Resin"] --> M1["Manufactured BOPET Film"]
    M1 --> U1["End Use Packaging and Industrial"]
    U1 --> C1["Post Consumer Collection"]
    C1 --> S1["Sorting by Resin Code 1"]
    S1 --> R1["Mechanical Recycling Wash and Flake"]
    R1 --> P2["Recycled PET rPET Pellets"]
    P2 --> M1
    U1 --> C2["Chemical Recycling Glycolysis or Hydrolysis"]
    C2 --> P1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PET Resin Code "1"** is the most widely recycled plastic globally, an environmental advantage emphasized throughout the KTU PEPOT632 syllabus.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define BOPET. List any four salient properties that make it suitable for flexible food packaging.*

**Model Answer:**

**BOPET (Biaxially Oriented Polyethylene Terephthalate)** is a polyester film produced by stretching an extruded PET sheet in **both** the machine direction (MD) and transverse direction (TD), typically at draw ratios of **3.0–3.5×** (MD) and **3.0–4.0×** (TD), followed by heat-setting above its glass transition temperature.

Four salient properties for food packaging:
1. **High optical clarity** (transparency > 88 %, haze < 1 %) — showcases the product.
2. **Excellent oxygen and moisture barrier** (OTR ≈ 50–100 cc/m²·day) — extends shelf life.
3. **High tensile strength** (200–250 MPa) and **puncture resistance** — survives logistics handling.
4. **Thermal stability** (service up to 200 °C) and **chemical inertness** to food acids, oils.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write "PET film" without specifying **"biaxially oriented"** (BOPET). Examiners specifically look for the *BOPET* terminology and the **draw ratios** — losing 1 mark for omission.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Why is drying of PET resin mandatory before melt extrusion? What is the acceptable moisture level?*

**Model Answer:**

PET resin is **hygroscopic** and absorbs atmospheric moisture (up to 0.5 % w/w under humid conditions). At melt-extrusion temperatures (275–290 °C), even trace moisture undergoes **hydrolytic degradation**, cleaving the ester linkages:

$$
\text{–CO–O–CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{–} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{–CO–OH} \;+\; \text{HO–CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{–}
$$

This causes a **drastic reduction in molecular weight** (and hence inherent viscosity), producing brittle film, bubbles, die-lip build-up, and fish-eyes. Therefore, drying is mandatory.

- **Drying conditions:** 150–180 °C for 4–6 hours in a desiccant (dehumidified-air) hopper.
- **Acceptable residual moisture:** **< 0.005 % (50 ppm)**, measured by a moisture analyzer before extrusion.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU's ESE Part-B for PEPOT632 Module 2 contains an **internal choice**. The two alternatives below are constructed to test distinct cognitive levels of the Revised Bloom's Taxonomy.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Synthesis + Properties + Numerical Crystallinity*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the industrial manufacturing of BOPET film with a neat block diagram. Highlight the role of the **tenter frame** in the process.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A BOPET film sample has a measured bulk density of **1.385 g/cm³**. Using the two-phase model, calculate the **per-cent crystallinity** of the film. Given: $\rho_c = 1.455$ g/cm³, $\rho_a = 1.335$ g/cm³.

---

**Model Solution (a):**

The BOPET process consists of **five sequential stages**:

1. **Resin Drying (150–180 °C, 4–6 h):** Removes moisture to < 50 ppm to prevent hydrolysis at melt temperatures.
2. **Melt Extrusion (275–290 °C):** Dried PET pellets are fed into a single-screw extruder; the melt is forced through a **T-die** producing a flat molten sheet.
3. **Casting:** The molten sheet is quenched on a chilled chill-roll (T ≈ 30 °C), forming an **amorphous, un-oriented cast film**.
4. **Biaxial Orientation:**
   - **MD stretching** between heated godet rolls at 3.0–3.5× draw ratio.
   - **TD stretching** in the **tenter frame** at 3.0–4.0× draw ratio, simultaneously raising temperature to **100–130 °C** (above $T_g$).
   - The tenter frame is a chain of clips riding on diverging rails; the rails widen progressively, stretching the film sideways while the clips hold the edges.
5. **Heat-Setting (230–240 °C):** Crystallizes the oriented film and locks in dimensions, reducing in-plane shrinkage to < 2 % at 150 °C.

**Role of the Tenter Frame:**
- Provides **uniform, stress-free transverse stretching** without necking.
- Enables **independent control** of TD draw ratio and annealing temperature.
- Triggers **strain-induced crystallization**, increasing density and barrier performance.
- Maintains **film flatness and width tolerance** ± 1 %.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

Using the two-phase density equation (derived in Section 3.1):

$$
\%\,\chi_c = \frac{\rho_c(\rho_s - \rho_a)}{\rho_s(\rho_c - \rho_a)} \times 100
$$

Substitute $\rho_c = 1.455$, $\rho_a = 1.335$, $\rho_s = 1.385$ g/cm³:

$$
\%\,\chi_c = \frac{1.455 \times (1.385 - 1.335)}{1.385 \times (1.455 - 1.335)} \times 100
$$

Step 1 — Numerator:

$$
1.455 \times 0.050 = 0.07275
$$

Step 2 — Denominator:

$$
1.385 \times 0.120 = 0.16620
$$

Step 3 — Division:

$$
\%\,\chi_c = \frac{0.07275}{0.16620} \times 100 \approx 43.77\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The film has **≈ 43.8 % crystallinity**, falling within the typical BOPET commercial range of 30–50 %.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Chemistry + Environmental Recycling*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With suitable chemical equations, explain the **two industrial routes** for the synthesis of PET from its monomers.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **environmental impact and recycling pathways** of PET films. Comment on the significance of **Resin Identification Code "1"**.

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Route 1 — Ester-Interchange (DMT route, older process):**

*Step 1 — Transesterification:*

$$
\text{CH}_3\text{OOC–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–COOCH}_3 + 2\,\text{HOCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OH} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Mn or Zn acetate, 180–200 °C}}\; \text{HOCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OOC–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–COOCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OH} + 2\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH}
$$

*Step 2 — Polycondensation (270–285 °C, vacuum, $\text{Sb}_2\text{O}_3$ catalyst):*

$$
n\,\text{BHET} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{HO–(CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OOC–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{COO)}_n\text{–H} + (n-1)\,\text{HOCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OH}
$$

**Route 2 — Direct Esterification (PTA route, modern preferred process):**

*Step 1 — Direct esterification:*

$$
\text{HOOC–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–COOH} + 2\,\text{HOCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OH} \;\xrightarrow{\text{200–230 °C}}\; \text{BHET} + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

*Step 2 — Same polycondensation as Route 1.*

The **PTA route is preferred industrially** because (i) it avoids the toxic methanol by-product, (ii) water is the easier by-product to remove, and (iii) it gives higher purity and molecular weight.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Environmental impact:**
- PET is **non-toxic, BPA-free, and food-safe**, but its persistence in the environment (~450 years to degrade) is a serious concern.
- Improperly discarded PET films contribute to **microplastic pollution** in oceans and soils.
- Incineration releases **CO₂ and toxic additives** (catalyst residues, plasticizers).

**Recycling pathways:**

1. **Mechanical recycling (most common):** Collection → sorting by Resin Code 1 → washing → shredding into flakes → re-extrusion into rPET pellets → reuse in fibres, strapping, food-grade bottles (after super-cleaning).
2. **Chemical recycling (depolymerization):**
   - **Glycolysis** with excess EG → BHET monomer → re-polymerization.
   - **Hydrolysis** with water/acid/alkali → PTA + EG.
   - **Methanolysis** → DMT + EG.
3. **Energy recovery:** Controlled incineration with heat recovery (less preferred due to carbon footprint).

**Significance of Resin Identification Code "1":**
- The **chasing-arrows triangle with the digit "1"** is the SPI (Society of the Plastics Industry) code for PET.
- It enables **automated sorting** at Material Recovery Facilities (MRFs) using **near-infrared (NIR) spectroscopy**.
- It signals to consumers that the product is **widely recyclable** through municipal streams.
- The code "1" position (PET) is the **single most-recycled plastic** globally, with established reclamation infrastructure, making PET films a model for a **circular polymer economy**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Marks are lost if you (i) confuse DMT and PTA routes, (ii) forget the catalyst in polycondensation, or (iii) fail to mention **Resin Code 1 = PET = widely recyclable**. Also, do **not** state that PET is "biodegradable" — it is **not** under ambient conditions; only specialized enzymatic or chemical processes degrade it.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — PET Films**

- [x] PET = **Poly(ethylene terephthalate)**, repeat unit contains **ester linkage (–COO–)** and **para-phenylene ring**.
- [x] Two synthesis routes: **DMT + EG** (transesterification) and **PTA + EG** (direct esterification); both yield **BHET → polycondensation**.
- [x] Catalysts: Mn/Zn acetate (transesterification), **Sb₂O₃ or GeO₂** (polycondensation).
- [x] **Drying mandatory** at 150–180 °C, 4–6 h, residual moisture **< 0.005 %**.
- [x] BOPET = biaxially oriented PET, MD draw **3.0–3.5×**, TD draw **3.0–4.0×**.
- [x] **Tenter frame** is the heart of the BOPET process — provides TD orientation and heat-setting.
- [x] Properties: $T_g \approx 75\text{–}80\,°\text{C}$, $T_m \approx 245\text{–}265\,°\text{C}$, tensile strength **200–250 MPa**, OTR **50–100 cc/m²·day**, haze **< 1 %**.
- [x] **Crystallinity formula** (mass-fraction): $\%\,\chi_c = \dfrac{\rho_c(\rho_s - \rho_a)}{\rho_s(\rho_c - \rho_a)} \times 100$ with $\rho_c = 1.455$, $\rho_a = 1.335$ g/cm³.
- [x] **Carothers' equation** $\bar{X}_n = 1/(1-p)$ — for commercial PET, $p > 0.99$.
- [x] **Resin Code 1** = PET = most recycled plastic globally.
- [x] Recycling: **Mechanical (most common), Chemical (glycolysis, hydrolysis, methanolysis), Energy recovery**.
- [x] Applications: food packaging, **Mylar®** electrical insulation, **PV backsheets**, label films, X-ray/imaging base.
- [x] Environmental note: PET is **non-biodegradable**; promote circular economy via **rPET** streams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Secondary Recycling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT632 - POLYMERS & ENVIRONMENT > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Secondary Recycling ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Secondary Recycling** is defined as the mechanical reprocessing of post-consumer or post-industrial plastic waste into secondary raw materials (granulates, pellets, flakes) and subsequently into new plastic products that exhibit different physical, mechanical, and aesthetic properties when compared to the original virgin material. It is the most commercially implemented route of plastic recovery and falls under the broader umbrella of **Mechanical Recycling**. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus for *PEPOT632 – Polymers & Environment*, secondary recycling specifically refers to the conversion of thermoplastic scrap through physical means (sorting, washing, size reduction, melting, and re-extrusion) without significantly altering the fundamental chemical structure of the polymer backbone. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Secondary recycling is to be studied in contrast to Primary (in-plant re-extrusion of uncontaminated scrap), Tertiary (chemical/feedstock recycling to monomers), and Quaternary (energy recovery via incineration) recycling. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you have a collection of **old PET water bottles**. You cannot drink from them anymore (they are contaminated, worn out, and aesthetically poor). Instead of throwing them away (landfill = Quaternary pollution) or chemically breaking them back to the original raw oil (Tertiary recycling), you do something simpler: 1. You collect them. 2. Wash and clean them. 3. Chop them into small flakes. 4. Melt the flakes into a thick molten paste. 5. Push this paste through a die to form small cylindrical granules. 6. Use these granules to manufacture something **different** — say, polyester fiber for a t-shirt, or a park bench, or a drainage pipe. This entire workflow is **Secondary Recycling**. You are not changing the chemistry of PET (it remains polyethylene terephthalate), but you are physically reshaping it into a new, often lower-grade, application. > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Takeaway:** Secondary recycling = *"Melt, reshape, reuse — but accept that the new product is not exactly the same as the old one."* The polymer undergoes some property loss (downcycling) due to thermal and mechanical degradation. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Secondary Recycling The following standardized parameters govern the quantitative evaluation of any secondary recycling operation: - **Yield (η):** Typically **65% – 90%** for clean post-consumer streams. - **Melt Flow Index (MFI):** Measured in **g/10 min** at standard temperature/load (e.g., 2.16 kg load). - **Intrinsic Viscosity ([η]):** Measured in **dL/g**, critical for PET recycling. - **Contamination Tolerance:** Usually **< 2% by mass** for premium recyclate grades. - **Bulk Density of Flakes:** Approximately **0.3 – 0.5 g/cm³** depending on polymer. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Reality Check:** Unlike virgin polymer synthesis where properties are tightly controlled, secondary recyclate properties are *inherently variable*. Each batch of waste plastic has a different thermal-mechanical history, making consistent output a major engineering challenge. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The Six Stages of Secondary Recycling The secondary recycling process is best understood as a sequential pipeline. Each stage has a specific physical purpose and contributes to the overall quality of the recyclate. #### Stage 1 — Collection and Segregation - **What:** Gathering of plastic waste from municipal, industrial, or marine sources. - **Why:** Centralized waste streams must be aggregated before processing. Segregation at source (household bins) dramatically improves downstream purity. - **How:** Manual sorting, automated near-infrared (NIR) spectroscopy, density-based float-sink tanks. #### Stage 2 — Sorting by Polymer Type - **What:** Separation of mixed plastics into mono-polymer streams (e.g., PET separated from HDPE from PVC). - **Why:** Different polymers are **immiscible** in the melt state. A blend of PET and PE produces a useless, brittle, delaminated material with no engineering value. - **How:** NIR optical sorters, electrostatic separation, froth flotation, X-ray fluorescence (for PVC identification via chlorine content). #### Stage 3 — Washing and Contaminant Removal - **What:** Removal of food residue, labels, adhesives, dirt, and biological contaminants. - **Why:** Organic impurities cause **hydrolysis** and **yellowing** in the recyclate. Labels and adhesives clog filters in the extruder. - **How:** Hot caustic soda wash (NaOH, 2–5% w/w, 80–90 °C), friction washers, centrifugal dryers. #### Stage 4 — Size Reduction (Shredding / Grinding) - **What:** Conversion of bulky waste into flakes typically **2 – 12 mm** in size. - **Why:** Uniform flake size ensures consistent feeding into the extruder and uniform melting. - **How:** Rotary granulators, shredders with rotating blades and screen meshes. #### Stage 5 — Melting and Re-extrusion (Agglomeration / Compounding) - **What:** Flakes are melted (typically 160 °C – 280 °C depending on polymer) and homogenized in an extruder. - **Why:** This is the *core* of secondary recycling. The molten polymer is devolatilized to remove residual moisture and volatiles. - **How:** Single-screw or twin-screw extruders with vacuum vents. Optional addition of stabilizers, compatibilizers, or virgin polymer to compensate for degradation. #### Stage 6 — Pelletizing and Reprocessing - **What:** The molten extrudate is cut into uniform cylindrical pellets (typically **2 – 5 mm** length, **2 – 4 mm** diameter). - **Why:** Pellets are the standard commodity form for downstream injection molding, blow molding, or film extrusion. - **How:** Strand pelletizer (cooled strand cut by rotating blades) or underwater die-face pelletizer. ### 2.2 Mechanism of Property Degradation During Secondary Recycling Secondary recycling is not without cost. Every thermal cycle induces irreversible changes in the polymer: - **Chain Scission:** Reduction in molecular weight due to random C–C bond breakage. The polymer becomes more brittle. - **Crosslinking (for some polymers like PE):** Formation of branched networks, increasing melt viscosity and reducing processability. - **Oxidative Degradation:** Reaction with residual oxygen creates carbonyl groups (C=O), causing yellowing and embrittlement. - **Hydrolysis (especially PET):** Water reacts with ester linkages, breaking chains and reducing [η]. - **Contamination Buildup:** Even trace impurities accumulate, accelerating degradation in subsequent cycles. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Concept Link:** Property degradation is the *fundamental reason* why secondary recyclate is usually "downcycled" into lower-grade applications (e.g., PET bottles → PET carpet fiber, not PET bottles again). This is called **Cascade Recycling**. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet The following table compiles the high-yield formulas and parameters relevant to secondary recycling that are examinable in KTU 2024 assessments. | **Parameter / Concept** | **Formula / Relation** | **Description** | **Units** | |---|---|---|---| | Melt Flow Index (MFI) | $\text{MFI} = \dfrac{m}{t}$ | Mass $m$ of polymer extruded in time $t$ under standard load | $\text{g}/10\,\text{min}$ | | Molecular Weight after Scission | $\dfrac{1}{\overline{M}_n(t)} = \dfrac{1}{\overline{M}_n(0)} + kt$ | Random chain scission kinetics; $k$ is rate constant | $\text{mol/g}$ | | Degradation Rate (Arrhenius) | $k = A\, e^{-E_a / RT}$ | Temperature dependence of degradation; $E_a$ = activation energy | $\text{s}^{-1}$ | | Yield of Recycling Process | $\eta = \dfrac{m_{\text{useful product}}}{m_{\text{input waste}}} \times 100\%$ | Mass efficiency of the process | dimensionless (%) | | Degree of Polymerization | $\overline{DP}_n = \dfrac{\overline{M}_n}{M_0}$ | Average monomer units per chain; $M_0$ = monomer molar mass | dimensionless | | Intrinsic Viscosity (PET) | $[\eta] = K\, \overline{M}_v^{\,a}$ | Mark–Houwink–Sakurada equation; $K, a$ are polymer–solvent constants | $\text{dL/g}$ | | Bulk Density of Flakes | $\rho_b = \dfrac{m_{\text{flakes}}}{V_{\text{container}}}$ | Critical for hopper feeding efficiency | $\text{g/cm}^3$ | | Energy Recovery Potential | $Q = m \cdot \text{NCV}$ | Net calorific value basis; useful when comparing to incineration | $\text{MJ/kg}$ | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Examination Tip:** Always quote MFI values with their **standard test conditions** (temperature and load). For example, "MFI of LDPE = 25 g/10 min (190 °C / 2.16 kg)". Examiners frequently deduct marks for omitting these conditions. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Molecular Weight Decay via Random Chain Scission During secondary recycling, the polymer is subjected to high temperatures in the presence of shear and trace oxygen. This causes **random chain scission**, where any C–C bond along the backbone can break with equal probability. Let us derive the relationship between molecular weight and processing time. **Step 1 — Define the Number of Cleavages** Let there be $N_0$ initial polymer molecules, each consisting of an initial number-average degree of polymerization $\overline{DP}_n(0)$. After time $t$, let there be $N(t)$ molecules remaining. The total number of **bond cleavages** that have occurred is: $$ N_{\text{cleavages}} = N(t) - N_0 $$ **Step 2 — Relate Cleavages to Scission Events** By definition of number-average degree of polymerization: $$ \overline{DP}_n(0) = \dfrac{N_{\text{monomer units, total}}}{N_0} $$ After scission, the same total number of monomer units is distributed among a larger number of chains: $$ \overline{DP}_n(t) = \dfrac{N_{\text{monomer units, total}}}{N(t)} $$ **Step 3 — Combine the Two Expressions** Taking the ratio: $$ \dfrac{\overline{DP}_n(0)}{\overline{DP}_n(t)} = \dfrac{N(t)}{N_0} = 1 + \dfrac{N(t) - N_0}{N_0} $$ Since number-average molecular weight is $\overline{M}_n = \overline{DP}_n \cdot M_0$ (where $M_0$ is the molar mass of the repeat unit), we have: $$ \dfrac{\overline{M}_n(0)}{\overline{M}_n(t)} = 1 + \dfrac{N(t) - N_0}{N_0} $$ **Step 4 — Introduce the Rate Constant** The number of cleavages per initial molecule per unit time is the **scission rate constant** $k$. Thus: $$ \dfrac{N(t) - N_0}{N_0} = k t $$ **Step 5 — Final Integrated Form** Substituting back: $$ \dfrac{\overline{M}_n(0)}{\overline{M}_n(t)} = 1 + kt $$ Or equivalently (the standard textbook form): $$ \dfrac{1}{\overline{M}_n(t)} = \dfrac{1}{\overline{M}_n(0)} + \dfrac{kt}{M_0} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation for KTU Exams:** This equation shows that **$1/\overline{M}_n$ increases linearly with processing time** $t$. Therefore, a plot of $1/\overline{M}_n(t)$ versus $t$ yields a straight line with slope $k/M_0$ and intercept $1/\overline{M}_n(0)$. This is a frequently asked derivation question in Module 2. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example > **Problem (KTU Practice Style):** A batch of PET flakes has an initial number-average molecular weight $\overline{M}_n(0) = 30{,}000\,\text{g/mol}$. During a secondary recycling extrusion step at 280 °C, the random chain scission rate constant is measured as $k = 4.0 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{min}^{-1}$. Calculate $\overline{M}_n$ after 60 minutes of processing. **Solution — Step 1: State the governing equation** $$ \dfrac{1}{\overline{M}_n(t)} = \dfrac{1}{\overline{M}_n(0)} + kt $$ **Step 2: Substitute numerical values** $$ \dfrac{1}{\overline{M}_n(60)} = \dfrac{1}{30{,}000} + (4.0 \times 10^{-4})(60) $$ **Step 3: Evaluate each term** $$ \dfrac{1}{30{,}000} = 3.333 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{mol/g} $$ $$ (4.0 \times 10^{-4})(60) = 2.4 \times 10^{-2}\,\text{min}^{-1} $$ Wait — note that the equation as written has units inconsistency for $k$. The rigorous form requires $k$ to have units of **cleavages per molecule per minute**, which is dimensionless per minute when divided by the number of molecules. Re-expressing using the cleaner form: $$ \dfrac{\overline{M}_n(0)}{\overline{M}_n(t)} = 1 + kt = 1 + (4.0 \times 10^{-4})(60) = 1 + 0.024 = 1.024 $$ **Step 4: Solve for $\overline{M}_n(t)$** $$ \overline{M}_n(60) = \dfrac{30{,}000}{1.024} \approx 29{,}297\,\text{g/mol} $$ **Step 5: Calculate percentage loss** $$ \%\,\text{loss} = \dfrac{30{,}000 - 29{,}297}{30{,}000} \times 100\% \approx 2.34\% $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** Even after 1 hour of extrusion, PET loses ~2.3% of its molecular weight. Cumulative over multiple recycling cycles (3–5 cycles), this loss becomes severe and eventually renders the polymer unsuitable for bottle-grade applications — explaining why downcycling to fibers or strapping is the typical end-of-life route. ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Recycling Hierarchies The following matrix situates Secondary Recycling against its sibling recovery routes. This comparison is a **favourite KTU question type**. | **Attribute** | **Primary Recycling** | **Secondary Recycling** | **Tertiary Recycling** | **Quaternary Recycling** | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Input** | Clean in-plant scrap | Post-consumer / mixed waste | Mixed / contaminated waste | All residual waste | | **Process** | Re-extrusion | Mechanical reprocessing | Chemical depolymerization | Incineration | | **Output Quality** | Virgin-equivalent | Lower grade (downcycled) | Monomers / feedstocks | Heat / electricity | | **Property Change** | None | Mild degradation | Chemical breakdown | Complete oxidation | | **Cost** | Low | Moderate | High | Low–Moderate | | **Energy Demand** | Very low | Moderate | High | Moderate (with emissions control) | | **Environmental Impact** | Minimal | Low–Moderate | Moderate (solvents, catalysts) | High (CO₂, dioxins if uncontrolled) | | **Example** | PET preform trimmings → preforms | PET bottles → PET fiber | PET → BHET monomer → virgin PET | Plastic waste → electricity | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram of Secondary Recycling The following block-level functional architecture flow captures every major unit operation, material stream, and decision point in a typical secondary recycling plant. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Source Collection
Municipal / Industrial] --> B[Manual Pre-sort
Remove Non-Plastics] B --> C[Automated NIR Sort
Polymer Identification] C --> D[Density Separation
Float-Sink Tank] D --> E[Washing Stage
Hot Caustic 80 C] E --> F[Rinse and Dry
Centrifugal Dryer] F --> G[Shredding Unit
Granulator 2 to 12 mm] G --> H[Flake Inspection
Metal Detection] H --> I[Extruder Melting
180 to 280 C Vacuum Vent] I --> J[Filter and Melt Pump
Remove Particulates] J --> K[Die Face Pelletizer
Strand or Underwater] K --> L[Quality Control
MFI Tensile Color] L --> M[Storage Silo
Recyclate Pellets] M --> N[Downstream Conversion
Injection Blow Film] N --> O[Secondary Product
Fiber Bench Pipe] style A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1 style O fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style I fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style L fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c ``` ### 4.2 Process Flow Description (Reading the Diagram) The diagram is divided conceptually into three zones: - **Blue zone (top, A–D):** Upstream handling — collection and sorting. - **Yellow zone (middle, E–J):** Cleaning, size reduction, and the *heart* of the operation — melting and melt filtration. - **Green/red zone (bottom, K–O):** Conversion to a marketable secondary product. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why filtration (J) matters:** During secondary recycling, the polymer is contaminated with paper fibers, residual metals, and degraded gel particles. A **melt filter** (typically 40–200 micron mesh) prevents these from weakening the final product. Examiners often ask why filtration is essential — the answer is *mechanical property preservation*. ### 4.3 Degradation Pathway Schematic The following diagram illustrates the dominant degradation pathways active during the extrusion stage. ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Virgin Polymer Chain
High MW] -- Thermal Scission --> D1[Lower MW Chains] P1 -- Oxidation --> D2[Carbonyl Groups
Yellowing] P1 -- Hydrolysis --> D3[Carboxyl Hydroxyl Ends] P1 -- Shear --> D4[Radical Formation
Crosslinking] D1 --> R[Recyclate
Reduced Properties] D2 --> R D3 --> R D4 --> R style P1 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1 style R fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** *Define secondary recycling of plastics. How does it differ from primary recycling?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Secondary recycling is the **mechanical reprocessing of post-consumer plastic waste** into new products, where the polymer retains its chemical identity but may have reduced mechanical properties. It involves physical operations like sorting, washing, shredding, melting, and re-extrusion. Difference from primary recycling: - **Primary recycling** uses **clean in-plant scrap** (e.g., sprues, runners, edge trims) and produces a recyclate **virtually identical** in properties to the virgin material. - **Secondary recycling** uses **post-consumer or mixed waste**, which is contaminated and degraded, producing a recyclate with **lower molecular weight, altered color, and reduced mechanical performance**. The product is usually *downcycled* into a different, lower-grade application. *(Examiner expects: definition + clear contrast + one example of each. 1 mark each.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** *List the major steps involved in the secondary recycling of plastics. Why is sorting by polymer type critical?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Major steps: 1. Collection and segregation 2. Sorting by polymer type (NIR, density) 3. Washing and contaminant removal 4. Size reduction (shredding/grinding into flakes) 5. Melting and re-extrusion 6. Pelletization and quality control Sorting by polymer type is critical because **different polymers are thermodynamically immiscible in the melt state**. Mixing PET with PE or PVC produces a heterogeneous, delaminated, and mechanically useless blend. Sorting ensures **mono-purity**, which is a prerequisite for engineering-grade recyclate. *(Examiner expects: at least 4 steps named correctly + 1 clear justification for sorting.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) #### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model — Module 2]** **(a)** With the help of a neat process flow diagram, describe the **complete secondary recycling process** for post-consumer PET bottles. Highlight the function of each unit operation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **mechanisms of polymer degradation** that occur during the extrusion step of secondary recycling. Derive the relation between number-average molecular weight and processing time under random chain scission kinetics. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Process Flow (7 marks):** The student should describe **all six stages** with the function of each: | Stage | Function | |---|---| | Collection | Aggregation from municipal waste streams | | Sorting (NIR) | Identification and separation by polymer type using near-infrared spectroscopy | | Washing | Removal of food residue, labels, adhesives using hot caustic solution | | Shredding | Conversion into uniform flakes (2–12 mm) for consistent feeding | | Extrusion | Melting at 270–280 °C under vacuum to remove volatiles and homogenize melt | | Pelletization | Conversion into uniform cylindrical pellets (2–4 mm) for downstream use | Marks distribution: 1 mark for each correctly described stage, plus 1 mark for any specific mention of **vacuum devolatilization** or **melt filtration** (engineering depth). **(b) Degradation Mechanisms and Derivation (7 marks):** **Mechanisms (4 marks):** The student should list and briefly explain: 1. **Random chain scission** — thermal breakage of C–C bonds 2. **Oxidative degradation** — formation of carbonyl groups; causes yellowing 3. **Hydrolytic degradation (PET-specific)** — ester linkage cleavage by residual water 4. **Shear-induced radical formation** — leading to crosslinking in polyolefins **Derivation (3 marks):** The student must derive: Starting with number of cleavages per initial molecule: $$ \dfrac{N(t) - N_0}{N_0} = kt $$ Recognizing that $\overline{DP}_n = \dfrac{\text{total monomer units}}{N}$, and that total monomer units is conserved: $$ \dfrac{\overline{DP}_n(0)}{\overline{DP}_n(t)} = \dfrac{N(t)}{N_0} = 1 + kt $$ Since $\overline{M}_n = \overline{DP}_n \cdot M_0$: $$ \dfrac{\overline{M}_n(0)}{\overline{M}_n(t)} = 1 + kt \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \dfrac{1}{\overline{M}_n(t)} = \dfrac{1}{\overline{M}_n(0)} + \dfrac{kt}{M_0} $$ *(Examiner expects: clean derivation, every step justified, final equation in standard form.)* --- #### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model — Module 2]** **(a)** Compare **primary, secondary, tertiary, and quaternary recycling** of plastics in a tabular manner. Discuss why secondary recycling is considered the most commercially viable route. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A recycled HDPE batch has $\overline{M}_n(0) = 80{,}000\,\text{g/mol}$. After one extrusion cycle at 200 °C, $\overline{M}_n = 65{,}000\,\text{g/mol}$. Estimate the chain scission rate constant $k$. If a second identical cycle is performed on this recyclate, what will be the final molecular weight? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Comparison (7 marks):** Present the table (as given in Section 3.3 above) with at least **6 attributes** correctly contrasted: - Input type, process, output quality, cost, energy demand, environmental impact. **Why secondary recycling is commercially viable (additional marks within part a):** - Lower capital investment than chemical (tertiary) recycling - Established technology with mature machinery (granulators, extruders) - Compatible with existing manufacturing infrastructure - Generates marketable recyclate for non-critical applications (bins, pipes, fibers) **(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):** **Step 1:** Use the derived equation for one cycle: $$ \dfrac{\overline{M}_n(0)}{\overline{M}_n(t)} = 1 + kt $$ Substitute $t = 1$ extrusion cycle (assume normalized time per cycle): $$ \dfrac{80{,}000}{65{,}000} = 1 + k(1) $$ $$ 1.2308 = 1 + k \quad \Longrightarrow \quad k = 0.2308\,\text{cycle}^{-1} $$ **Step 2:** Apply the same equation for the second cycle, using $\overline{M}_n(0) = 65{,}000\,\text{g/mol}$: $$ \dfrac{65{,}000}{\overline{M}_n(\text{final})} = 1 + (0.2308)(1) = 1.2308 $$ $$ \overline{M}_n(\text{final}) = \dfrac{65{,}000}{1.2308} \approx 52{,}805\,\text{g/mol} $$ **Step 3:** Comment on the engineering significance: $$ \%\,\text{total loss} = \dfrac{80{,}000 - 52{,}805}{80{,}000} \times 100\% \approx 34\% $$ After two cycles, HDPE has lost ~34% of its molecular weight, making it unsuitable for film or blow molding applications. This demonstrates **the inherent limitation of secondary recycling** and the necessity of downcycling or blending with virgin polymer. *(Examiner expects: correct numerical evaluation with clear units + interpretation in the context of secondary recycling.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Missing "secondary" qualifier:** Many students write generic "plastic recycling" definitions and lose 1 mark. Always specify *secondary* = mechanical reprocessing of post-consumer waste. > 2. **Confusing MFI direction:** Higher MFI = lower molecular weight = more degradation. Examiners penalize students who claim higher MFI means *better* quality. > 3. **Skipping the vacuum vent in extrusion:** This is the *key engineering detail* that distinguishes secondary recycling extrusion from simple melting. Always mention devolatilization. > 4. **Forgetting the factor of 100 in yield:** Yield $\eta$ is reported as a **percentage**. Writing 0.75 instead of 75% loses 0.5 marks. > 5. **Unit inconsistency in chain scission derivation:** The rate constant $k$ must have units consistent with the time base (cycles⁻¹ or min⁻¹). State this explicitly. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Secondary Recycling** - **Definition:** Mechanical reprocessing of post-consumer plastic waste into new products with **altered** (usually lower) properties. The polymer identity is preserved; only its physical form is changed. - **Six Stages:** Collection → Sorting → Washing → Shredding → Extrusion → Pelletization. - **Key Engineering Units:** Twin-screw extruder with **vacuum devolatilization vent**, **melt filter** (40–200 µm), strand or underwater pelletizer. - **Critical Engineering Concept — Downcycling:** Secondary recyclate is usually converted into **lower-grade products** (PET bottles → carpet fiber; HDPE bottles → drainage pipes) because molecular weight and aesthetics decline with each thermal cycle. - **Governing Equation:** $\dfrac{1}{\overline{M}_n(t)} = \dfrac{1}{\overline{M}_n(0)} + \dfrac{kt}{M_0}$ — random chain scission model. This equation appears in nearly every KTU Module 2 exam. - **Four Degradation Pathways:** Thermal scission, oxidative degradation, hydrolysis (PET), shear-induced crosslinking (PE). - **Standard Test Conditions for MFI:** Always quote temperature (°C) and load (kg). Example: "MFI = 2.1 g/10 min (190 °C / 2.16 kg)". - **Hierarchy Position:** Secondary recycling sits between **Primary** (clean scrap, virgin-like output) and **Tertiary** (chemical depolymerization to monomers). It is more commercially mature than tertiary recycling but produces lower-quality output than primary recycling. - **Sustainability Trade-off:** Secondary recycling consumes **less energy** than virgin polymer synthesis (~60–80% energy savings) but produces **inferior material**. Blending with 10–30% virgin polymer is a common industrial compromise. - **Future Direction:** Compatibilizer additives, enzymatic depolymerization hybrids, and AI-based NIR sorting are the active frontiers of secondary recycling research. ---

topics.title: Plastics Wastes Containing Paper
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT632 - POLYMERS & ENVIRONMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Plastics Wastes Containing Paper

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Paper-plastic composite waste** refers to post-consumer and industrial waste streams in which cellulose-based paper (or paperboard) is intimately bonded to one or more layers of thermoplastic polymers (such as Polyethylene (PE), Polypropylene (PP), Polyethylene Terephthalate (PET), or Ethylene-Vinyl Acetate (EVA)) — and in some cases an aluminum (Al) foil layer. The result is a **multi-layer laminated packaging waste (MLPW)** that cannot be separated by conventional mechanical sorting.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, these are classified under **Mixed/Multilayer Plastic Wastes** (as per Plastic Waste Management Rules, India) and constitute one of the most challenging **End-of-Life (EoL)** fractions in municipal solid waste (MSW).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PEPOT632 – Module 2):**
> The classification of plastic wastes is incomplete without addressing **laminated, coextruded, and fiber-reinforced** plastic-paper systems, because the presence of cellulose fibers fundamentally alters the pyrolysis, incineration, and recycling behavior of the plastic phase.

## 1.2 Common Examples (Intuitive Overview)

| Commercial Product | Layer Structure | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| Tetra Pak / Aseptic carton | PE / Paperboard / PE / Al / PE | Milk, juice, soup |
| Laminated paper cup | PE / Paper / PE | Hot beverages |
| Pet food pouch | Paper / Al / PET | Dry food packaging |
| Wallpaper (vinyl-coated) | PVC / Paper | Interior décor |
| Foil-laminated sachets | Paper / PE / Al / PET | Condiments, single-serve |
| Tetra Brik | PE / Paper / PE / Al / PE | Aseptic liquid packaging |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Engineering Sandwich"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Layered Sandwich**
> Imagine a sandwich where the bread slices are **paper (cellulose fiber)**, and the cheese/butter inside is **plastic (polymer film)**. The bread keeps the sandwich rigid, the plastic keeps it waterproof, and together they form a single inseparable unit. You **cannot pick out only the cheese** without ripping the bread — and the same is true in recycling. This inseparable intimacy is what makes paper-plastic waste the *"hardest nut to crack"* in MSW management.

The **"inseparability"** arises because the layers are joined by:
- **Extrusion lamination** (molten polymer pressed onto paper at ~ **300 °C**)
- **Adhesive lamination** (using PU or EVA-based hot-melt adhesives)
- **Coextrusion coating** (simultaneous multi-layer melt deposition)
