Because the bonding is at the **molecular/polymer-chain level** (interdiffusion and mechanical interlocking at the interface), simple shredding cannot separate them.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme

The 2024 syllabus emphasizes the **environmental footprint of polymers** and mandates students understand wastes that **defeat conventional recycling**. Paper-plastic composites are a flagship example because:

1. They occupy **15–20 %** of municipal packaging waste streams (by volume).
2. They contaminate **paper recycling pulpers** (stickies, plastic residues).
3. They contaminate **plastic recycling extruders** (char-forming cellulose).
4. They require **specialized recycling routes**: hydropulping, pyrolysis, or chemical delamination.
5. They are explicitly mentioned in the **Plastic Waste Management (Amendment) Rules, 2022 (India)** under EPR (Extended Producer Responsibility).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metric to Remember:** A standard **Tetra Pak** aseptic carton of 1 litre contains approximately **75 % paperboard, 20 % polyethylene, and 5 % aluminum** by weight. This single number is enough to predict its recycling challenge and is frequently asked in KTU ESE.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Composition of Paper-Plastic Composite Waste

A typical paper-plastic laminate (PPL) contains the following distinct phases:

1. **Cellulose fiber phase (paper/board)**
   - Provides mechanical strength and printability
   - Grammage: **200–350 g/m²** (food cartons), **80–120 g/m²** (cups)
2. **Polymer film phase (PE, PP, PET, EVA)**
   - Provides moisture barrier, heat-sealability
   - Coating weight: **15–30 g/m²** per layer
3. **Optional metallic phase (Aluminum foil)**
   - Provides oxygen, light, and aroma barrier
   - Thickness: **6–9 µm**; coat weight **~ 30 g/m²**
4. **Adhesive phase (PU, EVA, acrylics)**
   - Coat weight: **2–5 g/m²**
5. **Inks and coatings**
   - Flexographic/rotogravure inks, overprint varnishes

## 2.2 Classification of Paper-Plastic Wastes

Based on KTU 2024 Module 2 framework, paper-plastic wastes are classified along three axes:

### (a) By Layer Count
- **Bilayer** (2 layers): Paper + PE — most common, e.g., paper cups
- **Trilayer** (3 layers): Paper + Al + PE — e.g., foil-lined pouches
- **Multilayer (≥4 layers)**: e.g., Tetra Pak (6 layers)

### (b) By Recycling Behavior
- **Water-disintegratable**: Paper layer disperses in hydropulper
- **Non-disintegratable**: Plastic-dominant or cross-linked laminates

### (c) By Source
- **Post-consumer**: Aseptic cartons, fast-food wrappers
- **Industrial**: Release liners, label backing, prepreg offcuts

## 2.3 The Three Major Recycling Routes (Theory)

### Route 1 — Hydropulping (Wet Process)
Used in paper mills. The laminate is fed into a **hydropulper** with water at **45–60 °C** and pH 7–10. The paper fibers disperse, while plastic/aluminum flakes remain as **rejects (plastics)**.
- **Recovery yield**: 80–90 % cellulose fibers
- **Reject stream**: 10–20 % (plastic + Al) — needs secondary treatment

### Route 2 — Pyrolysis (Dry Thermochemical Process)
The laminate is heated in an oxygen-free environment at **400–700 °C**. The plastic phase cracks into hydrocarbon oils/waxes, the paper phase chars, and aluminum is recovered as solid residue.
- **Pyrolysis oil yield**: 40–65 wt%
- **Char yield**: 15–25 wt%
- **Gas yield**: 15–25 wt%
- **Al recovery**: ~ 95 %

### Route 3 — Chemical Delamination
Uses **solvents, enzymes, or supercritical CO₂** to break the polymer-paper interface bond.
- Lab-scale only; not yet commercial.
- **Supercritical CO₂ conditions**: T = 40–80 °C, P = 100–300 bar.

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind Recycling Difficulty

> [!NOTE]
> **Why can't we just melt it?**
> If you melt a paper-plastic laminate in an extruder, the cellulose fibers:
> - Do NOT melt (cellulose decomposes at **~ 230 °C** before melting)
> - Char and form **black specks** in the polymer
> - Release **water vapor** causing voids and bubbles
> - Catalyze PE degradation at process temperatures (~ 200 °C)
>
> Conversely, in a paper mill pulper, plastic films form **stickies** that contaminate the paper furnish and break the recycling paper machine.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Layer weight fraction (i-th layer) | $w_i = \dfrac{m_i}{\sum_j m_j}$ | dimensionless | Used to find composition |
| Bulk density of laminate | $\rho_{bulk} = \dfrac{\sum m_i}{\sum (m_i / \rho_i)}$ | $\text{g/cm}^3$ | Harmonic mean |
| Cellulose decomposition temp. | $T_{d,\text{cellulose}} \approx 230\,^\circ\text{C}$ | °C | Below PE melt (115–135 °C) |
| PE pyrolysis range | $T_{pyr} = 400 \text{ to } 700\,^\circ\text{C}$ | °C | Standard for mixed waste |
| Al foil coat weight | $cw_{Al} = \rho_{Al} \cdot t_{Al}$ | $\text{g/m}^2$ | t = thickness, ρ = 2.7 g/cm³ |
| Hydropulping yield | $Y_{cell} = \dfrac{m_{fiber,out}}{m_{fiber,in}} \times 100$ | % | Should exceed 80 % |
| Pyrolysis mass balance | $m_{feed} = m_{oil} + m_{char} + m_{gas}$ | g or kg | Closure to ± 2 % |
| Energy recovery (incineration) | $CV_{mix} = \sum x_i \cdot CV_i$ | $\text{MJ/kg}$ | x = mass fraction |
| Reject fraction in pulping | $R_{rej} = \dfrac{m_{plastic,out}}{m_{feed}} \times 100$ | % | Typically 10–20 % |
| EPR compliance ratio | $EPR_{ratio} = \dfrac{m_{recycled}}{m_{placed\,on\,market}}$ | dimensionless | Per PWMR 2022 |

> [!WARNING]
> When writing the **bulk density** equation, students often mistakenly use the arithmetic mean of densities. The correct form is the **harmonic mean** because thicknesses (not masses) add linearly in a laminate stack.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Paper industry**: Decides whether a mill can accept aseptic cartons without fouling the Fourdrinier wire.
- **Plastic industry**: Determines if pyrolysis feedstock is viable; cellulose chars reduce oil quality.
- **Cement kilns**: Used as **alternative fuel (RDF)**; CV calculation above is critical.
- **EPR auditing**: Compliance requires accurate weight-fraction tracking.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Layer Composition Analysis

**Problem (KTU-style 7-mark):**
A 1-litre aseptic juice carton weighs **31.0 g**. Its layer weights are: paperboard = 23.25 g, polyethylene (inner + outer) = 6.20 g, aluminum foil = 1.55 g. Calculate:
(a) Mass fractions of each layer.
(b) Bulk density of the laminate, given individual densities: ρ_paper = 0.80 g/cm³, ρ_PE = 0.92 g/cm³, ρ_Al = 2.70 g/cm³.

### Solution (a) — Mass Fractions

Total mass:
$$m_{total} = 23.25 + 6.20 + 1.55 = 31.00 \text{ g}$$

Mass fraction of paper:
$$w_{paper} = \frac{23.25}{31.00} = 0.750$$

Mass fraction of PE:
$$w_{PE} = \frac{6.20}{31.00} = 0.200$$

Mass fraction of Al:
$$w_{Al} = \frac{1.55}{31.00} = 0.050$$

**Sanity check:** 0.750 + 0.200 + 0.050 = 1.000 ✓

> This matches the **75 / 20 / 5 rule** for Tetra Pak cartons (highlighted earlier).

### Solution (b) — Bulk Density

Each layer has a mass $m_i$ and density $\rho_i$, so the volume of each layer is:
$$V_i = \frac{m_i}{\rho_i}$$

Total volume of the laminate is the **sum of layer volumes** (because layers are stacked in series, not in parallel):

$$V_{total} = \sum_i \frac{m_i}{\rho_i} = \frac{23.25}{0.80} + \frac{6.20}{0.92} + \frac{1.55}{2.70}$$

Computing each term step-by-step:

$$\frac{23.25}{0.80} = 29.0625 \text{ cm}^3$$

$$\frac{6.20}{0.92} = 6.7391 \text{ cm}^3$$

$$\frac{1.55}{2.70} = 0.5741 \text{ cm}^3$$

Summing:
$$V_{total} = 29.0625 + 6.7391 + 0.5741 = 36.3757 \text{ cm}^3$$

Bulk density:
$$\rho_{bulk} = \frac{m_{total}}{V_{total}} = \frac{31.00}{36.3757} = 0.8522 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

> **Result:** $\rho_{bulk} \approx \mathbf{0.852 \text{ g/cm}^3}$ — dominated by the low-density paperboard.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Pyrolysis Mass Balance

**Problem:**
1.00 kg of paper-PE laminate (60 % paper, 40 % PE) is pyrolyzed. The output is **420 g of oil, 230 g of char, and 340 g of non-condensable gas** (plus 10 g of water from cellulose dehydration). Verify the mass balance and determine the closure error.

### Solution

Input:
$$m_{in} = 1000 \text{ g}$$

Output (total):
$$m_{out} = 420 + 230 + 340 + 10 = 1000 \text{ g}$$

Closure error:
$$\Delta = \vert m_{in} - m_{out} \vert = 0 \text{ g}$$

Closure percentage:
$$\text{Closure} = \frac{1000}{1000} \times 100 = 100.0 \,\%$$

> A closure within **± 2 %** is considered excellent in pyrolysis experiments.

Yield on a dry-feed basis (excluding water):
$$Y_{oil} = \frac{420}{990} \times 100 = 42.4 \,\%$$

$$Y_{char} = \frac{230}{990} \times 100 = 23.2 \,\%$$

$$Y_{gas} = \frac{340}{990} \times 100 = 34.3 \,\%$$

Note: Cellulose contributes predominantly to **char** (due to high oxygen content), while PE contributes to **oil + gas** (due to its hydrocarbon backbone).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Calorific Value for Co-incineration

**Problem (KTU-style 7-mark):**
Calculate the calorific value (CV) of a paper-PE laminate having 70 % paper (CV = 16.5 MJ/kg) and 30 % PE (CV = 46.0 MJ/kg). If 1.2 tonnes of this waste is co-fired in a cement kiln with 30 % thermal efficiency, how much useful heat (in MJ) is delivered?

### Solution

Mass-fraction weighted CV:
$$CV_{mix} = \sum x_i \cdot CV_i = (0.70 \times 16.5) + (0.30 \times 46.0)$$

$$CV_{mix} = 11.55 + 13.80 = 25.35 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

Total chemical energy in 1.2 tonnes:
$$E_{in} = 1200 \text{ kg} \times 25.35 \text{ MJ/kg} = 30420 \text{ MJ}$$

Useful heat delivered at 30 % efficiency:
$$E_{useful} = 0.30 \times 30420 = 9126 \text{ MJ}$$

$$\boxed{E_{useful} = 9126 \text{ MJ} \approx 9.13 \text{ GJ}}$$

> **Engineering note:** A typical Indian household uses ~ 3 GJ/year for cooking. So 1.2 tonnes of this RDF (Refuse-Derived Fuel) displaces ~ 3 households' annual energy.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process Selection

| Process | Best Feed Composition | Output | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hydropulping** | ≥ 60 % paper, no Al | Clean cellulose pulp | Plastic reject stream |
| **Pyrolysis** | Any ratio, plastic ≥ 30 % | Fuel oil + char | High CAPEX |
| **Incineration (RDF)** | CV > 15 MJ/kg | Heat + ash | CO₂ emissions |
| **Chemical delamination** | Lab-scale | Pure materials | Not commercial |
| **Landfilling** | Last resort | None | Environmental harm |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**Q: List three examples of paper-plastic composite waste and state one recycling difficulty for each.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Tetra Pak aseptic carton** (Milk, juice) — contains an aluminum foil layer that cannot be pulped, and PE forms stickies in paper mills.
2. **PE-coated paper cup** (Hot beverages) — thin PE film (15 µm) fouls hydropulpers and is difficult to delaminate.
3. **Vinyl wallpaper** (Interior décor) — PVC layer releases HCl during incineration, requiring scrubbers.

> **Valuation key:** A complete answer requires **3 examples + 3 distinct difficulties**. Half-marks are awarded if difficulties are repeated.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]`
**Q: Why is the cellulose decomposition temperature critical in pyrolysis of paper-plastic laminates?**

**Model Answer:**
Cellulose decomposes at approximately **230 °C**, which is **above the melting point of PE (~ 115–135 °C)** but **below typical pyrolysis temperatures (400–700 °C)**. This means:
- During plastic pyrolysis, cellulose **chars** rather than melts, producing solid char.
- The char acts as an **in-situ catalyst** that cracks plastic vapors into lighter gases.
- It reduces overall **oil yield** and increases **gas yield** compared to pure plastic pyrolysis.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe with a neat diagram the **layered structure of an aseptic carton (Tetra Pak)**. Mention the function of each layer.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A multilayer laminate has the following composition: paper = 70 %, PE = 25 %, Al = 5 % by mass. Calculate the bulk density of the laminate, given: ρ_paper = 0.78 g/cm³, ρ_PE = 0.95 g/cm³, ρ_Al = 2.70 g/cm³.

---

#### Model Solution (a) — Layered Structure

A Tetra Pak aseptic carton has **six layers** (top to bottom, from outside to inside):

1. **Polyethylene (outer)** — 12 g/m²
   *Function:* Moisture barrier, printability surface
2. **Paperboard** — 220 g/m²
   *Function:* Mechanical rigidity, bulk, print substrate
3. **Polyethylene (tie layer)** — 12 g/m²
   *Function:* Adheres Al to paperboard
4. **Aluminum foil** — 30 g/m² (≈ 6–9 µm)
   *Function:* Oxygen, light, and aroma barrier
5. **Polyethylene (tie layer)** — 12 g/m²
   *Function:* Adheres food-contact PE to Al
6. **Polyethylene (food contact)** — 18 g/m²
   *Function:* Heat-seal layer, food safety

> **Mark distribution:** 1 mark for naming each layer, 1 mark for function, 1 mark for neat diagram. Total 7 marks.

---

#### Model Solution (b) — Bulk Density Calculation

Assume 100 g of laminate (so masses are m_paper = 70 g, m_PE = 25 g, m_Al = 5 g).

Volume of each layer:
$$V_{paper} = \frac{70}{0.78} = 89.7436 \text{ cm}^3$$

$$V_{PE} = \frac{25}{0.95} = 26.3158 \text{ cm}^3$$

$$V_{Al} = \frac{5}{2.70} = 1.8519 \text{ cm}^3$$

Total volume:
$$V_{total} = 89.7436 + 26.3158 + 1.8519 = 117.9113 \text{ cm}^3$$

Bulk density:
$$\rho_{bulk} = \frac{100}{117.9113} = 0.8481 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

$$\boxed{\rho_{bulk} \approx 0.848 \text{ g/cm}^3}$$

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **three major recycling routes** for paper-plastic composite waste. State one advantage and one limitation of each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** 800 kg of a paper-PE laminate (65 % paper, 35 % PE) is fed to a pyrolysis reactor. The yields are: oil = 38 %, char = 26 %, gas = 33 %, water = 3 %. Calculate the mass of each product. If the pyrolysis oil has a CV of 38 MJ/kg, find the total chemical energy stored in the oil.

---

#### Model Solution (a) — Three Recycling Routes

| Route | Principle | Advantage | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hydropulping** | Paper fibers dispersed in water; plastic/Al rejected | Recovers high-quality cellulose | Plastic reject stream needs further treatment |
| **Pyrolysis** | Thermal cracking in absence of O₂ at 400–700 °C | Recovers fuel oil, char, gas, Al | High capital and operating cost |
| **RDF / Co-incineration** | Combustion in cement kilns or WtE plants | Simple, energy recovery | CO₂ emissions, no material recovery |

---

#### Model Solution (b) — Pyrolysis Mass & Energy

Total feed:
$$m_{feed} = 800 \text{ kg}$$

Mass of each product:
$$m_{oil} = 0.38 \times 800 = 304 \text{ kg}$$

$$m_{char} = 0.26 \times 800 = 208 \text{ kg}$$

$$m_{gas} = 0.33 \times 800 = 264 \text{ kg}$$

$$m_{water} = 0.03 \times 800 = 24 \text{ kg}$$

**Mass balance check:**
$$304 + 208 + 264 + 24 = 800 \text{ kg} \checkmark$$

**Energy stored in oil:**
$$E_{oil} = m_{oil} \times CV_{oil} = 304 \text{ kg} \times 38 \text{ MJ/kg} = 11552 \text{ MJ}$$

$$\boxed{E_{oil} = 11552 \text{ MJ} \approx 11.55 \text{ GJ}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in This Topic:**
> 1. **Wrong density formula** — Using arithmetic mean $\rho_{avg} = \sum x_i \rho_i$ instead of harmonic-mean-based bulk density. This is a **3-mark killer**.
> 2. **Ignoring the Al layer** — In Tetra Pak problems, omitting the aluminum layer's contribution leads to incorrect mass fractions and wrong CV calculations.
> 3. **Confusing melt vs. decomposition** — Saying "cellulose melts at 230 °C" — cellulose does NOT melt, it **decomposes/chars**.
> 4. **Forgetting closure check** — In pyrolysis, examiners deduct 1 mark if mass balance closure is not shown.
> 5. **Mixing up EoL hierarchy** — KTU expects the order: **Reduce → Reuse → Recycle → Energy Recovery → Landfill**. Listing routes in the wrong order loses 1 mark in descriptive questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

- Paper-plastic composites are **multi-layer laminated wastes** combining cellulose with PE/PP/PET (and often Al).
- **Standard Tetra Pak composition** is **75 % paper, 20 % PE, 5 % Al** by mass.
- **Bulk density** must be computed as **harmonic mean** (volume additivity), not arithmetic.
- **Cellulose decomposes at ~ 230 °C** (does not melt) — critical for pyrolysis and extrusion behavior.
- **Three recycling routes**: Hydropulping (paper-dominant), Pyrolysis (plastic-dominant), RDF co-incineration (energy recovery).
- **Pyrolysis yields** typically: Oil 40–65 %, Char 15–25 %, Gas 15–25 %, Water 2–5 %.
- **CV mix rule**: $CV_{mix} = \sum x_i \cdot CV_i$ (mass-fraction weighted).
- **EPR (Extended Producer Responsibility)** under Indian PWMR 2022 covers these multi-layer packaging wastes.
- **Mass balance closure** must be within ± 2 % for valid pyrolysis data.
- **EoL hierarchy** order: Reduce → Reuse → Recycle → Energy Recovery → Landfill.
- **Stickies problem**: PE fragments foul paper machines; one of the top reasons PPL is rejected by recyclers.
- **Al foil benefit in pyrolysis**: Aluminum remains as solid residue, recoverable as a value-added by-product.
- **HCl risk**: PVC-paper laminates (e.g., vinyl wallpaper) release HCl above 250 °C; needs scrubbers during incineration.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrolytic Treatment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT632 - POLYMERS & ENVIRONMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hydrolytic Treatment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hydrolytic Treatment** is a chemical depolymerization process in which polymer chains are cleaved by the action of water (H₂O), typically catalyzed by acids, alkalis, or enzymes, to regenerate their constituent monomers or oligomers. In the context of polymer waste management, it is classified as one of the principal **tertiary (chemical) recycling** routes, exploiting the reversible nature of condensation polymerization equilibrium.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, hydrolytic treatment is specifically studied for **heterochain (condensation) polymers** whose backbones contain hydrolyzable linkages such as ester (–COO–), amide (–CONH–), urethane (–NHCOO–), and carbonate (–OCOO–) groups. The general stoichiometric representation is:

$$[–A–B–]_{n} + n \, H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{catalyst}} n \, A\text{–OH} + n \, B\text{–H}$$

where **A–B** represents the hydrolyzable bond and the products are typically the original difunctional monomers used in synthesis (e.g., terephthalic acid + ethylene glycol from PET).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Hydrolytic treatment is the *most industrially mature* depolymerization route for PET (polyethylene terephthalate) bottles and polyester textile waste. It is therefore a guaranteed question topic in Part A or Part B of the University Examination.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Molecular Scissors" View

Imagine a long **paper-clip chain** where each clip is connected to the next by a tiny dab of glue. Now imagine throwing this chain into a warm, slightly acidic water bath. Over time, the water molecules act like patient, microscopic scissors — each scissor snips one glue joint at a time until every clip is separated individually.

In hydrolytic treatment:
- The **paper-clip chain** = the polymer macromolecule.
- The **glue joints** = ester / amide / urethane linkages.
- The **warm acidic water** = the hydrolyzing medium (H⁺ or OH⁻ catalyst + temperature).
- The **individual paper clips** = recovered monomers (e.g., terephthalic acid, ethylene glycol, caprolactam, bisphenol-A).

The "patience" of the scissors (i.e., the **rate**) depends on three knobs the engineer can turn:
1. **Temperature** (higher T → faster scissor movement).
2. **pH / Catalyst type** (acid or base changes the scissor sharpness).
3. **Water-to-polymer ratio** (more water dilutes products and pushes equilibrium forward via Le Chatelier's principle).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** Industrial hydrolytic depolymerization of PET is commonly performed at temperatures between **200 °C and 250 °C**, pressures of **1.0 – 4.0 MPa**, and reaction times of **1 – 5 hours**, yielding >95% monomer recovery.

## 1.3 Why Hydrolytic Treatment Matters in Environmental Engineering

Polymer waste — particularly packaging, textiles, and engineering plastics — represents a massive environmental burden. Hydrolytic treatment addresses three critical engineering concerns simultaneously:

- **Resource Recovery:** Converts post-consumer polymer waste back into virgin-grade monomers, enabling a *closed-loop* material economy (circular economy principle).
- **Carbon Footprint Reduction:** Repolymerization of recovered monomers consumes **30–60% less energy** than synthesizing monomers from petrochemical feedstocks.
- **Microplastic Mitigation:** Complete depolymerization eliminates persistent microplastic residues that mechanical recycling cannot address.

The process is governed by standard thermodynamic and kinetic descriptors, primarily the **rate constant (k)**, **activation energy (Eₐ)**, and **Arrhenius factor (A)**, which we will derive in Section 3.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Hydrolytic Treatment

Hydrolytic treatment is sub-classified based on the **pH of the aqueous medium** and the **operating conditions**. The following hierarchical breakdown is essential for KTU answers:

### 2.1.1 Acid Hydrolysis (Acid-Catalyzed)

- **Catalyst:** Mineral acids — typically H₂SO₄ (sulfuric acid), HNO₃ (nitric acid), or H₃PO₄ (phosphoric acid).
- **Operating pH:** Strongly acidic (pH 0 – 2).
- **Typical Conditions:** 60 – 100 °C, atmospheric pressure for PET; sometimes elevated pressure for higher crystallinity grades.
- **Mechanism:** Protonation of the carbonyl oxygen of the ester linkage, followed by nucleophilic attack of water.
- **Industrial Use:** Recovery of **terephthalic acid (TPA)** from PET scrap.
- **By-products:** Ethylene glycol remains in the aqueous phase.

### 2.1.2 Alkaline Hydrolysis (Saponification)

- **Catalyst:** NaOH or KOH (typically 4 – 20 wt% aqueous solution).
- **Operating pH:** Strongly alkaline (pH 12 – 14).
- **Typical Conditions:** 70 – 100 °C, atmospheric pressure for PET; lower temperature suffices compared to acid route.
- **Mechanism:** Hydroxide (OH⁻) direct nucleophilic attack on the carbonyl carbon.
- **Industrial Use:** Surface etching of PET bottles, recovery of **disodium terephthalate** (which is then acidified to TPA).
- **Advantage:** Faster kinetics than acid hydrolysis for PET at moderate temperatures.

### 2.1.3 Neutral Hydrolysis

- **Catalyst:** Pure water or steam (sometimes with metal-salt catalysts such as zinc acetate).
- **Operating pH:** 6 – 8.
- **Typical Conditions:** 200 – 250 °C, 1 – 4 MPa (requires autoclave or pressure reactor).
- **Mechanism:** Thermal activation without acid/base catalysis; high T drives bond cleavage.
- **Industrial Use:** Recycling of **polyamides (nylon-6)** to caprolactam via pressure hydrolysis.

### 2.1.4 Enzymatic Hydrolysis (Bio-hydrolysis)

- **Catalyst:** Hydrolase enzymes — **PETase**, **MHETase**, lipases, esterases, cutinases.
- **Operating pH:** Mild (6 – 8), temperature 30 – 70 °C.
- **Mechanism:** Enzyme-substrate complex formation at the ester bond; lower activation energy.
- **Industrial Use:** Still in pilot/research scale; ideal for low-T, low-energy future recycling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** Be ready to compare acid vs. alkaline vs. neutral hydrolysis in a comparative table — a very common 7-mark sub-question.

## 2.2 Step-Wise Logic of the Hydrolytic Reaction Mechanism (Acid Catalysis)

The following six-stage reasoning explains *why* and *how* the ester bond is cleaved. This logical flow is what examiners award marks for in descriptive answers.

1. **Protonation:** The carbonyl oxygen of the ester group is protonated by H⁺ from the acid. This activates the carbonyl carbon by making it more electrophilic.
2. **Nucleophilic Attack:** A water molecule attacks the now-electrophilic carbonyl carbon, forming a tetrahedral intermediate.
3. **Proton Transfer:** A proton migrates from the attacking –OH₂⁺ group to the alkoxy oxygen (–OR) of the original ester.
4. **Bond Cleavage (Rate Determining Step — RDS):** The C–OR bond breaks, ejecting an alcohol (R–OH) and leaving a protonated carboxylic acid.
5. **Deprotonation:** The carboxylic acid loses its extra proton to the solvent, regenerating the H⁺ catalyst.
6. **Net Result:** One ester bond + one water molecule → one carboxylic acid + one alcohol. The catalyst is **regenerated** (true catalysis).

> **Why each step matters:**
> - Step 1 *enables* attack by making the carbonyl carbon hungry for electrons.
> - Step 4 *is the bottleneck* — its energy barrier defines the overall activation energy Eₐ.
> - Step 5 *completes the catalytic cycle* — without it, the process would be stoichiometric, not catalytic.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Hydrolytic Treatment Kinetics

The following cheat sheet is *examination-critical*. Each equation appears in past KTU question papers in either derivation or numerical form.

| # | Equation / Parameter | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pseudo-first-order rate law | $-\frac{dC}{dt} = k\,C$ | Polymer concentration decays exponentially | $s^{-1}$ |
| 2 | Integrated rate expression | $\ln\!\left(\frac{C_0}{C}\right) = k\,t$ | Linear plot for first-order kinetics | dimensionless |
| 3 | Degree of hydrolysis (α) | $\alpha = 1 - \frac{C}{C_0}$ | Fraction of bonds broken | dimensionless (0 – 1) |
| 4 | Arrhenius equation | $k = A\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{R\,T}\right)$ | Temperature dependence of k | $s^{-1}$ |
| 5 | Linearized Arrhenius | $\ln k = \ln A - \frac{E_a}{R}\!\cdot\!\frac{1}{T}$ | Used to extract Eₐ from k vs T data | — |
| 6 | Half-life (1st order) | $t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k} = \frac{0.693}{k}$ | Time for 50% conversion | s |
| 7 | Equilibrium constant | $K = \frac{[A\text{–OH}][B\text{–H}]}{[H_2O][A\text{–B}]}$ | Reversibility indicator | dimensionless |
| 8 | Le Chatelier driving force | $\text{Excess }H_2O \Rightarrow \text{forward shift}$ | High water/polymer ratio favors hydrolysis | — |
| 9 | Activation energy (PET, acid) | $E_a \approx 80 - 110$ | From experimental Arrhenius plots | kJ/mol |
| 10 | Activation energy (PET, alkaline) | $E_a \approx 60 - 90$ | Faster kinetics, lower barrier | kJ/mol |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Notation Rule (KTU 2024):** When writing absolute values inside markdown tables, use `\vert` or `\mid` — **never the bare pipe character** `\vert x \vert` as it breaks table rendering.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PET Bottle Recycling:** Treated at **M/s. Indorama Ventures** (largest PET chemical recycler globally) using alkaline hydrolysis to produce virgin-grade TPA.
- **Nylon-6 Carpet Recycling:** The **Aquafill / Evergreen Nylon** process hydrolyzes caprolactam-containing carpet waste at ~300 °C under pressure to recover ε-caprolactam.
- **Polycarbonate (PC) Recycling:** Hydrolysis with NaOH yields **bisphenol-A (BPA)** and disodium carbonate — both feedstocks for new PC.
- **Polyurethane (PUR) Recycling:** Glycolysis-hydrolysis hybrid routes recover polyols.
- **Textile Industry:** Polyester-cotton blend fabrics are depolymerized to recover terephthalic acid, separating the blend chemically rather than mechanically.

These industrial examples demonstrate that hydrolytic treatment is not merely a textbook concept but a **commercial reality** with measurable environmental impact.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Integrated First-Order Rate Expression for Hydrolysis

### Assumption
Polymer hydrolysis in dilute systems follows **pseudo-first-order kinetics** when water is present in large stoichiometric excess, making its concentration effectively constant. This is the most common kinetic model used in KTU textbooks for polymer hydrolysis problems.

### Step 1 — Define the rate law

The rate of disappearance of ester linkages (or polymer concentration C) is proportional to C itself:

$$\frac{dC}{dt} = -k\,C$$

> The negative sign indicates that polymer concentration *decreases* with time. The proportionality constant k is the **rate constant** with units of s⁻¹.

### Step 2 — Separate variables

Move all C-terms to one side and the t-term to the other:

$$\frac{dC}{C} = -k\,dt$$

> This is the standard separation-of-variables technique. It is valid because k is treated as a constant at fixed temperature (isothermal batch reactor).

### Step 3 — Integrate both sides

Integrate from initial concentration $C_0$ at $t = 0$ to concentration $C$ at time $t$:

$$\int_{C_0}^{C} \frac{dC}{C} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

$$\ln C \Big|_{C_0}^{C} = -k\,t$$

$$\ln C - \ln C_0 = -k\,t$$

### Step 4 — Final integrated form

Rearrange the result:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{C}{C_0}\right) = -k\,t$$

$$\boxed{\;\ln\!\left(\frac{C_0}{C}\right) = k\,t\;}$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** A plot of $\ln(C_0 / C)$ versus $t$ should be a *straight line passing through the origin* with slope $k$. If experimental data deviates from linearity, the kinetics are not first-order (often zero-order at very high conversions due to crystallinity effects in PET).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Arrhenius Activation Energy from Dual-Temperature Data

### Problem Setup
Given two rate constants $k_1$ and $k_2$ measured at two absolute temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$, derive the activation energy $E_a$ without needing the pre-exponential factor $A$.

### Step 1 — Write Arrhenius at both temperatures

$$k_1 = A\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{R\,T_1}\right)$$

$$k_2 = A\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{R\,T_2}\right)$$

> Both equations share the *same* pre-exponential factor A (assumed temperature-independent in the classical Arrhenius framework).

### Step 2 — Form the ratio

$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{A\,\exp(-E_a/RT_2)}{A\,\exp(-E_a/RT_1)} = \exp\!\left[-\frac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)\right]$$

> The pre-exponential factors cancel exactly. This is the *key trick* — it removes A without ever knowing its numerical value.

### Step 3 — Take natural logarithm

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = -\frac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

### Step 4 — Solve for Eₐ

Multiply both sides by $-R$ and rearrange:

$$E_a = R \cdot \frac{\ln(k_2/k_1)}{\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}}$$

$$\boxed{\;E_a = \frac{R\,\ln(k_2/k_1)}{\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}}\;}$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** This "two-point Arrhenius equation" is the workhorse formula in KTU numerical problems. A student who memorizes only this one equation can solve 80% of past numerical questions on hydrolysis kinetics.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU-Style Problem)

### Problem Statement
PET hydrolysis is carried out under acidic conditions. The rate constants measured are:
- $k_1 = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{min}^{-1}$ at $T_1 = 60\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$
- $k_2 = 8.9 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{min}^{-1}$ at $T_2 = 80\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$

The initial polymer concentration is $C_0 = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{mol/L}$. Calculate:
**(a)** The activation energy $E_a$ in kJ/mol.
**(b)** The polymer concentration after 60 minutes at 60 °C.
**(c)** The half-life $t_{1/2}$ at 60 °C.

Use $R = 8.314\ \text{J}\cdot\text{mol}^{-1}\cdot\text{K}^{-1}$.

### Part (a) — Activation Energy

Convert temperatures to Kelvin:
$$T_1 = 60 + 273.15 = 333.15\ \text{K}$$
$$T_2 = 80 + 273.15 = 353.15\ \text{K}$$

Apply the two-point Arrhenius equation:

$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{8.9 \times 10^{-4}}{2.5 \times 10^{-4}} = 3.56$$

$$\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{333.15} - \frac{1}{353.15}$$

Compute each reciprocal separately:

$$\frac{1}{333.15} = 3.0017 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{K}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{1}{353.15} = 2.8317 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{K}^{-1}$$

Subtract:

$$3.0017 \times 10^{-3} - 2.8317 \times 10^{-3} = 1.7000 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{K}^{-1}$$

Compute $\ln(k_2/k_1)$:

$$\ln(3.56) = 1.2693$$

Substitute into the Arrhenius expression:

$$E_a = \frac{(8.314)(1.2693)}{1.7000 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$E_a = \frac{10.5535}{1.7000 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$E_a = 62\,080\ \text{J/mol}$$

$$\boxed{\;E_a \approx 62.1\ \text{kJ/mol}\;}$$

> This value lies within the expected range for acid-catalyzed PET hydrolysis (**80 – 110 kJ/mol** is reported for harsher systems; the lower value here indicates the polymer grade is low-crystallinity, amorphous PET).

### Part (b) — Concentration after 60 minutes at 60 °C

Apply the integrated first-order expression:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{C_0}{C}\right) = k_1\,t$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{C}\right) = (2.5 \times 10^{-4})(60)$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{C}\right) = 1.5 \times 10^{-2}$$

Exponentiate both sides:

$$\frac{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{C} = \exp(0.015) = 1.01511$$

$$C = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{1.01511}$$

$$\boxed{\;C \approx 9.85 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{mol/L}\;}$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** After 1 hour, only ~1.5% of the ester bonds are cleaved at 60 °C. Industrial reactors therefore operate at **higher temperatures** to achieve economically viable conversion rates (>95% in 1 – 5 hours).

### Part (c) — Half-life at 60 °C

For first-order kinetics:

$$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k_1} = \frac{0.693}{2.5 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{\;t_{1/2} = 2\,772\ \text{min} \approx 46.2\ \text{hours}\;}$$

> **Insight:** At 60 °C, it takes nearly **two days** to hydrolyze half the ester bonds — confirming why industrial processes must operate at **>200 °C** or use strong alkaline catalysts to bring $t_{1/2}$ into the minute-to-hour range.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Choice of Hydrolysis Route

| Parameter | Acid Hydrolysis | Alkaline Hydrolysis | Neutral (Steam) Hydrolysis | Enzymatic Hydrolysis |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Catalyst** | H₂SO₄ / HNO₃ | NaOH / KOH | H₂O (with Zn-acetate) | PETase / Lipase |
| **Temperature (°C)** | 60 – 100 | 70 – 100 | 200 – 250 | 30 – 70 |
| **Pressure** | Atmospheric | Atmospheric | 1 – 4 MPa | Atmospheric |
| **Eₐ (kJ/mol)** | 80 – 110 | 60 – 90 | 100 – 140 | 40 – 70 |
| **Reaction Time** | 1 – 5 h | 0.5 – 3 h | 1 – 4 h | 6 – 24 h |
| **Monomer Recovery (%)** | >95 | >95 | >90 | 50 – 90 (lab scale) |
| **Industrial Maturity** | High | High | Medium | Pilot |
| **Environmental Cost** | Acid waste | Alkaline waste | Low | Very Low |

> **Analytical conclusion:** For KTU answers, the *optimal industrial choice* for PET is **alkaline hydrolysis** because it balances fast kinetics, mild temperatures, and high monomer recovery. For future sustainability, **enzymatic hydrolysis** is the most promising but not yet fully industrial.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Industrial Hydrolytic Treatment Plant

The following Mermaid diagram maps the full functional architecture of a continuous hydrolytic depolymerization plant for PET (alkaline route):

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feedstock: Post-Consumer PET Flakes] --> B[Sorting and Washing Unit]
    B --> C[Shredder and Size Reduction]
    C --> D[Pre-Heater: 80 °C]
    D --> E[Reactor Feed Pump]
    E --> F[Hydrolysis Reactor: NaOH 10 wt percent, 90 °C, 3 h]
    F --> G[Filter: Solid Crude TPA Salt]
    G --> H[Acidification Tank: H2SO4 Addition]
    H --> I[Pure TPA Precipitate]
    I --> J[Drying and Purification]
    J --> K[Repolymerization to Virgin PET]
    F --> L[Distillation Column: EG Recovery]
    L --> M[Pure Ethylene Glycol Product]
    K --> N[Closed-Loop Circular Output]
    M --> N
    O[Catalyst Recovery Loop] --> F
    G --> O
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **horizontal axis** represents material flow (feedstock → product).
- **Vertical branch (G → O → F)** represents the catalyst / solvent recycle loop — essential for process economics and waste minimization.
- The terminal node **N (Closed-Loop Circular Output)** represents the goal of the circular polymer economy.

---

## 4.2 Mechanism Flow Diagram — Acid Hydrolysis of an Ester Linkage

The following diagram captures the six-step catalytic cycle described in Section 2.2. Each oval is a stable intermediate; each arrow is an elementary step.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Protonation of Carbonyl Oxygen] --> S2[Step 2: Water Nucleophilic Attack]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Tetrahedral Intermediate Formation]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Proton Transfer to Leaving Group]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: C-OR Bond Cleavage RDS]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Deprotonation and H+ Regeneration]
    S6 --> P1[Product 1: Carboxylic Acid RCOOH]
    S6 --> P2[Product 2: Alcohol ROH]
    S6 --> CAT[H plus Catalyst Regenerated]
```

> **Examiner's note:** Labeling Step 5 as **RDS (rate-determining step)** is a 2-mark bonus in descriptive answers because it explicitly identifies the kinetic bottleneck.

---

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Choosing the Correct Hydrolysis Route

The following block-level architecture helps students answer comparative-process questions:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Polymer Type?} --> Q2A[PET / Polyester]
    Q1 --> Q2B[Polyamide Nylon-6]
    Q1 --> Q2C[Polycarbonate PC]
    Q1 --> Q2D[Polyurethane PUR]
    Q2A --> R1A{Desired T?}
    R1A --> LT[Below 100 C]
    R1A --> HT[Above 100 C]
    LT --> A1[Alkaline Hydrolysis: NaOH]
    HT --> A2[Neutral Steam Hydrolysis]
    Q2B --> A3[Neutral Pressure Hydrolysis: Caprolactam Recovery]
    Q2C --> A4[Alkaline Hydrolysis: BPA Recovery]
    Q2D --> A5[Glycolysis-Hydrolysis Hybrid Route]
```

**Pedagogical purpose:** This diagram transforms abstract process selection logic into a navigable visual, allowing students to defend a choice in 14-mark descriptive questions ("*Justify why you would select route A over route B for PET recycling*").

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

### Question A1
**Define hydrolytic treatment of polymers. Mention two industrially significant polymers that undergo this process.**

**Model Answer:**
Hydrolytic treatment is a chemical depolymerization process in which polymer chains containing hydrolyzable linkages (ester, amide, urethane) are cleaved by water, typically under acidic, alkaline, or neutral conditions, to regenerate monomers.

Two industrially significant polymers are:
1. **Polyethylene terephthalate (PET)** — hydrolyzed to recover terephthalic acid (TPA) and ethylene glycol (EG).
2. **Polyamide-6 (Nylon-6)** — hydrolyzed to recover ε-caprolactam, the parent monomer.

> **Marks distribution:** Definition (2 marks) + Polymer examples (1 mark).

---

### Question A2
**What is the role of a catalyst in acid hydrolysis of polyesters? Mention one example catalyst.**

**Model Answer:**
In acid hydrolysis of polyesters, the catalyst (e.g., H₂SO₄) donates a proton to the carbonyl oxygen of the ester linkage, increasing its electrophilicity and thereby accelerating nucleophilic attack by water. The catalyst is regenerated at the end of the cycle and is therefore required only in small (catalytic) amounts.

Example: **Concentrated sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄)**.

> **Marks distribution:** Mechanistic role (2 marks) + Example (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2, Question Pattern]**

### Question A (14 Marks) — *Hydrolytic Treatment: Principles and PET Recycling*

**(a)** Explain with a stepwise mechanism the acid-catalyzed hydrolysis of an ester linkage in PET. Highlight the rate-determining step and the role of the catalyst. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the integrated first-order rate expression for polymer hydrolysis. Given that PET hydrolysis at 80 °C yields a rate constant $k = 8.9 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{min}^{-1}$ and at 60 °C yields $k = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{min}^{-1}$, calculate the activation energy $E_a$ in kJ/mol. Use $R = 8.314\ \text{J}\cdot\text{mol}^{-1}\cdot\text{K}^{-1}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The acid-catalyzed hydrolysis of an ester linkage proceeds through the following six elementary steps:

**Step 1 — Protonation:** The carbonyl oxygen of the ester group accepts a proton from H₂SO₄, forming a protonated ester. This activates the carbonyl carbon toward nucleophilic attack.

**Step 2 — Nucleophilic Attack:** A water molecule attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon.

**Step 3 — Tetrahedral Intermediate:** The attack yields a tetrahedral intermediate bearing –OH and –OH₂⁺ groups.

**Step 4 — Proton Transfer:** A proton shifts from –OH₂⁺ to the –OR alkoxy oxygen.

**Step 5 — C–O Bond Cleavage (RDS):** The C–OR bond breaks, releasing the alcohol (R–OH). This is the **rate-determining step (RDS)** because it has the highest activation energy among all elementary steps.

**Step 6 — Deprotonation:** The protonated carboxylic acid loses a proton to solvent, regenerating H⁺ and closing the catalytic cycle.

**Role of catalyst:** H⁺ lowers the activation energy of the RDS by stabilizing the developing negative charge on the leaving alkoxy oxygen through prior protonation. Without catalyst, RDS would require much higher temperatures.

> **Marks split:** Mechanism steps (4 marks) + Identification of RDS (1 mark) + Catalyst role (2 marks).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

The integrated first-order expression is derived as follows (see Section 3.1 of these notes for full derivation):

$$\boxed{\;\ln\!\left(\frac{C_0}{C}\right) = k\,t\;}$$

For the numerical part, apply the two-point Arrhenius equation:

$$E_a = \frac{R\,\ln(k_2/k_1)}{\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}}$$

**Convert temperatures:**
$$T_1 = 60 + 273.15 = 333.15\ \text{K}$$
$$T_2 = 80 + 273.15 = 353.15\ \text{K}$$

**Compute ratio:**
$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{8.9 \times 10^{-4}}{2.5 \times 10^{-4}} = 3.56$$

**Compute reciprocal temperature difference:**
$$\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} = (3.0017 - 2.8317) \times 10^{-3} = 1.700 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{K}^{-1}$$

**Compute natural log:**
$$\ln(3.56) = 1.2693$$

**Substitute and evaluate:**
$$E_a = \frac{(8.314)(1.2693)}{1.700 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{10.5535}{1.700 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{\;E_a = 62\,080\ \text{J/mol} \approx 62.1\ \text{kJ/mol}\;}$$

> **Marks split:** Derivation of integrated law (3 marks) + Setup of Arrhenius equation (2 marks) + Numerical computation (2 marks).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice — Hydrolysis of Polyamides & Comparative Analysis*

**(a)** Describe the neutral (pressure) hydrolysis of polyamide-6 (nylon-6). What monomers are recovered, and what operating conditions are required? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare acid hydrolysis, alkaline hydrolysis, and neutral hydrolysis of PET in tabular form across **six parameters**: catalyst, temperature, pressure, Eₐ range, reaction time, and environmental impact. State, with justification, which route you would recommend for industrial PET bottle recycling. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Polyamide-6 (Nylon-6) Neutral Hydrolysis:**

Polyamide-6 contains repeating amide linkages (–CONH–) along its backbone. Under neutral hydrolysis, these bonds are cleaved by water at elevated temperature and pressure to recover ε-caprolactam, the parent monomer.

**Reaction:**
$$[–NH(CH_2)_5CO–]_{n} + n\,H_2O \xrightarrow{\Delta,\,P} n\,\varepsilon\text{-caprolactam}$$

**Operating Conditions:**
- **Temperature:** 280 – 320 °C
- **Pressure:** 1.5 – 3.0 MPa (superheated steam / water)
- **Catalyst:** None required (or small amount of Zn-acetate / NaH₂PO₄ to accelerate)
- **Time:** 30 – 90 minutes
- **Yield:** >90% caprolactam recovery

**Industrial example:** The **Aquafill process** (developed by AlliedSignal, now Honeywell) and **Evergreen Nylon Recycling** use this route to recycle nylon-6 carpet waste.

**Why it works:** High temperature provides the activation energy needed to break the strong amide bond. Pressure prevents water from vaporizing, ensuring liquid-phase contact. The absence of acid/base avoids salt by-products and simplifies monomer purification.

> **Marks split:** Reaction scheme (2 marks) + Conditions (3 marks) + Industrial example + reasoning (2 marks).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Comparative Table:**

| Parameter | Acid Hydrolysis | Alkaline Hydrolysis | Neutral Hydrolysis |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Catalyst** | H₂SO₄ / HNO₃ | NaOH / KOH | None (or Zn-acetate) |
| **Temperature** | 60 – 100 °C | 70 – 100 °C | 200 – 250 °C |
| **Pressure** | Atmospheric | Atmospheric | 1 – 4 MPa |
| **Eₐ (kJ/mol)** | 80 – 110 | 60 – 90 | 100 – 140 |
| **Reaction Time** | 1 – 5 h | 0.5 – 3 h | 1 – 4 h |
| **Environmental Impact** | Acid waste stream | Alkaline waste stream | Minimal waste |

**Recommendation: Alkaline Hydrolysis** for industrial PET bottle recycling.

**Justification (any 3 valid reasons for full marks):**
1. **Lowest activation energy** (60 – 90 kJ/mol) means fastest kinetics at mild temperature.
2. **Atmospheric pressure** operation → no expensive pressure vessels required → lower capital cost.
3. **Shorter reaction times** (0.5 – 3 h) → higher throughput and lower energy per kg.
4. **High monomer recovery** (>95%) meets industrial purity standards.
5. Although it generates an alkaline waste stream, modern plants recover and recycle NaOH, making it economically and environmentally attractive.

> **Marks split:** Table with 6 parameters × 0.5 mark each = 3 marks. Recommendation (1 mark) + Justification with ≥3 reasons (3 marks).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 places students lose marks in hydrolytic-treatment questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert °C to K** in the Arrhenius equation — instant 1–2 mark penalty. Always write $T(\text{K}) = T(^{\circ}\text{C}) + 273.15$.
> 2. **Misidentifying the rate-determining step** as Step 1 (protonation) instead of Step 5 (C–O cleavage). Step 5 has the highest Eₐ barrier.
> 3. **Confusing hydrolysis with glycolysis / methanolysis.** Hydrolysis uses *water*; glycolysis uses *ethylene glycol*; methanolysis uses *methanol*. KTU examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 4. **Writing reaction schemes without balancing stoichiometry.** Always show water molecules and products in correct molar ratios.
> 5. **Omitting units in numerical answers.** Every numerical answer must state $E_a$ in kJ/mol, $k$ in min⁻¹ or s⁻¹, $t$ in minutes or hours.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

The following rapid-revision checklist distills every high-yield fact in this module. Read it once before each exam.

- **Definition (must memorize):** Hydrolytic treatment = chemical depolymerization of heterochain polymers using water (acid / base / neutral / enzyme catalyzed) to recover monomers.
- **Key polymers:** PET, Nylon-6, Polycarbonate, Polyurethane.
- **Mechanism (acid route) — 6 steps:** Protonation → Water attack → Tetrahedral intermediate → Proton transfer → C–O cleavage (RDS) → Deprotonation.
- **First-order integrated rate law:** $\ln(C_0/C) = k\,t$.
- **Half-life formula:** $t_{1/2} = 0.693 / k$.
- **Arrhenius two-point formula:** $E_a = R\,\ln(k_2/k_1) / (1/T_1 - 1/T_2)$.
- **Always convert °C → K** before substituting into Arrhenius.
- **Acid hydrolysis Eₐ:** 80 – 110 kJ/mol; **Alkaline Eₐ:** 60 – 90 kJ/mol; **Neutral Eₐ:** 100 – 140 kJ/mol.
- **Industrial recommendation for PET:** Alkaline hydrolysis (lowest Eₐ, atmospheric pressure, fastest kinetics).
- **Industrial recommendation for Nylon-6:** Neutral pressure hydrolysis (280 – 320 °C, 1.5 – 3.0 MPa).
- **Do NOT confuse** hydrolysis (H₂O) with glycolysis (EG) or methanolysis (MeOH).
- **Standard conditions for industrial PET hydrolysis:** 90 – 250 °C, 0.1 – 4.0 MPa, 0.5 – 5 hours, >95% monomer recovery.
- **Le Chatelier's principle** drives hydrolysis forward when water is in large stoichiometric excess.
- **Environmental benefit:** Reduces CO₂ footprint by 30 – 60% vs. virgin monomer synthesis; eliminates microplastics through complete depolymerization.

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Mixed Plastics Waste (MPW)** is a heterogeneous polymeric waste stream composed of two or more *immiscible* thermoplastic and/or thermoset polymers, often contaminated with non-polymer additives (fillers, pigments, stabilizers), food residues, paper labels, metals, and other organic/inorganic impurities. In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEPOT632), the *processing* of MPW refers to the integrated chain of **collection → segregation → size reduction → washing → drying → separation/identification → conversion into secondary raw materials or energy** through mechanical, chemical, or thermal routes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEPOT632 – Module 3):**
> "Processing of mixed plastics waste" specifically addresses the engineering technologies used to recover value from commingled post-consumer plastic streams — the *real-world* waste that enters Material Recovery Facilities (MRFs).

The formal processing hierarchy, in decreasing order of polymer-chain preservation, is:

1. **Primary Recycling** — re-extrusion of in-plant scrap (closed loop).
2. **Secondary (Mechanical) Recycling** — physical reprocessing of mixed waste.
3. **Tertiary (Chemical) Recycling** — depolymerization back to monomers or feedstocks.
4. **Quaternary (Energy) Recovery** — incineration with heat capture.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Salad Bowl" Perspective

Imagine a **salad bowl** containing lettuce (PE), tomatoes (PET), cucumber (PP), olives (PVC), and chickpeas (PS) all tossed together with a vinaigrette (oil, food waste, labels).

- You **cannot simply re-heat the salad** and expect it to taste the same — the ingredients separate, degrade, and ruin the texture. That is exactly what happens when you melt mixed plastics.
- You have three options:
  1. **Sort and re-use each ingredient** (mechanical recycling with sorting).
  2. **Distill the vinaigrette into pure oil and water** (chemical recycling → monomers).
  3. **Burn the entire salad for cooking fuel** (energy recovery).

This is the engineering decision a KTU polymer technologist must make when designing a processing line for MPW.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in MPW Processing

The following standardized metrics, defined by **ISO 15270:2008** and **ASTM D7209**, govern MPW processing:

- **MFI (Melt Flow Index):** measured in **g/10 min** at prescribed temperature/load.
- **Density separation threshold:** **0.90 – 1.00 g/cm³** (water), **1.00 – 1.20 g/cm³** (saturated brine).
- **Pyrolysis temperature window:** **400 – 700 °C**.
- **Compatibilizer dosage range:** **2 – 8 phr** (parts per hundred resin).
- **Calorific value of mixed plastics:** **30 – 46 MJ/kg** (≈ 7,200 – 11,000 kcal/kg).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Takeaway:** Mixed plastics are *not a single material* — they are a *composite waste ore* whose individual "minerals" (PE, PP, PET, PS, PVC) must be unlocked through one of four value-recovery pathways.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Processing Pathways — Structured Logic

### Pathway 1 — Mechanical Recycling (Secondary)

This is the *most preferred* and *most economically viable* route when streams can be adequately sorted.

**Step-wise Operational Logic:**

- **Step A — Collection & Baling:** Commingled waste is compacted at MRFs into **bales of 200 – 500 kg** at pressures of **15 – 30 MPa**.
- **Step B — Manual/Auto Sorting:** Near-Infrared (NIR) spectroscopy identifies polymers by their characteristic **C–H, C=O, C–O–C absorption bands** (PE at 1735 nm, PP at 1735 nm + 1200 nm, PET at 1410 nm, PS at 1204 nm, PVC at 1200 nm + 1400 nm).
- **Step C — Granulation:** Shredding to flakes of **4 – 10 mm** using rotary or shredder granulators.
- **Step D — Washing:** Hot caustic wash (**2 – 4 % NaOH at 60 – 80 °C**) removes organics, labels, adhesives.
- **Step E — Density Separation (Sink–Float):** Exploits the polymer density ladder: PE (0.92), PP (0.90), PS (1.05), PET (1.38), PVC (1.40). Multi-stage tanks with water, then NaCl brine (ρ = 1.20 g/cm³), then ZnCl₂ (ρ = 1.60 g/cm³) achieve separation.
- **Step F — Drying & Extrusion:** Centrifugal + hot-air drying to **moisture < 0.1 %**, then melt compounding in co-rotating twin-screw extruders at **180 – 260 °C**.
- **Step G — Pelletizing & Reuse:** Strand or underwater pelletization.

### Pathway 2 — Compatibilization (Blends with Reactive Coupling)

When *full* sorting is uneconomical, mixed plastics are melt-blended with a **compatibilizer** — a block or graft copolymer that localizes at the polymer–polymer interface and reduces interfacial tension.

**Why It Works — The Thermodynamic Argument:**

A polymer blend is a multi-phase system. The **interfacial tension** $\gamma_{AB}$ between two polymers A and B (e.g., PE and PET) is typically **5 – 15 mN/m**, far higher than oil–water (≈ 30 mN/m in some blends). The **spreading coefficient** $\lambda_{31}$ controls whether a third polymer forms a continuous encapsulating layer:

$$\lambda_{31} = \gamma_{12} - \gamma_{32} - \gamma_{13}$$

Compatibilizers (e.g., **SEBS-g-MA, EVA, EPDM-g-MA, PE-g-MA**) migrate to the interface and reduce $\gamma_{AB}$ by **40 – 70 %**, allowing finer dispersion of the minor phase (droplet size $d$):

$$d = \frac{4 \cdot \gamma_{12} \cdot \phi}{(\dot{\gamma} \cdot \eta_m)}$$

where $\dot{\gamma}$ is shear rate and $\eta_m$ is matrix viscosity. A 5-fold reduction in $\gamma_{12}$ → 5-fold reduction in droplet size → dramatically improved mechanical properties.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Clarification:** Compatibilization does *not* "mix" the polymers at the molecular level — it creates a *mechanically stable micro/nano-emulsion* whose interfacial adhesion prevents phase coalescence during melt processing.

### Pathway 3 — Chemical Recycling (Tertiary)

**A. Hydrolysis/Methanolysis of PET:**
PET + 2 CH₃OH → DMT + EG (Methanolysis, 60 – 80 atm, 180 – 280 °C, Mn(OAc)₂/Zn(OAc)₂ catalyst)
or
PET + 2 H₂O → TPA + EG (Hydrolysis, neutral/acid/base)

**B. Glycolysis of PET:**
PET + 2 EG → BHET (Bis-Hydroxyethyl Terephthalate) at 180 – 240 °C with Zn(OAc)₂ catalyst. BHET can re-polymerize to virgin-grade PET.

**C. Pyrolysis (Thermal Cracking, 400 – 700 °C):**
Mixed polyolefins (PE, PP) crack to a **pyrolysis oil** (C₅ – C₃₀ hydrocarbons) with yield of **70 – 85 wt %** at **500 °C** in fluidized bed reactors. This oil is a drop-in feedstock for steam crackers producing new olefins.

**D. Catalytic Cracking:**
Zeolites (ZSM-5, USY) reduce cracking temperature to **300 – 450 °C** and increase aromatic content for gasoline-range products.

**E. Depolymerization of PS:**
PS → Styrene monomer (90 % yield) at 300 – 400 °C via random chain scission — *the cleanest* depolymerization among commodity plastics.

### Pathway 4 — Energy Recovery (Quaternary)

Mixed plastics — especially those contaminated with PVC (which releases HCl) and food waste — are processed in:

- **Cement kilns** (T > 1450 °C) as alternative fuel (AF) — co-processing.
- **Waste-to-Energy (WtE) plants** with grate furnaces (850 – 1100 °C) and flue-gas cleaning.
- **Pyrolysis + CHP** for combined heat and power.

The **net calorific value (NCV)** determines energy yield:

$$Q_{net} = Q_{gross} - \left[ 2.442 \cdot \left(\frac{9H}{100}\right) \right] \text{ MJ/kg}$$

where $H$ is hydrogen content (%) of the polymer. For PE: $H \approx 14\%$, giving $Q_{net} \approx 43$ MJ/kg.

## 2.2 The Density Ladder — Cornerstone of Sink–Float Separation

| Polymer | Density (g/cm³) | Separation Medium |
|---|---|---|
| PP | 0.90 – 0.91 | Floats on water |
| LDPE | 0.91 – 0.93 | Floats on water |
| HDPE | 0.94 – 0.97 | Floats on water |
| PS | 1.04 – 1.06 | Sinks in water, floats on brine |
| PVC (rigid) | 1.38 – 1.45 | Sinks in 1.20 brine |
| PET | 1.30 – 1.40 | Sinks in 1.20 brine |
| ABS | 1.04 – 1.07 | Sinks in water |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **3-bath cascade** (water → 1.20 brine → 1.40 ZnCl₂) yields up to **95 %** purity for the recovered PE/PP fraction and **90 %** for PET/PVC — the minimum purity threshold for downstream extrusion in commercial plants (IS 14534:1998).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Equation / Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Net Calorific Value | $Q_{net} = Q_{gross} - 2.442 \cdot \frac{9H}{100}$ | $Q$ in MJ/kg, $H$ in wt% | Energy recovery yield |
| Interfacial tension reduction | $\gamma_{eff} = \gamma_{0}(1 - k \cdot \phi_c)$ | $\phi_c$ = compatibilizer vol. frac. | Blend design |
| Droplet size in blend | $d = \dfrac{4 \gamma_{12} \phi}{\dot{\gamma} \cdot \eta_m}$ | $d$ in µm, $\dot{\gamma}$ in s⁻¹ | Dispersion quality |
| Pyrolysis oil yield (kinetic) | $Y = Y_{\infty}\left(1 - e^{-k\tau}\right)$ | $k$ = 0.05 – 0.3 min⁻¹, $\tau$ = residence time | Reactor design |
| Spreading coefficient | $\lambda_{31} = \gamma_{12} - \gamma_{32} - \gamma_{13}$ | All $\gamma$ in mN/m | Encapsulation prediction |
| Hydrolysis conversion | $X = 1 - e^{-k_{h} \cdot t}$ | $k_{h}$ = 0.01 – 0.1 h⁻¹ | PET hydrolysis design |
| Mass balance (sorting line) | $\sum_{i} m_{i,in} = \sum_{i} m_{i,out} + m_{loss}$ | $m$ in kg/h | Material accounting |
| Energy efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{E_{useful}}{E_{input}} \times 100$ | Dimensionless | Plant benchmarking |
| Twin-screw residence time | $\tau \approx \dfrac{L \cdot A_{free}}{Q_v}$ | $L$ = screw length, $A_{free}$ = free area | Extruder design |
| WtE steam yield | $m_{steam} = \dfrac{m_{waste} \cdot NCV}{h_{fg}}$ | $h_{fg}$ = 2.26 MJ/kg | Boiler sizing |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Pyrolysis Oil Yield from Mixed Polyolefins

We model mixed polyolefin (PE + PP) pyrolysis as a **first-order decomposition** in a well-mixed batch reactor.

### Given:
- Feed mass: $m_0 = 100$ kg of mixed PE/PP
- Temperature: $T = 500$ °C $= 773$ K
- Reaction rate constant at 500 °C (from Kissinger/KAS kinetics): $k = 0.18 \text{ min}^{-1}$
- Reactor residence time: $\tau = 30$ min
- Ultimate oil yield (long time): $Y_\infty = 0.80$ (80 wt%)

### Step 1 — First-order kinetic equation

The mass of unconverted polymer $m(t)$ follows:

$$m(t) = m_0 \cdot e^{-k t}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute known values

$$m(30) = 100 \cdot e^{-(0.18)(30)} = 100 \cdot e^{-5.4}$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the exponential

$$e^{-5.4} = 0.0045$$

### Step 4 — Compute unconverted mass

$$m(30) = 100 \times 0.0045 = 0.45 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 5 — Compute mass converted to gas + oil

$$m_{converted} = 100 - 0.45 = 99.55 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 6 — Apply ultimate yield fraction (only 80 % goes to oil; 20 % to non-condensable gas + char)

$$m_{oil} = 0.80 \times 99.55 = 79.64 \text{ kg}$$

$$m_{gas+char} = 0.20 \times 99.55 = 19.91 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 7 — Mass balance closure check

$$m_{unconverted} + m_{oil} + m_{gas+char} = 0.45 + 79.64 + 19.91 = 100.00 \text{ kg} \checkmark$$

### Final Result:

$$\boxed{Y_{oil} = 79.64\% \text{ of feed}}$$

This means **100 kg of mixed PE/PP** yields approximately **80 kg of pyrolysis oil** suitable as a steam-cracker feedstock at 500 °C, 30 min residence time.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Compatibilizer Dosage for HDPE/PET Blend (70/30)

We want to find the **minimum compatibilizer (PE-g-MA) concentration** that reduces HDPE/PET interfacial tension from 12.0 mN/m to 2.5 mN/m.

### Given:
- Initial interfacial tension: $\gamma_0 = 12.0$ mN/m
- Target interfacial tension: $\gamma_{target} = 2.5$ mN/m
- Empirical constant for PE-g-MA: $k = 12$ (dimensionless, system-specific)
- Compatibilizer volume fraction: $\phi_c$ to be found

### Step 1 — Write the reduction equation

$$\gamma_{eff} = \gamma_0 \cdot (1 - k \cdot \phi_c)$$

### Step 2 — Rearrange for $\phi_c$

$$\phi_c = \frac{1 - \gamma_{eff}/\gamma_0}{k}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute numerical values

$$\phi_c = \frac{1 - 2.5/12.0}{12} = \frac{1 - 0.2083}{12} = \frac{0.7917}{12}$$

### Step 4 — Compute

$$\phi_c = 0.0660 = 6.6\%$$

### Step 5 — Convert to phr (parts per hundred resin)

If 100 g of HDPE + 30 g of PET = 130 g total blend, then:

$$\phi_c \text{ in phr} = 6.6 \times \frac{130}{100} = 8.6 \text{ phr}$$

### Final Result:

$$\boxed{\phi_c = 6.6 \text{ vol\%} \;(\approx 8.6 \text{ phr})}$$

This is a *realistic* dosage for industrial HDPE/PET compatibilization — within the typical **5 – 10 phr** range observed in practice.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Processing Route for MPW

| Waste Characteristic | Recommended Route | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| PE + PP only, clean, single-stream | **Mechanical recycling (extrusion)** | Compatible polyolefins; minimal sorting needed |
| PE + PP + PET mixture | **Compatibilization + extrusion** OR **mechanical with sortation** | PET has high melting point; degrades at 260 °C |
| PS-rich stream | **Chemical (depolymerization) → styrene** | PS cracks cleanly to monomer |
| PET-rich stream | **Glycolysis/methanolysis → BHET/DMT** | Monomer recovery gives virgin-grade resin |
| PVC-contaminated (>2 wt%) | **Pyrolysis with HCl scrubbing** OR **WtE** | HCl release corrodes equipment; energy recovery avoids it |
| Heavily contaminated (food, oil) | **Pyrolysis (400 – 700 °C)** | Tolerates organic contamination |
| Mixed plastics + biomass | **Co-pyrolysis / co-gasification** | Synergistic H/C ratio adjustment |
| Foamed PS (low density) | **Dissolution + precipitation** OR **chemical** | Density separation fails (PS foam floats with PE) |
| Multi-layer packaging (PE/PET/Al) | **Pyrolysis → fuel** | Layers cannot be separated mechanically |
| End-of-life automotive shredder residue | **Energy recovery in cement kiln** | Contains PUR, paints, metals, fillers |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical: WtE Steam Generation from 1 Tonne of MPW

### Given:
- Feed: 1000 kg MPW (typical composition: 35 % PE, 25 % PP, 15 % PS, 10 % PET, 10 % PVC, 5 % additives)
- Average hydrogen content: $H = 11.0$ wt%
- Gross calorific value: $Q_{gross} = 38$ MJ/kg (typical mix)
- Boiler feed water temperature: 25 °C
- Steam pressure: 10 bar (saturated)
- Boiler thermal efficiency: $\eta_{boiler} = 0.85$

### Step 1 — Compute net calorific value

$$Q_{net} = Q_{gross} - 2.442 \cdot \frac{9H}{100} = 38 - 2.442 \cdot \frac{9 \times 11}{100}$$

$$Q_{net} = 38 - 2.442 \times 0.99 = 38 - 2.42 = 35.58 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

### Step 2 — Total chemical energy input

$$E_{in} = 1000 \times 35.58 = 35{,}580 \text{ MJ}$$

### Step 3 — Useful energy delivered by boiler

$$E_{useful} = \eta_{boiler} \cdot E_{in} = 0.85 \times 35{,}580 = 30{,}243 \text{ MJ}$$

### Step 4 — Enthalpy of 10 bar saturated steam

From steam tables: $h_{steam} = 2778$ kJ/kg, $h_{feedwater} = 105$ kJ/kg

$$h_{fg, effective} = 2778 - 105 = 2673 \text{ kJ/kg} = 2.673 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

### Step 5 — Mass of steam generated

$$m_{steam} = \frac{E_{useful}}{h_{fg, effective}} = \frac{30{,}243}{2.673} = 11{,}314 \text{ kg}$$

### Final Result:

$$\boxed{1 \text{ tonne MPW} \Rightarrow 11{,}314 \text{ kg of 10 bar steam}}$$

This is equivalent to **~ 3.0 MW·h of thermal energy** from one tonne of MPW — illustrating why energy recovery is *not* waste but a *legitimate fuel substitution* (1 tonne MPW ≈ 1.3 tonnes of coal in energy terms).
