<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define recoverable material. How is it different from recycled material?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> A **recoverable material** is any polymeric substance that possesses the technical and economic feasibility to be extracted from a waste stream and reintroduced into a useful application through reuse, recycling, chemical processing, or energy recovery. It is a *potential* property of the material.
>
> In contrast, a **recycled material** has *already been processed* through a recovery operation and exists as a secondary raw material (e.g., r-PET pellets). The distinction lies in *state*: recoverable = capability; recycled = accomplished fact.
>
> *Exam Tip:* Always cite an example (e.g., a discarded PET bottle is recoverable; r-PET pellets used for polyester fibre are recycled). **(1 mark for example)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*List any four methods of recovering value from polymer waste.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 0.75 each):**

1. **Mechanical Recycling** – shredding, washing, melting, re-extrusion (e.g., HDPE bottle → pipe).
2. **Chemical Recycling / Feedstock Recovery** – depolymerization to monomers (e.g., PET glycolysis → BHET).
3. **Energy Recovery** – incineration with heat recovery (calorific value ~46 MJ/kg for PE).
4. **Organic Recovery** – composting/anaerobic digestion of biodegradable polymers (e.g., PLA, PHB).
5. *(Optional bonus)* **Reuse** – direct reuse after cleaning (e.g., refillable glass-reinforced PET containers).

---

### PART B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]**
*(a)* Discuss in detail the **mechanical recovery process for poly(ethylene terephthalate)** with a neat process flow diagram. Explain the quality degradation occurring during each recycle cycle.
*(7 marks)*

*(b)* Explain **chemical recovery of PET via glycolysis**. Write the reaction mechanism, process conditions, and industrial significance.
*(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Mechanical Recovery of PET — 7 Marks**

The mechanical recovery of PET follows a sequential industrial pathway:

**Step 1 – Collection (1 mark):**
Post-consumer PET bottles are collected via drop-off centres, buy-back schemes, or kerbside programmes. Source segregation at household level is critical for purity.

**Step 2 – Sorting (1 mark):**
NIR (Near-Infrared) spectroscopy identifies polymer type. Manual sorting removes PVC contamination (which causes yellowing and hydrolytic degradation in PET during processing).

**Step 3 – Label Removal and Washing (1 mark):**
Bottles pass through a hot caustic wash (~80°C, 1–2% NaOH) to remove adhesives, labels, and surface contaminants. A final rinse ensures residual alkali is removed.

**Step 4 – Shredding (1 mark):**
Cleaned bottles are shredded into flakes of 8–12 mm size. Flake density (~1.38 g/cm³) enables sink-float separation of cap/washer impurities.

**Step 5 – Extrusion and Pelletization (1 mark):**
Flakes are melted in an extruder (260–280°C), filtered through screen packs, and pelletized. The resulting r-PET pellets are used for fibre, strapping, or bottle applications.

**Step 6 – Quality Degradation Mechanism (1 mark):**
Each melt cycle induces **thermal-oxidative degradation** and **hydrolysis**, reducing molecular weight (lower intrinsic viscosity $[\eta]$). Chain scission produces carboxyl end groups, accelerating further degradation. After 4–5 cycles, the polymer becomes unsuitable for high-grade applications.

**Step 7 – Solid-State Polycondensation (SSP) (1 mark):**
For food-grade r-PET, pellets undergo SSP at 200–220°C under vacuum/N₂ to rebuild molecular weight ($[\eta]$ from 0.6 to 0.8 dL/g).

**(b) Chemical Recovery of PET via Glycolysis — 7 Marks**

**Reaction (1.5 marks):**

$$
PET + EG \xrightarrow{Zn(OAc)_2, \, 190{-}220°C} BHET
$$

The polymer chain is cleaved by ethylene glycol (EG) in the presence of a transesterification catalyst (zinc acetate or manganese acetate), yielding **bis(2-hydroxyethyl) terephthalate (BHET)** monomer.

**Mechanism (2 marks):**
1. Catalyst coordinates with carbonyl oxygen of PET ester linkage.
2. EG hydroxyl attacks the activated carbonyl carbon.
3. Tetrahedral intermediate collapses, cleaving the C–O bond.
4. BHET is liberated; new ester bond formation completes the cycle.

**Process Conditions (1.5 marks):**
- Temperature: 190–220°C
- Pressure: atmospheric to slightly elevated
- Catalyst: Zn(OAc)₂ (0.5–1 wt%)
- Reaction time: 1–3 hours
- EG:PET molar ratio: 4:1 to 6:1

**Industrial Significance (2 marks):**
- BHET can be repolymerized to **virgin-grade PET** — closing the material loop.
- Glycolysis operates at lower temperatures than methanolysis or hydrolysis.
- Enables circular economy compliance under EPR mandates.
- Limitation: catalyst residue removal is necessary for food-contact applications.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]**
*(a)* With a flow diagram, explain **feedstock recovery** of polymers. Compare it with **energy recovery** in terms of environmental impact and product value.
*(7 marks)*

*(b)* A polymer waste stream of 5,000 kg contains 60% PET, 25% HDPE, and 15% mixed multilayer films. Calculate the mass of recoverable material via (i) mechanical recycling, (ii) chemical recycling, and (iii) energy recovery. Assume 90% efficiency for mechanical, 70% for chemical, and 95% for energy recovery. Comment on the results.
*(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Feedstock vs. Energy Recovery — 7 Marks**

**Feedstock Recovery Definition (1 mark):**
Feedstock recovery (also called chemical or tertiary recycling) breaks polymer chains into monomers, oligomers, or petrochemical feedstocks that can re-enter polymerization reactors or refineries.

**Process Flow (2 marks):**
1. Sorted polymer → Reactor (hydrolysis/glycolysis/methanolysis).
2. Monomer separation via distillation/crystallization.
3. Repolymerization → virgin-equivalent polymer.

**Energy Recovery (1 mark):**
Combustion of non-recyclable polymer in presence of oxygen to capture heat, which is converted to electricity/steam.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Feedstock Recovery | Energy Recovery |
|--------|---------------------|------------------|
| Product | Monomers | Heat/Electricity |
| Material Loop | Closed | Open (one-way) |
| CO₂ Emissions | Low | High (1 tonne plastic → ~3 tonnes CO₂) |
| Hierarchy Rank | Higher (preferred) | Lower (last before landfill) |

---

**(b) Numerical Problem — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- Total mass = 5,000 kg
- Composition: PET = 60%, HDPE = 25%, Multilayer = 15%
- Efficiencies: Mechanical = 90%, Chemical = 70%, Energy = 95%

**Step 1: Mass of Each Fraction (1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{PET} &= 0.60 \times 5{,}000 = 3{,}000 \text{ kg} \\
M_{HDPE} &= 0.25 \times 5{,}000 = 1{,}250 \text{ kg} \\
M_{Film} &= 0.15 \times 5{,}000 = 750 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Mechanical Recovery (Applied to PET + HDPE) — 2 marks**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{mech} &= (M_{PET} + M_{HDPE}) \times \eta_{mech} \\
&= (3{,}000 + 1{,}250) \times 0.90 \\
&= 4{,}250 \times 0.90 = 3{,}825 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Chemical Recovery (Applied to PET residue not mechanically recovered — or directly to a fraction) — 2 marks**

For this problem, chemical recovery applies to PET fraction with 70% efficiency:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{chem} &= M_{PET} \times \eta_{chem} \\
&= 3{,}000 \times 0.70 = 2{,}100 \text{ kg of monomer}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Energy Recovery (Applied to Multilayer Films) — 1 mark**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{energy} &= M_{Film} \times \eta_{energy} \\
&= 750 \times 0.95 = 712.5 \text{ kg} \;\text{(as fuel equivalent)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Comments (1 mark):**
- Mechanical recovery yields the highest mass output (3,825 kg) but with **degraded properties**.
- Chemical recovery yields **2,100 kg of virgin-grade monomer** — highest material value.
- Energy recovery of films at **712.5 kg** is the only viable option for multilayer films, which are non-recyclable mechanically.
- **Total diverted from landfill: 100%** — exemplary closed-loop design.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - In Part B numericals, **always show the unit clearly** (kg, MJ, %). Losing 1 mark for missing units is the most common error.
> - For recovery efficiency problems, **state your assumptions** explicitly (e.g., "Assuming PET is the only polymer amenable to glycolysis").
> - In process diagrams, **label every arrow** (e.g., "EG input", "BHET output"). A flow diagram without labels loses 1–2 marks.
> - Never confuse *recovery* with *recycling* — KTU examiners explicitly test this distinction.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU PEPOT632 Module 3 — Recoverable Material:**

- ☐ **Recoverable ≠ Recycled** — recoverable is a *potential*; recycled is the *realized output*.
- ☐ **Hierarchy**: Reuse → Recycle → Energy Recovery → Disposal (strict order).
- ☐ **Mechanical Recovery** applies only to **thermoplastics**, not thermosets.
- ☐ **PET Glycolysis** yields BHET; catalyst is **Zn(OAc)₂**; temperature 190–220°C.
- ☐ **PET Hydrolysis** uses water/acid/base; yields pure TPA + EG.
- ☐ **Calorific value** of common polymers: PE ≈ 46 MJ/kg, PP ≈ 46 MJ/kg, PS ≈ 40 MJ/kg, PET ≈ 26 MJ/kg.
- ☐ **NIR spectroscopy** is the industrial standard for polymer sorting.
- ☐ **Density-based separation**: HDPE floats in water (ρ < 1), PET sinks (ρ > 1).
- ☐ **SSP (Solid-State Polycondensation)** upgrades r-PET to food-grade.
- ☐ **EPR (Extended Producer Responsibility)** under PWM Rules 2022 binds producers to recovery obligations.
- ☐ **EU Target**: 55% recovery of plastic packaging by 2030 (Directive 2018/851).
- ☐ **Recyclability formula**: $\eta_{MR} = \dfrac{M_{recovered}}{M_{input}} \times 100\%$ — remember the *100* factor!
- ☐ Each melt cycle reduces $[\eta]$ — quality degrades, not quantity.
- ☐ **Biodegradable polymers** (PLA, PHB, PHA) are recoverable via **organic/biological routes**.
- ☐ Mixed multilayer films → **energy recovery is the only practical option**.

> **End of Notes — Module 3: Recoverable Material (PEPOT632, KTU 2024 Scheme)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Disposal of Residuals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT632 - POLYMERS & ENVIRONMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Disposal of Residuals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of polymer and environmental engineering under the KTU 2024 syllabus, **Disposal of Residuals** refers to the systematic, scientifically-regulated set of engineering processes and environmental management strategies employed to handle, treat, neutralize, recycle, or permanently contain the **solid, liquid, and gaseous by-products** (residuals) generated during polymer synthesis, processing, fabrication, and end-of-life stages. Residuals include monomers, oligomers, catalysts, solvents, off-spec products, sludges, dust, volatiles, and post-consumer plastic waste.

According to KTU terminology aligned with the **National Environmental Engineering Research Institute (NEERI)** and the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** frameworks, residuals are categorized by the **4R principle** — **Reduce, Reuse, Recycle, and Recover** — with disposal being the final, least-preferred step in the integrated solid waste management hierarchy (ISWM).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes that disposal is *not* a singular act of dumping; it is a **decision-making hierarchy** governed by environmental legislation including the **Environmental Protection Act, 1986**, the **Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2016 (amended 2022)**, and the **Hazardous and Other Wastes (Management and Transboundary Movement) Rules, 2016**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a polymer manufacturing plant as a large-scale **kitchen** producing a specific dish (the polymer). While cooking, several things are generated:
- **Peelings and trimmings** (reactor scrap, edge trim, runners)
- **Spilled oil and grease** (solvent residues, monomer spills)
- **Used cooking gas and steam** (VOCs, styrene fumes, isocyanate vapors)
- **Expired ingredients** (off-spec polymer, degraded batches)
- **Empty containers** (empty drums, catalyst containers, solvent cans)

You cannot just throw all of these into a single garbage bin. Some must be **refrigerated and reused** (recyclable monomers), some must be **composted** (biodegradable organics), some must be **incinerated in a controlled furnace** (hazardous chlorinated waste), and the rest must be carefully **sealed in a sanitary landfill** (ultimate inert residue).

This kitchen analogy makes one principle crystal clear: **the goal of disposal is not just to "get rid of" waste, but to manage its entire lifecycle in a way that protects human health and ecosystems**.

### 1.3 Classification of Residuals (KTU Framework)

Residuals in the polymer industry are broadly classified into the following categories:

| Category | Examples | Typical Source |
|---|---|---|
| **Process Residuals** | Unreacted monomer, oligomers, off-spec polymer | Reactor, extrusion line |
| **Chemical Residuals** | Catalysts, solvents, stabilizers, blowing agents | Synthesis & compounding |
| **Fabrication Scrap** | Runners, sprues, edge trim, purge waste | Injection moulding, blow moulding |
| **Post-Consumer Waste** | Used packaging, end-of-life automotive plastics, e-waste | Collection centers, MRFs |
| **Air Emissions** | VOCs, HCl, CO, NOx, dioxins, furans | Processing, incineration |
| **Liquid Effluents** | Wash water, condensates, scrubber liquor | Plant wastewater |
| **Sludges & Ash** | Biological sludge, fly ash, bottom ash | ETP, incineration |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric (KTU):** Plastic waste generation in India is approximately **3.5 million metric tonnes per annum (MMTPA)** as per CPCB 2021-22 estimates, of which only about **60%** is recycled, while the remainder enters landfills or the open environment.

### 1.4 The Waste Management Hierarchy (The 5-Step Pyramid)

The KTU syllabus mandates that students understand disposal in the context of the **integrated waste management hierarchy**, from most preferred to least preferred:

1. **Prevention / Source Reduction** — designing polymers that generate less scrap
2. **Minimization** — efficient process design to reduce residuals at source
3. **Reuse** — direct reuse of off-spec polymer as a lower-grade product
4. **Recycling / Recovery** — mechanical, chemical, or feedstock recycling
5. **Treatment & Disposal** — incineration with energy recovery, landfilling

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** When asked to "discuss disposal of residuals," always frame the answer around the **waste hierarchy**. Examiners award marks for recognizing that disposal is the **last resort**, not the first option.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Principal Disposal Routes (KTU High-Yield)

The KTU 2024 Module 3 syllabus specifically focuses on the engineering methods used to dispose of polymer-related residuals. Each method has distinct physico-chemical principles, advantages, and limitations.

#### 2.1.1 Sanitary Landfilling (Ultimate Disposal)

- **Principle:** Engineered containment of non-recyclable, non-hazardous waste in a controlled site with impermeable liners, leachate collection, and gas management.
- **Why it works:** The composite liner (typically **HDPE geomembrane** of thickness **1.5 mm to 2.5 mm** over compacted clay of permeability $\le 1 \times 10^{-9}$ m/s) isolates contaminants from groundwater.
- **Limitations:** Long-term leachate contamination risk, methane emissions, and scarcity of land near urban centers.

#### 2.1.2 Incineration with Energy Recovery

- **Principle:** Controlled combustion of polymer waste at **850 °C to 1100 °C** with a residence time of at least **2 seconds** in the afterburner to ensure destruction of toxic organics.
- **Why it works:** Polymers have high calorific value (PE ≈ 46 MJ/kg, PP ≈ 46 MJ/kg, PS ≈ 41 MJ/kg), comparable to fuel oil.
- **Limitations:** Risk of dioxin/furan formation from chlorinated polymers (PVC), heavy metal emissions, public opposition (NIMBY syndrome).

#### 2.1.3 Mechanical Recycling

- **Principle:** Physical reprocessing (shredding, washing, melting, re-extrusion) of sorted polymer waste into granules for reuse.
- **Why it works:** Preserves the polymer backbone; economically viable at scale.
- **Limitations:** Property degradation due to thermo-mechanical chain scission; contamination reduces quality.

#### 2.1.4 Chemical Recycling (Feedstock Recovery)

- **Principle:** Depolymerization back to monomers using chemolysis (hydrolysis, glycolysis, methanolysis, aminolysis).
- **Why it works:** For condensation polymers (PET, PU, PA), the reaction can be reversed under heat and a catalyst.
- **Limitations:** Energy-intensive; requires pure, sorted feedstock.

#### 2.1.5 Biological Treatment (Composting & Anaerobic Digestion)

- **Principle:** Microbial degradation of biodegradable polymers (PLA, PHA, starch blends) under aerobic (composting) or anaerobic (biogas generation) conditions.
- **Why it works:** Converts organic carbon to CO₂, water, humus, or CH₄.
- **Limitations:** Conventional plastics (PE, PP, PS) are not biodegradable under ambient conditions.

#### 2.1.6 Secure Landfilling for Hazardous Residuals

- **Principle:** Specially engineered landfills with double liner systems, leak detection, and strict monitoring for hazardous waste as per the **Hazardous Waste Management Rules**.
- **Why it works:** Prevents leaching of toxic catalysts, heavy metals, and unreacted monomers into the environment.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table compiles the **essential equations and standard parameters** required for solving problems on disposal of residuals. Note: in the table cells, we use `\vert` (not the pipe character `|`) to denote absolute value, preserving markdown table integrity.

| Concept | Equation / Parameter | Units | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Calorific Value of polymer waste | $CV = \sum_i x_i \cdot CV_i$ | MJ/kg | Predicts energy recovery from incineration |
| Combustion air requirement | $V_{air} = \frac{m_{fuel} \times O_2^{stoich}}{0.21 \times \rho_{air}}$ | m³ | Sizing of incinerator blower |
| Leachate generation (Schmidt formula) | $L_c = \frac{P \times A}{1000}$ | m³/yr | Landfill design |
| Biogas yield from AD | $Y_{CH_4} = B_0 \times \frac{VS}{VS_{ref}} \times f_d$ | m³ CH₄/tonne | Anaerobic digester sizing |
| Landfill gas generation | $Q_{CH_4} = k \times L_0 \times M \times e^{-kt}$ | m³/yr | Gas recovery system design |
| Recycling mass balance | $M_{out} = M_{in} \times \eta_{recycle}$ | kg | Recovery efficiency tracking |
| Residual monomer limit (FDA / EU) | $RM_{limit} \le 1 \text{ ppm}$ (food contact) | mg/kg | Quality compliance |
| Half-life of polymer in environment | $t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k_{degrad}}$ | years | Persistence prediction |
| Thermal destruction efficiency | $\eta_{dest} = \frac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100\%$ | % | Incinerator performance |
| Density of polymer waste (compacted) | $\rho_{compacted} = 400 \text{ to } 650$ | kg/m³ | Landfill volume design |

Where:
- $x_i$ = mass fraction of polymer $i$ in waste stream
- $L_c$ = annual leachate volume
- $P$ = annual precipitation depth (mm)
- $A$ = landfill area (m²)
- $B_0$ = ultimate methane yield per unit VS
- $f_d$ = biodegradability fraction
- $k$ = first-order decay constant
- $L_0$ = methane generation potential
- $M$ = mass of waste in place
- $C_{in}, C_{out}$ = pollutant concentration at inlet/outlet

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The disposal hierarchy is not merely academic — it is the **operational backbone** of:
- **Plastic parks and PET bottle recycling plants** (e.g., India's first plastic park in Paradeep, Odisha)
- **Co-processing of plastic waste in cement kilns** (an emerging KTU-relevant disposal route)
- **Waste-to-energy (WtE) plants** in Indian metros (Delhi, Hyderabad, Bengaluru)
- **Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR)** compliance systems under PWM Rules 2022

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Landfill Gas (LFG) Generation Rate

This derivation is **high-yield** for KTU Part B (14-mark) numerical sub-questions. The **Scholl Canyon first-order kinetic model** is the standard.

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation.**

The mass of methane generated in a landfill at any time $t$ follows first-order decay kinetics of the biodegradable organic fraction.

$$Q_{CH_4}(t) = k \cdot L_0 \cdot M \cdot e^{-k t}$$

Where:
- $Q_{CH_4}(t)$ = methane generation rate at time $t$ (m³/yr)
- $k$ = first-order decay constant (yr⁻¹)
- $L_0$ = methane generation potential (m³ CH₄ per kg of waste)
- $M$ = mass of degradable waste placed at time zero (kg)
- $t$ = time elapsed since waste placement (years)

**Step 2 — Determine the methane generation potential $L_0$.**

The methane potential is calculated from the stoichiometric decomposition of organic matter:

$$L_0 = B_0 \times \frac{MSW_{frac}}{1 - MC}$$

Where $B_0$ is the ultimate methane yield per kg of dry volatile solids, $MSW_{frac}$ is the fraction of municipal solid waste that is biodegradable polymer/organic, and $MC$ is the moisture content (decimal).

**Step 3 — Apply material balance on carbon.**

The theoretical maximum methane volume per kg of biodegradable carbon is derived from the Buswell equation:

$$C_n H_a O_b + \left(n - \frac{a}{4} - \frac{b}{2}\right) H_2 O \rightarrow \left(\frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4}\right) CO_2 + \left(\frac{n}{2} + \frac{a}{8} - \frac{b}{4}\right) CH_4$$

**Step 4 — Compute total cumulative methane.**

Integrating the rate equation from $0$ to $T$ gives the total methane produced over the landfill life:

$$\int_0^T Q_{CH_4} \, dt = L_0 \cdot M \cdot \left(1 - e^{-kT}\right)$$

This equation is critical for **flare system sizing** and **electricity generation potential** estimation in sanitary landfills.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem (14-mark, sub-part style):** A polymer processing unit generates **500 kg/day** of mixed plastic scrap (70% PE, 30% PP). The waste is fed to a co-generation incinerator with an overall thermal efficiency of **28%** and an electrical conversion efficiency of **18%**. Calorific values: $CV_{PE} = 46.5$ MJ/kg, $CV_{PP} = 46.0$ MJ/kg. Calculate the **(a)** daily energy output in kWh and **(b)** the annual CO₂ emission avoided if the same energy were produced from a coal-fired plant with specific emission of **1.05 kg CO₂/kWh**.

**Step 1 — Calculate the weighted calorific value of the mixed waste.**

$$CV_{mix} = (0.70 \times 46.5) + (0.30 \times 46.0)$$

$$CV_{mix} = 32.55 + 13.80 = 46.35 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate total daily thermal energy available.**

$$E_{thermal} = m_{waste} \times CV_{mix}$$

$$E_{thermal} = 500 \text{ kg/day} \times 46.35 \text{ MJ/kg} = 23{,}175 \text{ MJ/day}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to kWh using the conversion $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \text{ MJ}$.**

$$E_{thermal} = \frac{23{,}175}{3.6} = 6{,}437.5 \text{ kWh/day}$$

**Step 4 — Apply electrical efficiency to obtain electrical output.**

$$E_{electrical} = E_{thermal} \times \eta_{electrical}$$

$$E_{electrical} = 6{,}437.5 \times 0.18 = 1{,}158.75 \text{ kWh/day}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate annual electrical energy.**

$$E_{annual} = 1{,}158.75 \times 365 = 422{,}943.75 \text{ kWh/yr}$$

**Step 6 — Calculate CO₂ emission avoided per year.**

$$\text{CO}_2 \text{ avoided} = 422{,}943.75 \times 1.05 = 444{,}091 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{yr}$$

$$\text{CO}_2 \text{ avoided} = 444.09 \text{ tonnes CO}_2/\text{yr}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Note:** Examiners expect students to show **all six steps** explicitly. A common error is omitting the unit conversion between MJ and kWh, or failing to show the **weighted average** for the mixed plastic calorific value.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Disposal Routes

This **comparison matrix** is a high-yield tool for KTU Part A (3-mark) and Part B descriptive questions.

| Parameter | Landfill | Incineration (with energy recovery) | Mechanical Recycling | Chemical Recycling | Composting |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Residue reduction** | None (storage) | Up to **90%** volume, **70%** mass | None (transformation) | None (transformation) | Up to **50%** mass |
| **Energy recovery** | Methane capture (partial) | High (WtE) | Moderate (reprocessing) | Moderate to high | Low (process energy) |
| **Environmental risk** | Leachate, methane | Dioxins, heavy metals | Microplastics, washing effluent | Chemical effluent | Methane (if anaerobic) |
| **Cost (INR/tonne)** | **800–1500** | **2000–4000** | **5000–12000** | **15000–30000** | **2000–5000** |
| **Polymer suitability** | All non-haz | High CV polymers | PE, PP, PET, PS | PET, PU, PA (condensation) | PLA, PHA, starch blends |
| **CO₂ footprint (kg/kg)** | 0.04–0.06 | 0.8–1.2 | 0.3–0.6 | 1.0–2.0 | 0.1–0.3 |
| **Regulatory status (India)** | PWM Rules 2016 (amended 2022) | PWM Rules 2016 (amended 2022), CPCB guidelines | PWM Rules 2016 (amended 2022) | Under development | PWM Rules 2016 (amended 2022) |
| **KTU Bloom's level** | L1–L2 | L3–L4 | L3 | L4 | L2–L3 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Integrated Residual Disposal System

The following **mermaid block diagram** maps the functional architecture of an integrated disposal system for polymer residuals, as required by the KTU 2024 outcome-based curriculum (CO3: design of waste management systems).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Polymer Plant Source] --> B[Residual Generation]
    B --> C{Segregation & Sorting}
    C --> D1[Recyclable Stream]
    C --> D2[Hazardous Stream]
    C --> D3[Biodegradable Stream]
    C --> D4[Non-Recyclable Stream]
    D1 --> E1[Mechanical Recycling Loop]
    D2 --> E2[Secure Hazardous Landfill]
    D3 --> E3[Composting / Anaerobic Digestion]
    D4 --> E4[Incineration with Energy Recovery]
    E1 --> F1[Re-granulated Polymer Output]
    E2 --> F2[Stabilized Residue]
    E3 --> F3[Compost / Biogas]
    E4 --> F4[Electricity / Steam to Grid]
    E1 --> G[Quality Check]
    G --> H{Conforms to Specs}
    H -->|Yes| I[Reintroduce to Process]
    H -->|No| J[Downgrade or Dispose]
    F2 --> K[Environmental Monitoring]
    F3 --> K
    F4 --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Regulatory Reporting CPCB / SPCB]
```

### 4.2 Process Flow: Sanitary Landfill Cross-Section

The following diagram illustrates the **engineered barrier system** of a modern sanitary landfill, as mandated by CPCB guidelines.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Top Cover Vegetative Layer 0.5 m] --> S2[Drainage Layer Gravel 0.3 m]
    S2 --> S3[Primary Clay Liner 0.6 m]
    S3 --> S4[Primary HDPE Geomembrane 1.5 mm]
    S4 --> S5[Leachate Collection Layer 0.3 m]
    S5 --> S6[Secondary HDPE Geomembrane 1.5 mm]
    S6 --> S7[Secondary Clay Liner 0.9 m]
    S7 --> S8[Prepared Subgrade]
    S5 --> P1[Leachate Sump and Pump]
    P1 --> P2[Leachate Treatment Plant]
    S1 --> G1[Gas Collection Wells]
    G1 --> G2[Gas Flare or WtE Engine]
    P2 --> M1[Effluent Monitoring]
    G2 --> M1
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree: Disposal Route Selection

This **decision flow** is a high-yield conceptual diagram for KTU Part A questions on disposal route justification.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is the polymer biodegradable?} -->|Yes| Q2{Is collection logistics available?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| R1[Composting / Anaerobic Digestion]
    Q2 -->|No| R2[Co-processing in Cement Kiln]
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{Is the polymer a condensation type?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q4{Is feedstock pure and sorted?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| R3[Chemical Recycling Glycolysis / Hydrolysis]
    Q4 -->|No| R4[Mechanical Recycling]
    Q3 -->|No| Q5{Is calorific value above 15 MJ/kg?}
    Q5 -->|Yes| R5[Incineration with Energy Recovery]
    Q5 -->|No| R6[Sanitary Landfilling]
    R1 --> M[Final Monitoring]
    R2 --> M
    R3 --> M
    R4 --> M
    R5 --> M
    R6 --> M
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** These block diagrams represent the **functional architecture** of disposal systems. In the KTU examination, students may be asked to **draw and label** a sanitary landfill cross-section or a recycling flow sheet. The diagrams above provide the structural template expected by examiners.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

Define the term "residuals" in the context of polymer processing. List any four categories of residuals generated in a polymer manufacturing unit.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Residuals in polymer processing refer to all unwanted solid, liquid, and gaseous by-products arising from polymer synthesis, compounding, fabrication, and end-of-life stages that require management to prevent environmental harm.

**Four categories (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
1. **Process residuals** — unreacted monomers, oligomers, off-spec polymer
2. **Chemical residuals** — spent catalysts, solvents, stabilizers
3. **Fabrication scrap** — runners, sprues, edge trim, purge waste
4. **Post-consumer plastic waste** — used packaging, end-of-life automotive plastics

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

What is the **4R principle** in waste management? Why is disposal considered the *last* option in the waste management hierarchy?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**4R Principle (1.5 marks):** The 4R principle stands for **Reduce, Reuse, Recycle, and Recover**. It is a hierarchical framework for managing waste that prioritizes waste prevention over end-of-pipe treatment.

**Why disposal is last (1.5 marks):** Disposal (landfilling or incineration without energy recovery) is the least preferred option because it:
1. Causes permanent loss of material resources (linear economy)
2. Generates secondary pollutants (leachate, dioxins, CO₂)
3. Has long-term environmental liability
4. Conflicts with **sustainable development goals** and **circular economy** principles

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3 Choice A]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss in detail the various methods of disposal of polymer residuals, with emphasis on the principles, advantages, and limitations of **sanitary landfilling** and **incineration with energy recovery**.

**(b) [7 marks]** A polymer waste stream contains 60% PET, 25% HDPE, and 15% PP by mass. The waste is fed to an incinerator at a rate of **800 kg/day**. Calorific values: $CV_{PET} = 32$ MJ/kg, $CV_{HDPE} = 46.5$ MJ/kg, $CV_{PP} = 46.0$ MJ/kg. The incinerator has an electrical efficiency of **22%**. Calculate the **annual electrical energy output in kWh** and the **annual CO₂ emission avoided** (use coal emission factor **1.05 kg CO₂/kWh**).

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

*Sanitary Landfilling (3 marks):*

Sanitary landfilling is the engineered, controlled disposal of solid waste in a prepared site designed to minimize environmental impact.

**Principles:**
- Isolation of waste from the biosphere using composite liners (HDPE geomembrane + compacted clay)
- Management of leachate through collection and treatment
- Management of landfill gas (LFG) through collection and flaring or energy recovery
- Daily cover with soil to prevent vector breeding, odor, and litter

**Advantages:**
- Low capital cost (₹800–1500 per tonne)
- Accepts all non-hazardous waste streams
- Final sink for non-recyclable residues
- Landfill gas can be recovered for power generation

**Limitations:**
- Permanent loss of land
- Long-term leachate contamination of groundwater
- Methane (a GHG 25× more potent than CO₂) emissions if not captured
- Public opposition (NIMBY syndrome)

*Incineration with Energy Recovery (3 marks):*

**Principles:**
- Controlled combustion of polymer waste at **850–1100 °C**
- Minimum residence time of **2 seconds** in the afterburner for complete destruction
- Heat recovery through boiler and steam turbine for electricity generation

**Advantages:**
- **90% volume reduction, 70% mass reduction** of waste
- Polymers have high calorific value (comparable to fuel oil)
- Waste-to-Energy (WtE) generation reduces fossil fuel use
- Modern plants have advanced air pollution control (electrostatic precipitator, scrubber, SCR for NOx)

**Limitations:**
- Risk of **dioxin and furan** formation from chlorinated polymers (PVC)
- High capital cost (₹2000–4000 per tonne)
- Heavy metal emissions if waste contains additives
- Public opposition and stringent emission norms (CPCB 2017 guidelines)

*Conclusion (1 mark):*
Both methods are essential components of integrated residual management. The choice between them depends on waste characteristics, calorific value, regulatory compliance, and economic feasibility.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Weighted calorific value of the mixed waste (2 marks):**

$$CV_{mix} = (0.60 \times 32) + (0.25 \times 46.5) + (0.15 \times 46.0)$$

$$CV_{mix} = 19.20 + 11.625 + 6.90 = 37.725 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

**Step 2 — Daily thermal energy (2 marks):**

$$E_{thermal} = 800 \text{ kg/day} \times 37.725 \text{ MJ/kg} = 30{,}180 \text{ MJ/day}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to kWh (1 mark):**

$$E_{thermal} = \frac{30{,}180}{3.6} = 8{,}383.33 \text{ kWh/day}$$

**Step 4 — Apply electrical efficiency (1 mark):**

$$E_{electrical} = 8{,}383.33 \times 0.22 = 1{,}844.33 \text{ kWh/day}$$

**Step 5 — Annual energy output (0.5 mark):**

$$E_{annual} = 1{,}844.33 \times 365 = 673{,}181.67 \text{ kWh/yr}$$

**Step 6 — CO₂ emission avoided (0.5 mark):**

$$\text{CO}_2 \text{ avoided} = 673{,}181.67 \times 1.05 = 706{,}840.75 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{yr} \approx 706.84 \text{ tonnes CO}_2/\text{yr}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3 Choice B]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the principles and steps of **mechanical recycling** of polymer residuals. Compare mechanical recycling with **chemical recycling** in terms of product quality, energy requirement, and environmental impact.

**(b) [7 marks]** A municipal solid waste stream contains **12% by mass** of plastic residuals (mostly PE and PET). The total waste generated is **500 tonnes/day**. The plastic fraction is sent to a mechanical recycling plant with an **overall recovery efficiency of 65%**. Calculate the **mass of recycled polymer granules produced per day** and the **percentage of input plastic that becomes process waste** (to be sent for energy recovery).

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

*Mechanical Recycling Principles (3 marks):*

Mechanical recycling is the physical reprocessing of polymer waste into secondary raw materials without significantly altering the chemical structure of the polymer.

**Steps:**
1. **Collection and sorting** — manual and automated sorting (NIR spectroscopy) by polymer type and color
2. **Shredding** — size reduction to flakes of **5–25 mm**
3. **Washing** — removal of organic and inorganic contaminants using hot water and detergents
4. **Drying** — typically to moisture content below **1%** to prevent hydrolysis during reprocessing
5. **Melt extrusion** — re-melting in an extruder at polymer-specific temperature, followed by filtration and pelletization
6. **Compounding and quality testing** — addition of stabilizers and property testing (MFI, tensile strength)

*Chemical Recycling Principles (2 marks):*

Chemical recycling (feedstock recycling) involves depolymerization of condensation polymers back to monomers or oligomers using chemolysis:
- **Hydrolysis** (water, acid, or base catalyzed) — used for PET → TPA + EG
- **Glycolysis** (ethylene glycol) — PET → BHET monomer
- **Methanolysis** (methanol) — PET → DMT + EG
- **Aminolysis** (amines) — PET → diamides

*Comparison Table (2 marks):*

| Parameter | Mechanical Recycling | Chemical Recycling |
|---|---|---|
| Product quality | Lower (property degradation) | Virgin-equivalent |
| Energy requirement | Moderate (150–300 kWh/tonne) | High (500–1000 kWh/tonne) |
| Polymer scope | Addition polymers (PE, PP, PS) | Condensation polymers (PET, PU, PA) |
| Environmental impact | Low CO₂, water use high | High CO₂, chemical effluent |

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Mass of plastic fraction in MSW (2 marks):**

$$M_{plastic} = 500 \text{ tonnes/day} \times 0.12 = 60 \text{ tonnes/day}$$

**Step 2 — Mass of recycled granules (2 marks):**

$$M_{recycled} = 60 \times 0.65 = 39 \text{ tonnes/day}$$

**Step 3 — Mass of process waste (1 mark):**

$$M_{waste} = 60 - 39 = 21 \text{ tonnes/day}$$

**Step 4 — Percentage of input plastic that becomes process waste (1 mark):**

$$\% \text{ waste} = \frac{21}{60} \times 100 = 35\%$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation and recommendation (1 mark):** The 35% process waste is sent for energy recovery via incineration. This represents a **mass balance closure** for an integrated recycling + WtE system, exemplifying the **circular economy** model.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Disposal of Residuals Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing disposal with recycling:** Many students write only about recycling, which is *upstream* of disposal. The KTU module specifically asks about *disposal* methods (landfilling, incineration, secure disposal, co-processing). Examiners deduct **2–3 marks** for this conflation.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the waste hierarchy:** Disposal is the **last** option, not the first. Answers that jump directly to "dump in landfill" without mentioning the 4R/5R hierarchy lose **1–2 marks** for missing the systems perspective.
> 
> 3. **Unit conversion errors in numerical questions:** Forgetting to convert **MJ to kWh** (using $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \text{ MJ}$) is the **single most common error**, costing **2 marks** in Part B numericals.
> 
> 4. **Ignoring Indian regulatory framework:** Failing to mention PWM Rules 2016 (amended 2022), CPCB guidelines, or EPR obligations results in a loss of **1–2 marks** because the KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes *Indian context*.
> 
> 5. **Mixing up co-processing and incineration:** Co-processing in cement kilns is **not** the same as municipal incineration. Examiners expect students to know the difference (cement kiln operates at **1450 °C** with **6+ second** residence time vs. incinerator at 850–1100 °C).
> 
> 6. **Not labelling diagrams:** Schematic drawings in Part B must be **labelled** (e.g., geomembrane thickness, clay liner permeability, gas collection well) to receive full marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Disposal of Residuals:**

- **Definition:** Residuals = solid + liquid + gaseous by-products of polymer synthesis, processing, fabrication, and end-of-life stages.
- **Waste hierarchy (5R/4R):** Prevention → Minimization → Reuse → Recycling/Recovery → Treatment & Disposal.
- **Six principal disposal routes:** Sanitary landfill, Incineration with energy recovery, Mechanical recycling, Chemical recycling, Biological treatment, Secure hazardous landfill.
- **Sanitary landfill key parameters:** HDPE geomembrane 1.5–2.5 mm; clay liner permeability $\le 1 \times 10^{-9}$ m/s; daily cover 0.15 m; final cover with vegetative layer.
- **Incineration key parameters:** Temperature **850–1100 °C**; residence time **≥ 2 seconds**; PE/PP calorific value ≈ **46 MJ/kg**; PET ≈ **32 MJ/kg**; PVC avoided due to HCl emission.
- **Mechanical recycling steps:** Collection → Sorting → Shredding → Washing → Drying → Extrusion → Pelletization.
- **Chemical recycling scope:** Condensation polymers (PET, PU, PA); methods include hydrolysis, glycolysis, methanolysis, aminolysis.
- **Biological treatment scope:** Only biodegradable polymers (PLA, PHA, starch blends); conventional plastics are not biodegradable.
- **Indian regulations:** PWM Rules 2016 (amended 2022), Hazardous Waste Rules 2016, EPR framework, CPCB/SPCB compliance.
- **Key energy unit:** $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \text{ MJ}$.
- **CO₂ emission factor (India grid average):** approximately **0.82 kg CO₂/kWh**; coal-fired: **1.05 kg CO₂/kWh**.
- **LFG model:** $Q_{CH_4}(t) = k \cdot L_0 \cdot M \cdot e^{-k t}$ (Scholl Canyon first-order decay).
- **Leachate generation (Schmidt):** $L_c = \frac{P \times A}{1000}$.
- **Co-processing:** Cement kiln route at **1450 °C**, destroys waste completely and substitutes fossil fuel.
- **Numerical fluency:** Always show weighted averages for mixed polymer streams; show all unit conversions explicitly.
- **Diagram expectations:** Students must be able to draw a labelled sanitary landfill cross-section and a recycling flow sheet.
- **Bloom's level alignment:** KTU questions test L1 (recall) for definitions, L2 (comprehension) for hierarchy, L3 (application) for route selection, L4 (analysis) for numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# 🔷 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Rubber Recycling

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Rubber Recycling** is the systematic series of processes employed to recover and reprocess waste rubber (predominantly cross-linked **vulcanized rubber** from end-of-life tires, industrial scrap, and post-consumer products) into reusable raw materials, secondary products, or energy, thereby mitigating environmental burden and conserving petrochemical feedstocks.

In the formal KTU 2024 Scheme context, rubber recycling encompasses a hierarchy of strategies governed by the **EU Waste Framework Directive** analogy (the "5 R's" — **Refuse, Reduce, Reuse, Recycle, Recover**) applied specifically to **elastomeric thermoset networks**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Definition to Memorize:**
> *Rubber recycling refers to the conversion of vulcanized waste rubber into a processable, reusable form by selectively cleaving the three-dimensional polysulfide cross-links (C–S and S–S bonds) while preserving the main polymer backbone (C–C bonds) intact.*

## 1.2 The Fundamental Recycling Challenge

Unlike thermoplastics, **vulcanized rubber is a thermoset** — once cross-linked, it **cannot be melted or remolded** by simple heating. The sulfur bridges between polyisoprene/polybutadiene chains create a permanent 3D network.

| Property | Thermoplastic (e.g., PET) | Vulcanized Rubber (e.g., Tire) |
|---|---|---|
| Network type | Linear/branched (physical) | Cross-linked (chemical) |
| Behavior on heating | Melts & flows | Decomposes (chars, no flow) |
| Recycling ease | Easy (remelt) | Difficult (network must be broken selectively) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lego Castle" Metaphor

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy for Students:**
> Imagine vulcanized rubber as a **giant Lego castle** where every single Lego brick (the rubber polymer chain) is *glued* to its neighbors by strong chemical adhesive (the sulfur cross-links). You cannot melt this castle back into individual bricks. To recycle it, you must carefully use a **chemical solvent** that dissolves **only the glue** between bricks — leaving the bricks (polymer backbone) intact. This selective glue-removal is exactly what **devulcanization** does to rubber.

## 1.4 Sources & Magnitude of Rubber Waste

The single largest contributor to rubber waste globally is **End-of-Life Tires (ELTs)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Global Rubber Waste Statistics (Industrial Standard Metrics):**
> - Approximately **1.5 billion tires** reach end-of-life globally each year.
> - **India** generates ≈ **1.0 million tonnes** of ELTs annually (CPCB estimate).
> - Tires contain ≈ **40–45% rubber** (natural + synthetic), 25–30% carbon black, 10–15% steel, 5–10% fiber/textile.
> - **Standard Abbreviation:** *ELT = End-of-Life Tire*

# 🔷 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Rubber Recycling Methods

Rubber recycling methodologies are hierarchically classified by the **degree of polymer backbone preservation** and **energy intensity**.

### A. Primary Recycling (Reuse)
Direct reuse of rubber articles (e.g., **retreading** of tires).

### B. Secondary Recycling (Mechanical/Physical)
- **Grinding / Crumbing**: Size reduction without chemical alteration.
- **Product Forms**: Crumb rubber, buffings, shredded rubber.

### C. Tertiary Recycling (Chemical)
- **Reclaiming**: Conversion of vulcanized waste into processable "reclaim rubber."
- **Devulcanization**: Selective S–S / C–S bond scission.
- **Pyrolysis**: Thermal decomposition in absence of oxygen → fuel oil + carbon black + gas.
- **Depolymerization**: Chemical breakdown to monomers (rare for rubber).

### D. Quaternary Recycling (Energy Recovery)
- **Incineration** with energy capture (Tire-Derived Fuel — **TDF**).
- Cement kiln co-firing.

## 2.2 The Crumb Rubber Production Process (Most Common Method)

Step-by-step industrial logic:

1. **Collection & Sorting**: ELTs collected, steel-belt removed.
2. **Shredding**: Primary shredder reduces tire to 50–100 mm chips.
3. **Granulation**: Secondary grinding → 1–10 mm granules.
4. **Steel Separation**: Magnetic separation removes steel belts.
5. **Fiber Separation**: Air classification / fiber extractor removes textile.
6. **Fine Grinding**: Ambient or cryogenic grinding → **crumb rubber** (0.1–2 mm).
7. **Classification**: Screening by mesh size.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Process Terminology:**
> - **Ambient Grinding** = grinding at room temperature; produces irregular, spiky particles.
> - **Cryogenic Grinding** = grinding at temperature < $-196^\circ C$ (liquid N₂); produces smooth, regular particles suitable for high-value devulcanization feed.

## 2.3 Reclaiming Process — The Core of Rubber Recycling

**Reclaim rubber** is a processable elastomeric material derived from waste vulcanized rubber in which the **cross-link density has been substantially reduced**, enabling re-vulcanization with fresh rubber compounds.

### Sub-categories of Reclaiming:

| Method | Mechanism | Typical Conditions | Bond Selectivity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pan Process** | Heat + chemical agents + pressure | 180–200°C, 4–8 h | Breaks S–S, C–S |
| **Digester Process** | Steam digestion with chemicals | 180°C, steam pressure | Breaks S–S, C–S |
| **Mechanical (Shear)** | High shear + heat | 200–250°C in extruder | Random scission (low selectivity) |
| **Microwave** | Dielectric heating | 2.45 GHz, 30–90 s | Selective S–S cleavage |
| **Ultrasonic** | Cavitation + shear | 20 kHz, high amplitude | Selective C–S cleavage |
| **Chemical Devulcanization** | Specific chemical reagents | 80–200°C | Highly selective |

### 2.3.1 Standard Chemical Reclaiming Agents (Industrial Reagents)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chemical Reclaim Agents to Memorize for KTU Exam:**
> 1. **Disulfides** (e.g., diphenyl disulfide — DPDS) — selectively cleave S–S bonds.
> 2. **Mercaptans** (e.g., thiophenol) — promote C–S bond scission.
> 3. **Inorganic bases** (NaOH, KOH) — neutralize acidic byproducts.
> 4. **Plasticizers / swelling agents** (paraffinic oil, pine oil) — penetrate network, aid reagent diffusion.
> 5. **Activators** (ZnO, stearic acid) — accelerate reclaiming kinetics.

### 2.3.2 Chemistry of Devulcanization (S–S Bond Cleavage)

The general reaction of **polysulfide cross-link cleavage by a disulfide reagent**:

$$ \text{R–S}_x\text{–R'} + \text{R''–S–S–R''} \longrightarrow \text{R–S}_y\text{–R''} + \text{R''–S}_{z}\text{–R'} $$

where:
- R–S$_x$–R' = original polysulfide cross-link in vulcanized rubber
- R''–S–S–R'' = devulcanizing agent (e.g., DPDS)
- x, y, z = sulfur rank (typically 1–4)
- y + z = x + 2 (sulfur balance)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this works:** The cross-link S–S bond energy is ≈ **266 kJ/mol**, whereas the main chain C–C bond energy is ≈ **346 kJ/mol**. By carefully choosing temperature and reagents, chemists exploit this **~80 kJ/mol difference** to break only the weaker bonds.

## 2.4 Pyrolysis of Rubber (Tertiary Recovery)

**Pyrolysis** = thermal decomposition in **inert atmosphere** (absence of oxygen).

### 2.4.1 Typical Product Yields from Tire Pyrolysis (450–700°C)

| Product | Yield (wt %) | Use |
|---|---|---|
| Pyrolysis oil (TPO) | 45–55 | Fuel, feedstock for chemicals |
| Carbon black (rCB) | 30–35 | Reinforcement filler (after activation) |
| Pyrolysis gas | 10–15 | Combustion fuel |
| Steel | 5–10 | Recycled steel |
| Fiber | 1–3 | Energy content |

### 2.4.2 Mass Balance Equation for Pyrolysis

$$ m_{\text{tire}} = m_{\text{oil}} + m_{\text{rCB}} + m_{\text{gas}} + m_{\text{steel}} + m_{\text{fiber}} + m_{\text{loss}} $$

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{\text{S–S}} \approx 266$ | S–S bond dissociation energy | kJ/mol |
| 2 | $E_{\text{C–S}} \approx 272$ | C–S bond dissociation energy | kJ/mol |
| 3 | $E_{\text{C–C}} \approx 346$ | C–C bond dissociation energy (main chain) | kJ/mol |
| 4 | $\rho_c = \dfrac{\nu}{V}$ | Cross-link density (Flory-Rehner derived) | mol/m³ |
| 5 | $M_c = \dfrac{\rho RT}{2\rho_c RT + ...}$ | Molecular weight between cross-links | g/mol |
| 6 | $\% \text{ Recycle} = \dfrac{m_{\text{reclaimed}}}{m_{\text{waste}}} \times 100$ | Recycling efficiency | dimensionless |
| 7 | $Q_{\text{cal}} = m \cdot \Delta H_c$ | Heat recovered from incineration | kJ |
| 8 | $\Delta H_c^{\text{rubber}} \approx 32{,}000$ | Heat of combustion of natural rubber | kJ/kg |
| 9 | $\eta_{\text{devulc}} = \dfrac{X_{\text{after}}}{X_{\text{before}}} \times 100$ | Devulcanization efficiency (% cross-link reduction) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $T_{\text{pyro}} \in [400, 700]$ | Pyrolysis temperature window | °C |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Formula Sheet Caution:**
> - For numerical problems, **always** check the units of cross-link density (mol/m³ vs mol/cm³). A factor of **$10^6$** error is common!
> - Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `\vert x \vert` inside markdown tables to avoid syntax breaks.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Area | Recycled Rubber Form | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| Road construction | Crumb rubber (CRM) | **Rubberized asphalt** — quieter roads, longer life |
| Civil engineering | Shredded tire | Lightweight backfill, drainage layers |
| Sports surfaces | Granulated rubber | Playground tiles, running tracks |
| Energy sector | Whole tires / TDF | Cement kilns, paper mills, power plants |
| New rubber products | Reclaim rubber | Industrial mats, shoe soles, gaskets |
| Pyrolysis products | rCB, TPO | Tire-grade carbon black substitute |

# 🔷 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Mass Balance for Tire Pyrolysis Reactor

### Problem Setup
A **continuous pyrolysis reactor** processes **10,000 kg/day** of waste tires. The product distribution (based on proximate analysis at 550°C) is given as:

- Pyrolysis oil (TPO): 50 wt%
- Recovered carbon black (rCB): 32 wt%
- Pyrolysis gas: 11 wt%
- Steel: 6 wt%
- Fiber residue: 1 wt%

**Determine:** Daily mass output of each product stream and verify closure of the mass balance.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Apply the law of conservation of mass to the control volume (reactor):**

$$
\text{Input mass} = \text{Output mass}
$$

$$
m_{\text{in}} = m_{\text{oil}} + m_{\text{rCB}} + m_{\text{gas}} + m_{\text{steel}} + m_{\text{fiber}} + m_{\text{loss}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the known input:**

$$
m_{\text{in}} = 10{,}000 \text{ kg/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Express each product mass as a percentage of input (mass fraction × input):**

$$
m_{\text{oil}} = 0.50 \times 10{,}000 = 5{,}000 \text{ kg/day}
$$

$$
m_{\text{rCB}} = 0.32 \times 10{,}000 = 3{,}200 \text{ kg/day}
$$

$$
m_{\text{gas}} = 0.11 \times 10{,}000 = 1{,}100 \text{ kg/day}
$$

$$
m_{\text{steel}} = 0.06 \times 10{,}000 = 600 \text{ kg/day}
$$

$$
m_{\text{fiber}} = 0.01 \times 10{,}000 = 100 \text{ kg/day}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the recovered mass streams:**

$$
\sum m_{\text{out, recovered}} = 5{,}000 + 3{,}200 + 1{,}100 + 600 + 100 = 10{,}000 \text{ kg/day}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute mass loss (closure check):**

$$
m_{\text{loss}} = m_{\text{in}} - \sum m_{\text{out, recovered}} = 10{,}000 - 10{,}000 = 0 \text{ kg/day}
$$

**Step 6 — Energy balance (optional extension):**

Total recoverable energy from pyrolysis gas + oil (assume heat of combustion $\Delta H_c$):

$$
Q_{\text{recovery}} = (m_{\text{oil}} \cdot \Delta H_c^{\text{oil}}) + (m_{\text{gas}} \cdot \Delta H_c^{\text{gas}})
$$

$$
Q_{\text{recovery}} = (5{,}000 \times 42{,}000) + (1{,}100 \times 35{,}000)
$$

$$
Q_{\text{recovery}} = 210 \times 10^6 + 38.5 \times 10^6 = 248.5 \text{ MJ/day}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 10,000 kg/day tire pyrolysis plant can offset the daily electricity consumption of approximately **60–70 Indian households** (assuming 4 kWh/day average).

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Devulcanization Efficiency

### Problem Setup
A vulcanized rubber sample has **initial cross-link density $\rho_{c,i} = 1.20 \times 10^{-4}$ mol/cm³**. After microwave devulcanization at 2.45 GHz for 60 seconds, the cross-link density is measured by **equilibrium swelling** as $\rho_{c,f} = 0.35 \times 10^{-4}$ mol/cm³.

**Determine:** Devulcanization efficiency and backbone integrity (assumed intact if efficiency ≤ 70%).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Write the devulcanization efficiency definition:**

$$
\eta_{\text{devulc}} = \frac{\rho_{c,i} - \rho_{c,f}}{\rho_{c,i}} \times 100
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$
\eta_{\text{devulc}} = \frac{(1.20 - 0.35) \times 10^{-4}}{1.20 \times 10^{-4}} \times 100
$$

**Step 3 — Numerator simplification:**

$$
\eta_{\text{devulc}} = \frac{0.85}{1.20} \times 100
$$

**Step 4 — Final numerical result:**

$$
\eta_{\text{devulc}} = 70.83 \%
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**
- Cross-link reduction = 70.83% (good reclaiming yield).
- However, this is **at the threshold** of backbone integrity.
- Operating parameters must be slightly tuned (reduce time to ~50 s) to achieve safer ~60% efficiency.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Heat Recovery from TDF Incineration

### Problem Setup
A cement kiln is co-fired with **2,000 kg/h** of shredded tires (Tire Derived Fuel, TDF). The calorific value of TDF is **32 MJ/kg** (similar to coal). Boiler efficiency is 70%.

**Determine:** Useful thermal energy recovered per hour.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Total chemical energy input:**

$$
Q_{\text{in}} = \dot{m} \times \Delta H_c = 2{,}000 \times 32 = 64{,}000 \text{ MJ/h}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply boiler efficiency:**

$$
Q_{\text{useful}} = \eta \times Q_{\text{in}} = 0.70 \times 64{,}000 = 44{,}800 \text{ MJ/h}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to kWh (1 kWh = 3.6 MJ):**

$$
E_{\text{useful}} = \frac{44{,}800}{3.6} = 12{,}444 \text{ kWh/h}
$$

**Step 4 — Daily extrapolation:**

$$
E_{\text{daily}} = 12{,}444 \times 24 \approx 298{,}667 \text{ kWh/day}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Take-away:** 2,000 kg/h of TDF ≈ **12.4 MW** of recovered thermal power. This is comparable to a small industrial boiler.

## 4.2 Process Flow — Devulcanization Reactor (Mermaid Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[CRUMB RUBBER FEED HOPPER] --> P2[CONVEYOR BELT]

```

### 4.2.1 Devulcanization Reactor — Parameter Callout Table

| Parameter | Typical Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | 180–200°C | Activate chemical scission |
| Pressure | Atmospheric / slight vacuum | Remove volatiles |
| Residence time | 4–8 h | Allow diffusion + reaction |
| Reagent loading | 2–5 phr (parts per hundred rubber) | Control cross-link cleavage |
| Agitator speed | 30–60 rpm | Mass transfer enhancement |
| Atmosphere | N₂ (inert) | Prevent oxidative degradation |

## 4.4 Conceptual Schematic — Cross-link Density Reduction

```mermaid
flowchart LR

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The ideal "closed-loop" rubber recycling achieves the cycle **X1 → X2 → X3 → ... → X1** with minimal backbone degradation. The key challenge is to maximize cleavage at S–S / C–S sites while minimizing C–C scission.

# 🔷 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Q:** Define **reclaim rubber**. List any **two** chemical reclaiming agents used in rubber recycling.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Reclaim rubber is a processable elastomeric material obtained from waste vulcanized rubber in which the **cross-link density has been selectively reduced** by chemical, thermal, or mechanical means, while preserving the polymer backbone substantially intact. The product can be re-vulcanized and reprocessed like virgin rubber compound.

**Reclaiming Agents (1 mark — any two):**
1. **Diphenyl disulfide (DPDS)** — selectively cleaves polysulfide S–S bonds.
2. **Thiophenol / mercaptans** — cleave C–S bonds.
3. **Inorganic bases like NaOH** — neutralize acidic byproducts.
4. **Zinc oxide / stearic acid** — activators.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Q:** Distinguish between **devulcanization** and **pyrolysis** of waste rubber.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Devulcanization | Pyrolysis |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Selective cleavage of S–S and C–S cross-links in vulcanized rubber | Thermal decomposition of rubber in absence of oxygen |
| **Temperature** | 150–250°C | 400–700°C |
| **Atmosphere** | Inert (N₂) | Inert (no O₂) |
| **Products** | Processable reclaim rubber | Oil + carbon black + gas |
| **Mechanism** | Chemical bond scission (selective) | Random bond breaking + recombination |
| **Backbone** | Preserved | Completely broken down |

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024]`

**Q (a):** Explain in detail the **classification of rubber recycling methods**. Discuss **mechanical recycling** (crumb rubber production) and **chemical reclaiming** processes with their engineering significance. **(7 marks)**

**Q (b):** With a neat process flow, describe the **pyrolysis of waste tires**. List the products obtained and their industrial applications. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q (a) — 7 Marks

**Rubber Recycling Methods (3 marks):**

1. **Primary Recycling (Reuse):**
   - Direct reuse of rubber articles.
   - Example: **Retreading** of commercial truck and aircraft tires.
   - Saves ~70% of material vs. new tire production.

2. **Secondary Recycling (Mechanical):**
   - Conversion into crumb rubber, buffings, and ground rubber.
   - **Cryogenic grinding** (LN₂) produces clean, regular particles.
   - **Ambient grinding** produces irregular particles for low-cost applications.

3. **Tertiary Recycling (Chemical):**
   - **Reclaiming** — converts waste rubber into processable reclaim.
   - **Devulcanization** — selective S–S / C–S cleavage.
   - **Pyrolysis** — depolymerization for fuel and rCB.

4. **Quaternary Recycling (Energy Recovery):**
   - Incineration of TDF in cement kilns, boilers.
   - High calorific value (~32 MJ/kg).

**Crumb Rubber Production Steps (2 marks):**
1. Collection → 2. Shredding → 3. Steel separation (magnetic) → 4. Fiber removal → 5. Granulation → 6. Fine grinding (ambient/cryogenic) → 7. Classification.

**Chemical Reclaiming (2 marks):**
- **Pan process:** 180–200°C, 4–8 h, with disulfide agents and swelling oil.
- **Mechanism:** Polysulfide bonds broken by DPDS → shorter S–S fragments; main chain C–C preserved.

---

#### Model Solution — Q (b) — 7 Marks

**Process Flow (4 marks):**

1. **Feed preparation:** Shredded tires (steel & fiber removed).
2. **Reactor loading:** Inert atmosphere (N₂ purge) to prevent combustion.
3. **Heating:** 450–700°C in rotary kiln or fixed-bed reactor.
4. **Vapor exit:** Pyrolysis vapors leave the reactor, sent to condenser.
5. **Condensation:** Heavy oil condensed at 100–200°C → **Tyre Pyrolysis Oil (TPO)**.
6. **Gas cleanup:** Non-condensable gases (H₂, CH₄, CO, CO₂, light hydrocarbons) scrubbed.
7. **Solid residue:** **Recovered Carbon Black (rCB)** discharged from reactor.

**Product Yields & Applications (3 marks):**

| Product | Yield | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Pyrolysis Oil (TPO) | 45–55% | Furnace fuel, feedstock for aromatics |
| rCB (recovered carbon black) | 30–35% | Tire-grade filler (after activation) |
| Pyrolysis Gas | 10–15% | Burner fuel for reactor heating |
| Steel | 5–10% | Recycled steel scrap |
| Fiber | 1–3% | Energy recovery (low-grade) |
