# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**Q:** List any **six advantages of reclaimed rubber** with respect to **cost** in rubber product manufacturing.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Lower raw material cost** — RR costs 2–4× less than virgin NR/SBR per kg.
2. **Reduced compounding cost** — RR arrives pre-loaded with carbon black, sulfur, and process oil, so less fresh additives are needed.
3. **Lower mixing energy** — Pre-softened RR requires less Banbury mixing time and lower motor load.
4. **Higher throughput** — Shorter batch cycles allow more batches per shift, increasing machine utilization.
5. **Price stability** — RR prices fluctuate only ±15% vs. ±40% for NR, providing a hedging benefit.
6. **Reduced rejection/scrap cost** — Improved dimensional stability of green compound lowers rejection rate.
7. *(Optional bonus point)* **Carbon-credit revenue** under CBAM and **EPR certificate trading** under Indian PWM Rules 2022.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write **technical advantages** (better aging, easier processing) when the question specifically asks for **cost advantages**. Always tie the answer back to **money saved**, not just technical merit.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Q:** Why is reclaimed rubber considered a **cost-stabilization tool** rather than merely a cheap substitute?

**Model Answer:**

Reclaimed rubber's price has a **low standard deviation** (~±15%) because its feedstock (scrap tires, factory reject) is governed by **domestic scrap availability** rather than **global commodity markets** like the Malaysian Rubber Exchange or the Tokyo Commodity Exchange where NR is traded. Therefore, when NR prices spike due to weather events in Kerala/Thailand or crude oil shocks, manufacturers using RR blends can **maintain stable product pricing** and protect margins. RR thus acts as a **financial hedge**, not just a cheap filler.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Main Paper]

**Q:** With the help of a suitable **worked example**, demonstrate how the use of **30 phr of reclaimed rubber** instead of virgin natural rubber in a **conveyor belt cover compound** leads to an overall **cost reduction of at least 10% per kg of compound**. Discuss the **cost pillars** involved.

#### Part (a) — Cost Calculation (7 Marks)

**Solution Outline:**

State the compound formulation:

| Ingredient (phr) | Virgin Mix | RR Blend |
|---|---|---|
| NR | 100 | 70 |
| RR | 0 | 30 |
| Carbon black | 50 | 35 |
| Process oil | 10 | 6 |
| Sulfur | 2.5 | 1.8 |
| Others | 12 | 12 |
| **Total phr** | **174.5** | **154.8** |

Apply market prices (NR = 200 INR/kg, RR = 55 INR/kg, CB = 95 INR/kg, Oil = 110 INR/kg, S = 40 INR/kg, Others = 250 INR/kg).

Compute cost per kg of compound (as shown in Section 3.1):

- Virgin: **INR 165.92/kg**
- RR Blend: **INR 146.69/kg**
- Material saving: **INR 19.23/kg ≈ 11.6%**

Add energy saving (~INR 0.53/kg) and scrap saving (~INR 4.80/kg).

$$
C_{\text{total saving}} \;=\; 19.23 + 0.53 + 4.80 \;=\; \text{INR } 24.56 \text{ /kg} \;\approx\; 13.7\%
$$

This **exceeds the 10% benchmark**, satisfying the question's premise.

#### Part (b) — Discussion of Cost Pillars (7 Marks)

Discuss the **eight pillars** listed in Section 2.1:

1. **Direct raw material cost** — Largest single contributor (~78% of total saving).
2. **Additive savings** — RR pre-loaded with CB/oil reduces additive budget.
3. **Energy savings** — Lower Mooney viscosity → lower mixing torque.
4. **Throughput gain** — Shorter cycles → more batches/shift.
5. **Price stability** — Hedging against NR volatility.
6. **Reduced scrap** — Better green strength & dimensional stability.
7. **Carbon-cost avoidance** — Lower CBAM liability under EU exports.
8. **EPR credit revenue** — Indian PWM Rules 2022 tradable certificates.

Conclude with a statement that **the cost advantage is multi-dimensional**, not just "cheaper raw material."

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Main Paper]

**Q:** Compare the **total cost of ownership (TCO)** of a **virgin natural rubber compound** vs. a **50 phr reclaimed rubber blend** for **industrial rubber mat** manufacturing. What **non-material cost factors** must be considered for a fair comparison?

#### Part (a) — TCO Calculation (7 Marks)

For a 1000 kg industrial mat compound:

**Virgin NR Compound:**
- Material: 165.92 INR/kg → **INR 165,920**
- Energy: 1.60 INR/kg → **INR 1,600**
- Scrap cost: 11.20 INR/kg → **INR 11,200**
- Carbon tax (CBAM): 1.9 kg × INR 8/kg CO₂e × 1.5 (USD-INR adjusted) = 22.8 INR/kg → **INR 22,800**
- EPR compliance: 0 INR/kg (no recycled content)
- **TCO Virgin** = **INR 201,520**

**50 phr RR Blend:**
- Material: 146.69 INR/kg → **INR 146,690**
- Energy: 1.07 INR/kg → **INR 1,070**
- Scrap cost: 6.40 INR/kg → **INR 6,400**
- Carbon tax: 0.6 kg × 12 INR/kg CO₂e = 7.2 INR/kg → **INR 7,200**
- EPR credit: −2.5 INR/kg (revenue from selling EPR certificate) → **−INR 2,500**
- **TCO RR-Blend** = **INR 158,860**

$$
\text{TCO Saving} \;=\; \frac{201{,}520 - 158{,}860}{201{,}520} \times 100 \;\approx\; 21.2\%
$$

#### Part (b) — Non-Material Cost Factors (7 Marks)

1. **Capital cost of devulcanization plant** (if in-house) vs. purchase from third-party reclaimer (if outsourced).
2. **Quality consistency risk** — RR properties vary with feedstock batch; testing costs increase.
3. **Storage and handling** — RR has shorter shelf life; may need climate-controlled storage.
4. **Logistics cost** — Reclaimers may be located far from consumer industries.
5. **Regulatory/documentation cost** — EPR certificate registration, CBAM carbon accounting.
6. **Brand perception cost** — Some premium segments (e.g., orthopedic shoe soles) may reject RR-blend products.
7. **End-of-life liability** — Future recycling of RR-blend products may be harder.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Do not confuse** "**cost of reclaimed rubber**" with "**cost saving by using reclaimed rubber**." The former is an absolute price (~INR 55/kg); the latter is a *differential*.
> - **Always include energy, scrap, and carbon** in cost calculations — many students forget these and lose 3–4 marks.
> - **Cite phr correctly** — phr means *parts per hundred of rubber*, not per hundred of compound. The denominator for mass-fraction calculations is **total phr**, not 100.
> - **Distinguish** between *whole-tyre reclaim (WTR)*, *tire-derived reclaim (TDR)*, *butyl reclaim*, and *EPDM reclaim* — each has different cost implications.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Advantages of Reclaimed Rubber in Cost:**
>
> - [ ] RR costs **2–4× less** than virgin NR/SBR per kg (typical 2024 India price: INR 35–70/kg vs. INR 130–220/kg).
> - [ ] RR is **pre-loaded with carbon black (~30 phr equivalent), sulfur, oil, and ZnO** — reducing additive cost.
> - [ ] Mixing energy drops by **20–35%** due to lower Mooney viscosity of RR.
> - [ ] **Throughput gain** of ~15–25% due to shorter Banbury cycles.
> - [ ] **Scrap/rejection rate reduction** of 30–50% due to green-strength stability.
> - [ ] **Price volatility hedge:** NR ±40% vs. RR ±15% — protects budget forecasts.
> - [ ] **Carbon cost saving:** ~1.4 kg CO₂e/kg avoided per kg of NR replaced → significant under CBAM.
> - [ ] **EPR certificate revenue** under Indian PWM Rules 2022 — tradable, additional income stream.
> - [ ] Total cost-of-ownership (TCO) saving of **13–22%** achievable with 30–50 phr RR substitution.
> - [ ] Use the **mass-fraction method** for per-kg cost: divide each phr by **total phr sum** before multiplying by price.
> - [ ] **Non-material factors:** quality consistency, storage, logistics, brand perception, future recyclability.
> - [ ] In **KTU exams**, always frame the answer around **INR saved per kg** or **% cost reduction** — never just "it is cheaper."

---

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Mixing in Polymer Engineering — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme)**
> *Mixing* in polymer processing refers to the unit operation in which two or more components (polymer–polymer blends, polymer–filler dispersions, polymer–additive masterbatches, or polymer–fiber composites) are combined to achieve a uniform spatial distribution of the constituent phases, without altering the chemical identity of the individual components. It is a **physical** process dominated by **rheology, shear, and diffusion mechanisms**.

In KTU parlance, mixing is classified as either:
- **Solid–Solid mixing** (dry blending of powders/granules) — *low-intensity*.
- **Melt mixing** (in plasticized/molten state) — *high-intensity*.
- **Liquid–Solid mixing** (suspension/emulsion polymerization feeds) — *medium-intensity*.

---

## 1.2 Processing of Polymers — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme)**
> *Polymer Processing* encompasses the engineering operations that convert raw polymeric feedstock (pellets, powders, liquids) into finished or semi-finished products of desired shape, size, structure, and properties. It involves the application of **heat, pressure, and deformation** in a controlled sequence.

The principal processing operations covered in KTU Module 3 are:
1. **Extrusion** (single & twin-screw)
2. **Injection Molding**
3. **Blow Molding**
4. **Compression Molding & Transfer Molding**
5. **Calendaring**
6. **Thermoforming**
7. **Rotational Molding**
8. **Film Blowing & Casting**

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Idli Batter" & "Kitchen Mixer" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (for first-time learners)**

Imagine you are preparing **idli batter** in your kitchen:

- **Solid–Solid mixing** is like mixing **rice flour + urad dal flour** in a dry container before adding water. You only get *spatial uniformity* — no chemical change. The KTU equivalent is **dry-blending PVC + plasticizer + stabilizer** in a ribbon blender.

- **Melt mixing** is when you add water and churn. The viscous batter resists flow, and only by applying **strong, sustained shear** (your mixer's blades) do the components become a homogeneous dough. The KTU equivalent is **Banbury mixing of rubber + carbon black** at 150 °C.

- **Distributive vs. Dispersive mixing** is like the difference between:
  - *Distributive*: spreading green chutney evenly across a dosa surface (no breaking of drops).
  - *Dispersive*: crushing cumin seeds into powder with a mortar (breaking agglomerates into smaller particles).

- **Processing analogy**: Once your batter is ready, *processing* is what shape you give it — steam-cook it in a mould (compression molding), extrude it through a noodle-maker (extrusion), or pour it into a shaped pan (casting). Same batter, different products.

This analogy maps directly onto the KTU learning outcomes for Module 3.

---

## 1.4 Critical Physical Quantities (Standard Metrics)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bold Standard Metrics You Must Memorize**

- **Shear rate ($\dot{\gamma}$)** : Units = **s⁻¹** (reciprocal seconds)
- **Shear stress ($\tau$)** : Units = **Pa (N/m²)**
- **Viscosity ($\eta$)** : Units = **Pa·s**
- **Power number ($N_p$)** : Dimensionless
- **Mixing index ($I_M$)** : Dimensionless, range 0 → 1
- **Residence time ($\bar{t}$)** : Units = **s**
- **Throughput ($Q$)** : Units = **kg/h** or **m³/s**
- **Specific energy input (SEI)** : Units = **kWh/tonne**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these matter in KTU exams:** Every numerical problem on extruders, mixers, or molding machines requires you to compute at least one of these quantities. The unit conversions alone carry 1–2 marks.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamentals of Mixing

### 2.1.1 The Two Pillars of Mixing

| **Mixing Type** | **Mechanism** | **Goal** | **KTU Example** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Distributive (Extensive)** | Spreading without breaking | Spatial uniformity of components | Pigment dispersion in PS, color masterbatch in PE |
| **Dispersive (Intensive)** | Rupture of agglomerates | Reducing particle/droplet size below critical | Carbon black agglomerates in natural rubber |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** When asked "distinguish between dispersive and distributive mixing," most students lose marks by forgetting the **rheological requirement**. Dispersive mixing requires **shear stress > cohesive strength of agglomerates** ($\tau > \sigma_c$), while distributive mixing only requires **strain (deformation)** irrespective of stress.

### 2.1.2 The Mixing Index Equation

The most-cited KTU expression for measuring mixing quality is the **Danckwerts uniformity index** (adapted to polymer engineering):

$$I_M = 1 - \frac{\sigma_c}{\sigma_0}$$

where:
- $I_M$ = mixing index (0 = segregated, 1 = perfectly mixed)
- $\sigma_c$ = standard deviation of concentration in the mixture
- $\sigma_0$ = standard deviation of concentration before mixing (totally segregated)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** As mixing progresses, $\sigma_c \to 0$ and therefore $I_M \to 1$. This is the **asymptotic limit** — perfect mixing is theoretically unattainable in finite time.

### 2.1.3 Power Consumption in Mixers

For a rotating impeller (Banbury, roll mill, helical screw), the **power draw** is given by:

$$P = N_p \cdot \rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D^5$$

where:
- $P$ = power (W)
- $N_p$ = power number (function of Reynolds number $Re$ and geometry)
- $\rho$ = melt density (kg/m³)
- $N$ = rotational speed (rev/s)
- $D$ = impeller diameter (m)

The **Reynolds number** for mixing is:

$$Re = \frac{\rho \cdot N \cdot D^2}{\eta}$$

For **laminar (viscous) regime** ($Re < 10$), $N_p \propto 1/Re$, giving:

$$P \propto \eta \cdot N^2 \cdot D^3$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** This equation lets you size the drive motor of a Banbury mixer. Polymer melts have $\eta \sim 10^3$–$10^5$ Pa·s, so power requirements are **2–3 orders of magnitude** higher than for water-based mixing.

---

## 2.2 Extrusion — The Workhorse of Polymer Processing

### 2.2.1 Single-Screw Extruder Zones

A single-screw extruder has three functional zones:
1. **Feed zone (solids conveying)** — friction-driven, no melting.
2. **Compression/Melting zone (transition)** — polymer melts via drag-induced dissipation.
3. **Metering zone (melt conveying)** — behaves as a *drag pump*.

### 2.2.2 Throughput Equation (Newtonian Approximation)

For a Newtonian melt in the metering zone, throughput is:

$$Q = \frac{\pi D^3 N \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2} - \frac{\pi D^5 \Delta P \sin^2\phi}{12 \eta L}$$

where:
- $Q$ = volumetric throughput (m³/s)
- $D$ = screw diameter (m)
- $N$ = screw speed (rev/s)
- $\phi$ = helix angle (rad)
- $\Delta P$ = pressure rise along the metering zone (Pa)
- $\eta$ = melt viscosity (Pa·s)
- $L$ = length of metering zone (m)

The first term is **drag flow** $Q_d$, and the second is **pressure flow** $Q_p$ (opposing drag flow). Net throughput:

$$Q = Q_d - Q_p$$

### 2.2.3 Twin-Screw Extruder Distinction (Frequently asked in KTU)

| **Parameter** | **Single-Screw** | **Twin-Screw (Co-rotating)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Conveying mechanism** | Friction drag | Positive displacement (calendar/gear pump action) |
| **Mixing efficiency** | Moderate (mainly distributive) | High (both dispersive + distributive) |
| **Self-wiping** | No | Yes (intermeshing) |
| **Typical L/D** | 20–30 | 30–50 |
| **KTU application** | Pipe, profile, film | Compounding, masterbatch, PVC, reactive extrusion |

---

## 2.3 Injection Molding

### 2.3.1 The Injection Molding Cycle

The cycle consists of four phases:
1. **Filling** (1–3 s): melt injected at 50–150 MPa.
2. **Packing/Holding** (2–8 s): additional melt to compensate for shrinkage.
3. **Cooling** (10–60 s): polymer solidifies to ejection stiffness.
4. **Ejection & Mold open/close** (3–5 s).

### 2.3.2 Clamping Force Equation

$$F_{clamp} = P_{cavity} \cdot A_{projected}$$

For typical KTU exam values: $P_{cavity} = 30$–$70$ MPa, $A_{projected} = 10^{-3}$–$10^{-1}$ m².

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** Students often forget that **cavity pressure** (not injection pressure) determines clamp tonnage. The injection pressure is the pressure at the nozzle; pressure drop through runner/gate reduces it.

### 2.3.3 Shrinkage and PVT Relationship

Volumetric shrinkage after molding:

$$S_v = \frac{V_{mold} - V_{part}}{V_{mold}} \times 100\%$$

Typical values:
- **Amorphous polymers (PS, PMMA)**: $S_v \approx 0.3$–$0.7\%$
- **Semi-crystalline (PE, PP, PA66)**: $S_v \approx 1.0$–$2.5\%$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU:** Semicrystalline polymers shrink more because crystallization occurs *after* cavity filling, during cooling. Amorphous polymers freeze at $T_g$ with little further volume change.

---

## 2.4 Blow Molding

### 2.4.1 Two Variants

1. **Extrusion Blow Molding (EBM)**: Parison extruded → clamped in mold → air blown.
2. **Injection Blow Molding (IBM)**: Preform injection-molded → re-heated → blow in mold.

### 2.4.2 Parison Swell (Extrudate Swell)

The **die swell ratio** is:

$$\chi = \frac{D_{extrudate}}{D_{die}}$$

For polymer melts, $\chi \approx 1.1$–$1.5$ (LDPE highest). Die swell is **recoverable elastic strain** released upon exiting the die.

$$S_R = \chi^2 - 1 \quad \text{(recoverable strain in shear)}$$

---

## 2.5 Compression Molding & Transfer Molding

### 2.5.1 Compression Molding (Thermosets & UHMWPE)

Sequence:
1. Charge placed in heated mold.
2. Mold closed under pressure (10–30 MPa).
3. Cure (thermoset) or sinter (UHMWPE) at $T \approx 150$–$200$ °C.
4. Open mold, eject part.

### 2.5.2 Transfer Molding (used in KTU for phenolic/epoxy)

Compound is preheated in a transfer pot and forced through a sprue into a closed mold cavity. Advantages:
- Better dimensional control than compression.
- Less flash.
- Suitable for intricate inserts.

---

## 2.6 Calendaring

Used for producing **films and sheets** (PVC, rubber, PE). The polymer mass passes through a series of **counter-rotating heated rolls**:

$$h_n = h_0 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{1 + \epsilon^n}\right)$$

where:
- $h_0$ = initial sheet thickness
- $h_n$ = thickness after $n$-th nip
- $\epsilon$ = roll separation reduction ratio
- $n$ = number of roll pairs

---

## 2.7 Thermoforming

A thermoplastic sheet is heated above $T_g$ (or $T_m$ for semi-crystalline) and formed against a mold using **vacuum, pressure, or mechanical plug assist**.

$$t_{part} = t_{sheet} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{A_{sheet}}{A_{part}}}$$

Conservation of polymer mass gives the **thinning rule** above (assuming uniform biaxial stretching).

---

## 2.8 Rotational Molding

A measured charge of polymer powder (typically **PE, PVC plastisol**) is placed in a mold, which is then **rotated biaxially** in an oven. The powder melts, coats the inner mold surface, and forms a hollow part.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU exam favorite:** Why is rotational molding used for *large hollow products* (tanks, kayaks, toys) with no internal stress? Because there is **no shear** during forming — only gravity and rotation. Thus, **no molecular orientation-induced warping**.

---

## 2.9 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet (Single Reference Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table. Every KTU Module 3 numerical will use at least one of these.**

| **#** | **Equation** | **Variables & Units** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau = \eta \dot{\gamma}$ | $\tau$ [Pa], $\eta$ [Pa·s], $\dot{\gamma}$ [s⁻¹] | Newtonian viscosity |
| 2 | $\eta = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ | $K$ [Pa·sⁿ], $n$ [–] | Power-law (shear-thinning) |
| 3 | $Re = \rho N D^2 / \eta$ | $\rho$ [kg/m³], $N$ [s⁻¹], $D$ [m] | Mixer Reynolds number |
| 4 | $P = N_p \rho N^3 D^5$ | $P$ [W] | Power draw in mixer |
| 5 | $I_M = 1 - \sigma_c/\sigma_0$ | $I_M, \sigma_c, \sigma_0$ [–] | Mixing index |
| 6 | $Q = Q_d - Q_p$ (screw extruder) | $Q$ [m³/s] | Extruder throughput |
| 7 | $Q_d = \frac{\pi D^3 N \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2}$ | see Section 2.2.2 | Drag flow |
| 8 | $Q_p = \frac{\pi D^5 \Delta P \sin^2\phi}{12 \eta L}$ | see Section 2.2.2 | Pressure flow |
| 9 | $F_{clamp} = P_{cavity} A_{proj}$ | $F_{clamp}$ [N] | Injection molding |
| 10 | $S_v = (V_{mold} - V_{part})/V_{mold} \times 100\%$ | $S_v$ [%] | Molding shrinkage |
| 11 | $\chi = D_{ext}/D_{die}$ | $\chi$ [–] | Die swell ratio |
| 12 | $t_{part} = t_{sheet} \sqrt{A_{sheet}/A_{part}}$ | thicknesses [m], areas [m²] | Thermoforming thinning |
| 13 | $h_n = h_0 (1 + \epsilon)^{-n}$ | $h$ [m], $\epsilon$ [–] | Calendaring thickness |
| 14 | $\tau > \sigma_c$ (for dispersion) | [Pa] | Dispersive mixing criterion |
| 15 | $D_s = 0.06\,Re^{0.6}$ | $D_s$ [m²/s] | Diffusivity in polymer (rough) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical formatting note:** All absolute values / magnitudes above use plain text — never the pipe symbol — to preserve the markdown table structure.

---

## 2.10 Why These Equations Matter in Industry

| **KTU Module 3 Concept** | **Industrial Application** |
|---|---|
| Melt mixing (Banbury, internal mixer) | Tire treads, rubber compounds, TPV |
| Single-screw extrusion | Pipe manufacturing, film extrusion, wire coating |
| Twin-screw compounding | Filled/reinforced thermoplastics, masterbatches |
| Injection molding | Automotive parts, mobile housings, medical disposables |
| Blow molding | Bottles, fuel tanks, hollow containers |
| Compression molding | Phenolic/BMC components, electrical switchgear |
| Calendaring | PVC leather cloth, vinyl flooring |
| Thermoforming | Blister packs, refrigerator liners, bathtubs |
| Rotational molding | Water tanks, kayaks, traffic cones |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical note:** All derivations below are written in exhaustive step form. Every algebraic move is justified, so you can reproduce the same steps in the KTU ESE answer booklet. Equation blocks are wrapped in `\begin{aligned}` with one blank line before and after.

---

## 3.1 Derivation I — Drag Flow in a Screw Extruder Metering Zone

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

> Derive the **drag-induced volumetric flow rate** $Q_d$ for a Newtonian fluid in the metering zone of a single-screw extruder, treating the screw channel as an unrolled inclined plane.

### 3.1.2 Setup

Unwrap the helical screw channel. The channel becomes a flat plate of width $W = \pi D \sin\phi - e$ (≈ $\pi D \sin\phi$ when flight width $e$ is neglected) and length $L$ along the helix, inclined at angle $\phi$ to the horizontal.

The barrel (top) moves relative to the screw root (bottom) with velocity:

$$V_{bx} = \pi D N \cos\phi \quad \text{(down-channel component)}$$

$$V_{bz} = \pi D N \sin\phi \quad \text{(cross-channel component)}$$

Only the **cross-channel component** $V_{bz}$ drags fluid in the $z$-direction (perpendicular to the screw root). This is the flow that builds pressure.

### 3.1.3 Governing Equation (Newtonian Fluid in Steady State)

For fully developed flow in the $z$-direction with no pressure gradient contribution to drag flow, the Navier–Stokes equation reduces to:

$$0 = \eta \frac{\partial^2 v_z}{\partial y^2}$$

### 3.1.4 Boundary Conditions

$$v_z(y=0) = 0 \quad \text{(no-slip at screw root, stationary)}$$

$$v_z(y=H) = V_{bz} \quad \text{(no-slip at moving barrel surface)}$$

### 3.1.5 Integration

Integrate the ODE once:

$$\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial y} = C_1$$

Integrate again:

$$v_z = C_1 y + C_2$$

### 3.1.6 Apply Boundary Conditions

At $y = 0$: $v_z = 0 \Rightarrow C_2 = 0$.

At $y = H$: $v_z = V_{bz} \Rightarrow C_1 = V_{bz}/H$.

Therefore:

$$v_z(y) = V_{bz} \cdot \frac{y}{H}$$

### 3.1.7 Compute Volumetric Flow Rate per Unit Width

$$q_z = \int_0^H v_z(y) \, dy = \int_0^H V_{bz} \frac{y}{H} dy$$

$$\begin{aligned}
q_z &= \frac{V_{bz}}{H} \left[ \frac{y^2}{2} \right]_0^H \\
&= \frac{V_{bz}}{H} \cdot \frac{H^2}{2} \\
&= \frac{V_{bz} H}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.1.8 Convert to Down-Channel Flow

The **down-channel** volumetric flow is $q_x = q_z / \tan\phi$ (geometric projection), but for the helical unwinding convention, the total drag throughput in the **axial (extruder) direction** is obtained by integrating over the entire cross-section. The classical result, after full geometric accounting, is:

$$Q_d = \frac{1}{2} V_{bx} \cdot W \cdot H = \frac{1}{2} (\pi D N \cos\phi) (\pi D \sin\phi - e) H$$

Neglecting flight width $e$ and substituting $H = $ channel depth (metering zone depth):

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_d &= \frac{1}{2} \pi D N \cos\phi \cdot \pi D \sin\phi \cdot H \\
&= \frac{\pi^2 D^2 N H \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

For a **square-pitched** screw where $H = D/2$ and substituting the identity $2 \sin\phi \cos\phi = \sin 2\phi$:

$$\boxed{\,Q_d = \frac{\pi^2 D^3 N \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2}\,} \quad \text{(Classical KTU form, Eq. 7)}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Pressure Flow in Screw Extruder

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

Derive the **pressure-induced back-flow** $Q_p$ in the metering zone, where a downstream die creates a pressure gradient $\partial P/\partial x$ along the channel.

### 3.2.2 Governing Equation (with Pressure Gradient)

$$0 = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} + \eta \frac{\partial^2 v_z}{\partial y^2}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{\partial^2 v_z}{\partial y^2} = \frac{1}{\eta} \frac{\partial P}{\partial x}$$

### 3.2.3 Integrate Twice

$$\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial y} = \frac{1}{\eta} \frac{\partial P}{\partial x} y + C_1$$

$$v_z = \frac{1}{2\eta} \frac{\partial P}{\partial x} y^2 + C_1 y + C_2$$

### 3.2.4 Apply No-Slip Boundary Conditions

$v_z(y=0) = 0 \Rightarrow C_2 = 0$.

$v_z(y=H) = 0 \Rightarrow C_1 = -\frac{1}{2\eta} \frac{\partial P}{\partial x} H$.

### 3.2.5 Velocity Profile (Superposition)

$$v_z(y) = \frac{1}{2\eta} \frac{\partial P}{\partial x} (y^2 - H y) = -\frac{1}{2\eta} \frac{\partial P}{\partial x} y(H-y)$$

This is a **parabolic (Poiseuille) profile**, opposing the linear Couette profile from drag flow.

### 3.2.6 Compute Volumetric Flow per Unit Width

$$q_p = \int_0^H v_z(y) \, dy = -\frac{1}{2\eta} \frac{\partial P}{\partial x} \int_0^H y(H-y) dy$$

The integral:

$$\begin{aligned}
\int_0^H y(H-y) dy &= \int_0^H (Hy - y^2) dy \\
&= \left[ \frac{Hy^2}{2} - \frac{y^3}{3} \right]_0^H \\
&= \frac{H^3}{2} - \frac{H^3}{3} \\
&= \frac{H^3}{6}
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$q_p = -\frac{H^3}{12 \eta} \frac{\partial P}{\partial x}$$

### 3.2.7 Convert to Axial Throughput

The axial pressure gradient is $\partial P/\partial x$ along the down-channel direction. With channel length $L$ in the down-channel direction and pressure rise $\Delta P$ (positive for pressure-driven back-flow opposing drag flow):

$$\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} = \frac{\Delta P}{L}$$

Substituting channel depth $H = W \sin\phi / ...$ (after full geometry) and squaring the channel width for a real cross-section, the classical result is:

$$\boxed{\,Q_p = \frac{\pi D^5 \Delta P \sin^2\phi}{12 \eta L}\,} \quad \text{(Eq. 8, KTU reference form)}$$

### 3.2.8 Net Throughput (Newtonian Melt)

$$Q_{net} = Q_d - Q_p = \frac{\pi^2 D^3 N H \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2} - \frac{\pi D^5 \Delta P \sin^2\phi}{12 \eta L}$$

For a square-pitched screw with $H = D/2$:

$$Q_{net} = \frac{\pi^2 D^3 N \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2} - \frac{\pi D^5 \Delta P \sin^2\phi}{12 \eta L}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Power Draw of a Banbury Internal Mixer

### 3.3.1 Problem Statement

Estimate the power draw of a Banbury mixer rotor of diameter $D = 0.5$ m, rotating at $N = 1.5$ rev/s in a polymer melt of viscosity $\eta = 5 \times 10^3$ Pa·s and density $\rho = 950$ kg/m³.

### 3.3.2 Step 1 — Compute Reynolds Number

$$\begin{aligned}
Re &= \frac{\rho N D^2}{\eta} \\
&= \frac{950 \times 1.5 \times (0.5)^2}{5 \times 10^3} \\
&= \frac{950 \times 1.5 \times 0.25}{5000} \\
&= \frac{356.25}{5000} \\
&= 0.0713
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.3.3 Step 2 — Determine Power Number

For a Banbury rotor in the **laminar regime** ($Re < 10$), the Metzner–Otto correlation (or empirical chart) gives:

$$N_p \approx \frac{C}{Re}$$

with $C \approx 50$ (typical for Banbury rotors). Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
N_p &= \frac{50}{0.0713} \\
&= 701.3
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.3.4 Step 3 — Compute Power

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= N_p \rho N^3 D^5 \\
&= 701.3 \times 950 \times (1.5)^3 \times (0.5)^5 \\
&= 701.3 \times 950 \times 3.375 \times 0.03125 \\
&= 701.3 \times 100.5469 \\
&= 70{,}520 \text{ W} \\
&\approx 70.5 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.3.5 Step 4 — Check the Result

Industrial Banbury mixers (size 11D, for example) typically operate at 100–500 kW. The calculated **70.5 kW** is in the right order of magnitude for a small lab-scale mixer. ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU 14-mark problems**, this is exactly the kind of multi-step calculation expected. Always show:
> 1. Re calculation.
> 2. $N_p$ from correlation.
> 3. Power formula evaluation.
> 4. Order-of-magnitude sanity check.

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Thermoforming Thickness Distribution

### 3.4.1 Problem Statement

A PS sheet of thickness $t_{sheet} = 3$ mm and area $A_{sheet} = 0.25$ m² is thermoformed into a part of area $A_{part} = 0.40$ m². Calculate the resulting part thickness (assuming uniform thinning, no loss of material).

### 3.4.2 Mass Conservation

$$m_{sheet} = m_{part} = \rho V_{sheet} = \rho V_{part}$$

For uniform density and no edge beading loss:

$$V_{part} = V_{sheet}$$

$$A_{sheet} \cdot t_{sheet} = A_{part} \cdot t_{part}$$

### 3.4.3 Solve for $t_{part}$

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{part} &= t_{sheet} \cdot \frac{A_{sheet}}{A_{part}} \\
&= 3 \text{ mm} \times \frac{0.25}{0.40} \\
&= 3 \times 0.625 \\
&= 1.875 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.4.4 Engineering Reality Check

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The formula $t_{part} = t_{sheet} (A_{sheet}/A_{part})$ assumes **uniform biaxial stretching**. In practice, thermoformed parts have **non-uniform** thickness (corners thinner than flat areas). Real KTU exam problems often include the wording "assuming uniform deformation" — do not apply this formula to plug-assisted forming or pre-stretched sheets.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Selecting the Right Process

| **Product** | **Best KTU Process** | **Why** |
|---|---|---|
| Drinking water bottle (PET) | **Injection stretch blow molding** | Biaxial orientation → clarity, barrier, strength |
| PVC pipe (100 mm OD) | **Single-screw extrusion** | Continuous profile, high throughput |
| Tractor mudguard (HDPE) | **Rotational molding** | Large, hollow, low-volume production |
| Phenolic switch socket | **Compression molding** | Thermoset cure, electrical insulation |
| PVC leather cloth | **Calendaring** | Continuous thin film with surface finish |
| Mobile phone housing (ABS) | **Injection molding** | Complex 3D geometry, high dimensional accuracy |
| Carbon-black filled rubber compound | **Banbury mixing + calendering** | Dispersive mixing of CB into high-viscosity rubber |
| Yogurt cup (PP) | **Thermoforming** | Thin-wall packaging from extruded sheet |
| Masterbatch (PE + 50% pigment) | **Twin-screw extrusion + strand pelletizing** | Dispersive + distributive mixing, devolatilization |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Question Bank Format:** Modeled on KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination) pattern. Part A: 3-mark short answers. Part B: 14-mark questions with internal choice and 7+7 sub-part structure. Each sub-part is fully solved.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: Distinguish between distributive and dispersive mixing with one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Distributive Mixing** | **Dispersive Mixing** |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Spatial rearrangement of components without breaking them | Rupture of agglomerates/droplets into smaller units |
| **Driving force** | Strain (deformation) | Stress exceeding cohesive strength $\sigma_c$ of agglomerates |
| **KTU example** | Pigment dispersion in molten PS for color masterbatch | Carbon-black agglomerate break-up in natural rubber during Banbury mixing |
| **Criterion** | High total strain $ \gamma = \dot{\gamma} \cdot t$ | Shear stress $\tau > \sigma_c$ of the agglomerate |

> **Key Mark-Winning Line:** "Distributive mixing achieves spatial uniformity by **spreading**; dispersive mixing achieves compositional uniformity by **size reduction**." (1 mark)

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: Define the 'mixing index' $I_M$. What is its range and what does $I_M = 1$ signify?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$I_M = 1 - \frac{\sigma_c}{\sigma_0}$$

where $\sigma_c$ is the standard deviation of concentration of a key component in the mixture at any instant, and $\sigma_0$ is the standard deviation of the same component in the completely segregated initial state.

- **Range:** $0 \le I_M \le 1$
- $I_M = 0$ → Completely segregated (no mixing).
- $I_M = 1$ → Perfectly homogeneous mixture (theoretical limit, never practically attained in finite time).
- The mixing index follows approximately an exponential approach: $I_M(t) = 1 - \exp(-k t)$, where $k$ is a process-dependent rate constant. **(1 mark for the kinetic expression)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice: Answer any ONE)

### ⭐ OPTION A: 14-Mark Question (Q.1(A))

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**Q1 (A). (a)** With a neat sketch, describe the working of a **Banbury internal mixer** for rubber compounding. List its advantages over the two-roll mill. **(7 marks)**

**Q1 (A). (b)** A Banbury mixer with rotor diameter $D = 0.6$ m operates at $N = 30$ rpm in a rubber compound of melt viscosity $\eta = 8 \times 10^3$ Pa·s and density $\rho = 1100$ kg/m³. Estimate the **power draw** of the mixer. Use the Metzner–Otto correlation with $C = 50$. **(7 marks)**

---

### ⭐ SOLUTION TO Q1 (A) (a) — Banbury Internal Mixer (7 marks)

**Construction (2 marks):**
A Banbury mixer consists of a **jacketed mixing chamber** (for heating/cooling), two **counter-rotating, non-intermeshing, sigma-shaped rotors** (or interlocking, depending on model), a **ram** that descends from the top to apply pressure, and a **discharge door** at the bottom. The rotors turn at slightly different speeds (e.g., 1:1.15 ratio) to enhance mixing.

**Working (3 marks):**

1. Rubber, fillers (carbon black), plasticizers, curatives, and other additives are charged into the hopper.
2. The **ram is lowered** to compress the charge, forcing polymer into the high-shear zone between rotors and chamber wall.
3. The rotors' **counter-rotation** with the wing geometry creates **complex flow fields** — axial, tangential, and radial components.
4. The mechanical energy of rotation is dissipated as **viscous heat**, raising the temperature to 150–180 °C, which is sufficient for the rubber to plasticize and accept fillers.
5. Mixing continues for 4–10 minutes, after which the **discharge door is opened** and the compound is dropped onto a two-roll mill for further homogenization and sheet formation.
6. The mill then performs **final dispersion** and cools the batch.

**Advantages over two-roll mill (2 marks):**
- **Higher shear intensity** (closed chamber + ram pressure) → better dispersion of carbon black.
- **Dust-free, clean operation** (no open rolls).
- **Reproducible batch-to-batch quality** (controlled temperature, time, ram pressure).
- **Faster cycle time** for large batches.
- **Better safety** (operator not exposed to nip points).

---

### ⭐ SOLUTION TO Q1 (A) (b) — Power Draw Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:**

- $D = 0.6$ m
- $N = 30$ rpm $= 30/60 = 0.5$ rev/s
- $\eta = 8 \times 10^3$ Pa·s
- $\rho = 1100$ kg/m³
- Metzner–Otto constant $C = 50$

**Step 1: Reynolds Number (2 marks)**

$$\begin{aligned}
Re &= \frac{\rho N D^2}{\eta} \\
&= \frac{1100 \times 0.5 \times (0.6)^2}{8 \times 10^3} \\
&= \frac{1100 \times 0.5 \times 0.36}{8000} \\
&= \frac{198}{8000} \\
&= 0.02475
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Power Number (2 marks)**

Since $Re < 10$, laminar regime applies. Using $N_p = C / Re$:

$$N_p = \frac{50}{0.02475} \approx 2020.2$$

**Step 3: Power Draw (2 marks)**

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= N_p \rho N^3 D^5 \\
&= 2020.2 \times 1100 \times (0.5)^3 \times (0.6)^5 \\
&= 2020.2 \times 1100 \times 0.125 \times 0.07776 \\
&= 2020.2 \times 1100 \times 0.00972 \\
&= 21{,}600 \text{ W (approx.)}
\end{aligned}$$

Let me re-compute step by step more carefully:

$$\begin{aligned}
(0.6)^5 &= 0.6^2 \times 0.6^2 \times 0.6 = 0.36 \times 0.36 \times 0.6 = 0.1296 \times 0.6 = 0.07776 \\
(0.5)^3 &= 0.125 \\
P &= 2020.2 \times 1100 \times 0.125 \times 0.07776 \\
&= 2020.2 \times 1100 \times 0.00972 \\
&= 2020.2 \times 10.692 \\
&= 21{,}600 \text{ W} \\
&\approx 21.6 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Conclusion (1 mark)**

The mixer requires approximately **21.6 kW** of power, which is realistic for a small Banbury mixer (industrial large-scale units draw 250–750 kW). ✓

---

### ⭐ OPTION B: 14-Mark Question (Q.1(B)) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**Q1 (B). (a)** With the help of a labelled schematic, describe the **single-screw extrusion process**. Identify the three functional zones and explain the dominant mechanism in each. **(7 marks)**

**Q1 (B). (b)** A single-screw extruder of diameter $D = 60$ mm, lead $L_s = 60$ mm (square pitch), and metering zone length $L = 1.2$ m operates at $N = 60$ rpm. The polymer melt has viscosity $\eta = 1500$ Pa·s, and the die creates a pressure rise $\Delta P = 5$ MPa. Compute the **net volumetric throughput** $Q_{net}$ of the extruder. Take the helix angle $\phi = 17.65°$ (sin$\phi \approx 0.303$, cos$\phi \approx 0.953$). **(7 marks)**

---

### ⭐ SOLUTION TO Q1 (B) (a) — Single-Screw Extruder (7 marks)

**Schematic description (2 marks):** A single-screw extruder consists of a **hopper** (top), a **barrel** (heated cylinder, three or more zones), a **screw** (rotating inside the barrel, with depth $H$ varying along its length), a **breaker plate and screen pack** (filter and pressure builder), and a **die** (shapes the extrudate). Drive is provided by an electric motor via a gearbox and thrust bearing.

**Three functional zones (5 marks):**

| **Zone** | **Length (% of L)** | **Channel Depth** | **Dominant Mechanism** | **KTU Example** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Feed (Solids Conveying)** | 20–30% | Deep (~0.15 D) | Friction between pellets and barrel wall. Polymer is solid; conveyed by drag against stationary root. No melting. | Cold pellets from hopper |
| **Compression / Melting (Transition)** | 40–50% | Tapered (deep → shallow) | Polymer melts due to (a) barrel conduction heat, and (b) viscous dissipation from sheared melt film. The melt film is dragged from the barrel onto the screw root. | LDPE begins to melt at 110 °C |
| **Metering (Melt Pumping)** | 20–30% | Shallow (~0.05 D) | Drag flow dominates. Acts as a positive displacement pump with leakage. Pressure builds up to overcome die resistance. | HDPE metering at 200 °C |

---

### ⭐ SOLUTION TO Q1 (B) (b) — Throughput Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:**

- $D = 60$ mm $= 0.06$ m
- Square pitch → $L_s = D$ → $\tan\phi = L_s / (\pi D) = 1/\pi$ → $\phi = 17.65°$
- $\sin\phi = 0.303$, $\cos\phi = 0.953$
- Metering zone length $L = 1.2$ m
- $N = 60$ rpm $= 1$ rev/s
- $\eta = 1500$ Pa·s
- $\Delta P = 5$ MPa $= 5 \times 10^6$ Pa
- Assume square pitch → metering zone depth $H = D/2 = 0.03$ m

**Step 1: Drag Flow $Q_d$ (3 marks)**

$$Q_d = \frac{\pi^2 D^3 N H \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_d &= \frac{\pi^2 \times (0.06)^3 \times 1 \times 0.03 \times 0.303 \times 0.953}{2} \\
&= \frac{9.8696 \times 2.16 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.03 \times 0.2888}{2} \\
&= \frac{9.8696 \times 2.16 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.008664}{2} \\
&= \frac{1.8474 \times 10^{-5}}{2} \\
&= 9.237 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \\
&\approx 33.25 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

Let me recompute more carefully:

$$\begin{aligned}
(0.06)^3 &= 0.000216 = 2.16 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 \\
H &= 0.03 \text{ m} \\
\sin\phi \cos\phi &= 0.303 \times 0.953 = 0.2888 \\
\text{Numerator} &= \pi^2 \times 2.16 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.03 \times 0.2888 \\
&= 9.8696 \times 2.16 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.03 \times 0.2888 \\
&= 9.8696 \times (2.16 \times 0.03 \times 0.2888) \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 9.8696 \times 0.01872 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 9.8696 \times 1.872 \times 10^{-6} \\
&= 1.8476 \times 10^{-5}
\end{aligned}$$

$$Q_d = \frac{1.8476 \times 10^{-5}}{2} = 9.238 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2: Pressure Flow $Q_p$ (3 marks)**

$$Q_p = \frac{\pi D^5 \Delta P \sin^2\phi}{12 \eta L}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
D^5 &= (0.06)^5 = 0.06^2 \times 0.06^2 \times 0.06 = 0.0036 \times 0.0036 \times 0.06 \\
&= 1.296 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.06 = 7.776 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^5 \\
\sin^2\phi &= (0.303)^2 = 0.09181
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_p &= \frac{\pi \times 7.776 \times 10^{-6} \times 5 \times 10^6 \times 0.09181}{12 \times 1500 \times 1.2} \\
&= \frac{3.1416 \times 7.776 \times 5 \times 0.09181}{21600} \\
&= \frac{3.1416 \times 3.569}{21600} \\
&= \frac{11.214}{21600} \\
&= 5.192 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Net Throughput (1 mark)**

$$Q_{net} = Q_d - Q_p = 9.238 \times 10^{-6} - 5.192 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Problem:** $Q_p > Q_d$, giving a **negative** net throughput!

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> This negative result is a **classic trap**. It means the assumed $\Delta P = 5$ MPa is too high for this screw — the die would starve the extruder. In practice, the actual pressure rise is determined by the **intersection of the extruder (screw) characteristic and the die characteristic**. The maximum throughput occurs at $\Delta P = 0$ (open discharge, no die), giving $Q_{max} = Q_d \approx 9.24 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/s ≈ **33.3 cm³/s**.
>
> **What KTU examiners want:** Identify that the *operating* $\Delta P$ must satisfy $Q_p < Q_d$. To find it, you need the die flow equation $Q_{die} = K_d \Delta P$ and solve $Q_d - Q_p = Q_{die}$. With the data given, students should conclude that the extruder is **die-limited** and the **maximum achievable throughput is $Q_d$**.

---

## 5.3 Additional Practice Problems (Self-Assessment)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern Note:** These are unworked but follow the KTU 2024 difficulty curve. Try them before consulting your class notes.

1. **[3 marks]** A PS sheet of $t_{sheet} = 4$ mm and $A_{sheet} = 0.5$ m² is thermoformed to $A_{part} = 0.8$ m². Find $t_{part}$ assuming uniform deformation. *(Answer: 2.5 mm)*

2. **[3 marks]** An injection mold has projected area $A = 200 \text{ cm}^2$ and average cavity pressure $P = 40$ MPa. Find the minimum clamp tonnage. *(Answer: 800 kN or 80 tonnes-force)*

3. **[14 marks]** With a diagram, describe the **extrusion blow molding** process. Compare it with **injection blow molding** in terms of (i) preform production, (ii) material wastage, (iii) biaxial orientation achievable, and (iv) typical product size. *(Standard theory — refer to Section 2.4)*

4. **[3 marks]** The die swell ratio of an LDPE extrudate is 1.4. Calculate the **recoverable elastic strain** $S_R$ in shear. *(Answer: $S_R = 1.4^2 - 1 = 0.96$)*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision — Tick These Off Before the Exam**

### ✅ Definitions
- [ ] Mixing index: $I_M = 1 - \sigma_c/\sigma_0$ — range [0, 1].
- [ ] Distributive mixing = spreading; dispersive mixing = breaking.
- [ ] Drag flow = $Q_d$ (positive, drives melt forward).
- [ ] Pressure flow = $Q_p$ (negative, opposes drag flow).
- [ ] Net throughput = $Q_d - Q_p$.
- [ ] Parison swell / die swell ratio: $\chi = D_{ext}/D_{die}$.

### ✅ Equations to Memorize
- [ ] Power-law viscosity: $\eta = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$.
- [ ] Reynolds number for mixers: $Re = \rho N D^2/\eta$.
- [ ] Power draw: $P = N_p \rho N^3 D^5$.
- [ ] Single-screw throughput (Newtonian): $Q_d$ and $Q_p$ formulas.
- [ ] Clamp force: $F_{clamp} = P_{cavity} \cdot A_{projected}$.
- [ ] Thermoforming thickness: $t_{part} = t_{sheet} \cdot (A_{sheet}/A_{part})$.
- [ ] Calendaring thickness reduction: $h_n = h_0 (1+\epsilon)^{-n}$.

### ✅ Process Comparison
- [ ] Single-screw → friction drag, distributive mixing, pipes, films.
- [ ] Twin-screw (co-rotating intermeshing) → positive displacement, dispersive + distributive, compounding, masterbatch, PVC, reactive extrusion.
- [ ] Injection molding → discrete parts, complex 3D shapes, high tonnage.
- [ ] Blow molding → hollow parts (PET bottles, tanks).
- [ ] Compression molding → thermosets, BMC, phenolic.
- [ ] Calendaring → continuous films/sheets, PVC.
- [ ] Thermoforming → packaging, large-area parts from sheet.
- [ ] Rotational molding → large hollow parts, no shear stress, no orientation.

### ✅ Common KTU Traps to Avoid
- [ ] Don't confuse **cavity pressure** (clamp) with **injection pressure** (nozzle).
- [ ] Don't say twin-screw has "better" mixing without specifying **dispersive** vs **distributive**.
- [ ] Don't assume perfect mixing is achievable — $I_M \to 1$ asymptotically.
- [ ] Don't forget that **die swell** is **recoverable elastic strain**, not viscous flow.
- [ ] In the screw throughput derivation, remember the sign convention: drag flow **adds**, pressure flow **subtracts**.

### ✅ Numerical Shortcuts
- [ ] Unit conversions: $1 \text{ MPa} = 10^6 \text{ Pa}$; $1 \text{ rpm} = 1/60 \text{ rev/s}$.
- [ ] Square pitch → $H = D/2$, $\phi = 17.65°$ (memorize sin and cos).
- [ ] For laminar mixers ($Re < 10$): $N_p \approx C/Re$, $C = 40$–$60$ (use 50 unless specified).

### ✅ Syllabus High-Yield Topics
- [ ] Banbury vs. two-roll mill comparison.
- [ ] Single vs. twin-screw extruder comparison.
- [ ] Injection molding cycle phases and clamp tonnage.
- [ ] Extrusion zones (feed, compression, metering).
- [ ] Mixing index and its physical meaning.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT632 - POLYMERS & ENVIRONMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Defining the Polymer-Environment Nexus

The **Polymer-Environment Interface** is the formal study of the interactions between synthetic and natural polymeric materials and the Earth's ecological systems throughout their entire lifecycle — from monomer synthesis and polymer production to product fabrication, service life, end-of-life disposal, and ultimate fate in soil, water, or atmosphere.

In the strict vocabulary of the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PEPOT632**, Module 4 introduces the student to the four pillars of the polymer-environment discipline:

1. **Polymer Waste Generation** – quantifying the global stockpile of plastic refuse.
2. **Polymer Waste Management Hierarchy** – the 5R philosophy (Refuse, Reduce, Reuse, Recycle, Recover).
3. **Recycling Technologies** – primary, secondary, tertiary, and quaternary recycling routes.
4. **Degradation Mechanisms** – abiotic (thermal, photo, oxidative, hydrolytic) and biotic (microbial, enzymatic) breakdown pathways.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 of PEPOT632 specifically expects the student to internalize that *polymer technology cannot be divorced from environmental stewardship*. Every polymer engineer must internalize the **Cradle-to-Cradle** (C2C) design philosophy rather than the older Cradle-to-Grave (C2G) linear economy model.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Polymer Lifetime Passport" Intuition

Imagine every plastic product you have ever touched — the water bottle, the laptop casing, the grocery bag — carries an invisible **"Lifetime Passport."** This passport stamps four critical checkpoints:

- **Checkpoint A — Birth Certificate:** Crude oil / natural gas → monomer → polymer → product.
- **Checkpoint B — Working Visa:** Useful service life (typically **5 to 50 years**).
- **Checkpoint C — Retirement:** End-of-life (EoL) decision — landfill, incineration, recycling, or composting.
- **Checkpoint D — Final Resting Place:** Soil microplastic, ocean gyre, atmospheric aerosol, or molecular reincarnation through monomer recovery.

> A polymer's **environmental footprint** is the cumulative impact across all four checkpoints. Polymer engineering is the science of making this passport "greener" — shorter stays in Checkpoint B with longer reincarnation cycles, or designing the polymer to biologically degrade after Checkpoint C.

## 1.3 Global Magnitude — The Hard Numbers

| Metric | Approximate Global Value (2024 baseline) |
|---|---|
| Annual virgin plastic production | **~400 million tonnes/year** |
| Cumulative plastic ever produced (since 1950) | **~10 billion tonnes** |
| Plastic recycled globally | **< 9 %** |
| Plastic landfilled or mismanaged | **> 50 %** |
| Estimated marine plastic leakage | **~8–12 million tonnes/year** |
| Microplastic particles in ocean (upper layer) | **> 24 trillion particles** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental Axiom:** Plastics are remarkable engineering materials — lightweight, corrosion-resistant, cheap, and versatile — precisely because their C–C and C–H backbones are **kinetically inert** to most natural degradation pathways. This very property that makes them useful is what makes them environmentally persistent. The central engineering challenge of Module 4 is to resolve this paradox.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Linear vs. Circular Economy Paradigm

The polymer industry historically operated on a **linear economic model**:

$$\text{Crude Oil} \xrightarrow{\text{Refining}} \text{Monomer} \xrightarrow{\text{Polymerization}} \text{Polymer} \xrightarrow{\text{Fabrication}} \text{Product} \xrightarrow{\text{Disposal}} \text{Waste}$$

Modern sustainable thinking mandates a **circular model** (Closed-Loop Material Economy):

$$\text{Product} \xrightarrow{\text{Recycle / Degrade}} \text{Monomer or CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{Resynthesis}} \text{Polymer} \xrightarrow{\text{Re-fabrication}} \text{Product}$$

> The **closed-loop efficiency** (η_loop) quantifies how successfully the loop is closed.

## 2.2 The 5R / 6R Waste Management Hierarchy

The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates explicit knowledge of the **waste management hierarchy**. It is ranked from most-preferred to least-preferred:

1. **Refuse** — Eliminate unnecessary polymer use.
2. **Rethink** — Design products for sustainability from inception.
3. **Reduce** — Minimize material per unit function.
4. **Reuse** — Extend product service life.
5. **Recycle** — Reprocess into secondary products.
6. **Recover** — Energy recovery via incineration with heat capture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** When asked to rank waste management options, never place recycling above reduction. The hierarchy is **conceptually and legally** ordered from highest to lowest ecological priority.

## 2.3 Four Recycling Routes — The KTU Classification

| Recycling Type | Synonym | Mechanism | Output Quality |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary** | Closed-loop / re-extrusion | Mechanical reprocessing of uncontaminated industrial scrap | Equivalent to virgin |
| **Secondary** | Mechanical recycling | Shredding, washing, melting of post-consumer waste | Slight to moderate degradation |
| **Tertiary** | Chemical / feedstock recycling | Depolymerization to monomer (hydrolysis, glycolysis, methanolysis) | Virgin-grade monomer |
| **Quaternary** | Energy recovery | Controlled incineration with energy capture | Heat + electricity |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Key Quantitative Relations

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are routinely tested in KTU ESE questions for PEPOT632 Module 4. Master the symbols and units; KTU examiners routinely award or deduct marks based on correct unit notation.

### Formula 1 — Cumulative Plastic Stock (Simple Linear Model)

The total accumulated plastic mass in the environment is approximated by:

$$M_{\text{stock}}(t) = \int_{0}^{t} \left[ P(\tau) - R(\tau) - D(\tau) - I(\tau) \right] d\tau + M_0$$

Where:
- $P(\tau)$ = annual production rate (tonnes/year)
- $R(\tau)$ = annual recycled mass (tonnes/year)
- $D(\tau)$ = annually degraded mass (tonnes/year)
- $I(\tau)$ = annually incinerated mass (tonnes/year)
- $M_0$ = pre-existing stock at $\tau = 0$

### Formula 2 — Recycling Yield Efficiency

$$\eta_{\text{recycle}} = \frac{m_{\text{useful output}}}{m_{\text{feedstock input}}} \times 100\%$$

Typical values:
- PET bottle mechanical recycling: **~70 – 85 %**
- HDPE milk-bottle recycling: **~60 – 75 %**
- Mixed plastic packaging: **~20 – 40 %**

### Formula 3 — First-Order Degradation Kinetics

For biodegradable polymers undergoing hydrolytic or microbial attack, the molecular weight decay follows:

$$\frac{dM_n}{dt} = -k_{\text{deg}} \cdot M_n$$

Which integrates to:

$$M_n(t) = M_n(0) \cdot e^{-k_{\text{deg}} t}$$

And the **half-life** is:

$$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k_{\text{deg}}} \approx \frac{0.693}{k_{\text{deg}}}$$

Where $k_{\text{deg}}$ is the first-order degradation rate constant (day⁻¹).

### Formula 4 — Carbon Footprint Approximation

For incineration energy recovery, the specific CO₂ emission is:

$$E_{\text{CO}_2} = m_{\text{polymer}} \times f_{\text{C}} \times \frac{44}{12}$$

Where:
- $f_{\text{C}}$ = mass fraction of carbon in polymer (e.g., PE ≈ 0.856, PP ≈ 0.857, PS ≈ 0.923)
- $\frac{44}{12}$ = molar mass ratio of CO₂ to C

### Formula 5 — Melt-Flow Index vs. Molecular Weight (Empirical)

$$\text{MFI} = K \cdot M_n^{-\alpha}$$

Where $\alpha \approx 3.4$ for most flexible-chain thermoplastics (the well-known 3.4-power law). Critical for assessing recyclate quality.

### Formula 6 — Degree of Polymerization from Intrinsic Viscosity (Mark-Houwink)

$$[\eta] = K \cdot M_v^{\,a}$$

Where $K$ and $a$ are polymer-solvent-temperature specific constants. Used to characterize recyclate chain scission.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Why This Module Matters

- **Packaging Industry:** Choosing between monolayer recyclable vs. multilayer barrier structures.
- **Automotive Sector:** Designing end-of-life vehicles (ELV) with ≥ 95 % recyclability (EU Directive 2000/53/EC).
- **Medical Devices:** Selecting biodegradable sutures and scaffolds (PLA, PGA, PCL).
- **Agricultural Films:** Balancing service life vs. soil-microplastic accumulation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Establishing the First-Order Degradation Rate Equation

We begin with the empirical observation that for many biodegradable polymers (e.g., PLA, PHB, PCL) under hydrolytic or microbial attack, the rate of molecular weight loss is proportional to the instantaneous molecular weight itself.

**Step 1 — State the governing differential law.**

$$\frac{dM_n}{dt} = -k_{\text{deg}} \cdot M_n(t)$$

**Step 2 — Separate variables.**

$$\frac{dM_n}{M_n} = -k_{\text{deg}} \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Integrate both sides from $t = 0$ (initial molecular weight $M_n(0)$) to arbitrary time $t$ (molecular weight $M_n(t)$).**

$$\int_{M_n(0)}^{M_n(t)} \frac{dM_n}{M_n} = -k_{\text{deg}} \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the standard integral on the left.**

$$\ln M_n(t) - \ln M_n(0) = -k_{\text{deg}} \cdot t$$

**Step 5 — Exponentiate to obtain the closed-form expression.**

$$M_n(t) = M_n(0) \cdot e^{-k_{\text{deg}} t}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the half-life (the time at which $M_n(t) = \frac{1}{2} M_n(0)$).**

$$\frac{1}{2} M_n(0) = M_n(0) \cdot e^{-k_{\text{deg}} t_{1/2}}$$

Cancel $M_n(0)$ on both sides:

$$\frac{1}{2} = e^{-k_{\text{deg}} t_{1/2}}$$

Take natural log of both sides:

$$\ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -k_{\text{deg}} t_{1/2}$$

Recognise that $\ln(1/2) = -\ln 2$:

$$-\ln 2 = -k_{\text{deg}} t_{1/2}$$

Therefore:

$$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k_{\text{deg}}} \approx \frac{0.6931}{k_{\text{deg}}}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — PLA Degradation

**Problem:** A PLA sample has initial number-average molecular weight $M_n(0) = 100{,}000$ g/mol. Under composting conditions at 58 °C, the first-order degradation rate constant is $k_{\text{deg}} = 0.015$ day⁻¹. Calculate:

(a) The molecular weight after 90 days.
(b) The half-life of the polymer under these conditions.

**Solution (a):**

$$M_n(90) = 100{,}000 \times e^{-0.015 \times 90}$$

Compute the exponent first:

$$-0.015 \times 90 = -1.35$$

Then evaluate the exponential:

$$e^{-1.35} \approx 0.2592$$

Therefore:

$$M_n(90) \approx 100{,}000 \times 0.2592 \approx 25{,}920 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Solution (b):**

$$t_{1/2} = \frac{0.6931}{0.015} \approx 46.21 \text{ days}$$

**Interpretation:** In less than 47 days, the PLA molecular weight halves — the polymer is approaching the threshold below which microbial assimilation becomes rapid.

## 3.3 Derivation II — Recycling Mass Balance for a Closed Loop

Consider a continuous process where recycled polymer is blended with virgin polymer. The **effective properties** follow the rule of mixtures. For tensile strength:

$$\sigma_{\text{blend}} = \phi_v \cdot \sigma_v + \phi_r \cdot \sigma_r$$

Where $\phi_v$ and $\phi_r$ are volume fractions (with $\phi_v + \phi_r = 1$). However, recyclate typically has *reduced* molecular weight due to chain scission. The molecular weight of the blend is the number-average weighted by mol fractions.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Polymer vs. End-of-Life Pathway Suitability

| Polymer | Mechanical Recycle | Chemical Recycle | Biodegradation | Energy Recovery |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PET** | Excellent (bottle-to-bottle) | Excellent (glycolysis, methanolysis) | Negligible | Good |
| **HDPE** | Good | Moderate | Negligible | Good |
| **PVC** | Difficult (chlorine issues) | Possible but costly | Negligible | Restricted (HCl emission) |
| **LDPE / LLDPE** | Moderate (film contamination) | Pyrolysis to wax/oil | Negligible | Good |
| **PP** | Moderate | Limited commercial scale | Negligible | Good |
| **PS** | Limited | Moderate | Negligible | Moderate (styrene emission) |
| **PLA** | Moderate | Hydrolysis to lactic acid | Excellent (industrial compost) | Moderate |
| **PHB / PHA** | Limited | Possible | Excellent (soil, marine) | Good |
| **PC** | Limited | Possible | Negligible | Restricted (BPA) |
| **PMMA** | Good | Thermal depolymerization | Negligible | Moderate |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Polymer Lifecycle in a Circular Economy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Crude Oil or Biomass Feedstock"]
    node2["Monomer Synthesis"]
    node3["Polymerization Reactor"]
    node4["Polymer Resin Pellets"]
    node5["Product Fabrication"]
    node6["Consumer Use Phase"]
    node7["End of Life Sorting"]
    node8["Mechanical Recycle Loop"]
    node9["Chemical Recycle Loop"]
    node10["Composting Biodegradation"]
    node11["Incineration Energy Recovery"]
    node12["Landfill Stockpile"]
    node13["Environmental Microplastic Pool"]

    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5
    node5 --> node6
    node6 --> node7
    node7 --> node8
    node7 --> node9
    node7 --> node10
    node7 --> node11
    node7 --> node12
    node12 --> node13
    node8 --> node5
    node9 --> node3
    node10 --> node13
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — The 5R Waste Management Hierarchy

```mermaid

    nodeA["Step 1 Refuse Eliminate Unnecessary Use"]
    nodeB["Step 2 Rethink Sustainable Design"]
    nodeC["Step 3 Reduce Material per Function"]
    nodeD["Step 4 Reuse Extend Service Life"]
    nodeE["Step 5 Recycle Mechanical or Chemical"]
    nodeF["Step 6 Recover Energy from Waste"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Classification of Polymer Degradation Pathways

```mermaid

    nodeP["Polymer Material"]
    nodeP --> nodeAbio["Abiotic Degradation"]
    nodeP --> nodeBio["Biotic Degradation"]

    nodeAbio --> nodeTherm["Thermal Degradation"]
    nodeAbio --> nodePhoto["Photo Oxidative UV"]
    nodeAbio --> nodeOxid["Chemical Oxidative"]
    nodeAbio --> nodeHydro["Hydrolytic"]
    nodeAbio --> nodeMech["Mechano Chemical"]

    nodeBio --> nodeMicro["Microbial Bacterial Fungal"]
    nodeBio --> nodeEnz["Enzymatic Surface Attack"]
    nodeBio --> nodeBurrow["Macro Burrowing Organisms"]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Polymer Engineering Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["New Polymer Product Concept"]
    nodeQ1{"Service Life Required"}
    nodeP1["Durable Polymer Pathway"]
    nodeP2["Single Use Polymer Pathway"]

    nodeQ2{"Recyclable Target"}
    nodeQ3{"Biodegradable Target"}

    nodeP3["Design Mono material Stream"]
    nodeP4["Add Chemical Depolymerization Route"]
    nodeP5["Use PLA PHB Starch Blend"]
    nodeP6["Use PCL PBS Additives"]

    nodeEnd["End of Life Plan"]

    nodeStart --> nodeQ1
    nodeQ1 -->|More than 5 years| nodeP1
    nodeQ1 -->|Less than 5 years| nodeP2
    nodeP1 --> nodeQ2
    nodeP2 --> nodeQ3
    nodeQ2 --> nodeP3
    nodeQ2 --> nodeP4
    nodeQ3 --> nodeP5
    nodeQ3 --> nodeP6
    nodeP3 --> nodeEnd
    nodeP4 --> nodeEnd
    nodeP5 --> nodeEnd
    nodeP6 --> nodeEnd
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — Define the 5R waste management hierarchy. Why is recycling not the most preferred option?

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PEPOT632 Module 4]**

**Model Answer:**

The 5R hierarchy, in descending order of preference, is:

1. **Refuse** — Refuse unnecessary polymer consumption at the source.
2. **Reduce** — Reduce the mass of polymer used per functional unit.
3. **Reuse** — Reuse the product for the same or different function without reprocessing.
4. **Recycle** — Recycle the material into secondary products via mechanical or chemical routes.
5. **Recover** — Recover energy through incineration with heat capture.

Recycling is not the most preferred option because every recycling step entails **energy expenditure, material loss, and downgrading of properties** (chain scission, contamination). Prevention at the source (refuse/reduce) eliminates environmental burden entirely, whereas recycling merely redistributes it. Hence the hierarchy privileges upstream interventions.

### Question 2 — Differentiate between primary, secondary, tertiary, and quaternary recycling with one example each.

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PEPOT632 Module 4]**

**Model Answer:**

| Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary** | Reprocessing of clean industrial scrap into identical-grade product | Re-extruded PET preform scrap → PET bottles |
| **Secondary** | Mechanical reprocessing of post-consumer waste | Shredded HDPE milk bottles → plastic lumber |
| **Tertiary** | Chemical depolymerization to monomer | PET glycolysis → BHET monomer → virgin PET |
| **Quaternary** | Energy recovery via incineration | Mixed plastic refuse → electricity in cement kiln |

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PEPOT632 Module 4]**

**(a)** Discuss the various sources of polymer waste, classifying them into industrial scrap, post-consumer municipal solid waste, and specialty waste streams. Explain why persistence in the environment is linked to the **C–C bond strength** of the polymer backbone. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 250 mL PET bottle has mass 25 g. The annual global PET bottle consumption is approximately 500 billion bottles. Calculate: (i) the annual PET bottle mass in million tonnes; (ii) the cumulative stock after 5 years if 75 % is landfilled with zero degradation; (iii) the volume this would occupy in a landfill assuming compacted plastic density of 500 kg/m³. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Sources of polymer waste include:
- **Industrial scrap:** Sprues, runners, off-spec pellets from injection moulding, blow moulding, and extrusion lines (typically 5–15 % of production).
- **Post-consumer MSW:** Packaging (largest share), household goods, single-use items, automotive shredder residue.
- **Specialty streams:** End-of-life vehicles (ELV), WEEE (e-waste), marine debris, agricultural mulch films, fishing gear.

Persistence in the environment is fundamentally rooted in the **high bond dissociation energy of the C–C backbone** (~348 kJ/mol) and the **C–H bond** (~413 kJ/mol). Microbial enzymes have evolved to cleave C–O, C–N, and peptide bonds but not the saturated aliphatic C–C bonds of polyethylene, polypropylene, and polystyrene. Polymers with heteroatoms in the backbone (e.g., polyesters like PET with C–O bonds, polyamides with C–N) are far more susceptible to hydrolysis and enzymatic attack, hence more degradable.

**(b)**

**(i)** Annual PET bottle mass:

$$m_{\text{annual}} = 500 \times 10^9 \times 25 \text{ g} = 12.5 \times 10^{12} \text{ g}$$

Convert to million tonnes (1 million tonnes = 10⁹ kg = 10¹² g):

$$m_{\text{annual}} = \frac{12.5 \times 10^{12}}{10^{12}} = 12.5 \text{ million tonnes/year}$$

**(ii)** Cumulative landfill stock after 5 years (75 % landfilled each year, accumulated linearly):

$$m_{\text{landfill, 5yr}} = 0.75 \times 12.5 \times 5 = 46.875 \text{ million tonnes}$$

**(iii)** Volume occupied in landfill:

$$V = \frac{m}{\rho} = \frac{46.875 \times 10^9 \text{ kg}}{500 \text{ kg/m}^3} = 93.75 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^3$$

$$V \approx 9.375 \times 10^7 \text{ m}^3 \approx 93.75 \text{ million cubic metres}$$

This is equivalent to a cube of side $\approx 455$ m.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PEPOT632 Module 4]**

**(a)** With suitable examples, explain the classification of polymers based on their degradability. Compare biodegradable polymers such as PLA, PHB, and PCL in terms of their degradation mechanism and end products. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A biodegradable polymer sample shows first-order degradation kinetics with $k_{\text{deg}} = 0.025$ day⁻¹ at composting temperature. If the initial molecular weight is 80,000 g/mol, determine the time required for the molecular weight to drop to 5,000 g/mol — a threshold below which microbial assimilation is rapid. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Polymers can be classified on degradability grounds as:

- **Non-biodegradable / conventional:** PE, PP, PS, PVC, PET — degrade over centuries.
- **Biodegradable / compostable:** PLA, PHB, PHBV, PCL, PBS, starch blends — degrade under controlled conditions.
- **Photodegradable:** Polymers with added chromophores (carbonyl groups) that cleave under UV.
- **Oxo-degradable:** LDPE with pro-oxidant additives (controversial; mostly fragments into microplastics).
- **Water-soluble:** PVA, PVP — dissolve but do not necessarily biodegrade.

Comparison of biodegradable polymers:

| Polymer | Degradation Mechanism | End Products | Environment |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PLA (Poly lactic acid)** | Hydrolysis of ester bond → lactic acid → CO₂ + H₂O via microbial action | CO₂ + H₂O | Industrial compost (58 °C) |
| **PHB (Poly 3 hydroxybutyrate)** | Enzymatic surface erosion by PHB depolymerases | 3-hydroxybutyric acid → CO₂ + H₂O | Soil, marine, compost |
| **PCL (Poly ε caprolactone)** | Hydrolysis + enzymatic (lipase) cleavage | ε-hydroxycaproic acid → CO₂ + H₂O | Soil, compost |

**(b)** Use the integrated first-order degradation equation:

$$M_n(t) = M_n(0) \cdot e^{-k_{\text{deg}} t}$$

Substitute the values:

$$5{,}000 = 80{,}000 \cdot e^{-0.025 \cdot t}$$

Divide both sides:

$$\frac{5{,}000}{80{,}000} = e^{-0.025 t}$$

$$0.0625 = e^{-0.025 t}$$

Take natural log:

$$\ln(0.0625) = -0.025 t$$

$$-2.7726 = -0.025 t$$

$$t = \frac{2.7726}{0.025} \approx 110.9 \text{ days}$$

Therefore, approximately **111 days** are required for the polymer to reach the microbial-assimilation threshold.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Typically Lose Marks in PEPOT632 Module 4 Questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit Confusion:** Mixing tonnes/year, kg/day, and million tonnes without conversion. KTU examiners explicitly check for **unit consistency** in the final numerical answer.
> 2. **Hierarchy Mis-ordering:** Writing recycling above reduction or reuse. This is a **direct 2-mark penalty**.
> 3. **Confusing Biodegradation with Disintegration:** Biodegradation must result in CO₂ + H₂O (mineralisation). Mere fragmentation into microplastics is *not* biodegradation — KTU 2024 examiners are alert to this distinction.
> 4. **First-order vs. Zero-order Kinetics:** Degradation is generally first-order only when scission is random and reaction-limited. Always state the assumption explicitly.
> 5. **Molar Mass Ratios in CO₂ Calculations:** Forgetting the $\frac{44}{12}$ conversion factor is the single most common computational error.
> 6. **Missing Real-world Examples:** Theory without an example (PET bottle, mulch film, fishing net) attracts partial marks only. KTU 2024 scheme rewards applied context.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** The Polymer-Environment interface studies the cradle-to-cradle lifecycle of polymeric materials, with emphasis on waste, recycling, and degradation.
- **Global Scale:** ~400 million tonnes of virgin plastic are produced annually; cumulative stock exceeds 10 billion tonnes since 1950.
- **Recycling Fraction:** Less than 9 % of all plastic ever produced has been recycled.
- **5R Hierarchy (descending priority):** Refuse → Reduce → Reuse → Recycle → Recover.
- **Four Recycling Routes:** Primary (re-extrusion), Secondary (mechanical), Tertiary (chemical/feedstock), Quaternary (energy recovery).
- **Master Formulae:** Recycling yield $\eta$, first-order degradation $M_n(t) = M_n(0) e^{-k_{\text{deg}} t}$, half-life $t_{1/2} = \ln 2 / k_{\text{deg}}$, CO₂ emission from incineration, Mark-Houwink intrinsic viscosity.
- **Persistence Cause:** High C–C (~348 kJ/mol) and C–H (~413 kJ/mol) bond dissociation energies.
- **Biodegradable Polymer Triad:** PLA (hydrolysis → lactic acid), PHB (enzymatic → 3-hydroxybutyrate), PCL (hydrolysis/lipase → ε-hydroxycaproic acid).
- **Critical Distinction:** Biodegradation = complete mineralisation to CO₂ + H₂O. Disintegration into microplastics ≠ biodegradation.
- **KTU 2024 Conceptual Emphasis:** Closed-loop circular economy > linear cradle-to-grave; design-for-recyclability is now a graduate attribute.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Enzymes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT632 - POLYMERS & ENVIRONMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Enzymes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Enzymes** are high-molecular-weight, globular **biopolymers** (predominantly proteins, with the exception of catalytic RNA molecules called *ribozymes*) produced by living cells, which act as highly specific biological catalysts that accelerate biochemical reactions without undergoing permanent chemical change or being consumed in the overall reaction.

Enzymes are essentially nature's own **nanomachines** — macromolecular polymers of **α-amino acid** monomers linked through peptide (amide) bonds, folded into a precise three-dimensional tertiary or quaternary structure. The catalytic activity is localized in a specific region called the **active site**, where substrate binding and conversion occur. From a polymer chemistry perspective, enzymes represent the most sophisticated class of *functional biopolymers*, with molecular weights ranging from **10,000 Da** (lysozyme) to over **2,000,000 Da** (glutamine synthetase).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEPOT632 — Module 4):**
> Enzymes are studied here as **biological polymers** that bridge polymer chemistry with environmental applications — particularly in **biodegradation of synthetic polymers**, **enzyme-mediated polymer synthesis**, and **biosensor development**. Understanding enzyme kinetics is essential for designing polymer-enzymatic bioreactors.

### 1.2 Classification of Enzymes (IUBMB Nomenclature)

The International Union of Biochemistry and Molecular Biology (IUBMB) classifies enzymes into **six major classes** based on the type of reaction catalyzed:

| Class | EC Number | Reaction Type | Example |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| Oxidoreductases | EC 1 | Electron transfer / redox | Alcohol dehydrogenase |
| Transferases | EC 2 | Group transfer between molecules | Hexokinase |
| Hydrolases | EC 3 | Hydrolytic bond cleavage | Lipase, Amylase |
| Lyases | EC 4 | Addition/removal of groups (non-hydrolytic) | Aldolase |
| Isomerases | EC 5 | Intramolecular rearrangement | Glucose isomerase |
| Ligases | EC 6 | Bond formation with ATP hydrolysis | DNA ligase |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Lock and Wrench"

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a key (substrate) trying to open a lock (enzyme). Only the correctly shaped key fits into the lock's keyhole (active site) — this is the **Lock-and-Key Model** proposed by Emil Fischer (1894). However, modern understanding is more like a *smart glove* — when your hand (substrate) slides into the glove (enzyme), the glove slightly changes shape to grip better. This refined model is called the **Induced-Fit Model** proposed by Daniel Koshland (1958).

The enzyme wraps around its substrate, strains specific chemical bonds, and lowers the **activation energy (Eₐ)** of the reaction — much like a crowbar lowers the effort needed to lift a heavy rock. The enzyme emerges unchanged, ready to catalyze another reaction cycle (typically **10² – 10⁸ substrate molecules per second** — the *turnover number*, **k_cₐₜ**).

### 1.4 Holoenzyme Architecture

$$\text{Holoenzyme} = \text{Apoenzyme (protein part)} + \text{Cofactor (non-protein part)}$$

- **Cofactors** may be **metal ions** (e.g., Zn²⁺ in carbonic anhydrase, Mg²⁺ in ATP-dependent enzymes) or **organic coenzymes** (e.g., NAD⁺, FAD, CoA — often derived from vitamins).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Enzyme Specificity

Enzymes exhibit **four types of specificity**:

1. **Group Specificity** — acts on a specific functional group (e.g., all –OH groups).
2. **Linkage Specificity** — acts on a specific bond type (e.g., peptide bond, glycosidic bond).
3. **Absolute Specificity** — acts on only one specific substrate.
4. **Stereochemical Specificity** — distinguishes between optical isomers and geometrical isomers.

### 2.2 Factors Affecting Enzyme Activity

The operational efficiency of an enzyme is governed by the following parameters:

- **Temperature** — Activity rises with temperature up to an optimum (typically **30–45 °C** for most enzymes), beyond which denaturation occurs due to disruption of the tertiary structure (breaking of hydrogen bonds, hydrophobic interactions).
- **pH** — Each enzyme has an optimum pH (e.g., **pepsin at pH 2.0**, **alkaline phosphatase at pH 9.5**) where ionization states of active-site residues are optimal.
- **Substrate Concentration** — Reaction velocity increases hyperbolically with [S] and saturates at **Vₘₐₓ**.
- **Enzyme Concentration** — At saturating [S], rate is directly proportional to [E].
- **Inhibitors** — Molecules that reduce enzyme activity (competitive, non-competitive, uncompetitive).

### 2.3 Mechanism of Enzyme Catalysis — Six Logical Steps

1. **Substrate Approach:** Substrate diffuses into the active-site cleft through Brownian motion.
2. **Binding:** Substrate forms multiple weak non-covalent interactions (H-bonds, van der Waals, ionic, hydrophobic) with active-site residues.
3. **Conformational Adjustment (Induced Fit):** The enzyme changes shape to optimize catalytic geometry.
4. **Transition-State Stabilization:** The enzyme stabilizes the highest-energy *transition state* by **lowering Eₐ** (typically by **10⁸ – 10¹² fold** rate enhancement).
5. **Chemical Transformation:** Bond making/breaking occurs (acid-base catalysis, covalent catalysis, metal-ion catalysis, electrostatic catalysis).
6. **Product Release & Enzyme Regeneration:** Products depart; enzyme returns to its native conformation.

### 2.4 Environmental Significance — Enzyme Role in Polymer Degradation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the context of PEPOT632 (Polymers & Environment), enzymes are critical agents in:
> - **Biodegradation of synthetic polymers** (PET hydrolases, PETase, cutinases, lipases degrade PET, PLA, PCL).
> - **Polymer biosynthesis** (enzymatic ring-opening polymerization of lactones).
> - **Bioremediation** of polymer-contaminated environments.
> - **Green polymer chemistry** — replacing toxic metal catalysts.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Michaelis-Menten Equation | $v = \dfrac{V_{max} \cdot [S]}{K_m + [S]}$ | Fundamental enzyme kinetics |
| Half-saturation constant | $[S] = K_m \;\Rightarrow\; v = \dfrac{V_{max}}{2}$ | Definition of K_m |
| Turnover number | $k_{cat} = \dfrac{V_{max}}{[E]_{total}}$ | Substrate molecules converted per second per enzyme |
| Catalytic efficiency | $\dfrac{k_{cat}}{K_m}$ | Specificity constant (diffusion limit ≈ **10⁸ – 10⁹ M⁻¹ s⁻¹**) |
| Lineweaver-Burk (double reciprocal) | $\dfrac{1}{v} = \dfrac{K_m}{V_{max}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{[S]} + \dfrac{1}{V_{max}}$ | Linear form for plotting |
| Competitive inhibition | $K_m^{app} = K_m \left(1 + \dfrac{[I]}{K_i}\right)$ ; $V_{max}$ unchanged | Inhibitor binds active site |
| Non-competitive inhibition | $V_{max}^{app} = \dfrac{V_{max}}{1 + [I]/K_i}$ ; $K_m$ unchanged | Inhibitor binds allosteric site |
| Uncompetitive inhibition | $K_m^{app} = \dfrac{K_m}{1 + [I]/K_i}$ ; $V_{max}^{app} = \dfrac{V_{max}}{1 + [I]/K_i}$ | Inhibitor binds ES complex |
| Arrhenius dependence | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ | Temperature effect on rate constant |
| Activation energy lowering | $E_{a,cat} \ll E_{a,uncat}$ | Enzyme lowers Eₐ by **80–120 kJ/mol** typical |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Michaelis-Menten kinetics underpins the design of industrial **enzymatic bioreactors** for polymer synthesis/degradation, **biosensors** for pollutant detection, and **pharmaceutical drug discovery** (where inhibitor kinetics are essential).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Michaelis-Menten Equation

The Michaelis-Menten model assumes a simple **two-step mechanism**:

$$E + S \;\underset{k_{-1}}{\overset{k_1}{\rightleftharpoons}}\; ES \;\overset{k_{cat}}{\longrightarrow}\; E + P$$

**Step 1 — Rate of ES formation:**
$$\frac{d[ES]}{dt} = k_1 \cdot [E] \cdot [S] - k_{-1} \cdot [ES] - k_{cat} \cdot [ES]$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Steady-State Approximation** (Briggs-Haldane, 1925):
$$\frac{d[ES]}{dt} \approx 0$$

**Step 3 — Conservation of total enzyme:**
$$[E]_0 = [E] + [ES]$$

**Step 4 — Express free enzyme [E]:**
$$[E] = [E]_0 - [ES]$$
