**Step 5 — Substitute into the steady-state expression:**
$$k_1 \left([E]_0 - [ES]\right) [S] = k_{-1}[ES] + k_{cat}[ES]$$

**Step 6 — Expand and isolate [ES]:**
$$k_1 [E]_0 [S] - k_1 [ES][S] = (k_{-1} + k_{cat})[ES]$$

$$k_1 [E]_0 [S] = (k_{-1} + k_{cat})[ES] + k_1 [ES][S]$$

**Step 7 — Factor [ES]:**
$$k_1 [E]_0 [S] = [ES] \left( k_{-1} + k_{cat} + k_1 [S] \right)$$

**Step 8 — Define the Michaelis constant:**
$$K_m = \frac{k_{-1} + k_{cat}}{k_1}$$

**Step 9 — Solve for [ES]:**
$$[ES] = \frac{k_1 [E]_0 [S]}{k_{-1} + k_{cat} + k_1 [S]} = \frac{[E]_0 [S]}{K_m + [S]}$$

**Step 10 — Initial reaction velocity** is governed by product formation:
$$v = k_{cat} \cdot [ES]$$

**Step 11 — Substitute [ES]:**
$$v = k_{cat} \cdot \frac{[E]_0 [S]}{K_m + [S]}$$

**Step 12 — Recognize that $V_{max} = k_{cat} \cdot [E]_0$ (at saturation, all enzyme is bound):**

$$\boxed{\; v = \frac{V_{max} \cdot [S]}{K_m + [S]} \;}$$

This is the **Michaelis-Menten equation (1913)**, foundational to all enzyme kinetics.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Lineweaver-Burk (Double Reciprocal) Linear Form

Starting from the Michaelis-Menten equation:

$$v = \frac{V_{max} [S]}{K_m + [S]}$$

**Step 1 — Invert both sides:**
$$\frac{1}{v} = \frac{K_m + [S]}{V_{max} [S]}$$

**Step 2 — Split the fraction:**
$$\frac{1}{v} = \frac{K_m}{V_{max} [S]} + \frac{[S]}{V_{max} [S]}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the second term:**
$$\frac{1}{v} = \frac{K_m}{V_{max}} \cdot \frac{1}{[S]} + \frac{1}{V_{max}}$$

**Step 4 — Identify y = 1/v, x = 1/[S]:**

$$\boxed{\; y = \underbrace{\frac{K_m}{V_{max}}}_{\text{slope}} \cdot x + \underbrace{\frac{1}{V_{max}}}_{\text{y-intercept}} \;}$$

- **y-intercept** → $1 / V_{max}$
- **x-intercept** → $-1 / K_m$
- **Slope** → $K_m / V_{max}$

This linearization is used experimentally to determine **$V_{max}$** and **$K_m$** and to differentiate between **competitive, non-competitive, and uncompetitive inhibition**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Inhibition Mechanisms

| Parameter | No Inhibitor | Competitive | Non-competitive | Uncompetitive |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $V_{max}$ | $V_{max}$ | Unchanged ($\uparrow K_m$) | Decreased | Decreased |
| $K_m$ | $K_m$ | Increased | Unchanged | Decreased |
| Lineweaver-Burk Lines | One line | Intersect on y-axis | Intersect on x-axis | Parallel lines |
| Binding Site | — | Active site | Allosteric site | ES complex only |
| Effect of ↑[S] | — | Overcome inhibition | Cannot overcome | Cannot overcome |

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** An enzyme has $V_{max} = 50 \;\mu\text{mol/min}$ and $K_m = 0.5 \;\text{mM}$. Calculate the reaction velocity at $[S] = 0.2 \;\text{mM}$ and at $[S] = 5 \;\text{mM}$. Comment on the result.

**Solution:**

At $[S] = 0.2 \;\text{mM}$ (sub-saturating, $S < K_m$):
$$v = \frac{50 \times 0.2}{0.5 + 0.2} = \frac{10}{0.7} = 14.29 \;\mu\text{mol/min}$$

At $[S] = 5 \;\text{mM}$ (saturating, $S \gg K_m$):
$$v = \frac{50 \times 5}{0.5 + 5} = \frac{250}{5.5} = 45.45 \;\mu\text{mol/min} \approx 0.91 \; V_{max}$$

**Comment:** At $[S] = 5 \;\text{mM}$, the enzyme is approaching saturation. To reach $0.99 \; V_{max}$, we would need $[S] \approx 99 \cdot K_m$, demonstrating that enzymes have a finite catalytic capacity governed by $V_{max}$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Enzyme Catalytic Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Free Enzyme E] --> B[E binds Substrate S]
    B --> C[Enzyme-Substrate Complex ES]
    C --> D[Transition State Stabilization]
    D --> E[Chemical Transformation]
    E --> F[Enzyme-Product Complex EP]
    F --> G[Product P Released]
    G --> A
    H[Inhibitor I] -. Competitive binding .-> A
    H -. Allosteric binding .-> C
    I[Environmental factors: pH, T, [S]] --> A
    J[Cofactor / Coenzyme] --> A
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Holoenzyme Structural Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Holoenzyme - Complete Active Enzyme] --> B[Apoenzyme - Protein Component]
    A --> C[Cofactor - Non-protein Component]
    B --> D[Primary Structure - Amino Acid Sequence]
    B --> E[Secondary Structure - Alpha Helix, Beta Sheet]
    B --> F[Tertiary Structure - 3D Fold]
    B --> G[Quaternary Structure - Multi-subunit Assembly]
    C --> H[Metal Ion Cofactor - Zn2+, Mg2+, Fe2+]
    C --> I[Coenzyme - NAD+, FAD, CoA]
    F --> J[Active Site - Catalytic Pocket]
    F --> K[Allosteric Site - Regulatory Pocket]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Graph — Enzyme Inhibition Mechanism Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Enzyme Inhibitor Analysis] --> B[Competitive Inhibitor]
    A --> C[Non-competitive Inhibitor]
    A --> D[Uncompetitive Inhibitor]
    B --> B1[Binds Active Site]
    B --> B2[Increases Km Apparent]
    B --> B3[Vmax Unchanged]
    C --> C1[Binds Allosteric Site]
    C --> C2[Km Unchanged]
    C --> C3[Vmax Decreased]
    D --> D1[Binds ES Complex]
    D --> D2[Both Km and Vmax Decrease]
    D --> D3[Rare in Single-substrate Reactions]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Enzyme in Polymer Biodegradation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Synthetic Polymer Substrate] --> B[Enzyme Adsorption on Polymer Surface]
    B --> C[Active Site Recognition of Polymer Chain]
    C --> D[Hydrolytic or Oxidative Cleavage of Backbone Bonds]
    D --> E[Formation of Low MW Oligomers]
    E --> F[Further Degradation to Monomers]
    F --> G[Mineralization: CO2 and H2O]
    H[Environmental Factors pH Temperature Moisture] --> B
    I[Enzyme Examples PETase Lipase Cutinase] --> C
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**
*Define enzymes. Mention any four classes of enzymes with one example each.*

**Model Answer:**
Enzymes are high-molecular-weight biological catalysts, which are proteinaceous in nature (except ribozymes) and accelerate biochemical reactions without being consumed.

Four classes:
1. **Oxidoreductases** — catalyze oxidation-reduction (e.g., alcohol dehydrogenase).
2. **Transferases** — transfer functional groups (e.g., hexokinase).
3. **Hydrolases** — cleave bonds by hydrolysis (e.g., lipase).
4. **Isomerases** — catalyze intramolecular rearrangement (e.g., glucose isomerase).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary Pattern]**
*Explain the "induced fit" model of enzyme action. How does it differ from the "lock and key" model?*

**Model Answer:**
The **induced fit model** (Koshland, 1958) proposes that the active site of an enzyme is not a rigid, pre-formed structure complementary to the substrate. Instead, when the substrate approaches, it induces a **conformational change** in the enzyme, reshaping the active site to achieve optimal catalytic geometry.

In contrast, the **lock and key model** (Fischer, 1894) proposes a rigid, pre-formed active site that exactly complements the substrate like a lock and key, with no conformational change.

**Key Difference:** Induced fit allows for *dynamic flexibility* and explains *transition-state stabilization*, whereas lock-and-key is a *static* representation. Induced fit is the currently accepted model.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme regulation: Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with sub-division typically **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Question Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern, PEPOT632 Module 4]**

**(a)** *Derive the Michaelis-Menten equation. Discuss the significance of $K_m$ and $V_{max}$ with a suitable graphical representation. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (5 marks):**
For the reaction $E + S \rightleftharpoons ES \rightarrow E + P$, applying the steady-state approximation to [ES]:

$$\frac{d[ES]}{dt} = k_1[E][S] - k_{-1}[ES] - k_{cat}[ES] = 0$$

With $[E] = [E]_0 - [ES]$ and $K_m = (k_{-1} + k_{cat})/k_1$, we obtain:

$$v = \frac{V_{max}[S]}{K_m + [S]}$$

**Significance (2 marks):**

- **$V_{max}$** = maximum velocity when all enzyme is saturated with substrate; equals $k_{cat} \cdot [E]_0$.
- **$K_m$** = substrate concentration at which $v = V_{max}/2$; inversely related to enzyme-substrate affinity.
- **Graphical plot:** Hyperbolic curve of $v$ vs $[S]$ that asymptotically approaches $V_{max}$.

**(b)** *Explain the different types of enzyme inhibition with the help of Lineweaver-Burk plots. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Competitive Inhibition (2 marks):** Inhibitor resembles the substrate and binds the active site reversibly. $K_m$ increases (apparent $K_m = K_m(1 + [I]/K_i)$), while $V_{max}$ remains unchanged. On Lineweaver-Burk plot, lines intersect at the **y-axis**.

**Non-competitive Inhibition (2 marks):** Inhibitor binds to a site other than the active site (allosteric site). $V_{max}$ decreases (apparent $V_{max} = V_{max}/(1 + [I]/K_i)$), while $K_m$ is unchanged. Lines intersect at the **x-axis**.

**Uncompetitive Inhibition (2 marks):** Inhibitor binds only to the ES complex. Both $V_{max}$ and $K_m$ decrease by the same factor. Lineweaver-Burk plot shows **parallel lines**.

**Graphical distinction (1 mark):** The pattern of intersection of Lineweaver-Burk lines on the plot reveals the inhibition mechanism.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Question Choice Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern, PEPOT632 Module 4]**

**(a)** *Discuss the structure of enzymes with a note on coenzymes and cofactors. Explain the role of enzymes in the biodegradation of synthetic polymers. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Structure of Enzymes (4 marks):**
Enzymes are globular proteins with a specific 3D structure:
- **Primary structure** — linear sequence of amino acids linked by peptide bonds.
- **Secondary structure** — α-helices and β-sheets stabilized by H-bonds.
- **Tertiary structure** — overall 3D fold; determines the active-site geometry.
- **Quaternary structure** — assembly of multiple polypeptide subunits.

The **active site** is a small pocket (typically <5% of total volume) containing:
- **Binding residues** — anchor the substrate.
- **Catalytic residues** — perform bond-making/breaking.

**Coenzymes & Cofactors (2 marks):**
Many enzymes require non-protein cofactors:
- **Metal ion cofactors** — Zn²⁺ (carbonic anhydrase), Mg²⁺ (kinases), Fe²⁺ (peroxidases).
- **Coenzymes** — organic molecules, often vitamin derivatives: NAD⁺ (from niacin), FAD (riboflavin), CoA (pantothenic acid), PLP (vitamin B6).

The complete active enzyme = **apoenzyme + cofactor = holoenzyme**.

**Role in Biodegradation (1 mark):**
Enzymes such as **PETase**, **lipase**, **cutinase**, and **MHETase** hydrolyze ester bonds in synthetic polyesters like PET, PLA, and PCL, producing environmentally benign monomers that undergo microbial mineralization to CO₂ and H₂O.

**(b)** *Explain the factors affecting enzyme activity. Add a note on the effect of temperature and pH. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Factors (4 marks):**
1. **Substrate concentration** — velocity increases hyperbolically with [S]; saturation at $V_{max}$.
2. **Enzyme concentration** — at saturating [S], rate is directly proportional to [E].
3. **Temperature** — follows Arrhenius behavior up to optimum, beyond which denaturation occurs.
4. **pH** — affects ionization of active-site residues; each enzyme has an optimum pH.
5. **Inhibitors** — competitive, non-competitive, uncompetitive.
6. **Cofactor availability** — many enzymes require metal ions or coenzymes.

**Effect of Temperature (1.5 marks):**
- Rate doubles approximately for every 10 °C rise (Q₁₀ ≈ 2).
- Optimum temperature typically **30–45 °C** (extremophile enzymes can withstand 80–120 °C).
- Above optimum, hydrogen bonds and hydrophobic interactions break → irreversible denaturation.

**Effect of pH (1.5 marks):**
- Affects ionization of carboxyl, amino, and other active-site groups.
- Optimum pH: **pepsin (2.0)**, **trypsin (8.0)**, **alkaline phosphatase (9.5)**.
- Extreme pH disrupts ionic and H-bonding networks → denaturation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting steady-state assumption:** Many students write $d[ES]/dt = 0$ but forget to justify *why* (because [ES] is small and constant during initial-rate experiments).
> 2. **Confusing $K_m$ with $K_s$:** $K_m = (k_{-1} + k_{cat})/k_1$, *not* $k_{-1}/k_1$. The two are equal only when $k_{cat} \ll k_{-1}$.
> 3. **Mixing up inhibitor effects:** Competitive inhibition **does not** change $V_{max}$. Writing "competitive inhibition decreases $V_{max}$" will cost 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Lock-and-key vs induced-fit:** If asked to differentiate, *always* mention Koshland (1958) for induced fit and Fischer (1894) for lock-and-key.
> 5. **Skipping units:** Always state K_m in **M (or mM)**, $V_{max}$ in **mol·L⁻¹·s⁻¹**, and $k_{cat}$ in **s⁻¹**.
> 6. **Polymers & Environment context:** Don't answer in pure biochem style — *always* connect enzymes to polymer degradation, biosynthesis, or environmental remediation for full marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Enzymes are biopolymers** of α-amino acids (proteins) with catalytic activity; a few are catalytic RNAs (*ribozymes*).
- **Holoenzyme = Apoenzyme + Cofactor** (metal ion or coenzyme).
- **Six IUBMB classes:** Oxidoreductases, Transferases, Hydrolases, Lyases, Isomerases, Ligases.
- **Michaelis-Menten equation:** $v = V_{max}[S]/(K_m + [S])$.
- **$K_m$** is the substrate concentration at $v = V_{max}/2$; inversely related to binding affinity.
- **$V_{max} = k_{cat} \cdot [E]_0$**; $k_{cat}$ = turnover number (s⁻¹).
- **Catalytic efficiency** = $k_{cat}/K_m$; upper limit ≈ **10⁸ – 10⁹ M⁻¹ s⁻¹** (diffusion-controlled).
- **Lineweaver-Burk plot:** $1/v$ vs $1/[S]$ gives linear form with intercepts $1/V_{max}$ and $-1/K_m$.
- **Inhibition patterns (must memorize):**
  - Competitive → $\uparrow K_m$, $V_{max}$ same → lines meet at y-axis.
  - Non-competitive → $K_m$ same, $\downarrow V_{max}$ → lines meet at x-axis.
  - Uncompetitive → both $K_m$ and $V_{max}$ decrease → parallel lines.
- **Induced-fit model** (Koshland, 1958) supersedes lock-and-key (Fischer, 1894).
- **Optimal pH and temperature** are enzyme-specific; denaturation occurs beyond the optimum.
- **Environmental relevance:** PETase, lipase, cutinase enable biodegradation of synthetic polyesters like PET and PLA.
- **Enzymes lower activation energy** by **80–120 kJ/mol**, accelerating reactions by **10⁸ – 10¹² fold**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Enzyme Nomenclature
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT632 - POLYMERS & ENVIRONMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Enzyme Nomenclature

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Enzyme Nomenclature** is the internationally standardized system of classifying, naming, and assigning a unique numerical code (the **Enzyme Commission / EC number**) to every known enzyme, as recommended by the **International Union of Biochemistry and Molecular Biology (IUBMB)** through the Nomenclature Committee of the International Union of Biochemistry (NC-IUB). Each enzyme receives:

- A **Systematic Name** — describes the reaction catalysed, including substrate(s) and product(s).
- A **Recommended (Trivial) Name** — short, easy-to-remember, often the historically used name.
- A **Four-Digit EC Code (EC x.x.x.x)** — machine-readable classification identifier.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** In the context of *Polymers & Environment*, enzyme nomenclature is crucial because biodegradable polymer degradation (e.g., polyesters, polyurethanes) relies on enzymes such as **lipases, cutinases, esterases, proteases, and peroxidases**, all of which follow the EC classification.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of enzyme nomenclature like the **postal address system of enzymes**:

- The **EC number** is like a **PIN code** — four precise digits that identify one unique enzyme, no matter where in the world you read about it.
- The **systematic name** is like the **full legal name** on a government document — accurate, descriptive, but long and cumbersome.
- The **recommended (trivial) name** is like a **nickname** — short and familiar, but sometimes ambiguous (just as “John” is common).

For instance, an enzyme called “**Catalase**” in daily biology textbooks is more precisely described as:
**Hydrogen-peroxide : hydrogen-peroxide oxidoreductase**, with the code **EC 1.11.1.6**.

### 1.3 Historical Context

Before the 1960s, enzymes were named haphazardly:
- by substrate (e.g., **urease** — splits urea),
- by action (e.g., **trypsin** — a digestive protease),
- or by discoverer (e.g., **Pavlov’s enzyme**).

This led to chaos. In **1961**, the Enzyme Commission (EC) was formed under the IUB, and in **1964** the first *Enzyme Nomenclature Report* was published, introducing the EC code system we still use today.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Used in Nomenclature:** Turnover Number (kcat) has units of **s⁻¹**, and Specific Activity uses **µmol·min⁻¹·mg⁻¹**. These are the canonical performance descriptors that complement the EC code.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four-Level EC Number — Logical Breakdown

The EC number follows the strict hierarchical pattern:

$$\text{EC} \; \underbrace{a}_{\text{Class}} \; . \; \underbrace{b}_{\text{Subclass}} \; . \; \underbrace{c}_{\text{Sub-subclass}} \; . \; \underbrace{d}_{\text{Serial index}}$$

**Step-by-step meaning of each digit:**

- **Digit 1 (a) → Class** — Broad reaction type (six possible values: 1–6).
- **Digit 2 (b) → Subclass** — Specifies the **type of bond or group** involved in the reaction (e.g., CH–OH, C=O, C–N).
- **Digit 3 (c) → Sub-subclass** — Specifies the **type of acceptor/donor or cofactor** involved.
- **Digit 4 (d) → Serial number** — A unique running serial assigned by the IUBMB committee within that sub-subclass.

### 2.2 The Six Master Classes of Enzymes

| Class | EC Digit | Class Name | Type of Reaction Catalysed |
| :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | EC 1.x.x.x | **Oxidoreductases** | Transfer of H or O atoms / electrons |
| 2 | EC 2.x.x.x | **Transferases** | Transfer of functional groups between molecules |
| 3 | EC 3.x.x.x | **Hydrolases** | Hydrolytic bond cleavage (addition of H₂O) |
| 4 | EC 4.x.x.x | **Lyases** | Non-hydrolytic bond cleavage (often forming C=C) |
| 5 | EC 5.x.x.x | **Isomerases** | Intramolecular rearrangements / isomerisations |
| 6 | EC 6.x.x.x | **Ligases (synthetases)** | Joining of two molecules using ATP |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for Polymer Degradation:**
> **EC 3.x.x.x (Hydrolases)** is the most relevant class for polymer biodegradation. **Lipase (EC 3.1.1.3)**, **cutinase (EC 3.1.1.74)**, and **PETase (EC 3.1.1.101)** — discovered in *Ideonella sakaiensis* — all belong here and are responsible for breaking down **PET, PLA, PCL**, and related polyesters.

### 2.3 Worked Examples of EC Classification

| Recommended Name | Systematic Name | EC Number |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Catalase | Hydrogen-peroxide : hydrogen-peroxide oxidoreductase | EC 1.11.1.6 |
| Alcohol dehydrogenase | Alcohol : NAD⁺ oxidoreductase | EC 1.1.1.1 |
| Hexokinase | ATP : D-hexose 6-phosphotransferase | EC 2.7.1.1 |
| Lipase | Triacylglycerol acylhydrolase | EC 3.1.1.3 |
| Cutinase | Cutin hydrolase | EC 3.1.1.74 |
| Aspartate ammonia-lyase | L-aspartate ammonia-lyase (deaminating) | EC 4.3.1.1 |
| Glucose-6-phosphate isomerase | D-glucose-6-phosphate aldose-ketose-isomerase | EC 5.3.1.9 |
| Glutamine synthetase | L-glutamate : ammonia ligase (ADP-forming) | EC 6.3.1.2 |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Parameter / Rule | Expression / Value | Unit / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| EC number format | EC $\,a.b.c.d$ | Pure classification code, dimensionless |
| Class count | 6 | Oxidoreductase, Transferase, Hydrolase, Lyase, Isomerase, Ligase |
| Specific Activity | $SA = \dfrac{V_{max}}{\text{protein mass}}$ | $\mu\text{mol}\cdot\text{min}^{-1}\cdot\text{mg}^{-1}$ |
| Turnover Number | $k_{cat} = \dfrac{V_{max}}{[E]_{total}}$ | $\text{s}^{-1}$ |
| Catalytic Efficiency | $\dfrac{k_{cat}}{K_{m}}$ | $\text{M}^{-1}\cdot\text{s}^{-1}$ |
| Michaelis–Menten Rate | $v = \dfrac{V_{max}[S]}{K_{m} + [S]}$ | $\text{M}\cdot\text{s}^{-1}$ |
| Lock-and-Key vs Induced-Fit | $K_{m}$ shifts under fit model | — |
| Coenzyme for EC 1 (dehydrogenases) | NAD⁺ / NADP⁺ / FAD | — |
| Hydrolase water stoichiometry | $\text{R–X–R'} + \text{H}_{2}\text{O} \rightarrow \text{R–OH} + \text{R'–H}$ | Net 1 H₂O consumed |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In environmental polymer engineering, EC-based screening of microbial genomes allows rapid identification of biodegradation pathways. The **PETase EC 3.1.1.101** discovery (Yoshida et al., *Science*, 2016) is a landmark in enzymatic plastic recycling.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the EC Naming Logic from Reaction Stoichiometry

The IUBMB rule states: **the systematic name must encode (substrate + donor/acceptor + reaction type)**. Let us derive the systematic name of **lactate dehydrogenase** step by step.

**Step 1 — Identify the chemical reaction catalysed:**

$$\text{L-lactate} + \text{NAD}^{+} \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{pyruvate} + \text{NADH} + \text{H}^{+}$$

This is a redox reaction (oxidation of L-lactate to pyruvate, with NAD⁺ as electron acceptor).

**Step 2 — Identify the EC class (Digit 1):**
Since electrons/H are transferred between substrates, the enzyme is an **oxidoreductase**.

$$\text{Digit 1} = 1$$

**Step 3 — Identify the subclass (Digit 2):**
The donor group is the **CH–OH group** of lactate (oxidised to C=O). IUBMB subclass 1.1 corresponds to enzymes acting on the **CH–OH group of donors**.

$$\text{Digit 2} = 1$$

**Step 4 — Identify the sub-subclass (Digit 3):**
The acceptor is **NAD⁺**. IUBMB sub-subclass 1.1.1 corresponds to enzymes with **NAD⁺ or NADP⁺ as acceptor**.

$$\text{Digit 3} = 1$$

**Step 5 — Identify the serial index (Digit 4):**
Lactate dehydrogenase is the **first** enzyme assigned in this sub-subclass group.

$$\text{Digit 4} = 1$$

**Step 6 — Final assembled EC code:**

$$\boxed{\text{Lactate dehydrogenase} \;\equiv\; \text{EC 1.1.1.1}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Systematic Name

Following the IUBMB format `donor : acceptor oxidoreductase`:

$$\text{Donor} = \text{(S)-lactate}, \quad \text{Acceptor} = \text{NAD}^{+}$$

Therefore the systematic name becomes:

$$\boxed{\text{(S)-lactate : NAD}^{+} \text{ oxidoreductase}}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix for EC Classification of Polymer-Degrading Enzymes

For each enzyme below, the classification logic is derived using the same four-step procedure:

| Enzyme | Reaction | Class Logic | Subclass Logic | Sub-subclass Logic | EC Code |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Lipase** | Triacylglycerol + H₂O → diacylglycerol + fatty acid | Hydrolase (3) | Acting on ester bond (1) | Carboxylic-ester hydrolase (1) | EC 3.1.1.3 |
| **Cutinase** | Cutin + H₂O → cutin monomers | Hydrolase (3) | Acting on ester bond (1) | Cutin hydrolase (74) | EC 3.1.1.74 |
| **PETase** | PET + H₂O → MHET + Terephthalic acid | Hydrolase (3) | Acting on ester bond (1) | PET-hydrolysing (101) | EC 3.1.1.101 |
| **Laccase** | 4 Benzenediol + O₂ → 4 Benzosemiquinone + 2 H₂O | Oxidoreductase (1) | Acting on diphenols (10) | With O₂ as acceptor (3) | EC 1.10.3.2 |
| **Manganese peroxidase** | 2 Mn²⁺ + 2 H⁺ + H₂O₂ → 2 Mn³⁺ + 2 H₂O | Oxidoreductase (1) | Acting on peroxide (11) | Peroxidase (1) | EC 1.11.1.13 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The serial index (digit 4) is assigned chronologically as enzymes are discovered and characterised. New polymer-degrading enzymes (e.g., MHETase = EC 3.1.1.102) are added by IUBMB based on peer-reviewed publication of biochemical characterisation.

### 3.4 Rate Equation Derivation for Enzymatic Polymer Degradation

For surface-eroding biodegradable polymers, the degradation rate often follows the **Michaelis–Menten model**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Initial velocity:} \quad & v_{0} = \dfrac{d[P]}{dt} \\[6pt]
\text{Steady-state assumption:} \quad & \dfrac{d[ES]}{dt} = k_{1}[E][S] - (k_{-1} + k_{cat})[ES] = 0 \\[6pt]
\text{Solving for } [ES]: \quad & [ES] = \dfrac{[E]_{t}[S]}{K_{m} + [S]} \\[6pt]
\text{Where:} \quad & K_{m} = \dfrac{k_{-1} + k_{cat}}{k_{1}} \\[6pt]
\text{Final rate equation:} \quad & \boxed{\; v_{0} = \dfrac{V_{max}[S]}{K_{m} + [S]} \;}
\end{aligned}
$$

Here:
- $[S]$ = polymer accessible surface ester bond concentration (in equivalents L⁻¹),
- $K_{m}$ = Michaelis constant (M), inversely related to enzyme–substrate affinity,
- $V_{max} = k_{cat}[E]_{t}$ = maximum degradation rate.

For **polymer surface erosion**, this is often combined with the **surface area to volume ratio**:

$$
\dfrac{dM_{polymer}}{dt} = -k_{cat}[E]\cdot A_{surface} \cdot \rho_{ester}
$$

This links **enzyme nomenclature** (EC code) directly to **macroscopic polymer mass loss** — a cornerstone concept in environmental polymer engineering.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Structure of EC Number

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[EC Number EC a.b.c.d] --> B[Digit a = Class 1 to 6]
    A --> C[Digit b = Subclass bond or group]
    A --> D[Digit c = Sub subclass acceptor or donor]
    A --> E[Digit d = Serial index]
    B --> B1[1 Oxidoreductases]
    B --> B2[2 Transferases]
    B --> B3[3 Hydrolases]
    B --> B4[4 Lyases]
    B --> B5[5 Isomerases]
    B --> B6[6 Ligases]
    B3 --> B3a[3.1 Ester bonds]
    B3 --> B3b[3.2 Glycosyl bonds]
    B3 --> B3c[3.4 Peptide bonds]
    B3a --> B3a1[3.1.1 Carboxylic ester hydrolases]
    B3a1 --> B3a1A[3.1.1.3 Lipase]
    B3a1 --> B3a1B[3.1.1.74 Cutinase]
    B3a1 --> B3a1C[3.1.1.101 PETase]
    E --> F[Assigned chronologically by IUBMB]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: From EC Code to Polymer Mass Loss

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SUB[Polymer Substrate PET PLA PCL] --> SA[Surface Adsorption]
    SA --> EB[Active Site Binding E with S]
    EB --> CAT[Enzymatic Catalysis EC 3.1.1.x]
    CAT --> PRO[Hydrolysis Products MHET Terephthalate Monomers]
    PRO --> MIN[Mineralisation CO2 H2O Biomass]
    subgraph Nomenclature Layer
        EC1[EC Number EC 3.1.1.101]
        EC2[Systematic Name PET hydrolase]
        EC3[Recommended Name PETase]
    end
    Nomenclature Layer -.metadata.-> CAT
    CAT --> RK[Rate Equation v = Vmax S over Km plus S]
    RK --> ML[Polymer Mass Loss dMdt]
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree for Assigning an EC Number to a New Enzyme

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Does the enzyme transfer electrons atoms or H?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> R1[Class 1 Oxidoreductase]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Does it transfer a functional group between molecules?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> R2[Class 2 Transferase]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Does it cleave a bond by hydrolysis addition of H2O?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> R3[Class 3 Hydrolase]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Does it cleave a bond by elimination leaving a double bond?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> R4[Class 4 Lyase]
    Q4 -- No --> Q5{Does it perform intramolecular rearrangement?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> R5[Class 5 Isomerase]
    Q5 -- No --> R6[Class 6 Ligase ATP dependent]
```

### 4.4 Mapping Polymer Types to Relevant EC Classes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[PET Polyethylene terephthalate] --> H1[EC 3.1.1.101 PETase]
    P2[PLA Polylactic acid] --> H2[EC 3.1.1.x Esterase]
    P3[PCL Polycaprolactone] --> H3[EC 3.1.1.3 Lipase]
    P4[Polyurethane PU] --> H4[EC 3.1.1.x Urethanase]
    P5[Polyhydroxyalkanoate PHA] --> H5[EC 3.1.1.x PHA depolymerase]
    P6[Cellulose based polymers] --> H6[EC 3.2.1.x Cellulase]
    P7[Lignin based polymers] --> H7[EC 1.10.3.x Laccase]
    P7 --> H8[EC 1.11.1.x Peroxidase]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2024 Scheme]**
*Define the term ‘EC number’ in enzyme nomenclature. Explain the meaning of each of the four digits in the code with the help of a suitable example.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Enzyme Commission (EC) number** is a four-digit numerical classification code assigned by the IUBMB to uniquely identify an enzyme based on the type of reaction it catalyses. The code follows the format **EC a.b.c.d**:

- **Digit a** → Class of the enzyme (1 = Oxidoreductase, 2 = Transferase, 3 = Hydrolase, 4 = Lyase, 5 = Isomerase, 6 = Ligase).
- **Digit b** → Subclass — specifies the **bond or group** involved.
- **Digit c** → Sub-subclass — specifies the **acceptor/donor/cofactor**.
- **Digit d** → Serial index — unique serial number within that sub-subclass.

**Example:** Lipase → EC 3.1.1.3 (Class 3 hydrolase, acting on 3.1 ester bond, 3.1.1 carboxylic ester hydrolases, serial no. 3).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2025 Scheme]**
*Distinguish between the ‘Systematic Name’ and ‘Recommended Name’ of an enzyme. Give one example each for a polymer-degrading enzyme.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Systematic Name | Recommended Name |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Basis** | Describes the exact chemical reaction catalysed | Traditional/trivial name widely used |
| **Length** | Long, formal, descriptive | Short, convenient |
| **Example for PETase** | Poly(ethylene terephthalate) hydrolase | PETase |
| **Example for Lipase** | Triacylglycerol acylhydrolase | Lipase |

The systematic name encodes *donor : acceptor reaction-type*, while the recommended name is for everyday scientific use.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**
**(a)** With a neat hierarchical diagram, describe the **IUBMB six-class enzyme classification system**, giving one example enzyme and one reaction for each class. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Apply the **four-step IUBMB nomenclature procedure** to systematically derive the EC number of **Lactate dehydrogenase**, whose reaction is:
L-lactate + NAD⁺ ⇌ pyruvate + NADH + H⁺. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer A(a) — IUBMB Six-Class System (7 Marks)

The IUBMB system classifies all enzymes into **six master classes**, based on the type of chemical reaction catalysed:

1. **Class 1 — Oxidoreductases (EC 1.x.x.x):** Catalyse oxidation–reduction reactions.
   - *Example:* Alcohol dehydrogenase (EC 1.1.1.1).
   - *Reaction:* Ethanol + NAD⁺ → Acetaldehyde + NADH + H⁺.

2. **Class 2 — Transferases (EC 2.x.x.x):** Transfer a functional group from donor to acceptor.
   - *Example:* Hexokinase (EC 2.7.1.1).
   - *Reaction:* D-Glucose + ATP → D-Glucose-6-phosphate + ADP.

3. **Class 3 — Hydrolases (EC 3.x.x.x):** Catalyse hydrolytic cleavage using water.
   - *Example:* Lipase (EC 3.1.1.3).
   - *Reaction:* Triacylglycerol + H₂O → Diacylglycerol + Fatty acid.

4. **Class 4 — Lyases (EC 4.x.x.x):** Non-hydrolytic bond cleavage, often forming C=C bonds.
   - *Example:* Aspartate ammonia-lyase (EC 4.3.1.1).
   - *Reaction:* L-aspartate → Fumarate + NH₃.

5. **Class 5 — Isomerases (EC 5.x.x.x):** Intramolecular rearrangements.
   - *Example:* Glucose-6-phosphate isomerase (EC 5.3.1.9).
   - *Reaction:* D-Glucose-6-phosphate ⇌ D-Fructose-6-phosphate.

6. **Class 6 — Ligases (EC 6.x.x.x):** Join two molecules with ATP hydrolysis.
   - *Example:* Glutamine synthetase (EC 6.3.1.2).
   - *Reaction:* L-Glutamate + NH₃ + ATP → L-Glutamine + ADP + Pi.

**(Block diagram already shown in SECTION 4.1.)**

#### Model Answer A(b) — Derivation of EC 1.1.1.1 (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Reaction:**
L-lactate + NAD⁺ ⇌ pyruvate + NADH + H⁺.

**Step 2 — Class (Digit 1):**
This is a redox reaction (oxidation of lactate with NAD⁺ as acceptor).
$$\text{Class} = 1 \; (\text{Oxidoreductase})$$

**Step 3 — Subclass (Digit 2):**
The oxidised group is the **CH–OH group** of lactate → C=O of pyruvate.
IUBMB subclass 1.1 = enzymes acting on the **CH–OH group of donors**.
$$\text{Subclass} = 1$$

**Step 4 — Sub-subclass (Digit 3):**
Electron acceptor = NAD⁺. IUBMB sub-subclass 1.1.1 = **NAD⁺ or NADP⁺ as acceptor**.
$$\text{Sub-subclass} = 1$$

**Step 5 — Serial index (Digit 4):**
First enzyme chronologically assigned in 1.1.1 group.
$$\text{Serial} = 1$$

**Step 6 — Final EC code and systematic name:**

$$
\boxed{\text{EC 1.1.1.1} \;\equiv\; \text{(S)-lactate : NAD}^{+} \text{ oxidoreductase}}
$$

This is the recommended name **Lactate dehydrogenase**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2025 Scheme, Module 4]**
**(a)** Explain the **three-tier naming convention** of enzymes (Systematic, Recommended, EC code) using the example **PETase**. Provide the full systematic name, recommended name, and the EC code of this enzyme. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **role of hydrolases (EC 3.x.x.x)** in **biodegradation of synthetic polymers** such as PET, PLA, and PCL. Include reaction equations and enzyme names in your answer. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer B(a) — PETase Naming (7 Marks)

**PETase** is the enzyme discovered by *Yoshida et al. (2016)* in the bacterium *Ideonella sakaiensis* that degrades **poly(ethylene terephthalate)**.

- **Recommended (Trivial) Name:** PETase
- **Systematic Name:** Poly(ethylene terephthalate) hydrolase
- **EC Number:** **EC 3.1.1.101**

**Interpretation of digits:**
- **3** → Hydrolase class (cleaves by hydrolysis).
- **3.1** → Subclass: acting on **ester bonds**.
- **3.1.1** → Sub-subclass: **carboxylic-ester hydrolases**.
- **3.1.1.101** → 101st enzyme assigned in this sub-subclass (chronologically).

---

#### Model Answer B(b) — Hydrolases in Polymer Biodegradation (7 Marks)

**Hydrolases (Class 3)** are the principal enzymes for **biodegradation of condensation polymers**, since these polymers contain hydrolysable bonds (ester, amide, urethane).

**1. PET (Polyethylene terephthalate):**
Catalysed by **PETase (EC 3.1.1.101)** and **MHETase (EC 3.1.1.102)**:
$$\text{PET}_{(n)} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{PETase}} \text{MHET} + \text{Terephthalate products}$$
$$\text{MHET} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{MHETase}} \text{Terephthalic acid} + \text{Ethylene glycol}$$

**2. PLA (Polylactic acid):**
Catalysed by **Lipase (EC 3.1.1.3)** and **Proteinase K (EC 3.4.21.64)**:
$$\text{PLA}_{(n)} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{lipase}} \text{Lactic acid oligomers/monomers}$$

**3. PCL (Polycaprolactone):**
Catalysed by **Pseudomonas lipase (EC 3.1.1.3)**:
$$\text{PCL}_{(n)} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{lipase}} \varepsilon\text{-caprolactone + hydroxycaproic acid}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> **Common Mark-Loss Mistakes in B(b):**
> 1. Writing “lipase degrades all polymers” — examiners deduct 1–2 marks for **non-specificity**. Each enzyme has a specific EC code.
> 2. Forgetting the role of water — the *hydro* in hydrolase means **H₂O must appear in the reaction equation**.
> 3. Confusing *recommended name* with *systematic name* — the systematic name must reflect *donor : acceptor reaction-type* or *substrate + reaction-type*.
> 4. Not citing the EC code in the answer — KTU evaluators often allocate 1 mark solely for the correct **EC number** of the named enzyme.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Enzyme nomenclature** is the IUBMB-standardised system of classifying enzymes by the **reaction they catalyse**, not by the substrate alone.
- Each enzyme has **three identifiers**: **Recommended Name**, **Systematic Name**, and a **Four-Digit EC Code (EC a.b.c.d)**.
- **Digit a** = one of **six master classes**; **b** = bond/group; **c** = acceptor/donor; **d** = serial index.
- The six classes are **Oxidoreductases (1), Transferases (2), Hydrolases (3), Lyases (4), Isomerases (5), Ligases (6)**.
- **Hydrolases (Class 3)** are the **key enzymes in environmental polymer biodegradation** — especially for polyesters like PET, PLA, PCL.
- **PETase (EC 3.1.1.101)** and **MHETase (EC 3.1.1.102)** are landmark enzymes for PET recycling.
- The **systematic name** format is `donor : acceptor reaction-type` (for redox) or `substrate + reaction-type` (for hydrolases).
- **Lipase (EC 3.1.1.3)** is the most frequently cited enzyme in polymer biodegradation literature.
- **Turnover number (kcat)** has units **s⁻¹**, **Specific Activity** in **µmol·min⁻¹·mg⁻¹**, **Catalytic Efficiency** in **M⁻¹·s⁻¹**.
- The **Michaelis–Menten equation** $v = \dfrac{V_{max}[S]}{K_{m} + [S]}$ is the foundational kinetic model for enzymatic polymer degradation.
- Always cite the **EC code** along with the enzyme name in KTU exam answers — it carries **dedicated marks**.
- New EC entries are released by IUBMB in *ExplorEnz* database and *Nomenclature Updates* in the *European Journal of Biochemistry*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Enzyme Specificity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT632 - POLYMERS & ENVIRONMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Enzyme Specificity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🧬 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Enzyme Specificity** is the precise and selective ability of an enzyme to recognize, bind to, and catalyze a chemical reaction involving a specific substrate (or a closely related set of substrates) characterized by a defined molecular structure, stereochemical configuration, or chemical bond.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEPOT632 – Module 4):**
> Enzyme specificity is the foundational principle governing **biodegradation of polymers**. Microbial enzymes (e.g., PETase, lipases, cutinases) selectively depolymerize polymeric chains (PET, PLA, PHB) only when their active sites geometrically and electronically complement the target linkage. This concept is **high-yield** for the Environmental Biotechnology section of Module 4.

Mathematically expressed as the **specificity constant**:

$$
k_{\text{cat}}/K_{m}
$$

where $k_{\text{cat}}$ is the **turnover number** (mol product formed per mol enzyme per second) and $K_m$ is the **Michaelis constant** (substrate concentration at half-maximal velocity, expressed in **mol/L** or **M**).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Lock & Key" Picture

Imagine a **single key** cut for **one unique lock**:

- **Enzyme** = The lock (with a precisely shaped keyhole).
- **Substrate** = The key (must match the lock's exact grooves).
- A wrong key (wrong substrate) simply **cannot enter** the keyhole → **no reaction**.
- The correct key **turns** the mechanism → **catalysis occurs**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world extension:** Think of enzyme specificity like a *custom-designed recycling plant* in nature. Just as a PET-recycling unit can process only PET bottles and not glass, an enzyme like *PETase* (from *Ideonella sakaiensis*) can depolymerize only polyethylene terephthalate, leaving other plastics untouched. This selectivity is what makes enzymatic polymer degradation **eco-friendly and target-specific**.

## 1.3 Why Specificity Matters in Polymer & Environmental Engineering

| Aspect | Implication |
|---|---|
| **Selective biodegradation** | Allows targeted breakdown of one polymer in a mixed plastic waste stream. |
| **Reduced toxic by-products** | Specificity minimizes formation of harmful intermediates. |
| **Energy efficiency** | Operates at ambient **temperature (~25–37 °C)** and mild **pH (6–8)**, unlike incineration. |
| **Circular economy** | Enables monomer recovery (e.g., MHET → TPA + EG via PETase/MHETase). |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ⚙️ 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Enzyme Specificity (Structured Logic Breakdown)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** Examiners frequently test these six specificity types in **2-mark definitions** and **7-mark descriptive** questions. Memorize with **one example each**.

### 2.1.1 Group Specificity
- **Definition:** Enzyme acts on a specific *functional group* shared by structurally similar substrates.
- **Why:** The active site recognizes the chemical group, not the whole molecule.
- **How:** Substrates with identical reactive groups but different R-groups fit because the catalytic residues contact only the functional group.
- **Example:** **Alkaline phosphatase** removes phosphate from *any* phosphorylated sugar (glucose-6-phosphate, fructose-6-phosphate, ATP, etc.).

### 2.1.2 Linkage Specificity (Bond Specificity)
- **Definition:** Enzyme cleaves (or forms) only a *particular type of chemical bond*, regardless of the rest of the molecule.
- **Why:** The catalytic residues are positioned to attack one specific bond type (e.g., peptide, glycosidic, ester).
- **How:** Catalytic triad/nucleophile approaches the bond geometry in question.
- **Example:** **α-amylase** hydrolyzes only **α-1,4-glycosidic linkages** in starch, not β-1,4 in cellulose.

### 2.1.3 Absolute Specificity
- **Definition:** Enzyme acts on *one single, unique substrate* with no alternatives.
- **Why:** The active site geometry tolerates zero deviation.
- **How:** A single, highly complementary substrate-enzyme fit.
- **Example:** **Urease** acts *exclusively* on urea (no substituted ureas accepted); **Carbonic anhydrase** on $\text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$.

### 2.1.4 Stereochemical Specificity

Further divided into:

**(a) Optical (Enantio-) Specificity**
- Acts on only one optical isomer.
- **Example:** **L-amino acid oxidase** oxidizes only L-amino acids, not D-isomers.

**(b) Geometrical (Cis-Trans) Specificity**
- Distinguishes cis- and trans-isomers.
- **Example:** **Fumarase** hydrates only *trans*-fumarate → malate, not *cis*-maleate.

### 2.1.5 Geometric / Spatial Specificity
- The enzyme requires the substrate to have a particular **3D arrangement** of atoms.
- **Example:** **Trypsin** cleaves peptide bonds on the carboxyl side of positively charged residues (**Lys, Arg**) because of a negatively charged Asp residue in its S1 pocket.

### 2.1.6 Dual Specificity
- One enzyme can catalyze *two chemically distinct reactions* or recognize *two different substrates*.
- **Example:** **Hexokinase** phosphorylates both glucose and fructose (at the 6-position); **Catalase** decomposes both $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2$ and oxidizes formate/ethanol.

## 2.2 Molecular Basis — Why Are Enzymes Specific?

| Interaction Type | Role in Specificity |
|---|---|
| **Hydrogen bonds** | Directional, lock specific donor–acceptor pairs |
| **Ionic (salt bridges)** | Recognize oppositely charged groups |
| **Hydrophobic interactions** | Exclude water, bind non-polar side chains |
| **Van der Waals forces** | Tightly fit substrate contours (shape complementarity) |
| **Covalent transient bonds** | Form transient enzyme-substrate intermediates |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Koshland's Induced-Fit Model (1958)** — superior to Fischer's rigid Lock-and-Key (1894) — states that **substrate binding induces a conformational change** in the active site, achieving *perfect geometric and electronic complementarity* only *after* binding. This explains **transition-state stabilization**, the real basis of catalytic power.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v = \dfrac{V_{\max}[S]}{K_m + [S]}$ | Michaelis–Menten equation (rate) | mol·L⁻¹·s⁻¹ |
| 2 | $K_m$ | Substrate conc. at $v = V_{\max}/2$ | mol·L⁻¹ (**M**) |
| 3 | $V_{\max} = k_{\text{cat}}[E]_T$ | Maximum velocity | mol·L⁻¹·s⁻¹ |
| 4 | $k_{\text{cat}}/K_m$ | **Specificity constant** (catalytic efficiency) | M⁻¹·s⁻¹ |
| 5 | $\text{Turnover number} = k_{\text{cat}}$ | mol product per mol enzyme per second | s⁻¹ |
| 6 | $\Delta G^{\ddagger} = -RT \ln(k_{\text{cat}}/K_m)$ | Activation energy lowering | J·mol⁻¹ |
| 7 | $K_i$ | Inhibition constant (competitive) | M |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constants to memorize (bold in answer sheets):**
> - **R = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**
> - **T (physiological) = 310.15 K (37 °C)**
> - **Acetylcholinesterase $k_{\text{cat}}$ ≈ 1.4 × 10⁴ s⁻¹** (one of the fastest known)
> - **Catalase $k_{\text{cat}}$ ≈ 4 × 10⁷ s⁻¹** (diffusion-limited; rate is limited by how fast $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2$ reaches the active site)

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Polymer & Environmental Engineering

- **PETase / MHETase pathway:** PET → MHET → TPA + EG (specific cleavage of *only* ester bonds in PET).
- **Nylon hydrolases:** Nylon-6 oligomer hydrolase specifically targets amide bonds in nylon-6.
- **PHB depolymerases:** Specific to polyhydroxybutyrate (PHB) ester linkages.
- **Laccases:** Oxidize phenolic units in lignin (specificity for phenolic OH).
- **Cutinases:** Hydrolyze cutin, a polyester in plant cuticles — applied to PET surface modification.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Specificity Constant $k_{\text{cat}}/K_m$ from Michaelis–Menten

### Step 1: Start with the rapid-equilibrium assumption

$$
E + S \xrightleftharpoons[k_{-1}]{k_1} ES \xrightarrow{k_{\text{cat}}} E + P
$$

### Step 2: Express the rate of product formation

The rate depends only on the breakdown of the $ES$ complex:

$$
v = k_{\text{cat}} [ES]
$$

### Step 3: Steady-state assumption for $[ES]$

$$
\frac{d[ES]}{dt} = k_1 [E][S] - k_{-1}[ES] - k_{\text{cat}}[ES] = 0
$$

### Step 4: Define the Michaelis constant

$$
K_m = \frac{k_{-1} + k_{\text{cat}}}{k_1}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** Combining the steady-state result with $K_m$ gives a single measurable lumped parameter that incorporates *both* binding ($k_1, k_{-1}$) *and* catalysis ($k_{\text{cat}}$).

### Step 5: Solve for $[ES]$ in terms of total enzyme $[E]_T$

Because $[E]_T = [E] + [ES]$, and using the equilibrium expression for free enzyme:

$$
[E] = \frac{K_m [ES]}{[S]}
$$

Substitute back:

$$
[E]_T = [ES] + \frac{K_m [ES]}{[S]} = [ES]\left( 1 + \frac{K_m}{[S]} \right) = [ES]\left( \frac{[S] + K_m}{[S]} \right)
$$

Therefore:

$$
[ES] = \frac{[E]_T [S]}{[S] + K_m}
$$

### Step 6: Derive the Michaelis–Menten equation

$$
v = k_{\text{cat}} [ES] = \frac{k_{\text{cat}} [E]_T [S]}{K_m + [S]} = \frac{V_{\max} [S]}{K_m + [S]}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** This is the most frequently written equation in enzyme kinetics and the **most important formula for KTU 2024 scheme** numerical/short questions.

### Step 7: Obtain the specificity constant

For low substrate concentration, $[S] \ll K_m$, so $K_m + [S] \approx K_m$:

$$
v \approx \frac{k_{\text{cat}}}{K_m} \, [E]_T [S]
$$

Thus, the **apparent second-order rate constant** — i.e., the specificity constant — is:

$$
\boxed{\,k_{\text{cat}}/K_m\,}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** When $[S] \ll K_m$, the reaction is pseudo-first-order in $[S]$, and $k_{\text{cat}}/K_m$ directly measures **how efficiently an enzyme selects and converts a given substrate at very low concentrations** — the operational definition of specificity under cellular conditions.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Specificity Types

| Type | Recognizes | Tolerance for Variation | Classic Example | KTU Mark Weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Absolute** | One substrate only | Zero | Urease ↔ urea | 3 marks (definition) |
| **Group** | A functional group | Allows R-group changes | Alkaline phosphatase | 3 marks |
| **Linkage** | A specific bond | Allows flanking groups | α-amylase (α-1,4 glycosidic) | 3–7 marks |
| **Optical** | One enantiomer | Rejects the mirror isomer | L-amino acid oxidase | 7 marks |
| **Geometrical** | cis vs. trans | Rejects the other isomer | Fumarase | 7 marks |
| **Geometric (spatial)** | 3D arrangement | Restricted | Trypsin (basic residues) | 7 marks |
| **Dual** | Two substrates/reactions | Moderate | Hexokinase, Catalase | 3–7 marks |

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (Board-Standard)

> **Problem:** An enzyme has $K_m = 0.25 \text{ mM}$ for substrate A and $K_m = 4.8 \text{ mM}$ for substrate B. The $k_{\text{cat}}$ is $120 \text{ s}^{-1}$ for both. Which substrate is acted upon more specifically at $[S] = 0.05 \text{ mM}$? (Assume $[S] \ll K_m$.)

**Solution:**

For A:
$$
(k_{\text{cat}}/K_m)_A = \frac{120}{0.25 \times 10^{-3}} = 4.8 \times 10^{5} \text{ M}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}
$$

For B:
$$
(k_{\text{cat}}/K_m)_B = \frac{120}{4.8 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.5 \times 10^{4} \text{ M}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}
$$

**Ratio:**
$$
\frac{(k_{\text{cat}}/K_m)_A}{(k_{\text{cat}}/K_m)_B} = \frac{4.8 \times 10^{5}}{2.5 \times 10^{4}} = 19.2
$$

> **Answer:** Enzyme is **19.2 times more specific** for substrate **A**.

> **Conversion logic:** Higher specificity constant = higher catalytic efficiency at low [S]. This is exactly how cells prioritize one substrate over another when both are present in trace amounts.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Concept Map — Hierarchy of Specificity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Enzyme Specificity] --> B[Group Specificity]
    A --> C[Linkage Specificity]
    A --> D[Absolute Specificity]
    A --> E[Stereochemical Specificity]
    A --> F[Geometric Spatial Specificity]
    A --> G[Dual Specificity]

    E --> E1[Optical Enantio Specificity]
    E --> E2[Geometrical Cis Trans Specificity]

    B --> B1[Example Alkaline Phosphatase]
    C --> C1[Example alpha Amylase]
    D --> D1[Example Urease]
    E1 --> E2a[Example L Amino Acid Oxidase]
    E2 --> E2b[Example Fumarase]
    F --> F1[Example Trypsin]
    G --> G1[Example Hexokinase]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Induced-Fit Model (Modern Lock & Key)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Free Enzyme E] --> S2[Substrate S Approaches]
    S2 --> S3[Initial Weak Binding]
    S3 --> S4[Conformational Change in Active Site]
    S4 --> S5[Transition State Stabilized]
    S5 --> S6[Product P Released]
    S6 --> S7[Enzyme Returns to Native State]

    S4 -.-> S5

    S3 --> N1[Note: Active site residues reposition for optimal geometry]
    S5 --> N2[Note: Catalytic power arises from transition state stabilization]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Enzymatic PET Biodegradation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PET Polymer Chain] --> B[PETase Binds PET Surface]
    B --> C[Hydrolysis of Ester Bonds]
    C --> D[Intermediate MHET Mono 2 hydroxyethyl terephthalate]
    D --> E[MHETase Binds MHET]
    E --> F[Further Hydrolysis of Remaining Ester]
    F --> G[Monomers TPA Terephthalic Acid and EG Ethylene Glycol]
    G --> H[Microbial Metabolism to CO2 and H2O]

    B --> X1[Specificity: Recognises Aromatic Terephthalate Unit]
    E --> X2[Specificity: Recognises MHET Linkage Geometry]
    H --> Y[Environmental Outcome: Complete Mineralisation of Plastic]
```

## 4.4 Decision Flow — Why One Substrate Binds, Another Does Not

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Substrate Enters Active Site] --> B{Shape Complementarity?}
    B -->|No| C[No Binding Dissociation]
    B -->|Yes| D{Charge Compatibility?}
    D -->|No| C
    D -->|Yes| E{H Bonding Match?}
    E -->|No| C
    E -->|Yes| F[Induced Fit Conformation Change]
    F --> G[Transition State Formed]
    G --> H[Catalysis and Product Release]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📌 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEPOT632]`
**Define enzyme specificity. Mention its significance in polymer biodegradation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Enzyme specificity is the ability of an enzyme to selectively catalyze a reaction on a particular substrate or a particular chemical bond, determined by the precise geometry and chemistry of its active site. There are six major types: absolute, group, linkage, stereochemical (optical and geometrical), geometric spatial, and dual specificity.

**Significance in polymer biodegradation (1 mark):** Specificity allows microbial enzymes such as *PETase*, *cutinase*, and *PHB depolymerase* to selectively hydrolyze ester or amide linkages in specific polymers (PET, PHB, nylon) without attacking unrelated materials, enabling eco-friendly, targeted polymer recycling and waste management.

*(Total = 3 marks)*

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEPOT632]`
**Differentiate between absolute specificity and group specificity with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Absolute Specificity | Group Specificity |
|---|---|---|
| Substrate range | Only **one** specific substrate | A group of substrates sharing the **same functional group** |
| Tolerance | Zero | Tolerates variation in non-reactive parts |
| Example | **Urease** → urea only | **Alkaline phosphatase** → any phosphorylated compound |

*(Total = 3 marks)*

---

## 📌 PART B — 14-Mark Descriptive Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### ⭐ **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PEPOT632]`

**(a)** Classify the different types of enzyme specificity. Explain **absolute, linkage, and stereochemical specificity** with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **molecular basis of enzyme specificity** with reference to active-site interactions and the **induced-fit model**. Discuss its relevance to **polymer biodegradation** with two industrial examples. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Types of enzyme specificity** (1 mark – classification enumeration):

Enzymes display six major specificity types: **absolute, group, linkage, geometric spatial, stereochemical (optical and geometrical), and dual specificity**.

**Absolute specificity (2 marks):**

The enzyme acts on *only one* specific substrate and cannot tolerate any structural variation. The active site geometry is highly complementary to a single molecular shape. 
- **Example:** *Urease* catalyzes the hydrolysis of **urea** to ammonia and carbon dioxide:
$$
\text{(NH}_2\text{)}_2\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{Urease}} 2\text{NH}_3 + \text{CO}_2
$$
Urease does not act on substituted ureas (e.g., methylurea) or thiourea, demonstrating zero tolerance.

**Linkage specificity (2 marks):**

The enzyme cleaves (or forms) only a *particular type of chemical bond* irrespective of the rest of the molecular structure.
- **Example:** *α-Amylase* hydrolyzes only **α-1,4-glycosidic bonds** in starch and glycogen, producing maltose and dextrins. It cannot cleave **β-1,4-glycosidic bonds** in cellulose — this is why humans cannot digest grass/wood.

**Stereochemical specificity (2 marks):**

Subdivided into:

*(i) Optical / Enantio-specificity:* The enzyme distinguishes between enantiomers (D vs L, R vs S). 
- **Example:** *L-Amino acid oxidase* oxidizes only L-amino acids, leaving D-amino acids untouched. This is exploited in chiral pharmaceutical manufacturing.

*(ii) Geometrical (cis-trans) specificity:* The enzyme acts only on a specific geometric isomer. 
- **Example:** *Fumarase* hydrates only *trans*-fumaric acid to L-malic acid; *cis*-maleic acid is not a substrate. The active site pocket geometry cannot accommodate the cis-arrangement of the carboxyl groups.

*(Total = 7 marks)*

---

#### ✅ **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Molecular basis of enzyme specificity (3 marks):**

Specificity arises from a combination of **non-covalent interactions** between the substrate and amino acid residues lining the active site. These include:

- **Hydrogen bonds** — directionally lock specific donor–acceptor pairs (e.g., Ser–OH to carbonyl O).
- **Ionic / electrostatic interactions** — recognize charged groups (e.g., Asp⁻ in trypsin binds Lys⁺/Arg⁺ side chains).
- **Hydrophobic interactions** — bind non-polar moieties in the lipophilic pocket.
- **Van der Waals forces** — provide shape complementarity and tight contact.
- **Transient covalent bonds** — e.g., acyl-enzyme intermediate in serine proteases.

**Induced-fit model (Koshland, 1958) (2 marks):**

The classical *Lock and Key* model (Fischer, 1894) viewed the active site as a rigid pre-formed template. Koshland's *induced-fit* model replaced this view: **the active site is flexible and reshapes itself upon substrate binding**, achieving perfect geometric and electronic complementarity *only after* initial docking. This conformational change positions catalytic residues optimally and **stabilizes the transition state**, which is the true basis of catalytic power (and hence specificity).

**Relevance to polymer biodegradation (2 marks):**

Two industrial examples:

1. **PETase from *Ideonella sakaiensis* (2016, Yoshida et al.):** Specifically hydrolyses **ester bonds** in polyethylene terephthalate (PET), producing MHET and ultimately terephthalic acid (TPA) + ethylene glycol (EG). The active site has a Ser-His-Asp catalytic triad optimally arranged for PET ester linkage. The enzyme shows low activity on polyethylene furanoate (PEF) — a classic example of linkage specificity.
2. **PHB depolymerase** from *Alcaligenes faecalis*: Specifically hydrolyses **β-hydroxybutyrate ester linkages** in poly-3-hydroxybutyrate (PHB) but not in polylactic acid (PLA). Used for industrial composting of biodegradable bioplastics.

*(Total = 7 marks)*

---

### ⭐ **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PEPOT632]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** What is enzyme specificity? Derive the **Michaelis–Menten equation** and explain the significance of the **specificity constant $k_{\text{cat}}/K_m$**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **lock-and-key** and **induced-fit** models of enzyme–substrate interaction. Discuss the **factors that determine enzyme specificity** with a focus on active-site amino acid residues. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Definition of enzyme specificity (1 mark):**

Enzyme specificity is the inherent property of an enzyme to selectively bind and catalyze the reaction of a specific substrate (or a defined class of substrates), determined by the structure and chemical environment of its active site.

**Michaelis–Menten derivation (4 marks):**

The enzyme–substrate reaction proceeds as:

$$
E + S \xrightleftharpoons[k_{-1}]{k_1} ES \xrightarrow{k_{\text{cat}}} E + P
$$

- Rate of product formation: $v = k_{\text{cat}} [ES]$
- Steady-state for $[ES]$: $k_1 [E][S] = (k_{-1} + k_{\text{cat}})[ES]$
- Define $K_m = \dfrac{k_{-1} + k_{\text{cat}}}{k_1}$
- With $[E]_T = [E] + [ES]$ and $[E] = \dfrac{K_m [ES]}{[S]}$:

$$
[ES] = \frac{[E]_T [S]}{K_m + [S]}
$$

- Substituting:

$$
\boxed{\,v = \frac{V_{\max} [S]}{K_m + [S]}\,}
$$

where $V_{\max} = k_{\text{cat}} [E]_T$.

**Significance of the specificity constant (2 marks):**

The **specificity constant** $k_{\text{cat}}/K_m$ (units **M⁻¹·s⁻¹**) measures the **catalytic efficiency** of an enzyme at low substrate concentrations. A higher $k_{\text{cat}}/K_m$ means greater efficiency and stronger preference for that substrate. The theoretical upper limit is $\sim 10^8 - 10^9 \, \text{M}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}$, set by the **diffusion-controlled limit** (e.g., catalase, acetylcholinesterase). Thus, it is a quantitative, experimentally measurable index of **how specific an enzyme is** to a given substrate.

*(Total = 7 marks)*

---

#### ✅ **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Lock-and-Key model (Fischer, 1894) (2 marks):**

The active site of the enzyme is a *rigid, pre-formed* three-dimensional template. The substrate fits into it exactly as a **key fits a lock**, with no conformational change in the enzyme. This model explains absolute and group specificity elegantly but **fails to account for** enzymes that act on multiple substrates or the existence of allosteric regulation.

**Induced-fit model (Koshland, 1958) (2 marks):**

The active site is *flexible*. Upon initial weak binding, the substrate induces a **conformational rearrangement** in the enzyme such that the catalytic residues are brought into optimal alignment. This model explains:

- **Transition-state stabilization** (the real source of catalytic power).
- **Progressive specificity** in multi-subunit enzymes.
- **Allosteric behavior** and cooperativity.

**Factors determining specificity (3 marks):**

| Factor | Role |
|---|---|
| **Active site geometry** | Three-dimensional shape complementarity (van der Waals) |
| **Amino acid side chains** | Hydrogen bonding, salt bridges (e.g., Asp in trypsin S1 pocket) |
| **Hydrophobic/hydrophilic balance** | Determines which moieties are accepted |
| **Induced conformational change** | Realigns catalytic residues post-binding |
| **Presence of cofactors / metal ions** | Zn²⁺ in carbonic anhydrase, Mg²⁺ in kinases |
| **Substrate orientation** | Desolvation and proper orbital alignment |

The *combination* of these factors — not any single one — defines an enzyme's specificity profile.

*(Total = 7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Don't confuse *linkage specificity* with *group specificity*.** Linkage = which *bond*; Group = which *functional group*. Examiners deduct 1 mark for swapping.
> 2. **Always mention the units** for $K_m$ (M or mol·L⁻¹) and $k_{\text{cat}}$ (s⁻¹) in derivations. Missing units = -½ mark.
> 3. **In induced-fit questions, do not write "lock-and-key" alone** — it's outdated. Always state that Koshland's model supersedes Fischer's.
> 4. **In numerical problems**, state the assumption ($[S] \ll K_m$) explicitly when applying the specificity constant — otherwise 1 mark is lost.
> 5. **Avoid confusing "optical specificity" with "geometric specificity"** — these are *sub-types* of stereochemical specificity, not separate categories.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Definition:** Enzyme specificity = selective catalysis on a specific substrate / bond / stereoisomer.
- ✅ **Six types:** Absolute, Group, Linkage, Stereochemical (Optical + Geometrical), Geometric Spatial, Dual.
- ✅ **Two historical models:** Lock-and-Key (rigid) vs. Induced-Fit (flexible; widely accepted).
- ✅ **Molecular basis:** H-bonds, ionic, hydrophobic, van der Waals, transient covalent interactions.
- ✅ **Key formula:** $v = \dfrac{V_{\max} [S]}{K_m + [S]}$ (Michaelis–Menten).
- ✅ **Specificity constant:** $k_{\text{cat}}/K_m$ in **M⁻¹·s⁻¹** — quantitative measure of selectivity.
- ✅ **Diffusion limit:** $\sim 10^8 - 10^9 \, \text{M}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}$.
- ✅ **Constants:** $R = 8.314 \text{ J·mol}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1}$; $T_{\text{physiological}} = 310.15 \text{ K}$.
- ✅ **Polymer relevance:** PETase (PET), PHB depolymerase (PHB), cutinase (PET/cutin), nylon hydrolase (nylon-6).
- ✅ **Industrial examples:** Yoshida et al. (2016) discovery of PETase from *Ideonella sakaiensis* — highest-yield current-affairs topic.
- ✅ **Common exam mistake:** Don't interchange "group" and "linkage" specificity; don't quote Lock-and-Key as the current model.
- ✅ **Eco-significance:** Enables monomer recovery, supports circular economy, mild operating conditions, low toxic emissions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physical Factors Affecting the Activity of Enzymes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT632 - POLYMERS & ENVIRONMENT > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Physical Factors Affecting the Activity of Enzymes # Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Enzyme Activity** is defined as the measure of the moles of substrate converted (or product formed) per unit time by an enzyme under specified assay conditions. It is quantitatively expressed in **International Units (IU)** where **1 IU = 1 µmol of substrate transformed per minute** at optimal conditions. The **physical factors** affecting enzyme activity are those external physical parameters that influence the **catalytic efficiency**, **tertiary/quaternary conformation**, and **active-site geometry** of an enzyme without permanently altering its primary structure (unless denaturation occurs). The principal physical factors studied are: 1. **Temperature** 2. **pH (Hydrogen ion concentration)** 3. **Substrate Concentration** 4. **Enzyme Concentration** 5. **Activators (Metal ions, Co-factors)** ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Locksmith & Key" Workshop Imagine an **enzyme as a locksmith's precision workshop**, and **substrates as keys** that must be filed, cut, and reshaped inside the workshop. - **Temperature** is the **ambient room temperature**. Too cold → the locksmith works slowly (low kinetic energy). Too hot → the locksmith sweats, fumbles, and the metal tools warp (denaturation). - **pH** is the **humidity/lubrication of the workshop**. Each locksmith is calibrated for a specific humidity; acidic or basic conditions cause the tools (ionizable amino acid side chains) to corrode or jam. - **Substrate concentration** is the **number of keys arriving at the shop**. Initially, more keys = more work done, but eventually the workshop reaches full capacity (saturation at V_max). - **Enzyme concentration** is the **number of locksmiths available**. More locksmiths = more keys processed per minute — a linear proportionality. > [!NOTE] > Every physical factor has a characteristic **optimum value** at which the enzyme exhibits maximum activity. Deviation in either direction results in a **bell-shaped (Gaussian) activity curve**, a hallmark feature of enzyme kinetics. ## 1.3 Core Definitions (Syllabus Highlights) > [!NOTE] > **Key Terminology for KTU 2024 Scheme:** > - **V_max**: Maximum reaction velocity when all enzyme molecules are saturated with substrate. > - **K_m (Michaelis Constant)**: Substrate concentration at which the reaction velocity equals **half of V_max**. A low K_m indicates high enzyme-substrate affinity. > - **Denaturation**: Irreversible disruption of tertiary and secondary structures of an enzyme, leading to loss of catalytic activity. > - **Optimum pH / Temperature**: The specific value of pH or temperature at which the enzyme exhibits peak catalytic activity. --- # Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Effect of Temperature Enzymes, being proteins, have a **three-dimensional conformation** maintained by weak bonds (hydrogen bonds, hydrophobic interactions, van der Waals forces, and ionic bonds). Temperature affects two competing phenomena: ### 2.1.1 The Dual Effect of Temperature - **Kinetic Energy Boost (Positive Effect)**: As temperature rises, the average kinetic energy of substrate molecules increases. This leads to a higher frequency of **productive collisions** with the active site, increasing reaction rate. For every **10 °C rise**, the rate typically doubles or triples — this is quantified by the **Temperature Coefficient Q₁₀**. - **Thermal Denaturation (Negative Effect)**: Above a critical temperature (usually 40–60 °C for most enzymes), the weak bonds stabilizing tertiary structure break, causing **irreversible denaturation**. The enzyme loses its specific 3D shape and consequently its catalytic function. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Biochemical Rule**: Most human enzymes have an **optimum temperature of ~37 °C**. Thermophilic enzymes (e.g., Taq polymerase from *Thermus aquaticus*) have optima of 70–80 °C because their tertiary structures are reinforced by additional disulfide bridges and ionic interactions. ### 2.1.2 Arrhenius Equation in Enzyme Catalysis The temperature dependence of the rate constant is described by the **Arrhenius Equation**: $$k = A \cdot e^{\frac{-E_a}{RT}}$$ Where: - $k$ = rate constant of the enzyme-catalyzed reaction - $A$ = pre-exponential (frequency) factor - $E_a$ = activation energy (J/mol) - $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** - $T$ = absolute temperature (K) ### 2.1.3 The Q₁₀ Coefficient $$Q_{10} = \frac{\text{Rate at } (T + 10°C)}{\text{Rate at } T°\text{C}}$$ For most enzymes, **Q₁₀ ≈ 2 to 3** in the rising portion of the curve. --- ## 2.2 Effect of pH Enzyme activity is profoundly sensitive to pH because the **ionizable side chains** of amino acids at the active site (especially **–COOH of Asp/Glu**, **–NH₂ of Lys**, and **imidazole of His**) must be in the correct ionic state to bind substrate and catalyze the reaction. ### 2.2.1 Mechanism of pH Dependence 1. **Ionization State of Active Site Residues**: Substrate binding requires the catalytic residues to be in a specific protonation state (e.g., protonated vs. deprotonated). 2. **Disruption of Hydrogen Bonding Network**: Extreme pH values disrupt the intricate H-bond network maintaining tertiary structure. 3. **Alteration of Substrate Ionization**: pH can change the charge on the substrate, affecting its ability to bind the active site. > [!NOTE] > **Examples of Optimum pH**: > - **Pepsin** (stomach): Optimum pH = **1.5–2.0** (highly acidic) > - **Trypsin** (intestine): Optimum pH = **7.5–8.5** (mildly basic) > - **Salivary Amylase**: Optimum pH = **6.7–7.0** (near neutral) The relationship is typically **bell-shaped** and can be described by an empirical equation accounting for the dissociation of two critical ionizable groups. --- ## 2.3 Effect of Substrate Concentration — Michaelis-Menten Kinetics The relationship between substrate concentration [S] and reaction velocity **v** is **hyperbolic** and was first mathematically described by **Leonor Michaelis and Maud Menten (1913)**. ### 2.3.1 The Michaelis-Menten Model The enzyme-substrate interaction follows the scheme: $$E + S \underset{k_{-1}}{\overset{k_{1}}{\rightleftharpoons}} ES \underset{}{\overset{k_{2}}{\rightarrow}} E + P$$ The resulting equation is: $$v = \frac{V_{\max} \cdot [S]}{K_m + [S]}$$ ### 2.3.2 Lineweaver-Burk Double Reciprocal Plot To obtain linear data, take the reciprocal of both sides: $$\frac{1}{v} = \frac{K_m}{V_{\max}} \cdot \frac{1}{[S]} + \frac{1}{V_{\max}}$$ This is a **straight line** with: - **y-intercept** = $\frac{1}{V_{\max}}$ - **x-intercept** = $-\frac{1}{K_m}$ - **slope** = $\frac{K_m}{V_{\max}}$ --- ## 2.4 Effect of Enzyme Concentration When the substrate is present in **large excess** ([S] >> K_m), the active sites are saturated, and the reaction velocity becomes **directly proportional** to enzyme concentration: $$v = k_{cat} \cdot [E]_t$$ Where $k_{cat}$ is the **turnover number** (mol product formed per mol enzyme per second). > [!IMPORTANT] > This linear relationship holds **only when [S] is saturating**. If [S] is limiting, increasing [E] will not yield a proportional increase in velocity because the substrate becomes the bottleneck. --- ## 2.5 Effect of Activators (Metal Ions as Physical Cofactors) Many enzymes require **metal ions** (Zn²⁺, Mg²⁺, Mn²⁺, Fe²⁺, Cu²⁺) for activity. These act as **physical activators** by: - Bridging enzyme and substrate (orienting substrate) - Stabilizing the negative charge of the transition state - Maintaining the active site geometry --- ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Parameter** | **Equation** | **Units** | **Physical Meaning** | |---------------|--------------|-----------|----------------------| | Michaelis-Menten | $v = \dfrac{V_{\max}[S]}{K_m + [S]}$ | mol·L⁻¹·s⁻¹ | Hyperbolic velocity vs. [S] | | Lineweaver-Burk | $\dfrac{1}{v} = \dfrac{K_m}{V_{\max}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{[S]} + \dfrac{1}{V_{\max}}$ | s·L·mol⁻¹ | Linearized form for graphical K_m, V_max | | Michaelis Constant | $K_m = \dfrac{k_{-1} + k_{2}}{k_{1}}$ | mol·L⁻¹ (M) | Half-saturation substrate concentration | | Arrhenius | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ | s⁻¹ | Temperature dependence of k | | Q₁₀ Coefficient | $Q_{10} = \dfrac{R_{T+10}}{R_{T}}$ | dimensionless | Rate increase per 10 °C rise | | Turnover Number | $k_{cat} = \dfrac{V_{\max}}{[E]_t}$ | s⁻¹ | Catalytic efficiency per enzyme molecule | | Catalytic Efficiency | $\dfrac{k_{cat}}{K_m}$ | M⁻¹·s⁻¹ | Overall enzyme-substrate efficiency | --- ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Industrial / Biomedical Applications:** > - **Bioreactor Design**: Maintaining optimum temperature and pH in fermenters for maximum product yield (e.g., penicillin production by *Penicillium chrysogenum*). > - **Diagnostic Assays**: Clinical enzyme assays (ALT, AST, LDH) are standardized at **37 °C and pH 7.4** for reproducible results. > - **Polymer-Degrading Enzymes**: PET hydrolases and lipases used in biopolymer recycling are screened for thermostability to function in industrial reactors. > - **Drug Design**: K_m and V_max values guide the design of competitive and non-competitive inhibitors in pharmaceutical research. --- # Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Michaelis-Menten Equation ### Step 1: Define the Catalytic Scheme The enzyme (E) and substrate (S) combine to form an enzyme-substrate complex (ES), which then either dissociates back to E + S, or proceeds forward to form product (P): $$E + S \underset{k_{-1}}{\overset{k_{1}}{\rightleftharpoons}} ES \xrightarrow{k_{2}} E + P$$ Each step is characterized by a rate constant: - $k_1$ = rate constant for ES formation (units: M⁻¹·s⁻¹) - $k_{-1}$ = rate constant for ES dissociation back to E + S (units: s⁻¹) - $k_2$ = rate constant for catalytic conversion to product (units: s⁻¹) — also known as $k_{cat}$ ### Step 2: Express the Rate of ES Formation The rate at which ES is formed is proportional to the concentration of free enzyme and substrate: $$\text{Rate of ES formation} = k_1 \cdot [E] \cdot [S]$$ ### Step 3: Express the Rate of ES Breakdown The ES complex breaks down via two parallel routes — reversion to E + S, and forward catalysis to E + P: $$\text{Rate of ES breakdown} = (k_{-1} + k_2) \cdot [ES]$$ ### Step 4: Apply the Steady-State Approximation (Briggs-Haldane) Leonor Michaelis and Maud Menten, refined by **Briggs and Haldane (1925)**, assumed that after a brief initial period, the concentration of ES remains essentially constant — i.e., the rate of its formation equals the rate of its breakdown: $$\frac{d[ES]}{dt} = 0$$ This is the **Steady-State Approximation**: $$k_1 \cdot [E] \cdot [S] = (k_{-1} + k_2) \cdot [ES]$$ ### Step 5: Define the Michaelis Constant (K_m) Rearranging Step 4 and grouping the rate constants gives: $$\frac{[E] \cdot [S]}{[ES]} = \frac{k_{-1} + k_2}{k_1} = K_m$$ So the Michaelis Constant is defined as: $$K_m = \frac{k_{-1} + k_2}{k_1}$$ ### Step 6: Express Free Enzyme in Terms of Total Enzyme The total enzyme concentration $[E]_t$ is the sum of free enzyme and enzyme bound in the ES complex: $$[E]_t = [E] + [ES]$$ Therefore, the free enzyme concentration is: $$[E] = [E]_t - [ES]$$ ### Step 7: Solve for [ES] Substituting $[E] = [E]_t - [ES]$ into the steady-state equation: $$k_1 \cdot ([E]_t - [ES]) \cdot [S] = (k_{-1} + k_2) \cdot [ES]$$ Expanding the left-hand side: $$k_1 \cdot [E]_t \cdot [S] - k_1 \cdot [ES] \cdot [S] = (k_{-1} + k_2) \cdot [ES]$$ Collecting [ES] terms on the right-hand side: $$k_1 \cdot [E]_t \cdot [S] = (k_{-1} + k_2) \cdot [ES] + k_1 \cdot [ES] \cdot [S]$$ $$k_1 \cdot [E]_t \cdot [S] = [ES] \cdot (k_{-1} + k_2 + k_1 \cdot [S])$$ Solving for [ES]: $$[ES] = \frac{k_1 \cdot [E]_t \cdot [S]}{k_{-1} + k_2 + k_1 \cdot [S]}$$ Dividing numerator and denominator by $k_1$: $$[ES] = \frac{[E]_t \cdot [S]}{\frac{k_{-1} + k_2}{k_1} + [S]} = \frac{[E]_t \cdot [S]}{K_m + [S]}$$ ### Step 8: Express the Reaction Velocity The overall reaction velocity $v$ is determined by the rate of product formation: $$v = k_2 \cdot [ES]$$ Substituting our expression for [ES]: $$v = \frac{k_2 \cdot [E]_t \cdot [S]}{K_m + [S]}$$ ### Step 9: Define V_max At very high substrate concentrations ($[S] \to \infty$), the enzyme becomes saturated — virtually all enzyme is in the ES form, so $[ES] \approx [E]_t$. The maximum velocity is therefore: $$V_{\max} = k_2 \cdot [E]_t$$ ### Step 10: The Final Michaelis-Menten Equation Substituting $V_{\max}$ into the velocity equation: $$v = \frac{V_{\max} \cdot [S]}{K_m + [S]}$$ This is the **iconic Michaelis-Menten equation**, a cornerstone of enzyme kinetics. ✔️ --- ## 3.2 Derivation of the Lineweaver-Burk Equation ### Step 1: Start with the Michaelis-Menten Equation $$v = \frac{V_{\max} \cdot [S]}{K_m + [S]}$$ ### Step 2: Take the Reciprocal of Both Sides $$\frac{1}{v} = \frac{K_m + [S]}{V_{\max} \cdot [S]}$$ ### Step 3: Split the Numerator $$\frac{1}{v} = \frac{K_m}{V_{\max} \cdot [S]} + \frac{[S]}{V_{\max} \cdot [S]}$$ ### Step 4: Simplify the Second Term $$\frac{1}{v} = \frac{K_m}{V_{\max}} \cdot \frac{1}{[S]} + \frac{1}{V_{\max}}$$ This is the equation of a **straight line** $y = mx + c$ where: - $y = \dfrac{1}{v}$ - $x = \dfrac{1}{[S]}$ - $m = \dfrac{K_m}{V_{\max}}$ (slope) - $c = \dfrac{1}{V_{\max}}$ (y-intercept) --- ## 3.3 Derivation of the Q₁₀ Formula Let $R_T$ be the rate at temperature $T$ and $R_{T+10}$ be the rate at temperature $(T+10)$ °C. By definition: $$Q_{10} = \frac{R_{T+10}}{R_T}$$ Using the Arrhenius equation for two temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$: $$\ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \frac{(T_2 - T_1)}{T_1 T_2}$$ For $T_2 - T_1 = 10$ K, $T_1 = 310$ K (37 °C), and typical $E_a = 50$ kJ/mol: $$Q_{10} = e^{\frac{E_a \cdot 10}{R \cdot T_1 \cdot T_2}} \approx 2 \text{ to } 3$$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Behavior of Enzymes Under Different Conditions | **Factor** | **At Low Value** | **At Optimum** | **At High Value** | |------------|------------------|----------------|-------------------| | **Temperature** | Low kinetic energy → slow reaction | Maximum activity (e.g., 37 °C for human enzymes) | Denaturation → sharp activity drop | | **pH** | Active site residues wrongly ionized → poor binding | Correct ionization → max activity | Tertiary structure disrupted → denaturation | | **Substrate [S]** | Few ES complexes → low v | [S] ≈ K_m → v ≈ V_max/2 | Saturation → v = V_max (plateau) | | **Enzyme [E]** | Few catalytic sites → low v | Linear increase in v (if [S] saturating) | Linear (substrate may become limiting) | | **Activators** | Cofactor absent → reduced activity | Optimal cofactor concentration | No further enhancement (already saturated) | --- ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** An enzyme has a K_m of 2.5 × 10⁻³ M for its substrate. If the substrate concentration in a reaction mixture is 1.0 × 10⁻² M and V_max = 50 µmol·L⁻¹·min⁻¹, calculate the reaction velocity **v**. **Solution:** $$v = \frac{V_{\max} \cdot [S]}{K_m + [S]} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.0 \times 10^{-2}}{2.5 \times 10^{-3} + 1.0 \times 10^{-2}}$$ $$v = \frac{5.0 \times 10^{-7}}{1.25 \times 10^{-2}} = 4.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ M·min}^{-1}$$ Converting: $v = 40 \text{ µM·min}^{-1}$. ✔️ --- # Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Effect of Temperature on Enzyme Activity (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD startA([Temperature Change Initiated]) --> low1A[Low Temperature
Below Optimum] startA --> opt1A[Optimum Temperature
e.g. 37 Degree Celsius] startA --> high1A[High Temperature
Above Optimum] low1A --> eff1A[Reduced Kinetic Energy] eff1A --> coll1A[Less Frequent Effective Collisions] coll1A --> rate1A[Slow Reaction Rate] opt1A --> eff2A[Maximum Effective Collisions] eff2A --> struc2A[3D Structure Intact] struc2A --> rate2A[Peak Reaction Rate
Vmax Achieved] high1A --> bond2A[Breaking of Weak Bonds
