# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**"List the four mandatory phases of Life Cycle Assessment as per ISO 14040."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Goal and Scope Definition** — establishes functional unit, system boundary, allocation rules. *(1 mark)*
2. **Life Cycle Inventory (LCI)** — quantifies inputs/outputs of energy, materials, emissions. *(1 mark)*
3. **Life Cycle Impact Assessment (LCIA)** — classifies and characterizes LCI data into environmental impact categories. *(0.5 mark)*
4. **Interpretation** — identifies significant issues, conducts sensitivity analysis, and draws conclusions. *(0.5 mark)*

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**"Define the term 'functional unit' in LCA. Why is it essential?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **functional unit (FU)** is the quantified performance of a product system that serves as the reference unit for all environmental inputs and outputs. *Example: "1 kg of packaging film providing a barrier for 1 m² of product surface for 1 year."* *(1.5 marks)*
It is **essential** because:
- It enables **fair comparison** between alternative products with different masses/sizes. *(0.75 mark)*
- Without an FU, LCA results become **dimensionless and uninterpretable**. *(0.75 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive LCA Problem

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Define **Life Cycle Assessment (LCA)** as per ISO 14040. List and briefly explain its four phases with a neat flow diagram. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A polymer processing industry consumes the following in 1 month:
- Natural gas: 500 m³ (EF = 2.02 kg CO₂/m³)
- Furnace oil: 2000 L (EF = 3.15 kg CO₂/L)
- Electricity: 10,000 kWh (EF = 0.82 kg CO₂/kWh)

Calculate the **monthly carbon footprint (kg CO₂-eq)** of the industry. Also compute the **specific carbon footprint per kg of polymer**, if production = 50,000 kg/month. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution:

**(a) LCA Definition & Phases (7 marks):**

LCA is a systematic, ISO 14040-compliant methodology to quantify the environmental burdens of a product "from cradle to grave," encompassing raw material extraction, production, use, and disposal. *(1 mark)*

**Phase 1 — Goal and Scope:** Defines purpose, functional unit, system boundary. *(1.5 marks)*
**Phase 2 — Life Cycle Inventory:** Data on all material/energy inputs and emissions. *(1.5 marks)*
**Phase 3 — Impact Assessment:** Classification + Characterization into categories like GWP, AP, EP. *(1.5 marks)*
**Phase 4 — Interpretation:** Sensitivity analysis and recommendations. *(0.5 mark)*
*Neat diagram = 1 mark.*

**(b) Carbon Footprint Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Natural gas contribution** *(2 marks)*
$$CF_{gas} = 500 \times 2.02 = 1010 \text{ kg CO}_2$$

**Step 2: Furnace oil contribution** *(2 marks)*
$$CF_{oil} = 2000 \times 3.15 = 6300 \text{ kg CO}_2$$

**Step 3: Electricity contribution** *(2 marks)*
$$CF_{elec} = 10000 \times 0.82 = 8200 \text{ kg CO}_2$$

**Step 4: Total & Specific CF** *(1 mark)*
$$CF_{total} = 1010 + 6300 + 8200 = 15{,}510 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq/month}$$
$$CF_{specific} = \frac{15510}{50000} = 0.3102 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq/kg polymer}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**:
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Forgetting to convert units (e.g., using m³ but EF given per liter) → loses **2 marks**.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Reporting total CF without computing **specific CF per kg** → loses **1 mark** even if total is correct.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Omitting "**CO₂-eq**" unit suffix in final answer → loses **0.5 mark** for unit accuracy.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Not showing intermediate sub-totals → examiner cannot award step-wise marks. **Always** write $CF_{gas}$, $CF_{oil}$, $CF_{elec}$ explicitly.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Biodegradability Assessment

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat labeled schematic, explain the **three-tier testing hierarchy** for biodegradability assessment of polymers. List any two ISO/ASTM standards used in each tier. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A **PLA film sample of 100 mg** ($C_{content} = 0.50$) is subjected to aerobic composting per **ISO 14852**. The cumulative CO₂ evolved at day 90 is **75 mg**. Compute:
(i) Theoretical CO₂ (ThCO₂) for complete mineralization.
(ii) % Mineralization achieved in 90 days.
(iii) Comment on whether the sample qualifies for "compostable" labeling as per EN 13432. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Three-Tier Testing Hierarchy (7 marks):**

**Tier 1 — Laboratory Respirometric Tests** *(2.5 marks)*
- ASTM D5338 (controlled composting, 58 °C)
- ISO 14852 (aqueous aerobic, O₂ consumption)
- ISO 17556 (soil, CO₂ evolution)

**Tier 2 — Pilot-Scale Tests** *(2 marks)*
- ASTM D6400 (industrial composting facility simulation)
- EN 13432 (European packaging compostability)

**Tier 3 — Real-Environment Validation** *(1.5 marks)*
- Field tests in soil, marine, or landfill conditions.
- *Neat pyramid diagram = 1 mark.*

**(b) Biodegradation Calculations (7 marks):**

**(i) Theoretical CO₂:** *(2.5 marks)*
$$\begin{aligned}
ThCO_2 &= m_p \times C_{content} \times \frac{44}{12} \\
       &= 100 \text{ mg} \times 0.50 \times \frac{44}{12} \\
       &= 50 \times 3.6667 \\
       &= 183.33 \text{ mg CO}_2
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) % Mineralization at day 90:** *(2.5 marks)*
$$\begin{aligned}
\%M(90) &= \frac{CO_2(90)}{ThCO_2} \times 100\% \\
        &= \frac{75}{183.33} \times 100\% \\
        &= 40.91\%
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) EN 13432 Compliance:** *(2 marks)*
EN 13432 requires **≥ 90% mineralization in ≤ 180 days**. Achieved = 40.91% in 90 days, which is **below the threshold**. Therefore, **the sample does NOT qualify** for "compostable" labeling as per EN 13432 at the measured time point.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**:
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Writing $\frac{44}{12}$ as **3.7** without explanation → only partial credit. **Always** show the ratio derivation. *(loses 1 mark)*
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing **ASTM D6400** with **ASTM D5338** (the latter is lab-scale, former is pilot-scale). *(loses 1 mark)*
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Saying "biodegradable" instead of "compostable" — these are **distinct regulatory terms**. *(loses 0.5 mark)*
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Forgetting to state the **EN 13432 threshold (90% in 180 days)** explicitly before commenting on compliance. *(loses 1 mark)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This & Paste in Your Notebook)**

### Must-Memorize Facts
- **LCA Phases (4):** Goal & Scope → LCI → LCIA → Interpretation *(iterative, not linear)*.
- **Standard:** **ISO 14040 (Principles) + ISO 14044 (Requirements)**.
- **Functional Unit:** The yardstick — every LCA result is meaningless without one.
- **ThCO₂ formula:** $ThCO_2 = m_p \cdot C_{content} \cdot \frac{44}{12}$.
- **% Mineralization:** $\%M(t) = \frac{CO_2(t)}{ThCO_2} \times 100\%$.
- **EN 13432 threshold:** **≥ 90% mineralization in ≤ 180 days** for compostable label.
- **GWP of CO₂ = 1** (reference); **GWP of CH₄ = 28** (100-year, AR5).
- **Carbon Footprint formula:** $CF = \sum (AD_j \times EF_j)$.

### Must-Avoid Conceptual Errors
- ❌ Treating "biodegradable" and "compostable" as synonyms.
- ❌ Reporting LCA results without a functional unit.
- ❌ Forgetting that ISO 14040 mandates **iterative revision** of phases.
- ❌ Confusing **CEC/CED** (energy) with **GWP** (climate).
- ❌ Mixing up **ASTM D6400** (pilot) with **ASTM D5338** (lab).

### Quick-Reference Acronyms
| Acronym | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **LCA** | Life Cycle Assessment |
| **LCI** | Life Cycle Inventory |
| **LCIA** | Life Cycle Impact Assessment |
| **GWP** | Global Warming Potential |
| **AP** | Acidification Potential |
| **EP** | Eutrophication Potential |
| **ODP** | Ozone Depletion Potential |
| **CED** | Cumulative Energy Demand |
| **FU** | Functional Unit |
| **ThCO₂** | Theoretical Carbon Dioxide |
| **CF** | Characterization Factor |
| **CTU** | Comparative Toxic Unit |
| **AD** | Activity Data |
| **EF** | Emission Factor |

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Board Tip**: In 14-mark questions, **always** begin with a **labeled diagram** of the relevant ISO framework. It guarantees **1–2 marks** even if your subsequent numerical content has minor errors. Then proceed with **Goal → LCI → LCIA → Interpretation** as your mental checklist for any LCA-related answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bio Polyesters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT632 - POLYMERS & ENVIRONMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bio Polyesters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Bio Polyester** is defined as a polymeric material containing the characteristic ester functional group (–COO–) along its repeating backbone, which is either synthesized from renewable biological (bio-based) feedstocks or synthesized via chemical routes but is fully biodegradable under environmental conditions (composting, soil, marine, or enzymatic action). According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PEPOT632, bio polyesters occupy a critical intersection between polymer chemistry and environmental engineering because they offer a closed-loop carbon cycle, replacing petroleum-derived plastics (PE, PP, PET) that persist for centuries in landfills.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** Bio polyesters = *Bio-based* (derived from renewable biomass such as corn, sugarcane, starch, or bacterial fermentation) **AND/OR** *Biodegradable* (cleaved into CO₂, H₂O, and biomass by microbial enzymatic action under specified conditions).

The general chemical architecture of a polyester is:

$$[-\text{O}-\text{R}-\text{CO}-]_n$$

where the **R** group denotes an aliphatic, aromatic, or aliphatic-aromatic segment linking two ester linkages.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine your kitchen's regular plastic food container (made of polypropylene, a petroleum product). If you throw it in a landfill, it will outlive your grandchildren — almost **500 years** of environmental persistence. Now imagine a similar container made from corn starch. After use, if you bury it in your backyard garden, in roughly **90–180 days**, microbes in the soil will recognize it as "food," chew it up using specialized enzymes, and convert it back into water, carbon dioxide, and harmless biomass — essentially completing nature's carbon cycle. That "magic" container is a **bio polyester**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Bio polyesters are like edible packaging for the planet — they look, feel, and behave like conventional plastic during use, but disappear gracefully back into nature at end-of-life.

### 1.3 Classification Framework

Bio polyesters are systematically classified into **three tiers** under the KTU syllabus framework:

| Class | Origin | Representative Examples |
|:------|:-------|:------------------------|
| **Bio-based & Biodegradable** | Fully from renewable resources AND biodegradable | PHB, PHBV, PLA, PHA family |
| **Bio-based, partially biodegradable** | From renewables, slow degradation | Bio-PET (30% bio-content), Bio-PE |
| **Petrochemical-based but biodegradable** | From crude oil, but biodegradable | PCL (Polycaprolactone), PGA |

### 1.4 Importance Metrics

- **Global bioplastics market (2024):** ≈ USD **12.5 billion**, projected to reach USD **45 billion by 2032** (CAGR ~ 17%).
- **Greenhouse gas reduction:** Bio polyesters reduce CO₂ footprint by **30–70%** compared to conventional plastics.
- **Standard degradation certification:** **EN 13432** (European) and **ASTM D6400** (US) certify industrial compostability within **180 days**.
- **Carbon-neutral principle:** Biomass absorbs CO₂ during growth → polymer stores carbon → biodegradation releases the same CO₂ — net atmospheric carbon = **0**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The syllabus explicitly emphasizes the difference between *bio-based* (carbon origin) and *biodegradable* (end-of-life behavior). A polymer can be bio-based but NOT biodegradable (e.g., bio-PE) — these two properties are independent.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Chemistry of the Ester Linkage

The **ester bond** (–C(=O)–O–) is the structural and functional heart of every polyester. It is formed via a **condensation (esterification)** reaction between a **carboxylic acid** (–COOH) and an **alcohol** (–OH), releasing one molecule of **water** as a byproduct.

$$\text{R-COOH} + \text{HO-R'} \rightleftharpoons \text{R-COO-R'} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

In polymers, this reaction occurs repeatedly between **difunctional monomers** (either diacid + diol, or hydroxy acids), generating long chains. The reverse reaction — hydrolysis of the ester bond — is what drives biodegradation.

### 2.2 Step-Growth Polymerization Logic (Bulleted Breakdown)

The classical synthesis of linear aliphatic polyesters follows the **step-growth polycondensation** mechanism:

- **Step 1 — Monomer selection:** Choose a diacid (HOOC–R–COOH) and a diol (HO–R'–OH), or a hydroxy acid (HO–R–COOH).
- **Step 2 — Pre-polymerization:** At **150–200 °C**, esterification proceeds with water removal (often using a Dean-Stark trap or N₂ purge).
- **Step 3 — Catalyst addition:** Common catalysts include **titanium(IV) butoxide** Ti(OBu)₄, **antimony trioxide** Sb₂O₃, or **zinc acetate** — accelerating the condensation without promoting side reactions.
- **Step 4 — Melt polycondensation:** Temperature raised to **220–280 °C**, high vacuum (≤ 1 mbar) applied to drive equilibrium toward the polymer and remove residual water.
- **Step 5 — Chain extension (optional):** Coupling agents (e.g., diisocyanates, bisoxazolines) are used to push molecular weight from ~ 20,000 to > 100,000 g/mol.

**Why this matters for KTU:** The **Carothers equation** governs the degree of polymerization in step-growth systems. For stoichiometric imbalance, the equation is:

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{1 + r}{1 + r - 2rp}$$

where:
- $\overline{X}_n$ = number-average degree of polymerization
- $r$ = stoichiometric ratio of functional groups (≤ 1)
- $p$ = extent of reaction (conversion)

For exact stoichiometry ($r = 1$), it reduces to:

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{1}{1 - p}$$

> **Engineering Utility:** This explains why bio polyesters derived from bio-based hydroxy acids (e.g., lactic acid) inherently avoid stoichiometric imbalance — they are mono-functional in a single molecule, pushing $\overline{X}_n$ higher and achieving commercially useful molecular weights (> 100,000 Da) for PLA via ring-opening polymerization instead.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Sheet for Bio Polyesters (KTU 2024 Module 4)**

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Key Variables | Units / Notes |
|:-:|:------------------|:-----------|:--------------|:--------------|
| 1 | Esterification equilibrium | $K_{eq} = \frac{[\text{ester}][\text{H}_2\text{O}]}{[\text{COOH}][\text{OH}]}$ | $K_{eq}$ ≈ 4–10 at 200 °C | Dimensionless; governs polycondensation equilibrium |
| 2 | Carothers equation (general) | $\overline{X}_n = \frac{1 + r}{1 + r - 2rp}$ | $r$ = ratio, $p$ = conversion | Used for AA + BB polycondensation |
| 3 | Carothers (stoichiometric) | $\overline{X}_n = \frac{1}{1-p}$ | $p$ → 0.99 gives $\overline{X}_n$ = 100 | High conversion needed for high MW |
| 4 | Number-average MW | $\overline{M}_n = \overline{X}_n \cdot M_0$ | $M_0$ = repeat unit MW | g/mol |
| 5 | Glass transition rule (Fox) | $\frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{w_1}{T_{g1}} + \frac{w_2}{T_{g2}}$ | $w_i$ = weight fractions, $T_{gi}$ in K | Predicts blend $T_g$ |
| 6 | Biodegradation rate (first-order) | $\frac{dM}{dt} = -kM$ | $k$ = rate constant | Half-life: $t_{1/2} = \ln 2 / k$ |
| 7 | Molecular weight vs. degradation | $\log k = \log A - \frac{E_a}{2.303RT}$ | Arrhenius form | Higher MW → slower biodegradation |
| 8 | Lactic acid → PLA stoichiometry | $\text{LA} \xrightarrow{\text{ROP}} \text{PLA} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ (catalytic) | — | Industrial route |

**Critical Rule:** NEVER confuse the **molecular weight threshold** for biodegradability — typically, polyesters with $\overline{M}_n \le 40{,}000$ Da degrade rapidly; $\ge 100{,}000$ Da degrade slowly. Microbial enzymes cleave chain ends, so long chains take longer to fully metabolize.

### 2.4 Mechanism of Biodegradation — The "Why and How"

Biodegradation of bio polyesters occurs in **two distinguishable phases**:

1. **Abiotic Hydrolysis (Chemical Step):** Water molecules attack the ester carbonyl carbon (electrophilic C of C=O), breaking the C–O bond. This fragments the polymer into shorter oligomers and eventually hydroxy acid monomers. Rate depends on **temperature**, **moisture**, **pH**, and **ester bond accessibility**.

2. **Biotic Assimilation (Microbial Step):** Soil bacteria (e.g., *Pseudomonas*, *Alcaligenes*, *Bacillus*) and fungi (*Aspergillus*) secrete extracellular **depolymerases** (PHB depolymerase, lipase, esterase) that catalyze hydrolysis far faster than abiotic water. The resulting monomers enter microbial metabolism via **β-oxidation** and the **TCA cycle**, releasing CO₂ and H₂O.

$$[\text{O-R-CO}]_n \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2\text{O, enzyme}} n\,\text{HO-R-COOH} \xrightarrow{\text{microbial metabolism}} \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{biomass}$$

> **Engineering Significance:** This dual mechanism is why bio polyesters are preferred for **medical implants** (sutures, scaffolds) — degradation rate can be tuned by **crystallinity, copolymer ratio, and molecular weight** to match tissue regeneration timescales.

### 2.5 Categories of Bio Polyesters — Deep Dive

#### 2.5.1 Polyhydroxyalkanoates (PHAs)
- **Definition:** A family of intracellular polyesters synthesized by bacteria as carbon/energy storage granules, structurally identical to conventional plastics but **100% biodegradable**.
- **Monomer:** (R)-3-hydroxyalkanoic acids (3-HA).
- **Repeat unit:** $[-\text{O-CHR-CH}_2-\text{CO}-]_n$
- **Most studied member:** **Poly(3-hydroxybutyrate)** — PHB, discovered by **Maurice Lemoigne (1925)** in *Bacillus megaterium*.
- **Copolymers:** **PHBV** (3-hydroxybutyrate-co-3-hydroxyvalerate), **PHBHHx** (3-hydroxybutyrate-co-3-hydroxyhexanoate) — copolymerization improves mechanical flexibility and lowers brittleness.

#### 2.5.2 Polylactic Acid (PLA)
- **Definition:** Aliphatic polyester derived from **lactic acid** (2-hydroxypropanoic acid), produced via fermentation of corn starch or sugarcane.
- **Synthesis route:** **Ring-Opening Polymerization (ROP)** of lactide (cyclic dimer of lactic acid) using **tin(II) 2-ethylhexanoate** $\text{Sn(Oct)}_2$ catalyst.
- **Stereoisomers:** L-lactide, D-lactide, meso-lactide → enables tuning of crystallinity and $T_g$ via stereocopolymerization.

#### 2.5.3 Polycaprolactone (PCL)
- **Definition:** Semi-crystalline aliphatic polyester from **ε-caprolactone** monomer via ROP.
- **Properties:** $T_m \approx 60\,°C$, $T_g \approx -60\,°C$, very low $T_g$ → rubbery at room temperature.
- **Degradation:** Slow hydrolysis (2–4 years), but enzymatically fast (lipase from *Pseudomonas*).

#### 2.5.4 Other Notable Bio Polyesters

| Polymer | Repeat Unit | Source | Degradation |
|:--------|:------------|:-------|:------------|
| **PGA** (Polyglycolide) | $[-\text{O-CH}_2-\text{CO}-]$ | Glycolic acid | 2–4 months |
| **PBS** (Polybutylene succinate) | $[-\text{O-(CH}_2)_4\text{-O-CO-(CH}_2)_2\text{-CO}-]$ | Bio-succinic acid + 1,4-butanediol | 3–6 months |
| **PBAT** (Polybutylene adipate-co-terephthalate) | Mixed aliphatic-aromatic | Adipic + terephthalic acid + BDO | 2–6 months |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Carothers Equation for Stoichiometric Polycondensation

**Starting Premise:** In step-growth polymerization of AA + BB monomers with perfect stoichiometry ($r = 1$), let:
- $N_0$ = initial number of functional groups (–COOH or –OH)
- $N$ = number of unreacted functional groups at extent of reaction $p$

**Step 1: Definition of Conversion (p)**

The fraction of functional groups that have reacted:

$$p = \frac{N_0 - N}{N_0}$$

Therefore, the fraction remaining is:

$$1 - p = \frac{N}{N_0}$$

**Step 2: Number of Molecules**

Each bond formed links two molecules together. Total number of bonds formed = $N_0 p$. By the principle of linkage, the total number of polymer molecules present:

$$N_{\text{mol}} = N_0 - N_0 p = N_0 (1 - p)$$

This is because each new bond consumes exactly one molecule.

**Step 3: Definition of Degree of Polymerization**

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{\text{Initial monomer molecules}}{\text{Final polymer molecules}} = \frac{N_0}{N_0(1-p)} = \frac{1}{1-p}$$

**Step 4: Generalization for Non-Stoichiometric Case**

When $r < 1$ (excess of one functional group, say –OH over –COOH), the minor functional groups limit chain growth. The total initial groups = $N_A + N_B$ where $N_B = rN_A$. The degree of polymerization becomes:

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{1 + r}{1 + r - 2rp}$$

This is the **Carothers equation in its complete form**, valid for AA + BB systems.

**Step 5: Numerical Verification**

Suppose $r = 0.99$ (1% stoichiometric imbalance) and $p = 0.995$ (99.5% conversion):

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{1 + 0.99}{1 + 0.99 - 2(0.99)(0.995)} = \frac{1.99}{1.99 - 1.9701} = \frac{1.99}{0.0199} = 100$$

This shows that even a 0.5% deviation in conversion or stoichiometry drastically lowers the degree of polymerization — a critical sensitivity for polyester synthesis.

### 3.2 Derivation: Molecular Weight from Monomer Properties

**Given:** Lactic acid monomer molecular weight = 90.08 g/mol. PLA repeat unit (–O–CH(CH₃)–CO–) = 72.06 g/mol (after loss of H₂O).

**Step 1:** Calculate degree of polymerization needed for commercial PLA ($\overline{M}_n \approx 100{,}000$ g/mol):

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{\overline{M}_n}{M_0} = \frac{100{,}000}{72.06} \approx 1388$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the conversion $p$ required to achieve this $\overline{X}_n$ (assuming stoichiometric case):

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{1}{1-p} \implies 1 - p = \frac{1}{1388} \implies p = 1 - 0.00072 = 0.99928$$

So the reaction must reach **99.93% conversion** — extraordinarily difficult via direct polycondensation. This is precisely **why PLA is industrially synthesized via ring-opening polymerization of lactide** rather than direct condensation of lactic acid.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Bio Polyester Property Comparison

| Property | PHB | PLA | PCL | PBS | PBAT |
|:---------|:----|:----|:----|:----|:-----|
| **$T_g$ (°C)** | 4 | 55–62 | -60 | -32 | -30 |
| **$T_m$ (°C)** | 170–180 | 150–180 | 60 | 115 | 110–120 |
| **Tensile Strength (MPa)** | 35–40 | 50–70 | 20–25 | 35–45 | 15–25 |
| **Elongation at break (%)** | 3–6 | 2–10 | 600–700 | 300–560 | 500–800 |
| **Density (g/cm³)** | 1.25 | 1.24 | 1.09 | 1.26 | 1.26 |
| **Biodegradation time** | 3–9 months (soil) | 6–24 months (industrial compost) | 2–4 years (soil), weeks (enzymatic) | 3–6 months (compost) | 2–6 months |
| **Source** | Bacterial | Corn starch | Petrochemical | Bio-succinic acid | Mixed |
| **Industrial production** | Pilot | Commercial (> 200 kt/yr) | Commercial | Commercial | Commercial |

### 3.4 Derivation: Glass Transition Temperature of PLA Stereocopolymers

Using the **Fox equation** for stereocopolymers of L-lactide and D-lactide, where the optical purity governs crystallinity:

$$\frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{w_L}{T_{g,L}} + \frac{w_D}{T_{g,D}}$$

With pure PLLA: $T_{g,L} \approx 333$ K (60 °C). Pure PDLA: $T_{g,D} \approx 333$ K (60 °C). For a 95:5 L:D ratio:

$$\frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{0.95}{333} + \frac{0.05}{333} = \frac{1.00}{333} \implies T_g = 333 \text{ K} = 60\,°C$$

But at 80:20 L:D ratio, $T_g$ drops to ~ 328 K (55 °C), and crystallization becomes impossible above ~ 15% D-content → **fully amorphous PLA**. This is exploited industrially to produce amorphous PLA grades for transparent packaging films.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification Flowchart of Bio Polyesters

```mermaid
flowchart TD

```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[Bio Polyester Article] --> nodeA[Stage 1 Abiotic Hydrolysis]
    nodeA --> nodeA1[Water attack on ester carbonyl]
    nodeA --> nodeA2[Chain scission into oligomers]
    nodeA1 --> nodeB
    nodeA2 --> nodeB

    nodeB[Stage 2 Enzymatic Hydrolysis] --> nodeB1[Microbial depolymerases secreted]
    nodeB1 --> nodeB2[Lipases esterases PHB depolymerases]
    nodeB2 --> nodeC

    nodeC[Stage 3 Monomer Assimilation] --> nodeC1[Hydroxy acid monomers]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC2[Beta oxidation]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC3[TCA cycle]
    nodeC3 --> nodeD
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
**Define bio polyesters. Distinguish between bio-based and biodegradable polymers with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**  
Bio polyesters are polymers containing ester linkages (–COO–) in their repeating units, derived either from renewable biological feedstocks (bio-based) or capable of microbial degradation (biodegradable), or both.  
*Bio-based* refers to the **carbon origin** — the polymer's carbon atoms come from contemporary biomass (e.g., **Bio-PE from sugarcane ethanol**). *Biodegradable* refers to **end-of-life behavior** — the polymer is enzymatically cleaved into CO₂ and H₂O by microorganisms (e.g., **PHB degraded by *Alcaligenes eutrophus***). The two properties are **independent**: bio-PE is bio-based but NOT biodegradable, while PCL is biodegradable but petrochemical-based.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**  
**What is PHB? Write its monomer structure and the microorganism involved in its production.**

**Model Answer:**  
PHB stands for **Poly(3-hydroxybutyrate)**, a biodegradable, bio-based aliphatic polyester belonging to the PHA family. Its monomer is **(R)-3-hydroxybutyric acid**, structure: $\text{CH}_3\text{-CH(OH)-CH}_2\text{-COOH}$. The repeat unit is $[-\text{O-CH(CH}_3\text{)-CH}_2\text{-CO}-]_n$. It was first isolated from *Bacillus megaterium* by **Maurice Lemoigne in 1925** and is industrially produced by *Cupriavidus necator* (formerly *Alcaligenes eutrophus*) through fermentation of glucose or palm oil under nitrogen-limiting conditions.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 4, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the synthesis, properties, and biodegradation mechanism of **Polyhydroxybutyrate (PHB)**. Compare its properties with **conventional polypropylene (PP)**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Synthesis of PHB:**

PHB is synthesized biologically via **bacterial fermentation** using *Cupriavidus necator*. The process involves three acetyl-CoA molecules condensing to form **acetoacetyl-CoA**, which is reduced to **3-hydroxybutyryl-CoA** by the enzyme **acetoacetyl-CoA reductase**, and finally polymerized by **PHB synthase (PhaC)** into intracellular granules.

$$2\,\text{Acetyl-CoA} \rightarrow \text{Acetoacetyl-CoA} \xrightarrow{\text{NADPH}} \text{3-HB-CoA} \xrightarrow{\text{PhaC}} \text{PHB}$$

The fermentation is conducted under **nitrogen-limiting but carbon-excess conditions** in fed-batch reactors at **30–37 °C**, pH 6.8–7.2, yielding up to **80% cell dry weight** as PHB. Cells are then harvested, lyophilized, and PHB is extracted using chloroform or sodium hypochlorite.

**(ii) Properties of PHB:**

- Molecular weight: $\overline{M}_w$ = 200,000–1,000,000 g/mol
- $T_g$ ≈ 4 °C, $T_m$ ≈ 170–180 °C
- High crystallinity (~ 60–70%), leading to brittleness
- Biocompatible, non-toxic, optically active
- Degrades completely in soil within 3–9 months
- UV-resistant, water-resistant

**(iii) Biodegradation Mechanism:**

PHB is degraded by **PHB depolymerases** secreted by soil bacteria (*Pseudomonas lemoignei*, *Alcaligenes faecalis*). The enzyme hydrolyzes the ester bond, releasing **3-hydroxybutyric acid monomers and dimers**, which enter microbial β-oxidation → acetyl-CoA → TCA cycle → CO₂ + H₂O.

**(iv) Comparison with Polypropylene (PP):**

| Property | PHB | PP |
|:---------|:----|:---|
| Source | Renewable (bacterial) | Petroleum |
| $T_m$ (°C) | 175 | 165 |
| Tensile Strength | 35 MPa | 30–40 MPa |
| Elongation | 3–6% | 100–600% |
| Biodegradability | Complete in soil | None (> 500 years) |
| CO₂ footprint | Carbon-neutral | High |
| Brittleness | High (limitation) | Low |

PHB has similar melting point and tensile strength but is **highly crystalline and brittle**, requiring copolymerization (PHBV) to overcome this.

---

**(b)** Describe the **ring-opening polymerization (ROP) of lactide** to produce **Polylactic Acid (PLA)**. Discuss the effect of stereochemistry on PLA properties. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) ROP Mechanism:**

PLA is industrially produced via ROP of **lactide** (the cyclic dimer of lactic acid). The process proceeds as:

- **Step 1:** Lactic acid is produced by fermentation of corn starch using *Lactobacillus* species.
- **Step 2:** Crude lactic acid is purified, concentrated, and **oligomerized** to low-MW prepolymer.
- **Step 3:** Prepolymer is depolymerized at **200–220 °C** under vacuum in the presence of **tin(II) octoate** catalyst to produce **lactide** (cyclic dimer).
- **Step 4:** Lactide is purified by distillation/crystallization to desired stereoisomer purity.
- **Step 5:** Purified lactide undergoes **ROP** at **130–180 °C** with $\text{Sn(Oct)}_2$ catalyst.

$$\text{Lactide} \xrightarrow{\text{Sn(Oct)}_2,\; 150\,°C} \text{PLA}$$

The mechanism is **coordination-insertion**: Sn(Oct)₂ coordinates the lactide carbonyl, an alcohol (initiator, ROH) attacks, and the ring opens via acyl-oxygen cleavage, propagating chain growth.

**(ii) Stereochemistry Effects:**

Lactide exists as **L-lactide, D-lactide, and meso-lactide**. The stereo-composition of the feed controls PLA properties:

- **100% L-lactide → PLLA:** Semicrystalline, $T_m$ ≈ 180 °C, slow crystallization.
- **100% D-lactide → PDLA:** Semicrystalline, similar to PLLA.
- **50:50 L:D → PDLLA:** Fully amorphous, $T_g$ ≈ 55 °C, transparent.
- **Stereocomplex (1:1 PLLA + PDLA blended):** $T_m$ ≈ 230 °C, superior heat resistance — used in high-performance applications.

Industrial PLA grades range from amorphous packaging films (high D-content) to semicrystalline injection molding resins (> 95% L).

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Module 4, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**Q. (a)** Discuss the **classification, synthesis, and applications** of **Polyhydroxyalkanoates (PHAs)** with special emphasis on **PHB and PHBV**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Classification of PHAs:**

PHAs are classified by the number of carbon atoms in their monomer side chain:
- **Short-chain-length (scl) PHAs:** 3–5 carbons (e.g., PHB, PHV) — highly crystalline, stiff.
- **Medium-chain-length (mcl) PHAs:** 6–14 carbons (e.g., PHO, PHN) — elastomeric, low crystallinity.

Copolymers include **PHBV** (3-hydroxybutyrate-co-3-hydroxyvalerate) and **PHBHHx** (3-hydroxybutyrate-co-3-hydroxyhexanoate).

**(ii) Biosynthesis Pathway:**

Three main pathways exist:
1. **Pathway I (scl-PHA, e.g., *C. necator*):** Acetyl-CoA → Acetoacetyl-CoA → 3-HB-CoA → PHB via PhaC synthase.
2. **Pathway II (mcl-PHA, e.g., *Pseudomonas putida*):** Acyl-ACP intermediates from fatty acid synthesis.
3. **Pathway III (in *E. coli* engineered):** Direct supply of 3-HA monomers → PhaC polymerization.

**(iii) PHB vs PHBV Comparison:**

| Property | PHB | PHBV |
|:---------|:----|:-----|
| 3-HV content | 0% | 5–20 mol% |
| $T_m$ | 175 °C | 130–170 °C |
| $T_g$ | 4 °C | -1 to 8 °C |
| Crystallinity | 60–70% | 35–55% |
| Toughness | Brittle | Improved flexibility |
| Processing window | Narrow | Wider |

Adding 3-HV units disrupts PHB crystallinity, improving **toughness, flexibility, and melt processability**.

**(iv) Applications:**

- Medical: sutures, bone scaffolds, drug delivery (FDA-approved)
- Packaging: single-use bags, food containers
- Agricultural: mulch films, controlled-release fertilizers
- Marine: biodegradable fishing nets

---

**(b)** Explain the **mechanism of biodegradation of bio polyesters**. Discuss the factors affecting the biodegradation rate. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Biodegradation Mechanism:**

As described in §2.4, biodegradation occurs in four stages:
1. **Surface adsorption** of microorganisms and secretion of depolymerases.
2. **Enzymatic hydrolysis** of ester bonds (PHB depolymerase, lipase, cutinase).
3. **Monomer/dimer release** and transport into microbial cells.
4. **Metabolic mineralization** → CO₂ + H₂O + biomass (aerobic) or CH₄ + CO₂ (anaerobic).

**(ii) Factors Affecting Biodegradation Rate:**

| Factor | Effect on Rate |
|:-------|:---------------|
| **Polymer crystallinity** | Higher crystallinity → slower degradation (enzymes prefer amorphous regions) |
| **Molecular weight** | Higher $\overline{M}_n$ → slower (more chain scissions needed) |
| **Surface area** | Larger surface area → faster degradation |
| **Temperature** | Higher $T$ (up to ~ 60 °C) → faster enzymatic activity |
| **Moisture** | Essential for hydrolysis; > 50% RH required |
| **pH** | Neutral pH (6–8) optimal for most depolymerases |
| **Copolymer composition** | PHBV > 20% HV degrades faster than PHB |
| **Additives / plasticizers** | Often accelerate (by lowering $T_g$, increasing chain mobility) |
| **Microbial population** | Compost > soil > marine (by microbial diversity) |

Quantitative example: PHB films degrade in **~ 90 days in activated sludge** but **~ 250 days in garden soil**, reflecting microbial diversity differences.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (KTU Examiner Insights):**
> 1. **Conflating "bio-based" with "biodegradable":** These are independent properties. Bio-PE is NOT biodegradable. Examiners deduct **2 marks** if confused.
> 2. **Writing PLA monomer as "lactic acid" without stereochemistry:** PLA properties depend critically on L:D ratio. Always mention stereoisomers.
> 3. **Skipping reaction conditions:** PHB requires **nitrogen limitation**; PLA ROP requires **tin catalyst and 150 °C**. Examiners expect specific numerical conditions.
> 4. **Confusing Carothers equation applicability:** It applies only to step-growth. For PLA (ROP), use $\overline{M}_n = \overline{X}_n \cdot M_0$ directly.
> 5. **Forgetting repeat unit structures:** Always draw or write the chemical structure of the repeat unit for full credit on "explain" questions.
> 6. **Not mentioning ASTM D6400 / EN 13432 standards** when discussing biodegradability — these are international certification standards often referenced in KTU questions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Bio Polyesters (Module 4)**

**Core Definitions:**
- ☐ Bio polyesters contain **ester linkages** (–COO–) and are bio-based and/or biodegradable.
- ☐ **Bio-based** ≠ **biodegradable** (independent properties).
- ☐ **PHB** = Poly(3-hydroxybutyrate), discovered 1925, produced by *C. necator*.
- ☐ **PLA** = Polylactic acid, from corn starch via ROP of lactide with $\text{Sn(Oct)}_2$ catalyst.
- ☐ **PCL** = Polycaprolactone, semi-crystalline, low $T_g$ (-60 °C).

**Key Reactions:**
- ☐ Esterification: $\text{R-COOH} + \text{HO-R'} \rightleftharpoons \text{R-COO-R'} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$
- ☐ PHB biosynthesis: $2\,\text{Acetyl-CoA} \rightarrow \text{Acetoacetyl-CoA} \rightarrow 3\text{-HB-CoA} \xrightarrow{\text{PhaC}} \text{PHB}$
- ☐ PLA synthesis: $\text{Lactide} \xrightarrow{\text{Sn(Oct)}_2,\; 150\,°C} \text{PLA}$
- ☐ Biodegradation: Polymer → oligomers → monomers → CO₂ + H₂O + biomass

**Key Equations:**
- ☐ Carothers: $\overline{X}_n = \frac{1 + r}{1 + r - 2rp}$, simplified to $\frac{1}{1-p}$ for $r = 1$.
- ☐ Fox equation: $\frac{1}{T_g} = \sum \frac{w_i}{T_{gi}}$
- ☐ Conversion requirement: 99.93% for $\overline{X}_n$ = 100 (stoichiometric).

**Key Numbers to Memorize:**
- ☐ PHB: $T_m$ = 175 °C, $T_g$ = 4 °C, 3–9 months soil degradation.
- ☐ PLA: $T_m$ = 150–180 °C, $T_g$ = 55–62 °C, 6–24 months industrial compost.
- ☐ PCL: $T_m$ = 60 °C, $T_g$ = -60 °C.
- ☐ Industrial compost standards: **EN 13432, ASTM D6400** — ≥ 90% degradation in 180 days.
- ☐ PLA commercial production: > 200,000 tonnes/year globally.

**Key Comparisons (Frequently Tested):**
- ☐ PHB vs PP: similar $T_m$, PHB is brittle but biodegradable.
- ☐ PLLA vs PDLLA: semicrystalline vs amorphous.
- ☐ PHB vs PHBV: PHBV has lower crystallinity, better toughness.
- ☐ ROP vs polycondensation: ROP gives higher MW without stoichiometric constraints.

**Sustainability Context:**
- ☐ Bioplastics market ≈ USD 12.5 billion (2024), CAGR 17%.
- ☐ Bio polyesters reduce CO₂ footprint by 30–70%.
- ☐ Carbon-neutral principle: biomass CO₂ = degradation CO₂.

**Top 5 KTU Frequently Asked Topics:**
1. PHB synthesis and properties
2. PLA ROP mechanism
3. Carothers equation derivation
4. Biodegradation mechanism and factors
5. PHB vs PP / PHB vs PHBV comparison

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Synthesis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT632 - POLYMERS & ENVIRONMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Synthesis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Polymer Synthesis** is the controlled chemical process by which small repeating units called **monomers** are covalently bonded together to form long-chain macromolecules (polymers) of high molecular weight (**typically 10⁴ – 10⁷ g/mol**). In the context of KTU PEPOT632 (Polymers & Environment), Module 4 specifically addresses the strategic pathways, reaction mechanisms, kinetic control parameters, and industrial reactor configurations used to manufacture commercially relevant polymers.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme Syllabus**, polymer synthesis is broadly classified into two primary mechanistic families:

1. **Chain-Growth (Addition) Polymerization** — initiated by an active centre (radical, cation, anion, or metal complex) that adds monomers sequentially to a propagating chain end.
2. **Step-Growth (Condensation) Polymerization** — proceeds by the stepwise reaction between bifunctional or multifunctional monomers, with each step independent of chain length, often eliminating a small byproduct (H₂O, HCl, CH₃OH).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The Module 4 weightage explicitly requires students to distinguish between *addition* and *condensation* polymerizations, comprehend the kinetic chain length concept, derive the Carothers equation, identify industrial polymerization techniques (bulk, solution, suspension, emulsion), and synthesize the mechanism for at least 4 commercial polymers (PE, PP, PVC, PS, PMMA, Nylon, PET).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are constructing a **garland of paper flowers** for a college fest:

- **Chain-Growth Polymerization** is like adding flowers **one-by-one to a single growing string**. You start with a clip (initiator), and each new flower attaches only at the **active end**. The garland is built rapidly, but only at one location at a time.
- **Step-Growth Polymerization** is like randomly joining **scattered beads of two colours** anywhere on the floor. Any bead can join any other bead — short chains first, longer chains slowly emerge as more beads connect. Eventually, almost everything fuses into one giant necklace near the end.

### Real-World Engineering Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — Free Radical Polymerization is like a "Relay Race":**
> The **initiator** is the starter's pistol that creates the first runner (radical). The **monomer** is the track that each runner adds lap to. The **propagation** is the runner sprinting and grabbing new baton segments. The **termination** is when two runners collide and stop. The **chain transfer** is when a runner hands the baton to a spectator, who then starts running — creating a new chain while killing the old one.

---

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics and Terminology

| Term | Symbol | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Monomer Molecular Weight** | $M_0$ | 28 – 200 g/mol |
| **Degree of Polymerization** | $\overline{X_n}$ | 10² – 10⁵ |
| **Number-average Molecular Weight** | $\overline{M_n}$ | 10⁴ – 10⁶ g/mol |
| **Weight-average Molecular Weight** | $\overline{M_w}$ | 10⁴ – 10⁷ g/mol |
| **Polydispersity Index** | PDI = $\overline{M_w}/\overline{M_n}$ | 1.0 (theoretical) – 20 (industrial) |
| **Conversion** | $p$ | 0 < $p$ < 1 |
| **Rate of Polymerization** | $R_p$ | mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹ |

---

## 1.4 Classification Snapshot (KTU Board Exam Favourite)

```text
POLYMER SYNTHESIS
├── CHAIN-GROWTH (Addition)
│   ├── Free Radical (FRP)         → PE, PVC, PS, PMMA
│   ├── Cationic                   → PIB, Butyl rubber
│   ├── Anionic                    → SBR, Living PS
│   └── Coordination (Z-N)         → HDPE, iPP, sPP
│
└── STEP-GROWTH (Condensation)
    ├── Polycondensation (with byproduct) → Nylon, PET, PC
    └── Polyaddition (no byproduct)       → PU, Epoxy
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:**
> In KTU ESE papers, students frequently lose marks by confusing **"addition polymerization"** (the mechanism — no byproduct) with **"addition polymer"** (the product class — e.g., polyethylene). Always clarify both the **mechanism** and the **monomer functionality** in your answer.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Chain-Growth (Addition) Polymerization — Mechanism Phases

Chain-growth polymerization proceeds through **four distinct kinetic phases**. Understanding each is mandatory for KTU Module 4 valuation.

### Phase 1 — Initiation
An external energy source (heat, light, or a chemical initiator) generates an active centre. For **free radical polymerization (FRP)**:

$$\text{I} \xrightarrow{k_d} 2\text{R}^{\bullet}$$

$$\text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{M} \xrightarrow{k_i} \text{R-M}^{\bullet}$$

- **$k_d$** = rate constant for initiator dissociation (s⁻¹)
- **$k_i$** = rate constant for initiation (L mol⁻¹ s⁻¹)
- Initiators commonly used: **AIBN** (azobisisobutyronitrile), **BPO** (benzoyl peroxide), **K₂S₂O₈** (potassium persulfate).

### Phase 2 — Propagation
The active chain-end repeatedly adds monomer units:

$$\text{M}_n^{\bullet} + \text{M} \xrightarrow{k_p} \text{M}_{n+1}^{\bullet}$$

The rate of propagation $R_p$ is **independent of chain length** because every propagating chain is assumed equally reactive (the *principle of equal reactivity*).

### Phase 3 — Termination
Two mechanisms exist:
- **Combination (Coupling):** $\text{M}_n^{\bullet} + \text{M}_m^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{M}_{n+m}$
- **Disproportionation:** $\text{M}_n^{\bullet} + \text{M}_m^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{M}_n \text{ (saturated)} + \text{M}_m \text{ (unsaturated)}$

### Phase 4 — Chain Transfer
The active centre transfers to another molecule (monomer, solvent, initiator, or polymer), terminating the original chain but starting a new one:

$$\text{M}_n^{\bullet} + \text{XH} \xrightarrow{k_{tr}} \text{M}_n\text{-H} + \text{X}^{\bullet}$$

---

## 2.2 Step-Growth (Condensation) Polymerization — Mechanism

Step-growth polymerization has **no active centre**. Every species — monomer, dimer, oligomer, polymer — carries reactive functional groups. At time $t$, with extent of reaction $p$:

- **Conversion $p$** = fraction of functional groups consumed.
- **Probability of finding an unreacted functional group** = $(1-p)$.
- **Probability of finding an $n$-mer** = $p^{(n-1)}(1-p)$.

### Carothers Equation (KTU Board Favourite — Repeatedly Asked)

The **number-average degree of polymerization** $\overline{X_n}$ for an **equimolar A–B** or **AA + BB** system is:

$$\overline{X_n} = \frac{1}{1-p}$$

### Carathers Equation for Stoichiometric Imbalance

When monomer stoichiometry is not perfectly equimolar ($N_A \neq N_B$), let $r = N_A/N_B < 1$:

$$\overline{X_n} = \frac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$$

When $r = 1$ (perfect stoichiometry), this reduces to the standard form.

---

## 2.3 Copolymerization — Mayo–Lewis Equation

When two monomers $M_1$ and $M_2$ copolymerize, the **reactivity ratios** $r_1$ and $r_2$ define chain composition:

$$\frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \frac{[M_1](r_1[M_1]+[M_2])}{[M_2]([M_1]+r_2[M_2])}$$

| Case | $r_1$, $r_2$ values | Resulting Copolymer |
|---|---|---|
| **Ideal (Random)** | $r_1 \cdot r_2 = 1$ | Random distribution (e.g., SBR) |
| **Alternating** | $r_1 = r_2 = 0$ | Strictly alternating (e.g., Styrene–Maleic Anhydride) |
| **Block** | $r_1 \gg 1$, $r_2 \gg 1$ | Block copolymer |
| **Living/Gradient** | $r_1$, $r_2$ → ∞ | Homopolymer blend |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas collectively cover ~85% of numerical questions in KTU PEPOT632 Module 4 ESE. Memorize these.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_p = k_p[M]\left(\dfrac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}\right)^{1/2}$ | Rate of FRP (steady-state) | mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹ |
| 2 | $R_i = 2 f k_d [I]$ | Rate of initiation | mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹ |
| 3 | $\nu = \dfrac{R_p}{R_i} = \dfrac{k_p[M]}{2(f k_d k_t [I])^{1/2}}$ | Kinetic chain length | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\overline{X_n} = 2\nu$ (combination) | Degree of polymerization | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\overline{X_n} = \nu$ (disproportionation) | Degree of polymerization | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\overline{X_n} = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ | Carothers (step-growth) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\overline{X_n} = \dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$ | Carothers (non-equimolar) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\dfrac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \dfrac{[M_1](r_1[M_1]+[M_2])}{[M_2]([M_1]+r_2[M_2])}$ | Mayo–Lewis copolymerization | mol ratio |
| 9 | $\overline{M_n} = \overline{X_n} \cdot M_0$ | Mn from DP | g/mol |
| 10 | $\text{PDI} = 1 + p$ | Polydispersity for step-growth | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\tau = \dfrac{[M]_0}{R_p}$ | Reaction time to desired conversion | seconds |
| 12 | $\overline{DP}_{\text{theor}} = \dfrac{[M]_0}{[I]_0}$ | DP for living polymerization | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where:**
> - $f$ = initiator efficiency (typically 0.3 – 0.8)
> - $[I]$, $[M]$ = initiator and monomer concentrations
> - $k_d, k_i, k_p, k_t$ = rate constants for dissociation, initiation, propagation, termination

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Carothers Equation for Step-Growth Polymerization

### Starting Premise
Consider a stoichiometric mixture of **AA** and **BB** bifunctional monomers. Let the initial total number of monomer molecules be $N_0$. After extent of reaction $p$, the remaining molecules (any size) is $N$.

### Step 1 — Define the extent of reaction
$$p = \frac{\text{Number of functional groups reacted}}{\text{Initial number of functional groups}}$$

Since each reaction consumes **2 functional groups** and creates **1 new bond linking 2 molecules** (reducing molecule count by 1):

$$N = N_0 - (\text{bonds formed}) = N_0 - p \cdot N_0$$

### Step 2 — Simplify
$$N = N_0 (1 - p)$$

### Step 3 — Define Degree of Polymerization
By definition, the **number-average degree of polymerization** $\overline{X_n}$ equals the initial number of molecules divided by the current number of molecules (since each "molecule" now contains $X_n$ original monomer units on average):

$$\overline{X_n} = \frac{N_0}{N}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute
Substituting $N = N_0(1-p)$:

$$\overline{X_n} = \frac{N_0}{N_0(1-p)} = \frac{1}{1-p}$$

$$\boxed{\overline{X_n} = \frac{1}{1-p}}$$

### Step 5 — Interpret
- When $p = 0.5$, $\overline{X_n} = 2$ → only dimers exist.
- When $p = 0.9$, $\overline{X_n} = 10$ → oligomers.
- When $p = 0.99$, $\overline{X_n} = 100$ → high polymer.
- When $p = 0.999$, $\overline{X_n} = 1000$ → engineering plastic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Insight:** Step-growth polymerization requires $p > 0.99$ to achieve commercially useful molecular weights. This is why **equilibrium removal of byproducts (water, HCl, methanol)** is essential — Le Chatelier's principle drives $p$ forward.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Carothers Equation for Non-Equimolar Stoichiometry

### Premise
Suppose we have $N_A$ moles of AA and $N_B$ moles of BB, with $N_A < N_B$ (minority reactant). Define stoichiometric imbalance ratio:

$$r = \frac{N_A}{N_B}, \quad r < 1$$

### Step 1 — Total initial functional groups
Total A-groups = $2N_A$, total B-groups = $2N_B$. The **minority** (A) limits the reaction.

### Step 2 — Functional group accounting
Number of A-groups initially: $2N_A$
Number of A-groups reacted: $2N_A p$
Number of unreacted A-groups: $2N_A (1-p)$

### Step 3 — Total molecules at conversion $p$
Each bond consumes 2 functional groups. Total molecules $N$:

$$N = N_A + N_B - (\text{bonds formed})$$

The number of bonds formed is constrained by the minority A-groups: $N_A p$. So:

$$N = N_A + N_B - N_A p$$

### Step 4 — Form the ratio
$$\overline{X_n} = \frac{N_A + N_B}{N_A + N_B - N_A p}$$

### Step 5 — Substitute $r = N_A/N_B$
Dividing numerator and denominator by $N_B$:

$$\overline{X_n} = \frac{r+1}{r+1 - rp}$$

$$\boxed{\overline{X_n} = \frac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}}$$

### Verification
Setting $r = 1$ (perfect stoichiometry):

$$\overline{X_n} = \frac{2}{2-2p} = \frac{1}{1-p} \quad \checkmark$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Rate of Free Radical Polymerization

### Step 1 — Rates of elementary steps

**Initiation:**
$$I \xrightarrow{k_d} 2R^{\bullet}, \quad R_i = 2 f k_d [I]$$

**Propagation:**
$$M_n^{\bullet} + M \xrightarrow{k_p} M_{n+1}^{\bullet}, \quad R_p = k_p [M^{\bullet}][M]$$

**Termination:**
$$M_n^{\bullet} + M_m^{\bullet} \xrightarrow{k_t} \text{dead polymer}, \quad R_t = 2 k_t [M^{\bullet}]^2$$

### Step 2 — Steady-state assumption
The radical concentration is very low and approximately constant. So $R_i = R_t$:

$$2 f k_d [I] = 2 k_t [M^{\bullet}]^2$$

Solving for $[M^{\bullet}]$:

$$[M^{\bullet}] = \left( \frac{f k_d [I]}{k_t} \right)^{1/2}$$

### Step 3 — Express $R_p$
$$R_p = k_p [M] [M^{\bullet}]$$

Substituting the steady-state radical concentration:

$$R_p = k_p [M] \left( \frac{f k_d [I]}{k_t} \right)^{1/2}$$

$$\boxed{R_p = k_p \left( \frac{f k_d}{k_t} \right)^{1/2} [M] [I]^{1/2}}$$

### Step 4 — Interpret orders
- $R_p \propto [M]^1$ → first order in monomer.
- $R_p \propto [I]^{1/2}$ → half order in initiator.
- This square-root dependence on $[I]$ is the **fingerprint of bimolecular termination** and is experimentally verifiable.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Kinetic Chain Length $\nu$ and $\overline{X_n}$

### Step 1 — Define kinetic chain length
The **kinetic chain length** $\nu$ is the average number of monomer units consumed per active centre initiated:

$$\nu = \frac{\text{Rate of monomer consumption}}{\text{Rate of radical generation}} = \frac{R_p}{R_i}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute expressions

$$\nu = \frac{k_p [M] [M^{\bullet}]}{2 f k_d [I]}$$

Using $[M^{\bullet}]$ from the steady-state result:

$$\nu = \frac{k_p [M]}{2(f k_d k_t [I])^{1/2}}$$

### Step 3 — Relate $\nu$ to $\overline{X_n}$
- If termination by **combination**: two chains merge → $\overline{X_n} = 2\nu$.
- If termination by **disproportionation**: chain length unchanged → $\overline{X_n} = \nu$.

$$\boxed{\overline{X_n} = \nu \text{ (disproportionation)}, \quad \overline{X_n} = 2\nu \text{ (combination)}}$$

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:**
A free radical polymerization of styrene ($M_0 = 104$ g/mol) is carried out with AIBN initiator at $60°$C. Given:
- $[M]_0 = 1.0$ mol/L
- $[I]_0 = 0.01$ mol/L
- $f = 0.6$
- $k_d = 9.2 \times 10^{-6}$ s⁻¹
- $k_p = 340$ L mol⁻¹ s⁻¹
- $k_t = 7.2 \times 10^7$ L mol⁻¹ s⁻¹
- Termination is by combination.

**Find (a) $R_p$, (b) $\overline{X_n}$, (c) $\overline{M_n}$.**

### Solution (a) — Rate of Polymerization

Apply steady-state:

$$[M^{\bullet}] = \left( \frac{f k_d [I]}{k_t} \right)^{1/2}$$

$$[M^{\bullet}] = \left( \frac{0.6 \times 9.2 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.01}{7.2 \times 10^7} \right)^{1/2}$$

$$\frac{0.6 \times 9.2 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.01}{7.2 \times 10^7} = \frac{5.52 \times 10^{-8}}{7.2 \times 10^7} = 7.67 \times 10^{-16}$$

$$[M^{\bullet}] = (7.67 \times 10^{-16})^{1/2} = 2.77 \times 10^{-8} \text{ mol/L}$$

Now compute $R_p$:

$$R_p = k_p [M] [M^{\bullet}] = 340 \times 1.0 \times 2.77 \times 10^{-8}$$

$$\boxed{R_p \approx 9.42 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mol L}^{-1}\text{ s}^{-1}}$$

### Solution (b) — Degree of Polymerization

First compute $\nu$:

$$\nu = \frac{R_p}{R_i} = \frac{R_p}{2 f k_d [I]}$$

$$R_i = 2 \times 0.6 \times 9.2 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.01 = 1.104 \times 10^{-7} \text{ mol L}^{-1}\text{ s}^{-1}$$

$$\nu = \frac{9.42 \times 10^{-6}}{1.104 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 85.3$$

For combination: $\overline{X_n} = 2\nu$:

$$\boxed{\overline{X_n} \approx 170.6}$$

### Solution (c) — Number-Average Molecular Weight

$$\overline{M_n} = \overline{X_n} \times M_0 = 170.6 \times 104$$

$$\boxed{\overline{M_n} \approx 17{,}742 \text{ g/mol} \approx 1.77 \times 10^4 \text{ g/mol}}$$

# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Free Radical Polymerization Mechanism (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initiator I] --> B[k_d]
    B --> C[Two free radicals R·]
    C --> D[k_i, add monomer M]
    D --> E[M· active center]
    E --> F[Propagation step k_p]
    F --> G[Growing chain M_n·]
    G --> H{Termination pathway}
    H --> I[Combination k_t,c]
    H --> J[Disproportionation k_t,d]
    I --> K[Dead polymer M_n+m]
    J --> L[Dead polymer M_n + M_m]
    G --> M[Chain transfer k_tr]
    M --> N[New active center X·]
    N --> E
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Step-Growth Polymerization Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[AA monomer] --> B1[Dimer A-B-A]
    B2[BB monomer] --> B1
    B1 --> C1[Trimer A-B-A-B-A]
    B1 --> C2[Tetramer B-A-B-A-B-A-B]
    C1 --> D1[Oligomer n=10]
    C2 --> D2[Oligomer n=20]
    D1 --> E[High polymer n=1000 at p=0.999]
    D2 --> E
    E --> F[Byproduct elimination H2O or HCl]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Polymerization Techniques Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Choose polymerization technique] --> Q1{Monomer is liquid?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Heat removal critical?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3[Suspension or Emulsion]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q4[Suspension]
    Q2 -->|No| Q5[Bulk polymerization]
    Q5 --> Q6{Purity needed?}
    Q6 -->|Yes| Q7[Solution polymerization]
    Q6 -->|No| Q5
    Q4 --> Q8[PVC, PS beads]
    Q3 --> Q9[Emulsion: latex, paints]
    Q3 --> Q10[Suspension: granular resin]
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Comparison of Step-Growth vs Chain-Growth

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SG[Step-Growth Polymerization]
        SG1[Any molecule can react with any molecule] --> SG2[Long chains only at high p]
        SG2 --> SG3[Byproduct released]
        SG3 --> SG4[Example: Nylon 6,6 and PET]
    end
    subgraph CG[Chain-Growth Polymerization]
        CG1[Active center required] --> CG2[Chain grows only at active end]
        CG2 --> CG3[No byproduct]
        CG3 --> CG4[Example: PE, PP, PS, PVC]
    end
```

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Coordination Polymerization (Ziegler–Natta) Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[TiCl4 catalyst] --> B[Alkyl aluminum cocatalyst R3Al]
    B --> C[Active site Ti-C bond]
    C --> D[Monomer coordination]
    D --> E[Insertion into Ti-C bond]
    E --> F[Chain elongation]
    F --> G{Termination}
    G --> H[beta-hydride elimination]
    G --> I[Transfer to monomer]
    H --> J[Dead polymer + Ti-H]
    I --> K[Dead polymer + new active site]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between addition polymerization and condensation polymerization with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | Addition Polymerization | Condensation Polymerization |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Chain-growth, propagates via active centre | Step-growth, any molecule reacts with any other |
| **Byproduct** | None (no small molecule eliminated) | Yes (e.g., H₂O, HCl, CH₃OH) |
| **Monomer Type** | Contains C=C or C≡C unsaturation | Contains –OH, –COOH, –NH₂, –NCO functional groups |
| **Activation Energy** | Lower | Higher |
| **Initiation** | Requires initiator, heat, or light | May require catalyst |
| **Molecular Weight Build-up** | High M.W. attained early in reaction | High M.W. only at very high conversion ($p > 0.99$) |
| **Example 1** | Polyethylene from ethylene | Nylon 6,6 from HMDA + adipic acid |
| **Example 2** | Polyvinyl chloride from vinyl chloride | PET from ethylene glycol + terephthalic acid |

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain the role of an initiator in free radical polymerization. Why is AIBN preferred over BPO for certain polymerizations?**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of Initiator:**
- The initiator provides the primary free radical that triggers chain propagation.
- It dissociates under mild conditions (heat or light) to generate two radical fragments.
- Each radical adds to a monomer's double bond, forming the active propagating chain end.

**Initiation Mechanism (AIBN):**

$$\text{(CH}_3)_2\text{C(CN)-N=N-C(CN)(CH}_3)_2 \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2(\text{CH}_3)_2\text{C}^{\bullet}\text{CN} + \text{N}_2$$

**Why AIBN is preferred over BPO:**

| Property | AIBN | BPO |
|---|---|---|
| **Decomposition Temperature** | 50–70 °C | 60–90 °C |
| **Byproducts** | N₂ gas (inert, easily removed) | Benzoic acid, CO₂ |
| **Solvent compatibility** | Better for polar monomers | Better for non-polar monomers |
| **Storage stability** | Higher | Lower (shock-sensitive) |
| **Radical formed** | Tertiary, resonance-stabilized | Benzoyloxy / phenyl radicals |

AIBN is preferred when **low-temperature polymerization** or **polar monomers (e.g., acrylates)** are involved because its radical is more electrophilic and less prone to side reactions.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Internal Choice Pattern (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Answer ANY ONE of Question A or Question B. Each Question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) Derive the Carothers equation for step-growth polymerization. Starting from $\overline{X_n} = 1/(1-p)$, show how the equation modifies when stoichiometric imbalance $r = N_A/N_B < 1$ exists between bifunctional monomers.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define extent of reaction:**
Let $N_A$ moles of AA and $N_B$ moles of BB monomers react. Define $p$ = fraction of A-groups reacted.

**Step 2 — Count functional groups:**
- Initial A-groups: $2N_A$
- A-groups remaining: $2N_A(1-p)$
- Bonds formed = $N_A \cdot p$ (constrained by A-groups)

**Step 3 — Total molecule count:**
Total molecules at time $t$:
$$N = N_A + N_B - N_A p$$

**Step 4 — Apply degree of polymerization definition:**
$$\overline{X_n} = \frac{N_A + N_B}{N_A + N_B - N_A p}$$

**Step 5 — Divide numerator and denominator by $N_B$ and substitute $r = N_A/N_B$:**

$$\overline{X_n} = \frac{r + 1}{r + 1 - rp}$$

$$\boxed{\overline{X_n} = \frac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}}$$

**Step 6 — Verify limit $r = 1$:**
$$\overline{X_n} = \frac{2}{2-2p} = \frac{1}{1-p} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 7 — Physical interpretation:**
- When $r < 1$, monomer $N_A$ is the minority reactant.
- The maximum attainable $\overline{X_n}$ is $\overline{X_n,\max} = (1+r)/(1-r)$.
- For example, with $r = 0.99$, $\overline{X_n,\max} = 1.99/0.01 = 199$, even at $p = 1$.

---

**(b) A step-growth polymerization of adipic acid with hexamethylenediamine is carried out with $r = 0.95$. Calculate $\overline{X_n}$ when (i) $p = 0.95$ and (ii) $p = 0.99$. Comment on the effect of stoichiometric imbalance on molecular weight.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

Using the modified Carothers equation:

$$\overline{X_n} = \frac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$$

**Case (i): $p = 0.95$, $r = 0.95$**

$$\overline{X_n} = \frac{1+0.95}{1+0.95 - 2(0.95)(0.95)}$$

$$= \frac{1.95}{1.95 - 1.805}$$

$$= \frac{1.95}{0.145}$$

$$\boxed{\overline{X_n} = 13.45}$$

**Case (ii): $p = 0.99$, $r = 0.95$**

$$\overline{X_n} = \frac{1.95}{1.95 - 2(0.95)(0.99)}$$

$$= \frac{1.95}{1.95 - 1.881}$$

$$= \frac{1.95}{0.069}$$

$$\boxed{\overline{X_n} \approx 28.26}$$

**Comment:**
Even at $p = 0.99$, with $r = 0.95$ the $\overline{X_n}$ is only ~28. This is far below the value of 100 achievable with perfect stoichiometry ($r = 1$) at the same conversion. **Conclusion:** Stoichiometric imbalance is a severe limiting factor in step-growth polymerization. Industrial nylon processes therefore employ exact metering of monomers and recycle of unreacted species to maintain $r$ extremely close to 1.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) Describe the mechanism of free radical polymerization in detail. Derive the rate expression $R_p = k_p [M] (f k_d [I]/k_t)^{1/2}$ using the steady-state assumption.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Mechanism — Four Phases:**

**1. Initiation:**
$$\text{I} \xrightarrow{k_d, f} 2 R^{\bullet}, \quad R^{\bullet} + M \xrightarrow{k_i} RM^{\bullet}$$

Rate: $R_i = 2 f k_d [I]$

**2. Propagation:**
$$RM^{\bullet} + M \xrightarrow{k_p} RM_2^{\bullet}, \quad R_p = k_p [M^{\bullet}][M]$$

**3. Termination:**
- Combination: $R_t = 2 k_t [M^{\bullet}]^2$
- Disproportionation: $R_t = 2 k_t [M^{\bullet}]^2$

**4. Chain Transfer:** $M_n^{\bullet} + XH \rightarrow M_nH + X^{\bullet}$ (rate $R_{tr}$)

**Derivation of $R_p$:**

**Steady-State Assumption:**
The radical concentration $[M^{\bullet}]$ reaches a quasi-steady state very quickly ($R_i = R_t$):

$$2 f k_d [I] = 2 k_t [M^{\bullet}]^2$$

$$[M^{\bullet}]^2 = \frac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}$$

$$[M^{\bullet}] = \left( \frac{f k_d [I]}{k_t} \right)^{1/2}$$

**Substitute into $R_p = k_p [M] [M^{\bullet}]$:**

$$R_p = k_p [M] \left( \frac{f k_d [I]}{k_t} \right)^{1/2}$$

$$\boxed{R_p = k_p \left( \frac{f k_d}{k_t} \right)^{1/2} [M] [I]^{1/2}}$$

**Kinetic Orders:**
- First order in monomer $[M]$
- Half order in initiator $[I]$
- The square-root dependence on $[I]$ is the experimental fingerprint confirming bimolecular termination.

---

**(b) Methyl methacrylate (MMA) is polymerized at 60 °C with AIBN initiator. Given $[M] = 2.0$ mol/L, $[I] = 0.02$ mol/L, $f = 0.5$, $k_d = 3.2 \times 10^{-5}$ s⁻¹, $k_p = 515$ L mol⁻¹ s⁻¹, $k_t = 2.3 \times 10^7$ L mol⁻¹ s⁻¹. Calculate (i) $R_p$, (ii) $\overline{X_n}$ assuming disproportionation termination, and (iii) the time required to achieve 25% conversion.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step (i) — Rate of Polymerization:**

$$[M^{\bullet}] = \left( \frac{f k_d [I]}{k_t} \right)^{1/2}$$

$$= \left( \frac{0.5 \times 3.2 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.02}{2.3 \times 10^7} \right)^{1/2}$$

$$= \left( \frac{3.2 \times 10^{-7}}{2.3 \times 10^7} \right)^{1/2} = (1.39 \times 10^{-14})^{1/2}$$

$$[M^{\bullet}] = 1.18 \times 10^{-7} \text{ mol/L}$$

$$R_p = k_p [M] [M^{\bullet}] = 515 \times 2.0 \times 1.18 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$\boxed{R_p \approx 1.21 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol L}^{-1}\text{ s}^{-1}}$$

**Step (ii) — Degree of Polymerization:**

$$R_i = 2 f k_d [I] = 2 \times 0.5 \times 3.2 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.02 = 6.4 \times 10^{-7} \text{ mol L}^{-1}\text{ s}^{-1}$$

$$\nu = \frac{R_p}{R_i} = \frac{1.21 \times 10^{-4}}{6.4 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 189.1$$

For disproportionation: $\overline{X_n} = \nu$:

$$\boxed{\overline{X_n} \approx 189}$$

**Step (iii) — Time for 25% conversion:**

Converted monomer = $0.25 \times [M]_0 = 0.25 \times 2.0 = 0.5$ mol/L.

$$\tau = \frac{0.5}{R_p} = \frac{0.5}{1.21 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{\tau \approx 4132 \text{ s} \approx 68.9 \text{ minutes}}$$

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Polymer Synthesis Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $R_p$ with $R_i$:** Students frequently use $R_i$ when calculating $\nu = R_p/R_i$. Always verify the numerator is the **propagation rate** and denominator is the **initiation rate**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in $R_i = 2 f k_d [I]$:** Each initiator molecule yields **two** radicals. Skipping the factor of 2 leads to $\overline{X_n}$ being underestimated by exactly 2x.
>
> 3. **Wrong termination factor:** $\overline{X_n} = \nu$ for disproportionation, $\overline{X_n} = 2\nu$ for combination. The question stem **always** specifies this — read carefully.
>
> 4. **Stoichiometric imbalance misuse:** For $r < 1$, students sometimes plug into the simple Carothers equation $\overline{X_n} = 1/(1-p)$ and lose 3–4 marks. Always check if $r = 1$ is given.
>
> 5. **Initiator efficiency assumption:** If $f$ is not given, KTU usually provides it. If absent, use $f \approx 0.5$ as standard, but state the assumption explicitly in the answer.
>
> 6. **Units mismatch:** $[I]$ and $[M]$ **must** be in mol/L; $k_p$ and $k_t$ units must match. Convert all values before plugging into equations.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Two mechanisms:** Chain-growth (active centre required, no byproduct) vs Step-growth (any molecule reacts, byproduct).
- **Carothers equation (equimolar):** $\overline{X_n} = 1/(1-p)$.
- **Carothers equation (non-equimolar):** $\overline{X_n} = (1+r)/(1+r-2rp)$.
- **Step-growth needs $p > 0.99$** to attain engineering-grade molecular weight.
- **Free radical $R_p \propto [M]^1 [I]^{1/2}$** — half-order in initiator is diagnostic.
- **Steady-state assumption:** $[M^{\bullet}] = (f k_d [I]/k_t)^{1/2}$.
- **Kinetic chain length:** $\nu = R_p/R_i = k_p[M]/[2(f k_d k_t [I])^{1/2}]$.
- **$\overline{X_n}$ vs termination:** $2\nu$ for combination, $\nu$ for disproportionation.
- **Copolymerization:** Mayo–Lewis with reactivity ratios $r_1, r_2$.
- **Industrial techniques:** Bulk (high purity, poor heat control), Solution (good heat removal, lower M.W.), Suspension (granular PVC/PS), Emulsion (latex, very high M.W.).
- **Initiators:** AIBN (50–70 °C, polar monomers), BPO (60–90 °C, non-polar).
- **Catalysts:** Ziegler–Natta (TiCl₄ + AlR₃) and Metallocene for stereoregular polyolefins (HDPE, iPP).
- **Examples:** PE, PP, PVC, PS, PMMA, Nylon 6,6, PET, Polycarbonate.
- **Always mention** monomer functionality (C=C for addition, –OH/–COOH/–NH₂ for condensation) when explaining a polymer synthesis.
- **KTU mark-earning phrases:** "principle of equal reactivity", "steady-state assumption", "Le Chatelier's principle for byproduct removal", "kinetic vs thermodynamic control".

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
