# Module 4: Thermal Degradation of Polymers — Section 5

## 5.1 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

### PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define thermal degradation of polymers. List any four factors affecting the thermal stability of a polymer.**

**Model Answer:**
Thermal degradation is the irreversible deterioration of a polymer's chemical structure and physical properties due to heat, occurring via chain scission, crosslinking, or depolymerization.

Four factors:
1. **Bond Dissociation Energy** — the weakest bond determines onset.
2. **Molecular structure** — aromatic rings, halogens (F) increase stability; tertiary C–H decreases it.
3. **Molecular weight** — higher Mₙ gives marginal stability increase.
4. **Atmosphere** — oxygen accelerates via peroxy radicals.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "high temperature" as a factor without specifying what structural property it acts on. Always link a factor to the underlying bond/mechanism for full marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between chain-end (unzipping) degradation and random chain scission with suitable polymer examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Unzipping | Random Scission |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Mechanism | Sequential monomer removal from chain end | Random bond cleavage throughout chain |
| Monomer yield | High (often >90%) | Low |
| Molecular weight | Decreases sharply (often linearly) | Decreases gradually |
| Examples | PMMA, PTFE, PS (radical) | PE, PP, Nylon |
| Activation energy | High (matches ceiling T) | Moderate |
| Product distribution | Narrow (mostly monomer) | Broad (oligomer distribution) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do not confuse PMMA (clean unzipping) with PS (which actually shows *mixed* unzipping + random scission). KTU strictly penalizes wrong polymer-to-mechanism pairing.

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]

**(a)** Explain the free-radical mechanism of thermal-oxidative degradation of polyethylene with a stepwise reaction scheme. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A polymer sample lost 8% mass at 250 °C and 40% mass at 290 °C in 60 minutes in nitrogen. Calculate the activation energy assuming first-order kinetics. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Free-radical thermal-oxidative degradation of polyethylene:**

**Step 1 — Initiation (thermal bond homolysis):**

$$-(\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_2)_n- \xrightarrow{\Delta} -(\text{CH}_2-\dot{\text{C}}\text{H})_m- + -(\dot{\text{C}}\text{H}_2-\text{CH}_2)_p-$$

**Step 2 — Propagation I (oxygen addition):**

$$\text{P}^\bullet + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{POO}^\bullet$$

**Step 3 — Propagation II (hydrogen abstraction):**

$$\text{POO}^\bullet + \text{PH} \longrightarrow \text{POOH} + \text{P}^\bullet$$

**Step 4 — Branching (hydroperoxide decomposition):**

$$\text{POOH} \longrightarrow \text{PO}^\bullet + {}^\bullet\text{OH}$$

**Step 5 — Termination (radical coupling/disproportionation):**

$$\text{P}^\bullet + \text{P}^\bullet \longrightarrow \text{P–P (cross-link)}$$

The result is chain scission, carbonyl formation, and embrittlement — typical of PE aging in air.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute k₁ and k₂ at each temperature:**
- α₁ = 0.08, t = 3600 s → k₁ = −ln(0.92)/3600 = 0.0834/3600 = 2.32 × 10⁻⁵ s⁻¹
- α₂ = 0.40, t = 3600 s → k₂ = −ln(0.60)/3600 = 0.5108/3600 = 1.42 × 10⁻⁴ s⁻¹

**Step 2 — Convert temperatures to Kelvin:**
- T₁ = 250 + 273 = 523 K
- T₂ = 290 + 273 = 563 K

**Step 3 — Compute the inverse-temperature difference:**

$$\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2} = \dfrac{1}{523} - \dfrac{1}{563} = 1.912 \times 10^{-3} - 1.776 \times 10^{-3} = 1.36 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the rate ratio:**

$$\dfrac{k_2}{k_1} = \dfrac{1.42 \times 10^{-4}}{2.32 \times 10^{-5}} = 6.12$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Eₐ formula:**

$$E_a = \dfrac{R \cdot \ln(k_2/k_1)}{(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)} = \dfrac{8.314 \times \ln(6.12)}{1.36 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\ln(6.12) = 1.812$$

$$E_a = \dfrac{8.314 \times 1.812}{1.36 \times 10^{-4}} = \dfrac{15.07}{1.36 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{E_a \approx 110.8 \text{ kJ/mol}}$$

This is a typical Eₐ for PE thermal degradation — **consistent with literature values**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to convert °C → K before substitution. This single error costs **2 marks** in valuation. Also, ensure ln(0.92) is calculated as **−0.0834** (not −0.08 by approximation).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified]

**(a)** With a neat schematic and reaction equations, describe the thermal degradation of PVC. Explain why PVC discolours during processing. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of the following stabilizers in preventing thermal degradation: (i) Lead-based stabilizers, (ii) Organotin compounds, (iii) Hindered amine light stabilizers (HALS). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Thermal Degradation of PVC:**

PVC undergoes **chain-stripping** (dehydrochlorination). The reaction:

$$-(\text{CH}_2-\text{CHCl})_n- \xrightarrow{\Delta} -(\text{CH}=\text{CH})_n- + n\,\text{HCl}$$

**Step-by-step mechanism:**

**Step 1 — Initiation:** A defect site (allylic chlorine, tertiary Cl) acts as the starting point, often an internal catalyst (HCl) also helps.

**Step 2 — Propagation:** A "zipper-like" elimination forms conjugated polyene sequences.

$$-(\text{CH}=\text{CH})-(\text{CH}=\text{CH})-(\text{CH}=\text{CH})-$$

**Step 3 — Polyene sequences** absorb visible light, causing **discolouration** (yellow → brown → black as conjugation length increases).

**Step 4 — Termination:** Cyclization or cross-linking may stop further propagation.

**Why PVC discolours:**
The conjugated C=C sequences are chromophores — the longer the polyene block (typically >7 double bonds), the more visible light it absorbs, giving the polymer its dark appearance. **Every HCl molecule lost creates one new C=C bond.**

**(b) Role of Stabilizers:**

**(i) Lead-based stabilizers (e.g., 2PbO·PbSO₄, tribasic lead sulfate):**
- Neutralize evolved HCl by forming PbCl₂.
- Prevent autocatalytic degradation since HCl acts as a catalyst for further dehydrochlorination.
- Cheapest and most effective; however, toxic — banned in many countries.

**(ii) Organotin compounds (e.g., dibutyltin dilaurate, dioctyltin maleate):**
- Replace labile Cl atoms in PVC with stable Sn–O bonds.
- Scavenge HCl and add to double bonds via Diels–Alder-type reactions.
- Heat-stable and food-grade approved variants exist.

**(iii) HALS (Hindered Amine Light Stabilizers):**
- Function primarily via the **Denisov cycle** — they are *regenerated* during operation, not consumed.
- They scavenge peroxy radicals (POO•) and alkyl radicals (P•).
- Convert to stable nitroxyl radicals (N–O•) which continue the protective cycle.
- Excellent for long-term thermal-oxidative protection, especially in polyolefins.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** KTU strictly differentiates between **thermal stabilizers** (e.g., Pb, organotin for PVC) and **antioxidants/HALS** (for thermal-oxidative protection in polyolefins). Mixing them up loses marks. Also, the Denisov cycle must be named for full credit on HALS.

---

## 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- Thermal degradation = irreversible property change due to heat.
- **Five types:** unzipping, random scission, chain-stripping, cross-linking, thermal-oxidative.
- **Atmosphere matters:** Inert = pyrolysis (clean radical chain); Air = oxidative (peroxy radicals, carbonyls).
- **Ceiling temperature (T_c):** maximum T for polymer existence — PS ~310 °C, PMMA ~220 °C.
- **BDE hierarchy:** C–F > C–H (aromatic) > C–H (aliphatic) > C–C > C–O > C–Cl > C–Br > C–I.
- **Activation energy range:** Eₐ = **80 – 250 kJ/mol** (single-step); higher = more stable.
- **First-order kinetics:** $m/m_0 = \exp(-kt)$, $t_{1/2} = 0.693/k$.
- **Two-point Eₐ formula:** $E_a = R \ln(k_2/k_1) / (1/T_1 - 1/T_2)$.
- **TGA/DTG peak temperature (T_max)** = temperature of max degradation rate.
- **PMMA = clean unzipping** → ~95% MMA monomer recovered (used in recycling).
- **PVC = chain-stripping** → HCl loss causes discolouration via polyene chromophores.
- **PE, PP = random scission** → broad fragment distribution; PE more stable than PP.
- **PTFE = highest thermal stability** (C–F bond ~485 kJ/mol).
- **Thermal stabilizers:** Pb compounds, organotins (for PVC).
- **HALS mechanism:** Denisov cycle, regenerable nitroxyl radicals.
- **Friedman method:** isoconversional, gives α-dependent Eₐ.
- **ASTM standards:** E1640 (TGA), D3418 (DSC), D3850 (TGA for carbon black).
- **KEEP CONVERTING °C → K** in every Arrhenius calculation — examiner's trap.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrolytic Degradation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT632 - POLYMERS & ENVIRONMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hydrolytic Degradation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Hydrolytic Degradation – Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hydrolytic Degradation** is defined as the chemical breakdown of polymer chains through a reaction with water (H₂O), wherein the water molecule cleaves susceptible chemical bonds along the polymer backbone (most commonly ester, amide, carbonate, anhydride, urea, or urethane linkages) to yield lower molecular weight fragments and ultimately small, water-soluble oligomers/monomers.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with **PEPOT632 – Polymers & Environment**, hydrolytic degradation is treated as a sub-class of *chemical degradation* and a primary pathway of *environmental/biological degradation* of condensation polymers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> Hydrolytic degradation is the dominant environmental degradation route for **biodegradable polyesters** (PLA, PHB, PCL, PHA), **polyamides** (nylon-6, nylon-6,6), **polycarbonates**, and **polyurethanes**, because their heteroatom-containing backbones are inherently reactive toward the nucleophilic attack of water.

The general stoichiometric representation of backbone hydrolysis is shown below:

$$\text{[Polymer]-X-R-Y-[Polymer]} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{[Polymer]-X-H} + \text{HO-R-Y-[Polymer]}$$

where **X–R–Y** represents the hydrolyzable linkage (commonly **–CO–O–** in polyesters, **–CO–NH–** in polyamides, **–O–CO–O–** in polycarbonates, or **–CO–O–CO–** in polyanhydrides).

## 1.2 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a long **zipper made of plastic (the polymer chain)**, where the teeth of the zipper are joined by tiny **glue dots (the ester/amide bonds)**. If you sprinkle water on the zipper for a long time, those glue dots slowly dissolve. The zipper does not break in one snap — instead, it gradually splits into two shorter pieces, then four, then eight, until eventually it becomes a pile of tiny broken fragments (monomers/oligomers). The "glue" is the chemical bond; the water is the silent cutter that opens the chain one link at a time.

- **Water** = the cutting/scissor agent
- **Polymer bond** = the glue being dissolved
- **Result** = chain scission → lower molecular weight → loss of mechanical strength → eventual mass loss

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Take-away:** Hydrolytic degradation is essentially *water attacking the weak chemical joints* of the polymer backbone. Polymers without such weak joints (e.g., polyethylene, polypropylene, PTFE) are therefore highly resistant and degrade only by other mechanisms (photo-oxidation, thermal oxidation).

## 1.3 Why Hydrolytic Degradation Matters in Polymer & Environmental Engineering

- **Environmental Persistence Control:** Determines how fast plastic litter disintegrates in soil, marine, or composting environments.
- **Biomedical Applications:** Forms the working principle of *resorbable sutures, drug-delivery scaffolds, and tissue-engineering matrices* (e.g., PLA, PGA, PLGA).
- **Recycling & Chemical Recovery:** Enables monomer recovery via hydrolysis (e.g., hydrolysis of PET to terephthalic acid + ethylene glycol).
- **Failure Analysis:** Predicts service life of polyamides in humid/tropical climates (e.g., Kerala's high humidity).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanistic Classification of Hydrolysis

Hydrolytic degradation can occur by **three fundamentally different catalytic pathways**, depending on the pH of the surrounding medium:

### 2.1.1 Acid-Catalyzed Hydrolysis (Low pH, H⁺)
- Mechanism: Protonation of the carbonyl oxygen → electrophilic carbon becomes more susceptible to water attack.
- Rate increases with **[H⁺]**.
- Common in: Landfill leachates, acidic industrial effluents, gastric environments (pH ~1.2) for oral drug-delivery polymers.

### 2.1.2 Base-Catalyzed Hydrolysis (Alkaline, OH⁻)
- Mechanism: Direct nucleophilic attack of hydroxide on the carbonyl carbon.
- Rate increases dramatically with **[OH⁻]** — typically **10² to 10⁶ times faster** than acid-catalyzed at comparable concentrations.
- Common in: Soaps/detergent exposure, cement matrices (highly alkaline), ocean environments for some polyesters.

### 2.1.3 Neutral Hydrolysis (Water alone)
- Slowest pathway; water acts directly as the nucleophile.
- Operates in pure water, neutral soil moisture, and physiological pH (7.4).

## 2.2 Modes of Hydrolytic Erosion (Spatial Classification)

| Mode | Mechanism | Erosion Front | Example Polymer |
|------|-----------|--------------|-----------------|
| **Surface Erosion** | Hydrolysis confined to outer surface; outer layers erode faster than water diffuses in | Sharp, well-defined | Polyanhydrides, Poly(ortho esters) |
| **Bulk Erosion** | Water diffuses faster than hydrolysis occurs; degradation happens uniformly throughout the matrix | Diffuse | PLA, PLGA, PCL, PHB |
| **Combined/Mixed Erosion** | Transition mode; bulk followed by surface as porosity develops | Intermediate | PLGA at late stages |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Memory Trick:**
> - *Surface erosion* → **PolyANhydrides & PolyORthoesters** (mnemonic: "**SAND**" — Surface, ANhydride, Rapid, Defined).
> - *Bulk erosion* → **PLA / PLGA / PCL / PHB** (mnemonic: "**BLP³**").

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat-Sheet)

| # | Equation / Law | Mathematical Form | Use in KTU Problems |
|---|----------------|------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **First-order hydrolysis kinetics** | $\dfrac{dC_t}{dt} = -k \cdot C_t$ | Mass loss vs. time plots |
| 2 | **Integrated first-order** | $\ln(C_t) = \ln(C_0) - k \cdot t$ | Determining rate constant *k* |
| 3 | **Half-life of hydrolyzable bond** | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k}$ | Predicting shelf-life |
| 4 | **Arrhenius temperature dependence** | $k = A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{R T}\right)$ | Accelerated aging tests |
| 5 | **Observed rate constant (pH)** | $k_{obs} = k_w + k_{H^+}[H^+] + k_{OH^-}[OH^-]$ | pH-dependent degradation |
| 6 | **Degree of hydrolysis (α)** | $\alpha = 1 - \dfrac{[\text{ester bonds}]_t}{[\text{ester bonds}]_0}$ | Conversion tracking |
| 7 | **Number-average chain scission** | $S_n = \dfrac{1}{M_{n,t}} - \dfrac{1}{M_{n,0}}$ | Molecular weight reduction |
| 8 | **Water uptake (Korsmeyer-Peppas)** | $\dfrac{M_t}{M_\infty} = k' \cdot t^{n}$ | Swelling/diffusion kinetics |
| 9 | **Random scission Mₙ relation** | $\dfrac{1}{M_{n,t}} = \dfrac{1}{M_{n,0}} + k_s \cdot t$ | Bulk degradation modeling |
| 10 | **Glass-transition shift (Fox eq.)** | $\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \dfrac{w_1}{T_{g,1}} + \dfrac{w_2}{T_{g,2}}$ | Plasticization by absorbed water |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipe-Symbol Warning for Markdown Tables:** All absolute-value and conditional symbols have been written using `\vert` or `\mid` notation (e.g., `\vert C_t \vert`). **Do NOT use the vertical bar `|` inside table cells** — it breaks the markdown table syntax.

## 2.4 Factors Influencing Hydrolytic Degradation Rate

1. **Chemical Structure of Backbone:** Ester > Anhydride >> Amide >> Ether in reactivity toward water.
2. **Hydrophilicity / Water Uptake:** Higher equilibrium water content ⇒ faster hydrolysis.
3. **Crystallinity:** Amorphous regions degrade first (water penetrates easily); crystalline lamellae degrade later.
4. **Glass Transition Temperature (Tg):** If **T < Tg**, matrix is glassy, diffusion is slow → bulk erosion delayed. If **T > Tg**, rubbery state → fast water diffusion.
5. **pH of Medium:** Alkaline (OH⁻) >> Acidic (H⁺) >> Neutral for most polyesters.
6. **Temperature:** Arrhenius relationship; rule of thumb: rate **doubles for every 10 °C** rise.
7. **Molecular Weight & Distribution:** Lower Mₙ → more chain-end initiated hydrolysis.
8. **Morphology:** Porosity, filler content, plasticizer level.
9. **Steric / Electronic Effects:** Bulky side groups (e.g., methyl in PLA vs. H in PGA) reduce water accessibility.
10. **Catalysts / Enzymes:** Lipases, esterases, cutinases accelerate hydrolysis dramatically.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|-------|-------------|
| **Biomedical** | Resorbable sutures (Vicryl = PLGA), bone screws (PLA), drug-eluting stents |
| **Packaging** | Compostable PLA cups, PLA-lined paper, PHA bags |
| **Agriculture** | Mulch films (PHB/PLA), controlled-release fertilizer coatings |
| **Marine Anti-fouling** | Hydrolyzable polymer binders releasing biocides |
| **Recycling** | Hydrolytic depolymerization of PET → TPA + EG |
| **Civil Engineering** | Alkali-resistant fiber reinforcement understanding (rebar corrosion of nylon ties) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Integrated First-Order Hydrolysis Rate Law

### 3.1.1 Starting Point
The rate of disappearance of hydrolyzable ester linkages (concentration $C_t$ at time $t$) follows a first-order law with respect to $C_t$:

$$\dfrac{dC_t}{dt} = -k \cdot C_t$$

### 3.1.2 Separation of Variables
Rearranging:

$$\dfrac{dC_t}{C_t} = -k \, dt$$

### 3.1.3 Integration from t = 0 (where C = C₀) to t = t (where C = Cₜ)

$$\int_{C_0}^{C_t} \dfrac{dC_t}{C_t} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

$$\ln(C_t) - \ln(C_0) = -k \, t$$

### 3.1.4 Final Integrated Form

$$\boxed{\; \ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_t}{C_0}\right) = -k \, t \;}$$

Or equivalently:

$$C_t = C_0 \cdot \exp(-k \, t)$$

This is the **foundation equation** for plotting $\ln(C_t/C_0)$ vs. $t$ to extract the rate constant $k$ from experimental mass-loss or molecular-weight data.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Half-Life of a Hydrolyzable Bond

### 3.2.1 Definition
Half-life $t_{1/2}$ is the time required for **half** of the original hydrolyzable bonds to be cleaved, i.e., $C_t = C_0 / 2$.

### 3.2.2 Substitution into the Integrated Law

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_0 / 2}{C_0}\right) = -k \cdot t_{1/2}$$

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right) = -k \cdot t_{1/2}$$

### 3.2.3 Solving for $t_{1/2}$

$$-\ln 2 = -k \cdot t_{1/2}$$

$$\boxed{\; t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k} = \dfrac{0.693}{k} \;}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** A PLA suture with $k = 0.05 \, \text{day}^{-1}$ in physiological saline has $t_{1/2} = 13.86$ days. Knowing $t_{1/2}$ allows design of biodegradable implants with predictable resorption times.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Relationship Between Number-Average Molecular Weight and Chain Scission (Random Scission Model)

### 3.3.1 Definition of Chain Scission Number
Define the number of chain scissions per original molecule as $S$:

$$S = \dfrac{M_{n,0}}{M_{n,t}} - 1$$

### 3.3.2 Rate of Chain Scission
For random (statistical) hydrolysis, scission events occur randomly along the backbone. If the rate of bond cleavage is $k_s$ (units: scissions per bond per unit time), then:

$$\dfrac{dS}{dt} = k_s \cdot (\text{effective bond concentration})$$

### 3.3.3 Assuming Each Scission Produces One Additional Molecule
For a polymer with initial number-average degree of polymerization $X_{n,0}$:

$$M_{n,0} = X_{n,0} \cdot M_0, \quad M_{n,t} = \dfrac{X_{n,0} \cdot M_0}{1 + S}$$

where $M_0$ is the molar mass of the repeat unit. Therefore:

$$\dfrac{1}{M_{n,t}} = \dfrac{1}{M_{n,0}} + \dfrac{S}{X_{n,0} \cdot M_0}$$

### 3.3.4 Substituting $S = k_s \cdot t$ (linear scission kinetics)

$$\boxed{\; \dfrac{1}{M_{n,t}} = \dfrac{1}{M_{n,0}} + \dfrac{k_s}{M_0} \cdot t \;}$$

This is the **canonical "1/Mₙ vs. t linearity test"** used in KTU laboratory experiments on PLA/PCL hydrolysis to confirm bulk random-scission behavior.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — pH-Dependent Observed Rate Constant

### 3.4.1 Three Concurrent Pathways
Hydrolysis proceeds simultaneously through (i) neutral water attack ($k_w$), (ii) H⁺ catalysis, and (iii) OH⁻ catalysis:

$$\text{Rate} = k_w \cdot [\text{ester}] + k_{H^+} \cdot [H^+] \cdot [\text{ester}] + k_{OH^-} \cdot [OH^-] \cdot [\text{ester}]$$

### 3.4.2 Factoring Out [ester]

$$\text{Rate} = k_{obs} \cdot [\text{ester}]$$

### 3.4.3 Therefore the Observed Rate Constant

$$\boxed{\; k_{obs} = k_w + k_{H^+} \cdot [H^+] + k_{OH^-} \cdot [OH^-] \;}$$

**Engineering Use:** Plot $\log(k_{obs})$ vs. pH — the curve has a **flat minimum** near neutrality (neutral regime dominates), a **negative slope of −1** in acidic regime (H⁺-catalysis dominates), and a **positive slope of +1** in alkaline regime (OH⁻-catalysis dominates).

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Arrhenius Acceleration for Shelf-Life Prediction

### 3.5.1 Shelf-Life by Extrapolation
Polymer samples are aged at **elevated temperatures** $T_1, T_2, T_3$ and corresponding rate constants $k_1, k_2, k_3$ are measured.

### 3.5.2 Linearized Arrhenius Form

$$\ln(k) = \ln(A) - \dfrac{E_a}{R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{T}$$

### 3.5.3 Plot of $\ln(k)$ vs. $1/T$ yields
- **Slope** $= -E_a / R$ → activation energy
- **Intercept** $= \ln(A)$ → frequency factor

### 3.5.4 Service-Life Extrapolation
Once $k$ at room temperature (say 298 K) is known:

$$\boxed{\; \ln\!\left(\dfrac{k_{T_2}}{k_{T_1}}\right) = -\dfrac{E_a}{R} \left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right) \;}$$

This is the standard **ASTM D3045 / D3041 protocol** referenced in KTU PEPOT632 polymer-durability problems.

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A PLA scaffold degrades in phosphate buffer (pH 7.4, 37 °C) with first-order rate constant $k = 0.018 \, \text{day}^{-1}$. Determine (a) the time for 80% mass loss and (b) the activation energy if doubling the temperature to 47 °C increases $k$ to $0.042 \, \text{day}^{-1}$.

### Part (a): Time for 80% Mass Loss
Mass loss of 80% ⇒ $C_t / C_0 = 0.20$.

$$\ln(0.20) = -k \cdot t$$

$$t = \dfrac{-\ln(0.20)}{0.018} = \dfrac{1.6094}{0.018}$$

$$\boxed{\; t \approx 89.4 \, \text{days} \;}$$

### Part (b): Activation Energy
$T_1 = 310 \, \text{K}$, $T_2 = 320 \, \text{K}$, $k_1 = 0.018$, $k_2 = 0.042$.

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{0.042}{0.018}\right) = -\dfrac{E_a}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{320} - \dfrac{1}{310}\right)$$

$$\ln(2.333) = -\dfrac{E_a}{8.314}\left(\dfrac{310 - 320}{320 \times 310}\right)$$

$$0.8473 = -\dfrac{E_a}{8.314} \times (-9.653 \times 10^{-5})$$

$$0.8473 = \dfrac{E_a}{8.314} \times 9.653 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$E_a = \dfrac{0.8473 \times 8.314}{9.653 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$\boxed{\; E_a \approx 7.30 \times 10^{4} \, \text{J mol}^{-1} = 73.0 \, \text{kJ mol}^{-1} \;}$$

This is consistent with literature values for PLA hydrolysis ($E_a \approx 70$–$90 \, \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Mechanism of Hydrolytic Ester Cleavage

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS[Start Polymer Chain containing ester C O O linkage] --> nodeA
    nodeA[Step 1 Water molecule approaches electrophilic carbonyl carbon] --> nodeB
    nodeB[Step 2 Nucleophilic addition of water to carbonyl C] --> nodeC
    nodeC[Step 3 Tetrahedral intermediate formed] --> nodeD
    nodeD[Step 4 Proton transfer and bond rearrangement] --> nodeE
    nodeE[Step 5 Cleavage of acyl oxygen bond] --> nodeF
    nodeF[Step 6 Two shorter chain fragments produced] --> nodeG
    nodeG[End One carboxyl group and one hydroxyl group formed]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Functional Architecture of Hydrolytic Degradation System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeInput[Polymer Matrix Bulk] --> nodeWater[Water Diffusion into Matrix]
    nodeWater --> nodeUptake[Water Uptake Mt Minfty]
    nodeUptake --> nodeErosion{Erosion Mode}
    nodeErosion -- High Hydrophobicity --> nodeSurf[Surface Erosion]
    nodeErosion -- Hydrophilic Amorphous --> nodeBulk[Bulk Erosion]
    nodeSurf --> nodeOut1[Mass Loss from Outside In]
    nodeBulk --> nodeOut2[Uniform Internal Degradation]
    nodeOut1 --> nodeProps[Property Changes Mwt Tg Crystallinity]
    nodeOut2 --> nodeProps
    nodeProps --> nodeFail[Mechanical Failure and Total Resorption]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Factors Influencing Hydrolytic Degradation Rate

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeCenter[Hydrolytic Degradation Rate] --> nodeF1
    nodeCenter --> nodeF2
    nodeCenter --> nodeF3
    nodeCenter --> nodeF4
    nodeCenter --> nodeF5
    nodeF1[Chemical Backbone Structure Ester Amide Carbonate] --> nodeCenter
    nodeF2[Environmental pH Temperature Humidity] --> nodeCenter
    nodeF3[Polymer Morphology Crystallinity Tg Porosity] --> nodeCenter
    nodeF4[Molecular Weight and Distribution Mw Mn PDI] --> nodeCenter
    nodeF5[Additives Catalysts Enzymes Plasticizers] --> nodeCenter
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Three Catalytic Pathways

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Polymer Substrate Ester Linkage] --> nodeBranch{Catalytic Pathway}
    nodeBranch -- Low pH High Hplus --> nodeAcid[Acid Catalyzed Hydronium attacks carbonyl oxygen]
    nodeBranch -- High pH High OHminus --> nodeBase[Base Catalyzed Hydroxide direct nucleophilic attack]
    nodeBranch -- Neutral pH --> nodeNeut[Neutral Water acts as nucleophile slowly]
    nodeAcid --> nodeOut[Chain Scission and Fragment Formation]
    nodeBase --> nodeOut
    nodeNeut --> nodeOut
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Flow of Hydrolytic Degradation Engineering Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStep1[Step 1 Identify polymer backbone chemistry] --> nodeStep2[Step 2 Determine hydrolyzable linkages present]
    nodeStep2 --> nodeStep3[Step 3 Select relevant catalysis pathway acid base neutral]
    nodeStep3 --> nodeStep4[Step 4 Measure water uptake and diffusion coefficient]
    nodeStep4 --> nodeStep5[Step 5 Conduct kinetic study at multiple temperatures]
    nodeStep5 --> nodeStep6[Step 6 Fit first order or random scission model]
    nodeStep6 --> nodeStep7[Step 7 Extract rate constant k and half life t1 2]
    nodeStep7 --> nodeStep8[Step 8 Apply Arrhenius extrapolation for service life]
    nodeStep8 --> nodeStep9[Step 9 Report degradation profile for biomedical or packaging use]
```

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Define hydrolytic degradation of polymers. Name any four polymers susceptible to it.**

**Model Answer:**
Hydrolytic degradation is the process by which polymer chains undergo chemical scission via reaction with water molecules, breaking hydrolyzable bonds such as ester, amide, carbonate, anhydride, or urethane linkages present in the backbone. The reaction ultimately yields low-molecular-weight fragments and water-soluble monomers/oligomers.

Four susceptible polymers:
1. **Polylactic acid (PLA)** — polyester
2. **Polycaprolactone (PCL)** — polyester
3. **Nylon-6,6** — polyamide
4. **Polycarbonate (PC)** — carbonate linkage

(Mention any four; full credit.)

**Marking Scheme:** Definition = 2 marks, examples = ½ mark each (any 2 examples).

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between surface erosion and bulk erosion with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Surface Erosion | Bulk Erosion |
|-----------|-----------------|--------------|
| Water diffusion vs. hydrolysis rate | Hydrolysis faster than diffusion | Diffusion faster than hydrolysis |
| Erosion front | Sharp | Diffuse |
| Geometry of degrading device | Retained | Shrinks uniformly |
| Example polymer | Polyanhydrides, Poly(ortho esters) | PLA, PLGA, PCL, PHB |
| Drug release kinetics | Zero-order | Often non-linear |

(Any four distinguishing points = 3 marks.)

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the mechanism of hydrolytic degradation of polyesters with suitable chemical equations. Discuss the effect of pH on the degradation rate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PLA sample has initial number-average molecular weight $M_{n,0} = 100{,}000 \, \text{g mol}^{-1}$. After 60 days of immersion in phosphate buffer (pH 7.4, 37 °C), the molecular weight reduces to $M_{n,t} = 50{,}000 \, \text{g mol}^{-1}$. Assuming random scission kinetics and a repeat-unit molar mass $M_0 = 72 \, \text{g mol}^{-1}$, determine (i) the rate constant of chain scission $k_s$ and (ii) the time required for $M_{n,t}$ to drop to $25{,}000 \, \text{g mol}^{-1}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Mechanism (4 marks):**

The polyester backbone contains repeating ester linkages of the type **–CO–O–**. Hydrolysis occurs in the following steps:

**Step 1 — Protonation (acid catalysis) or activation of carbonyl:**

$$\text{R-CO-O-R'} \longrightarrow \text{R-C(OH}^+\text{)-O-R'}$$

**Step 2 — Nucleophilic attack by water:**

$$\text{R-C(OH}^+\text{)-O-R'} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{R-C(OH)(OH)-O-R'}$$

**Step 3 — Tetrahedral intermediate collapse, scission:**

$$\text{R-C(OH)(OH)-O-R'} \longrightarrow \text{R-COOH} + \text{HO-R'}$$

The products are a **carboxylic acid** (R–COOH) and a **hydroxyl group** (HO–R′). One hydrolyzable bond is cleaved per reaction event.

**Effect of pH (3 marks):**

- **Acidic (low pH):** H⁺ protonates carbonyl oxygen, enhancing electrophilicity; rate $\propto [H^+]$.
- **Neutral (pH ~7):** Slow, water-only attack; rate $\approx k_w$.
- **Alkaline (high pH):** OH⁻ directly attacks carbonyl; rate $\propto [OH^-]$; **fastest regime** for most polyesters (10²–10⁶× faster than neutral).

The combined effect is captured by:

$$k_{obs} = k_w + k_{H^+}[H^+] + k_{OH^-}[OH^-]$$

A V-shaped curve of $\log k_{obs}$ vs. pH results, with a flat minimum near neutrality.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:** $M_{n,0} = 100{,}000 \, \text{g mol}^{-1}$, $M_{n,t} = 50{,}000 \, \text{g mol}^{-1}$ at $t = 60$ days, $M_0 = 72 \, \text{g mol}^{-1}$.

**Step 1:** Apply the random-scission relation:

$$\dfrac{1}{M_{n,t}} = \dfrac{1}{M_{n,0}} + \dfrac{k_s}{M_0} \cdot t$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $k_s$:

$$\dfrac{k_s}{M_0} \cdot t = \dfrac{1}{M_{n,t}} - \dfrac{1}{M_{n,0}}$$

$$\dfrac{k_s}{72} \cdot 60 = \dfrac{1}{50{,}000} - \dfrac{1}{100{,}000}$$

$$\dfrac{k_s}{72} \cdot 60 = \dfrac{2 - 1}{100{,}000} = 1 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$k_s \cdot 60 = 72 \times 10^{-5} = 7.2 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$k_s = 1.2 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{day}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 (i) Answer:**

$$\boxed{\; k_s = 1.2 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{day}^{-1} \;}$$

**Step 4:** Find time when $M_{n,t} = 25{,}000 \, \text{g mol}^{-1}$:

$$\dfrac{1}{25{,}000} = \dfrac{1}{100{,}000} + \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^{-5}}{72} \cdot t$$

$$4 \times 10^{-5} - 1 \times 10^{-5} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^{-5}}{72} \cdot t$$

$$3 \times 10^{-5} = 1.667 \times 10^{-7} \cdot t$$

$$t = \dfrac{3 \times 10^{-5}}{1.667 \times 10^{-7}} = 180 \, \text{days}$$

**Step 5 (ii) Answer:**

$$\boxed{\; t = 180 \, \text{days} \;}$$

**Verification:** Notice that halving the molecular weight from 100,000 → 50,000 took 60 days, and from 50,000 → 25,000 took another 120 days — confirming the **non-linear (accelerating)** nature of random-scission hydrolysis.

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Alternate]

**(a)** With neat sketches, classify the various modes of hydrolytic erosion. Discuss the role of crystallinity and glass transition temperature (Tg) on the rate of hydrolytic degradation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A polyester film degrades in seawater at 30 °C with first-order rate constant $k = 4.2 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{day}^{-1}$. If the activation energy of hydrolysis is $E_a = 78 \, \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$, estimate the rate constant at 40 °C. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Modes of erosion (3 marks):** Surface, bulk, and combined erosion (already explained in Section 2.2; student should redraw the diagram and label the erosion front).

**Crystallinity (2 marks):**

- Crystalline regions are **tightly packed** with low free volume → water molecules cannot easily penetrate.
- Hydrolysis therefore proceeds **selectively in amorphous regions** first.
- As amorphous regions erode, crystallinity (%) **increases** during degradation (because crystals remain while amorphous chains dissolve away).
- Highly crystalline polymers (e.g., high-density polyethylene terephthalate) degrade much slower than amorphous analogs.

**Glass transition temperature (2 marks):**

- If the polymer is in the **glassy state (T < Tg)**, segmental mobility is low → water diffusion is slow → degradation is delayed.
- If **T > Tg**, the polymer is rubbery → free volume is high → fast water diffusion → bulk erosion dominates.
- During degradation, $M_n$ drops, which lowers Tg (Fox equation); once Tg falls below operating temperature, the polymer **transitions from glassy to rubbery**, accelerating further degradation — a **self-catalytic acceleration**.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:** $T_1 = 303 \, \text{K}$ (30 °C), $k_1 = 4.2 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{day}^{-1}$, $E_a = 78{,}000 \, \text{J mol}^{-1}$, $T_2 = 313 \, \text{K}$ (40 °C), $R = 8.314 \, \text{J mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$.

**Step 1:** Apply the two-temperature Arrhenius relation:

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = -\dfrac{E_a}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Compute the temperature term:

$$\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1} = \dfrac{1}{313} - \dfrac{1}{303} = \dfrac{303 - 313}{313 \times 303} = \dfrac{-10}{94{,}839} = -1.054 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the exponent:

$$-\dfrac{E_a}{R} = -\dfrac{78{,}000}{8.314} = -9381.4 \, \text{K}$$

$$-\dfrac{E_a}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right) = (-9381.4) \times (-1.054 \times 10^{-4}) = 0.9889$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $k_2$:

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = 0.9889$$

$$\dfrac{k_2}{k_1} = \exp(0.9889) = 2.688$$

$$k_2 = 2.688 \times 4.2 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{\; k_2 = 1.13 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{day}^{-1} \;}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** A 10 °C rise (30 °C → 40 °C) increases the hydrolysis rate by **~2.7 times**, consistent with the rule-of-thumb that polymer degradation roughly triples per 10 °C in tropical/marine environments — directly relevant to **Kerala's coastal climate**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error in Arrhenius:** Students often write $\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}$ reversed, yielding negative exponent → $k_2 < k_1$ (physically wrong).
> 2. **Forgetting to convert °C → K** before plugging into $1/T$.
> 3. **Mixing % mass loss and molecular weight loss** — they are NOT equivalent; one must apply $\alpha$ or $1/M_n$ relations correctly.
> 4. **Confusing $k$ (concentration-based) with $k_s$ (scission-based)** — these have different units (day⁻¹ vs. day⁻¹·mol·g⁻¹).
> 5. **Forgetting to mention** that neutral, acid, and base pathways occur simultaneously — partial credit lost if only one is discussed.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Hydrolytic degradation** = chain scission by **water** attacking backbone ester / amide / carbonate / anhydride / urethane bonds.
- **Most susceptible polymers:** polyesters (PLA, PCL, PHB), polyamides (nylon), polycarbonates, polyurethanes, polyanhydrides.
- **Resistant polymers:** polyethylene, polypropylene, PTFE (no hydrolyzable bonds).
- **Three catalytic pathways:** acid (H⁺), base (OH⁻, fastest), neutral (water only, slowest).
- **Two erosion modes:** *Surface* (polyanhydrides, poly(ortho esters)) vs. *Bulk* (PLA, PLGA, PCL).
- **Key rate law:** first-order with respect to hydrolyzable bond concentration; $\ln(C_t/C_0) = -k \cdot t$.
- **Half-life formula:** $t_{1/2} = 0.693 / k$ — used to predict shelf/service life.
- **Random-scission molecular weight test:** $1/M_{n,t} = 1/M_{n,0} + (k_s/M_0) \cdot t$ — linearity confirms bulk random scission.
- **Arrhenius acceleration:** $\ln(k_2/k_1) = -(E_a/R)(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)$ — used in ASTM aging tests.
- **Crystallinity** shields bonds from water; amorphous regions degrade first.
- **Tg crossing during degradation** accelerates further hydrolysis (self-catalysis via Fox equation).
- **Alkaline conditions** (pH > 9) cause the fastest hydrolysis of polyesters — important for **marine litter, cement composites, and detergent-exposed plastics**.
- **Typical $E_a$ for PLA hydrolysis:** 70–90 kJ/mol; for nylon-6: ~85 kJ/mol; for PET: ~110 kJ/mol (more resistant).
- **Major applications:** resorbable sutures, drug-delivery implants, compostable packaging, marine-degradable fishing nets, agricultural mulch films.
- **Mnemonic to recall susceptible polymers:** "**EPA-PU-N**" — **E**sters, **P**olycarbonates, **A**nhydrides, **P**olyurethanes, U**re**as, **N**ylon-amides.
- **Always convert temperature to Kelvin** in Arrhenius problems (a classic KTU trap).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Environmental Degradation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT632 - POLYMERS & ENVIRONMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Environmental Degradation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Environmental Degradation of Polymers** refers to the irreversible alteration of a polymer's chemical structure, molecular weight, mechanical properties, and aesthetic characteristics resulting from exposure to environmental agents during its service life or after disposal. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Polymers & Environment* (PEPOT632), environmental degradation encompasses all physical, chemical, and biological processes that lead to the progressive deterioration of polymeric materials when subjected to atmospheric, thermal, photochemical, hydrolytic, chemical, and biological stresses present in the natural environment.

The phenomenon is governed by the breakage of primary covalent bonds in the polymer backbone (chain scission) and/or cross-linking reactions, both of which deviate the polymer from its original design specifications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Environmental degradation is a *Module 4* high-weightage topic. Students must be able to differentiate between **degradation**, **deterioration**, and **weathering** — these terms are often used interchangeably in textbooks but carry distinct technical meanings in KTU board examinations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a polymer chain as a **long, perfectly arranged necklace of beads** (where each bead is a monomer unit). When this necklace is placed in the environment, several agents act upon it:

- **Sunlight (UV radiation)** acts like scissors that snip the thread at random places.
- **Heat (thermal energy)** jiggles the beads so violently that the thread loosens and breaks.
- **Water (hydrolysis)** dissolves the knots connecting the beads.
- **Oxygen** rusts the bead-link junctions, weakening them.
- **Microorganisms** chew the beads directly as food.

The result? The once-strong, glossy necklace becomes a brittle, discolored, crumbly, and weakened remnant of its former self. This is precisely **environmental degradation** — a slow, cumulative, and often irreversible process that limits the service life of every polymer product.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Summary:** Polymers don't degrade because they "wear out" like metals — they degrade because their **molecular bonds** are broken by environmental energy inputs and chemical attacks, fundamentally altering the polymer's architecture.

### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

| Aspect | Significance in KTU 2024 Scheme |
|---|---|
| **Engineering Relevance** | Determines service life of plastics, rubbers, coatings, and composites used in infrastructure, packaging, and biomedical devices. |
| **Environmental Policy** | Directly linked to plastic waste management, recycling, and the design of biodegradable alternatives. |
| **Industrial Application** | Stabilizer selection, additive formulation, and product design in the polymer industry. |
| **Bloom's Taxonomy Level** | Tests range from *L2 (Understand)* to *L5 (Evaluate)* per KTU 2024 CO mapping. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for Exams:**
> - **Degradation:** Change in chemical structure (chain scission/crosslinking).
> - **Deterioration:** Loss of useful properties (mechanical, optical, electrical).
> - **Weathering:** Degradation specifically due to outdoor environmental exposure (sun, rain, pollutants).
> - **Aging:** General term for property changes over time, which may include degradation.

### 1.4 Classification of Environmental Degradation (Overview)

Environmental degradation of polymers is broadly classified into the following categories — each of which will be analyzed in subsequent sections:

1. **Photo-oxidative Degradation** (UV + Oxygen)
2. **Thermal Degradation** (Heat-induced)
3. **Thermo-oxidative Degradation** (Heat + Oxygen)
4. **Hydrolytic Degradation** (Water/Moisture)
5. **Chemical Degradation** (Acids, Alkalis, Solvents, Ozone)
6. **Biodegradation** (Microbial action)
7. **Mechanical Degradation** (Stress, Fatigue, Wear)
8. **Radiation Degradation** (Gamma, Electron beam)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Mechanisms of Major Degradation Types

#### 2.1.1 Photo-oxidative Degradation

**Definition:** The combined action of ultraviolet (UV) radiation (wavelength **290–400 nm**) and atmospheric oxygen on a polymer, leading to chain scission, crosslinking, and the formation of carbonyl/hydroperoxide groups.

**Mechanism (Free-Radical Chain Mechanism):**

**Step 1 — Initiation (Photolysis):**
The polymer (PH) absorbs a UV photon, producing an alkyl radical.

$$ \text{PH} \xrightarrow{h\nu} \text{P}^{\bullet} + \text{H}^{\bullet} $$

**Step 2 — Propagation I (Oxygen attack):**
The alkyl radical rapidly combines with atmospheric oxygen to form a peroxy radical.

$$ \text{P}^{\bullet} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{POO}^{\bullet} $$

**Step 3 — Propagation II (Hydrogen abstraction):**
The peroxy radical abstracts a hydrogen atom from another polymer chain, forming a hydroperoxide and a new alkyl radical (chain reaction).

$$ \text{POO}^{\bullet} + \text{PH} \rightarrow \text{POOH} + \text{P}^{\bullet} $$

**Step 4 — Branching (Hydroperoxide decomposition):**
Hydroperoxides decompose under UV light, generating new radicals that propagate the reaction further.

$$ \text{POOH} \xrightarrow{h\nu} \text{PO}^{\bullet} + {}^{\bullet}\text{OH} $$

**Step 5 — Termination:**
Radicals combine to form inert products, ending the chain reaction.

$$ \text{POO}^{\bullet} + \text{POO}^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{POOP} + \text{O}_2 $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why it happens:** Polymers containing chromophores (C=O, C=C, aromatic rings) absorb UV light. The energy absorbed exceeds the dissociation energy of C–C (~**350 kJ/mol**) and C–H (~**410 kJ/mol**) bonds, causing homolytic cleavage.

#### 2.1.2 Thermal Degradation

Polymers undergo thermal degradation when exposed to elevated temperatures either in inert (pyrolysis) or oxidative atmospheres.

**Three Primary Mechanisms:**

**(a) Chain-end Scission (Unzipping / Depolymerization):**
Most common in polymers with weak chain-end bonds (e.g., PMMA, polystyrene). Monomers are released sequentially.

$$ \text{...(CH}_2\text{-CR(X))}_n \rightarrow \text{...(CH}_2\text{-CR(X))}_{n-1} + \text{CH}_2\text{=CR(X)} $$

**(b) Random Chain Scission:**
Bond cleavage occurs randomly along the backbone (typical of polyethylene, polypropylene). Molecular weight decreases rapidly without significant monomer yield.

**(c) Chain-stripping (Side-group elimination):**
Side groups are eliminated as small molecules (e.g., HCl from PVC, acetic acid from PVA).

$$ \text{...(CH}_2\text{-CHCl)}_n \rightarrow \text{...(CH=CH)}_n + n\,\text{HCl} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** PVC undergoes **chain-stripping** with HCl elimination, which causes severe discoloration (from white → yellow → brown → black) due to conjugated polyene sequences.

#### 2.1.3 Hydrolytic Degradation

Affects condensation polymers containing hydrolyzable linkages (esters, amides, carbonates). Water cleaves the backbone.

**General Hydrolysis Reaction:**

$$ \text{...-CO-NH-...} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{...-COOH} + \text{H}_2\text{N-...} $$

**Rate of Hydrolytic Degradation (KTU Formula):**

$$ \frac{dM_n}{dt} = -k \cdot [\text{H}_2\text{O}] \cdot M_n $$

Where:
- $M_n$ = number-average molecular weight at time $t$
- $k$ = hydrolytic rate constant (depends on pH, temperature, polymer structure)
- $[\text{H}_2\text{O}]$ = water concentration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Polymers Susceptible to Hydrolysis:** Polyesters, polyamides (nylon), polycarbonates, polyurethanes, and biopolymers like PLA, PHB, and PCL.

#### 2.1.4 Biodegradation

A two-step process mediated by microorganisms (bacteria, fungi, algae):

**Step 1 — Biodeterioration:** Microbial colonization on the surface; enzymes (hydrolases, oxidases) attack the polymer.
**Step 2 — Biofragmentation:** Polymer chains are cleaved into oligomers and monomers.
**Step 3 — Assimilation & Mineralization:** Microorganisms metabolize fragments, releasing **CO₂**, **H₂O**, **CH₄**, and biomass.

**KTU Indicator of Biodegradation:**

$$ \text{BOD}_{28} = \frac{\text{Oxygen consumed in 28 days}}{\text{Theoretical oxygen demand}} \times 100\% $$

A polymer is considered "biodegradable" per ASTM D6400 if **>90% mineralization** occurs within **180 days** under composting conditions.

#### 2.1.5 Chemical Degradation

- **Ozone Cracking:** Occurs in elastomers with C=C double bonds (natural rubber, polybutadiene). Ozone attacks double bonds, forming ozonides that crack under stress.
- **Acid/Alkali Attack:** Especially affects polyamides, polyesters, and natural polymers (cellulose).
- **Solvents:** Cause swelling, plasticization, or dissolution (environmental stress cracking).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Formula | Units / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Beer-Lambert Law (UV absorption) | $A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$ | $A$ = absorbance, $\varepsilon$ = molar absorptivity (L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹), $c$ = concentration (mol/L), $l$ = path length (cm) |
| 2 | Hydrolytic degradation rate | $\frac{dM_n}{dt} = -k[\text{H}_2\text{O}] M_n$ | $M_n$ in g/mol, $k$ in s⁻¹ |
| 3 | First-order rate constant (Arrhenius) | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$ | $E_a$ = activation energy (kJ/mol), $R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹ |
| 4 | Degree of degradation (chain scissions) | $\alpha = \frac{1}{P_{n,t}} - \frac{1}{P_{n,0}}$ | $P_n$ = number-average degree of polymerization |
| 5 | Mass loss during degradation | $\text{ML}\% = \frac{m_0 - m_t}{m_0} \times 100$ | $m_0$ = initial mass, $m_t$ = mass at time $t$ |
| 6 | BOD-based biodegradability | $\text{BOD}_{28}\% = \frac{\text{BOD}_{28}}{\text{ThOD}} \times 100$ | Threshold for biodegradability: $\geq 60\text{–}90\%$ |
| 7 | Tensile strength retention | $\sigma_{\text{ret}}\% = \frac{\sigma_t}{\sigma_0} \times 100$ | Often used in weathering studies |
| 8 | Quantum yield of photodegradation | $\Phi = \frac{\text{Number of chains scissioned}}{\text{Number of photons absorbed}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 9 | Activation energy for thermal degradation | $E_a = 2.19 \cdot R \cdot T_{50\%}$ (Broido's method) | $T_{50\%}$ = temperature at 50% mass loss (K) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on units:** All temperatures in thermodynamic formulas MUST be in **Kelvin (K)**. A common error in KTU exams is using Celsius — this is a guaranteed **0.5 mark** deduction as per the KTU official marking scheme.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: First-Order Hydrolytic Degradation Kinetics

**Problem Setup:** A polyester sample (PET) with initial number-average molecular weight $M_{n,0} = 30{,}000$ g/mol is exposed to humid conditions at 60 °C. Given the hydrolytic rate constant $k = 1.2 \times 10^{-7}$ s⁻¹ and assuming constant water activity, determine the molecular weight after 100 days.

**Step 1 — State the governing differential equation:**

$$ \frac{dM_n}{dt} = -k \cdot M_n $$

This is a first-order ordinary differential equation describing exponential decay of molecular weight with time.

**Step 2 — Separate variables:**

$$ \frac{dM_n}{M_n} = -k \, dt $$

**Step 3 — Integrate both sides from initial state $(t=0, M_n = M_{n,0})$ to arbitrary time $t$:**

$$ \int_{M_{n,0}}^{M_n} \frac{dM_n}{M_n} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt $$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integrals:**

$$ \ln(M_n) - \ln(M_{n,0}) = -k \cdot t $$

$$ \ln\left( \frac{M_n}{M_{n,0}} \right) = -k \cdot t $$

**Step 5 — Exponentiate to obtain the explicit solution:**

$$ M_n(t) = M_{n,0} \cdot e^{-k t} $$

**Step 6 — Convert the time to consistent units (seconds):**

$$ t = 100 \text{ days} \times 24 \text{ h/day} \times 3600 \text{ s/h} = 8.64 \times 10^{6} \text{ s} $$

**Step 7 — Compute the exponent:**

$$ k \cdot t = (1.2 \times 10^{-7}) \times (8.64 \times 10^{6}) = 1.0368 $$

**Step 8 — Compute the final molecular weight:**

$$ M_n(t) = 30{,}000 \times e^{-1.0368} $$

$$ e^{-1.0368} = 0.3547 $$

$$ M_n(t) = 30{,}000 \times 0.3547 = 10{,}641 \text{ g/mol} $$

**Step 9 — Interpretation:** After 100 days, the molecular weight has dropped to **~35.5% of its original value**, indicating significant hydrolytic degradation. The material would have lost most of its mechanical strength.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Style Mark Distribution:** Stepwise derivation = 5 marks, substitution = 2 marks, final answer with units = 1 mark, interpretation = 1 mark.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Activation Energy via Broido's Method

**Broido's equation** relates the activation energy of thermal degradation to the temperature at 50% mass loss (obtained from TGA):

$$ \ln\left[ \ln\left( \frac{1}{1 - \alpha} \right] \right) = -\frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T} + \text{constant} $$

Where $\alpha$ is the fractional mass loss and $T$ is the absolute temperature.

**Step 1 — Linearization:** Plotting $\ln[\ln(1/(1-\alpha))]$ versus $1/T$ yields a straight line with slope $-E_a/R$.

**Step 2 — Slope determination:** Suppose a TGA curve gives the following data:

| $\alpha$ | $T$ (K) | $\frac{1}{T}$ (K⁻¹) | $\ln[\ln(1/(1-\alpha))]$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.20 | 620 | $1.613 \times 10^{-3}$ | $-1.500$ |
| 0.40 | 645 | $1.550 \times 10^{-3}$ | $-0.673$ |
| 0.50 | 655 | $1.527 \times 10^{-3}$ | $-0.367$ |
| 0.60 | 665 | $1.504 \times 10^{-3}$ | $-0.087$ |
| 0.80 | 685 | $1.460 \times 10^{-3}$ | $0.476$ |

**Step 3 — Linear regression (or two-point method):**
Using the first and last points:

$$ \text{slope} = \frac{0.476 - (-1.500)}{(1.460 - 1.613) \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1.976}{-1.53 \times 10^{-4}} = -12{,}915 \text{ K} $$

**Step 4 — Calculate activation energy:**

$$ E_a = -\text{slope} \times R = 12{,}915 \times 8.314 = 107{,}380 \text{ J/mol} \approx 107.4 \text{ kJ/mol} $$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:** A high $E_a$ value (~107 kJ/mol) suggests good thermal stability — typical of aromatic polymers (e.g., PET, polyimides). Aliphatic polymers like PE typically show $E_a \approx 80$ kJ/mol.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Degradation Susceptibility

| Polymer | UV | Thermal | Hydrolytic | Biodegradable | Common Degradation Pathway |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Polyethylene (PE)** | Moderate | Low | Very low | No | Crosslinking, chain scission |
| **Polypropylene (PP)** | High | Moderate | Very low | No | Chain scission (tertiary carbon) |
| **Polystyrene (PS)** | Moderate | Moderate | Very low | No | Crosslinking, yellowing |
| **PVC** | Moderate | Low | Very low | No | HCl elimination, discoloration |
| **PET** | Moderate | Good | Moderate | Slow | Ester hydrolysis |
| **Nylon 6,6** | Moderate | Good | Moderate | Slow | Amide hydrolysis |
| **PMMA** | Excellent | Moderate | Very low | No | Unzipping depolymerization |
| **Natural Rubber** | High | Moderate | Low | Moderate | Crosslinking, ozone cracking |
| **PLA (bioplastic)** | Moderate | Low | High | Yes | Ester hydrolysis + microbial |
| **PHB (bioplastic)** | Moderate | Low | High | Yes | Hydrolysis + enzymatic |
| **PC (Polycarbonate)** | Moderate | Good | Moderate | No | Hydrolysis (slow) |

### 3.4 Derivation 3: Quantum Yield Calculation for Photodegradation

**Problem:** A polystyrene film of mass 10 g, exposed to UV radiation ($\lambda = 300$ nm) for 1 hour, undergoes chain scission reducing $M_n$ from 200,000 to 180,000. Calculate the quantum yield.

**Step 1 — Number of chain scissions per gram:**

$$ \text{scissions/gram} = \frac{1}{M_{n,t}} - \frac{1}{M_{n,0}} = \frac{1}{180{,}000} - \frac{1}{200{,}000} $$

$$ = 5.556 \times 10^{-6} - 5.000 \times 10^{-6} = 5.56 \times 10^{-7} \text{ mol/g} $$

**Step 2 — Total scissions in 10 g sample:**

$$ N_{\text{scissions}} = 5.56 \times 10^{-7} \times 10 = 5.56 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mol} $$

$$ = 5.56 \times 10^{-6} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = 3.35 \times 10^{18} \text{ events} $$

**Step 3 — Energy of one photon at 300 nm:**

$$ E_{\text{photon}} = \frac{hc}{\lambda} = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^{8})}{300 \times 10^{-9}} $$

$$ = 6.626 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} = 3.99 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} $$

**Step 4 — Total photons absorbed (assuming complete absorption in film):**

If the UV source provides $I = 1 \times 10^{15}$ photons/second:

$$ N_{\text{photons}} = 1 \times 10^{15} \times 3600 = 3.6 \times 10^{18} \text{ photons} $$

**Step 5 — Quantum yield:**

$$ \Phi = \frac{N_{\text{scissions}}}{N_{\text{photons}}} = \frac{3.35 \times 10^{18}}{3.6 \times 10^{18}} = 0.93 $$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:** A quantum yield close to 1 indicates a highly efficient photodegradation process, characteristic of a chain reaction where one photon initiates multiple bond breakages via radical propagation.

### 4.1 Diagram: Free-Radical Photo-oxidative Degradation Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Polymer PH"] -->|UV photon hν| B["Alkyl Radical P• + H•"]
    B -->|O2 attack| C["Peroxy Radical POO•"]
    C -->|H abstraction| D["Hydroperoxide POOH + New P•"]
    D -->|UV photon hν| E["Alkoxy PO• + Hydroxyl •OH"]
    E --> F["Chain Scission Products"]
    E --> G["Carbonyl Groups C=O"]
    E --> H["Crosslinked Network"]
    C -->|Termination| I["Stable Inert Products POOP"]
    E --> I
```

**Description:** This flow diagram illustrates the classical free-radical chain mechanism of photo-oxidative degradation. The process is initiated by UV absorption, propagated by oxygen attack, and branched by hydroperoxide decomposition, leading to chain scission, crosslinking, and the formation of oxygenated functional groups.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between degradation, deterioration, and weathering of polymers with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Degradation:** Refers to irreversible changes in the *chemical structure* of the polymer, such as chain scission, crosslinking, or oxidation. *Example:* Hydrolysis of PET ester linkages reducing molecular weight.
- **Deterioration:** Refers to the *loss of useful physical/mechanical properties* of a polymer, which may or may not be accompanied by chemical changes. *Example:* Loss of tensile strength in aged rubber.
- **Weathering:** A *specific form of degradation* caused by outdoor environmental exposure — UV light, moisture, heat cycling, and atmospheric pollutants. *Example:* Yellowing and chalking of PVC window frames after prolonged outdoor use.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write these three terms as synonyms. KTU answer key explicitly demands *chemical-level vs. property-level vs. environmental-source* distinction. **0.5 mark deducted** for ambiguous definitions.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain the role of hydroperoxides (POOH) in the photo-oxidative degradation of polymers.**

**Model Answer:**

Hydroperoxides (POOH) are the *key intermediates* in photo-oxidative degradation. They are formed during the propagation step when peroxy radicals (POO•) abstract hydrogen atoms from polymer chains. Although they are relatively stable in the dark, **UV radiation causes their homolytic cleavage** to produce alkoxy (PO•) and hydroxyl (•OH) radicals. These radicals initiate *new chain reactions*, leading to chain scission, crosslinking, and the formation of carbonyl groups. The accumulation and subsequent photolysis of POOH is therefore a *branching step* that accelerates the overall degradation process. Quantification of POOH concentration (e.g., via FTIR or iodometric titration) is a common method to assess the extent of photo-oxidative aging.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the free-radical mechanism of photo-oxidative degradation of polymers. Discuss the role of UV absorbers and HALS as stabilizers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A polymer sample has an initial number-average molecular weight of 150,000 g/mol. After 200 days of hydrolytic exposure, the molecular weight drops to 90,000 g/mol. Assuming first-order kinetics, calculate: (i) the rate constant, and (ii) the time required for the molecular weight to drop to 50,000 g/mol. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Mechanism (5 marks):**

**Step 1 — Initiation:** Polymer absorbs a UV photon, generating an alkyl radical:
$$ \text{PH} \xrightarrow{h\nu} \text{P}^{\bullet} + \text{H}^{\bullet} $$

**Step 2 — Propagation:** The alkyl radical reacts with oxygen to form a peroxy radical, which abstracts hydrogen from another chain:
$$ \text{P}^{\bullet} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{POO}^{\bullet} $$
$$ \text{POO}^{\bullet} + \text{PH} \rightarrow \text{POOH} + \text{P}^{\bullet} $$

**Step 3 — Branching:** UV-induced cleavage of POOH generates more radicals:
$$ \text{POOH} \xrightarrow{h\nu} \text{PO}^{\bullet} + {}^{\bullet}\text{OH} $$

**Step 4 — Termination:** Radical combination gives inert products.

**Step 5 — Result:** Chain scission, crosslinking, formation of carbonyl (C=O) and hydroxyl (–OH) groups, yellowing, embrittlement.

**Role of Stabilizers (2 marks):**

- **UV Absorbers (e.g., benzophenones, benzotriazoles):** Absorb harmful UV radiation and dissipate it as harmless thermal energy via tautomerization, preventing photon absorption by the polymer.
- **HALS (Hindered Amine Light Stabilizers, e.g., Tinuvin):** Convert peroxy radicals to stable nitroxyl radicals via the *Denisov cycle*, effectively interrupting the radical chain reaction. HALS are *regenerative* and highly efficient at low concentrations.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $M_{n,0} = 150{,}000$ g/mol, $M_n(200 \text{ days}) = 90{,}000$ g/mol.

**Step 1 — First-order integrated rate law:**

$$ M_n(t) = M_{n,0} \cdot e^{-k t} $$

**Step 2 — Solve for k:**

$$ 90{,}000 = 150{,}000 \cdot e^{-k \times 200} $$

$$ \frac{90{,}000}{150{,}000} = e^{-200 k} $$

$$ 0.6 = e^{-200 k} $$

$$ \ln(0.6) = -200 k $$

$$ k = \frac{-(-0.5108)}{200} = 2.554 \times 10^{-3} \text{ day}^{-1} $$

**Step 3 — Time to reach 50,000 g/mol:**

$$ 50{,}000 = 150{,}000 \cdot e^{-2.554 \times 10^{-3} \times t} $$

$$ \frac{1}{3} = e^{-2.554 \times 10^{-3} t} $$

$$ \ln\left(\frac{1}{3}\right) = -2.554 \times 10^{-3} \cdot t $$

$$ t = \frac{1.0986}{2.554 \times 10^{-3}} = 430.2 \text{ days} $$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $k = 2.554 \times 10^{-3}$ day⁻¹
- (ii) $t \approx 430$ days

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the different mechanisms of thermal degradation of polymers with suitable examples. Compare chain-end scission and random chain scission. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the mechanism of biodegradation of polymers. What are the conditions required for a polymer to be classified as biodegradable as per ASTM standards? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Three mechanisms of thermal degradation (6 marks):**

**(i) Chain-end Scission / Unzipping (Depolymerization):**
The polymer chain "unzips" from the chain end, releasing monomer units sequentially. Common in polymers with *weak chain-end bonds* and *high ceiling temperature*. *Example:* Polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) at 300 °C gives >95% methyl methacrylate monomer. Also seen in polystyrene and polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE).

$$ \text{P}_n \rightarrow \text{P}_{n-1} + \text{M (monomer)} $$

**(ii) Random Chain Scission:**
Bond cleavage occurs *randomly* along the polymer backbone, producing fragments of varying sizes. Molecular weight drops rapidly, but monomer yield is low. *Example:* Polyethylene, polypropylene, polyisobutylene.

$$ \text{P}_n \rightarrow \text{P}_p + \text{P}_{n-p} $$

**(iii) Chain-stripping / Side-group Elimination:**
Side groups are eliminated as small volatile molecules, leaving behind an unsaturated polymer backbone. *Example:* PVC loses HCl at ~200 °C to form conjugated polyene sequences (causing discoloration); poly(vinyl acetate) eliminates acetic acid.

$$ \text{(CH}_2\text{-CHCl)}_n \rightarrow \text{(CH=CH)}_n + n\,\text{HCl} $$

**Comparison (1 mark):**

| Feature | Chain-end Scission | Random Chain Scission |
|---|---|---|
| Monomer yield | Very high (>90%) | Very low (<5%) |
| Molecular weight drop | Gradual | Rapid |
| Initiation site | Chain end | Random backbone |
| Typical polymers | PMMA, PS, PTFE | PE, PP, PIB |

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Mechanism of Biodegradation (5 marks):**

Biodegradation is a *two-stage* process:

**Stage 1 — Abiotic Hydrolysis (for most biodegradable polymers):**
Water cleaves hydrolyzable bonds (esters, amides) in the polymer backbone, reducing molecular weight. This is a *precondition* for biodegradation because microorganisms cannot directly attack high-molecular-weight polymers. *Example:* PLA, PHB, PCL.

**Stage 2 — Biotic Mineralization:**
Microorganisms (bacteria, fungi) secrete extracellular enzymes (esterases, lipases, oxidases) that further degrade the oligomers into monomers, which are then assimilated into microbial cells and metabolized through aerobic (→ CO₂ + H₂O) or anaerobic (→ CH₄ + CO₂) pathways.

**Sequence:**
$$ \text{High MW polymer} \xrightarrow{\text{Hydrolysis}} \text{Oligomers} \xrightarrow{\text{Enzymes}} \text{Monomers} \xrightarrow{\text{Metabolism}} \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Biomass} $$

**ASTM D6400 Standards (2 marks):**

A polymer is classified as **biodegradable** if:
1. **>90% conversion to CO₂** within **180 days** under controlled composting conditions (58 ± 2 °C).
2. The degradation products are **non-toxic** to soil and plants.
3. The material must **not leave more than 10% of its mass** in fragments larger than 2 mm.
4. Heavy metal content must be **below specified limits** (e.g., Zn < 150 mg/kg, Pb < 50 mg/kg, Cd < 0.5 mg/kg).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. Forgetting to include the **ASTM 180-day criterion** in the biodegradability answer is the single most common mark-loss error. **−1.5 marks.**
> 2. In mechanism questions, students often skip the **chain propagation steps** and write only initiation and termination. KTU answer key awards marks for **each step explicitly**. **−1 mark per missing step.**
> 3. In numerical problems, **forgetting to convert time units** (days → seconds or vice versa) is a recurring error. Always state the unit conversion explicitly. **−0.5 mark.**
> 4. Do NOT confuse *biodegradation* with *biostabilization* — they are opposites.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU PEPOT632 Module 4: Environmental Degradation**

**Core Concepts:**
- Degradation = **chemical change**; Deterioration = **property loss**; Weathering = **outdoor environmental exposure**.
- Photo-oxidative degradation follows a **free-radical chain mechanism**: Initiation → Propagation → Branching → Termination.
- Thermal degradation has three mechanisms: **chain-end scission, random scission, and chain-stripping**.
- Hydrolytic degradation affects **condensation polymers** (esters, amides, carbonates).
- Biodegradation is a **two-step process**: abiotic hydrolysis followed by biotic mineralization.

**Key Numbers to Memorize:**
- UV range for polymer degradation: **290–400 nm**.
- C–C bond energy: **~350 kJ/mol**; C–H bond energy: **~410 kJ/mol**.
- Threshold for biodegradability (ASTM D6400): **>90% mineralization in 180 days**.
- BOD₂₈ test period: **28 days**.
- Universal gas constant: $R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹ (or **8.314 × 10⁻³ kJ·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**).

**Key Equations (Must-Know for KTU 2024):**
- First-order degradation: $M_n(t) = M_{n,0} \cdot e^{-k t}$
- Arrhenius equation: $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$
- Broido's equation: $\ln[\ln(1/(1-\alpha))]$ vs. $1/T$ plot → slope = $-E_a/R$
- Mass loss: $\text{ML}\% = \frac{m_0 - m_t}{m_0} \times 100$
- Quantum yield: $\Phi = \frac{\text{Chain scissions}}{\text{Photons absorbed}}$

**Stabilizers (Frequently Asked):**
- **UV Absorbers** (benzophenones, benzotriazoles): block UV.
- **HALS**: trap radicals via the Denisov cycle — regenerative.
- **Antioxidants** (phenolic, phosphite): scavenge free radicals.
- **Heat stabilizers** (metal soap stabilizers for PVC): prevent HCl elimination.

**Common Exam Traps:**
- Do not confuse **biodegradable** with **biostable**.
- Do not confuse **photodegradation** (UV only) with **photo-oxidative degradation** (UV + O₂).
- PVC discoloration = **chain-stripping**, not random scission.
- Always use **Kelvin** in Arrhenius/Broido equations.

**KTU 2024 Bloom's Level Distribution:**
- L1–L2 (Remember/Understand): Definitions, mechanisms, classifications.
- L3 (Apply): Solving numerical problems (rate constants, molecular weight prediction).
- L4–L5 (Analyze/Evaluate): Comparative analysis, stabilizer selection, case studies.
